a WAV file. In this study, the sound was acquired at above 0.3 MW/m2 because the boiling state was developed over the heat transfer surface. The sound data were divided by the stride in sequence length as 0.1 s for 3 s. The amplifier (Focusrite Clarett+ 2 Pre USB) had a maximum sampling rate of 192 kHz.
2.2.2. Feature extraction
The data processing for the measured sound source is shown in Fig. 3. A Hamming window was applied to the extracted data. A fast Fourier transform (FFT) algorithm was then applied to the data, and a spectrum was obtained. A common logarithm was applied to the obtained spectrum 20 times, and the spectrum was converted to decibels (dB). The dB data in the spectrum model were cut and brought within the 200–1800
Hz band for ML. We applied an inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) algorithm to the dB data to obtain the cepstrum. We abandoned four points at the beginning of the cepstrum model and carried out ML using 160 points beginning from the fifth point. The libraries used for the FFT and IFFT were scipy.fft.fft and scipy.fft.ifft, respectively; the version was scipy 1.7.1.
2.2.3. Classification and regression models
The Tensor Flow and Keras ML libraries were used, along with the Anaconda3 2021.11 and Python 3.9.7 program development environments. We employed a sequential model with supervised learning. The classification model was constructed using heat flux as a label to detect profiles on the boiling curves. The label numbers of the subcooling degrees of 40, 50, and 60 K were 10, 12, and 13, respectively. In the network architecture of the spectrum classification model, the number of inputs was 4800 and the number of outputs of layers 1–4 resulted in five output levels, matching the number of the labels with 2400, 1200, 600, and 300 outputs in all five levels of the combination neural networks. The five levels used the softmax function as an activation function for layers 1–4 via a rectified linear unit (ReLU) function. In the network of the cepstrum classification model, the number of inputs was 160 and the number of outputs of layers 1–4 resulted in five output levels, matching the number of the labels with 80, 40, 20, and 10 in all five levels of each combination neural network. The five levels used the softmax function as an activation function for layers 1–4 layer via an ReLU function. The learning rate was set at 0.001. The optimization algorithm was cross entropy. The Adam algorithm was used to optimize the loss function in the classification models. We constructed regression models for heat flux and wall heat. In the network of the spectrum regression model, the number of inputs was assumed to be 4800, the number of outputs of layers 1–5 was 2400, 1200, 600, 300, and 1 in all five levels of each combination neural network. An ReLU function was used as an activation function for layers 1–4. In the network of the cepstrum regression model, the number of inputs was assumed to be 160 and the number of outputs of layers 1–5 was 80, 40, 20, 10, and 1 in all five levels of each combination neural network. An ReLU function was used as an activation function for layers 1–4. The learning rate was set at 0.001 in mean squared error (MSE) levels, and the Adam algorithm was used to optimize the loss function in these regression models. To inspect the performance of the learned model, we prepared sound data with Gaussian noise added. With respect to the standard deviation (SD) of the Gaussian noise, the ratio for the SD of the sound-pressure level in the original sound data was 4%, 8%, 12%, 16%, and 20%. The spectrum and cepstrum were obtained for the data to which this noise was added equally; the spectrum and cepstrum were then input into the model that had been learned. We used Microsoft Windows 10 Enterprise 64-bit OS, an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 12GB GPU, and an Intel Core i5-10400 2.9GHz CPU to process the data.
3. Results and discussion
Fig. 4 shows boiling bubbles in the nucleate boiling regime, the CHF, and the MEB at various degrees of liquid subcooling. Fig. 5 depicts the obtained boiling curves, along with the predictions of boiling heat transfer in the nucleate boiling regime (Kutateladze, 1952; Stephan and Abdelsalam, 1980). Despite the different subcooling, no significant difference was observed in the boiling behavior in the nucleate boiling regime. Therefore, the boiling curves in Fig. 5 correspond well to each other in this regime. However, coalescing bubbles were generated on the heat-transfer surface at the CHF. The Ivey–Morris correlation is used to

predict the CHF in subcooled pool boiling (Ivey and Morris, 1966), and a higher CHF is known to be observed with greater liquid subcooling. In
the present study, the obtained CHFs were 3.4, 4.6, and 5.2 MW/m2 at liquid subcooling degrees of 40, 50, and 60 K, respectively. After the transition to MEB, large coalescing bubbles were not generated and many microbubbles were emitted from the heat transfer surface. ML was used for three cases of calculations of ΔTsub; however, the results for ΔTsub = 40 and 60 K are not shown. Only the results for ΔTsub = 50 K are discussed here because the tendency of the results does not change. Fig. 6 shows confusion matrices of the deep neural network (DNN) classification for the spectrum dataset with various SDs. The classification performance worsens from the low-heat-flux side because of increasing noise. Confusion matrices of the DNN classification for the cepstrum dataset with various SDs are shown in Fig. 7. The classification performance of the cepstrum model decreases with increasing noise near the heat-flux bunch of the medium domain. In the case of 20% noise, the learning rate is maintained in the high-heat-flux regime. The accuracy and cross-entropy loss distributions for the DNN classification model with Gaussian noise are shown in Fig. 8. In the accuracy and cross-entropy loss distributions, the cepstrum model shows greater noise tolerance than the spectrum model. Fig. 9 shows the heat flux predicted by the regression DNN model for the spectrum dataset with various SDs. In the absence of noise, the predictions agree well with the experimental values. However, the precision deteriorates in the intermediate-heat-flux region as noise increases. The heat flux predicted by the regression DNN model for the cepstrum dataset with various SDs is shown in Fig. 10. The predictions show good agreement with the experimental values in the absence of noise. As noise increases, the predicted values tend to be too small in the intermediate-heat-flux region but well match the experimental values in the other heat-flux
regions. Fig. 11 shows the wall superheat predicted by the regression DNN model for the spectrum dataset with various SDs. With increasing noise, the predicted wall superheat values well match the experimental results. Fig. 12 shows the wall superheat predicted by the regression DNN model for the cepstrum dataset with various SDs. In this case, the predictive precision in certain ranges is well maintained even when the noise is increased. These results indicate that predictions based on the cestrum dataset show better predictive precision for both the heat flux and the wall superheat compared with the predictions based on the spectrum dataset. To quantify this observation, we calculated another quantity. Fig. 13
shows the coefficient of determination (R2) for the heat-flux distribution and the wall-superheat distribution for the DNN regression model with
Gaussian noise added. Even in the case of 20% noise, the R2 of the predictions of the cepstrum model does not deteriorate; by contrast, the
R2 of the predictions of the spectrum model deteriorate sharply beginning at 4% noise. Fig. 14 shows the mean absolute error (MAE) for the DNN regression models with Gaussian noise. Even when the noise increases, the model based on the cepstrum dataset can suppress the error.
4. Conclusions
Three cases of subcooling degrees were investigated, and the boiling sound in the high-heat-flux area before MEB occurred was acquired and used for ML. The predictions showed high precision when no noise was added, irrespective of whether the classification was based on the regression, spectrum, or cepstrum model. Deterioration of the predictive precision was remarkable in the model based on spectrum data with noise. By contrast, the model based on cepstrum data maintain constant precision. title: Simultaneous audio-visual-thermal characterization of transition boiling regime
abstractNote: Safety concerns due to the highly unstable nature of boiling during the transition from the nucleate to the film boiling regime has limited investigations on this topic. Here we perform simultaneous characterization of the thermal, acoustic, and the bubble visualization data during the transition boiling regime. We show that the key features missed due to the high latency in the thermal data are captured in the audio (( fl kJzO. We correlate the acoustic emissions with the high-speed visualization to elucidate the presence of an explosive boiling subregime, also referred to as the microbubble emission boiling (MEB). This regime is typically observed after the critical heat flux (CHF) and is characterized by numerous small sized bubbles ( 300 ± 50 µm) emanating from an unstable vapor film on the heater surface. We correlate this change in bubble behavior with the corresponding increase in the intensity and peak frequency of acoustic emissions up until the onset of a completely silent and stable film boiling regime. Conversely, MEB features are also observed during the transition from film boiling to the nucleate boiling regime in quenching experiments. We modify the heater surface and the boiling fluid, and vary the test conditions, to confirm the existence of MEB in all experiments over a wide range of subcoolings ranging from 2 °C to 25 °C. Our experiments suggest that the extent of the MEB increases with, (i) the increase in subcooling, (ii) the decrease in the surface tension of the boiling fluid, and (iii) the increase in surface roughness/wettability of the heater surface. We believe these unique acoustic features of the transition boiling regime can be used as a feedback to design measures to avoid catastrophic failures in boiling based energy and thermal management systems.

Simultaneous audio-visual-thermal characterization of transition boiling regime
Kumar Nishant Ranjan Sinha, Durgesh Ranjan, Nirbhay Kumar, Md. Qaisar Raza, Rishi Raj⁎∗
Thermal and Fluid Transport Laboratory, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, 801103, India
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Transition boiling Explosive boiling Acoustics Subcooling Spectrogram
ABSTRACT
Safety concerns due to the highly unstable nature of boiling during the transition from the nucleate to the film boiling regime has limited investigations on this topic. Here we perform simultaneous characterization of the thermal, acoustic, and the bubble visualization data during the transition boiling regime. We show that the key features missed due to the high latency in the thermal data are captured in the audio (( fl kJzO. We correlate the acoustic emissions with the high-speed visualization to elucidate the presence of an explosive boiling subregime, also referred to as the microbubble emission boiling (MEB). This regime is typically observed after the critical heat flux (CHF) and is characterized by numerous small sized bubbles ( 300 ± 50 μm) emanating from an unstable vapor film on the heater surface. We correlate this change in bubble behavior with the corresponding increase in the intensity and peak frequency of acoustic emissions up until the onset of a completely silent and stable film boiling regime. Conversely, MEB features are also observed during the transition from film boiling to the nucleate boiling regime in quenching experiments. We modify the heater surface and the boiling fluid, and vary the test conditions, to confirm the existence of MEB in all experiments over a wide range of subcoolings ranging from 2 °C to 25 °C. Our experiments suggest that the extent of the MEB increases with, (i) the increase in subcooling, (ii) the decrease in the surface tension of the boiling fluid, and (iii) the increase in surface roughness/wettability of the heater surface. We believe these unique acoustic features of the transition boiling regime can be used as a feedback to design measures to avoid catastrophic failures in boiling based energy and thermal management systems.
1. Introduction
Boiling-based systems have demonstrated high heat dissipation potential in various applications such as nuclear reactors, material processing industries, desalination plants, refrigeration, and electronic cooling, among others [1–5]. While these systems utilize the advantage of latent heat of vaporization during boiling, they are often constrained by the upper limit of the safe and reliable operation, known as the critical heat flux (CHF) [6]. At CHF, a vapor layer blankets the heated surface and the heat transfer deteriorates significantly. Subsequently, a sudden temperature excursion followed by system/device failure/ burnout is often reported [2]. Accordingly, these systems operate at heat fluxes much below the CHF to ensure a high factor of safety against the undesired thermal runaway [7]. Transition boiling regime is observed right after CHF until the heat flux reaches a minimum value (start of the film boiling). Transition boiling regime is a combination of the nucleate and the film boiling regimes occurring interchangeably [8], which makes it highly unstable
in nature. Moreover, the abrupt and uncontrollable rise in temperature during this mixed (nucleate-film) boiling regime has deterred investigations on this topic. Consequently, literature on the transition boiling is limited [8–12] in comparison to the nucleate and the film boiling regimes. Accordingly, it is important to understand the underlying bubble dynamics and hence heat transfer performance during transition boiling regime. Such efforts may feed into the design of safety measures required to avoid undesired thermal runaway in boiling based energy and thermal management systems. Few studies have however attempted to explore the nature of boiling curve in the transition boiling regime. For example, Berenson [8] suggested to join the CHF and Leidenfrost point on a log–log graph paper to get the boiling curve (heat transfer characteristics) in the transition boiling regime. Further, Witte and Lienhard [9] reported two boiling curves in the transition boiling regime depending on direction of experiments, i.e., the nucleate-transition while heating and filmtransition while cooling. Ramilison and Lienhard [10] anticipated that the contact angle (receding contact angle in case of nucleate-transition

T


 and advancing contact angle in film-transition) of liquid can affect the transition boiling regime and hence proposed two boiling curves in the transition boiling regime. Similarly, Ungar and Eichhorn [11] reported two transition boiling curves obtained by heating and cooling of a cylinder. Following the above-mentioned reports, few studies [13,14] performed quenching experiments to confirm the presence of filmtransition, a sub-regime in transition boiling, however only on nanoporous surfaces. While some of these studies primarily focus on the interfacial dynamics using the tool of high-speed imaging [8,9,12], many others [5,8,10,11,13,14] only discuss heat transfer characteristics without much physical insights on the underlying bubble behavior. Nonetheless, exact details regarding the bubble dynamics and mechanism of heat transfer are debated till date. This is primarily due to the fact that there is a need to devise advanced characterization strategies which can overcome the limitations of thermal (high latency-low frequency and mostly wall averaged behavior) and visual (two-dimensional peripheral visualization) data to elucidate the governing mechanisms in this highly unstable boiling regime. In this regard, acoustic emissions (AE) during boiling is a promising tool (see Table 1). However, most of the studies on AE during boiling (Table 1) primarily focus on the nucleate boiling regime. We recently demonstrated the potential of AE for successful detection of CHF and devised a peak frequency based feedback control strategy to arrest the post-CHF thermal runaway [15]. Such results encouraged us to perform simultaneous audio-visual-thermal characterization of the transition boiling regime reported in this study. Here we simultaneously characterize the acoustic emissions, highspeed visualization, and the thermal data during transition boiling regime. We correlate the visualization and acoustic features to show the presence of an explosive boiling regime often referred to as microbubble emission boiling (MEB), a sub-regime in the transition boiling regime. We first demonstrate that the increase in heat flux post-CHF under highly subcooled conditions (25 °C)is accompanied by a sudden increase/shift in peak frequency of acoustic emissions. Visualization data correlates the rise in peak frequency with the formation of relatively small bubbles ( 300 ± 50 μm) emanating from the unstable vapor film. Conversely, a seamless transition to the film boiling without any increase in heat flux post-CHF was observed at lower subcoolings ( 20 °C). While this trend in thermal data is in line with the literature, a relatively short-lived period of characteristic shift in peak frequency due to the formation of microbubbles was nonetheless observed even at
these low subcoolings ( ° °
2 C 20 C). Hence, in sharp contrast with studies only reporting thermal data, audio-visual signatures in our study confirm the presence of MEB or explosive boiling regime at all subcoolings. Interestingly, MEB was also observed in transition boiling regime during quenching experiments. Further, we explain that strategies which destabilize vapor film via suitable boiling surface/fluid modifications strengthen MEB in comparison to the baseline case of boiling with pure water on a flat surface.
2. Experimental apparatus and procedure
2.1. Experimental setup
The schematic of the pool boiling experimental setup shown in Fig. 1 is similar to our previous report [15]. The heater assembly was made of a stepped copper block with a boiling surface of diameter )/mm enclosed within a Teflon insulation (see Fig. 1). Two cartridge heaters (Make: Marathon, flq( G, fl[( Ö ) were inserted in the copper block and connected to a direct current (DC) power supply (Make: Agilent, Model: N[–[) A) to achieve the required heat flux at the boiling surface. Three thermocouples (J-Type, probe diameter: (W[ mm) were embedded at a distance of [ mm , )) mm , and )– mm below the boiling surface (see inset of Fig. 1). These thermocouples were connected to a data acquisition unit (Make: Agilent, Model: Ufl5(flA). A transparent cylindrical Borosilicate glass container (capacity 8 5 L) was used as the boiling chamber. The glass container was covered with a transparent acrylic lid with holes to maintain the atmospheric pressure during experiments. Accordingly, the test fluid used in the present study can be considered as gas saturated similar to many other pool boiling studies in literature [4]. A silicone rubber heater (Make: Marathon, flfl( G, [(( Ö ) wrapped around the boiling chamber and connected to a proportional integral derivative (PID) controller (Make: Omega) and a thermocouple maintained the required subcooling. An ultrasensitive miniature hydrophone (Make: B&K, Type: Piezoelectric transducer, Model: 5)(q ) was connected to a pre-amplifier (Make: B&K, Model: Nexusfl1]fl) and a data acquisition unit (Make: B& K, Model: q)1( A (/fl) to capture the acoustic emissions during boiling experiments. A high-speed camera (Make: Phantom research, Model: v–W)qwas employed to capture bubble images at [((( frames per second (fps).

 2.2. Test fluids and heater surfaces
We used de-ionized water (Merck, Milli-Q, resistivity: 15 M . cm) and aqueous surfactant solutions as the test fluids for the experiments. The aqueous surfactant solution was prepared by adding an appropriate quantity of anionic surfactant Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS, Make: Sigma Aldrich, CAS Number 151 21 3, purity 98.5%) at critical micelle concentration (CMC) 2500 ppm [28] in the water. The solution was then properly mixed using a magnetic stirrer for two hours. The details of the thermophysical properties of the aqueous surfactant solution are provided in our previous report [29]. We performed experiments on smooth copper (Cu-F) and copper oxide nanostructured (Cu-n) surfaces. The smooth copper surface was polished using a 600-grit size emery paper. The nanostructures on the copper surface were fabricated using a self-limiting chemical oxidation method widely discussed in literature [29,30]. The fabrication and characterization details of the nanostructured copper surfaces are well reported in our previous report [29]. The adopted nanostructured surface in the present study was superhydrophilic in nature with diminishing contact angle [29].
2.3. Experimental procedure and data reduction
The heater assembly was placed in the boiling chamber (capacity = 5 L) and test fluid was poured in. The test fluid was heated using the silicone rubber heater to attain the required subcooling and monitored using the PID controller. The power input to the cartridge heaters was initiated using a programmable DC power supply. The initial voltage was kept constant (100 V and a power level of 84.5 W) for not less than 500 s to initiate the boiling on the heater surface during the start of
the experiments with water. The DC power supply was next programmed using a computer software to increase the voltage input at a slow rate (0.02 V/s which implies increase in power level of 0.03 W/s). The adopted slow heating rate allowed us to maintain a quasi-steady boiling behavior during the transient experiments [15] with continuously varying power level in experiments. Thermal, acoustic, and visualization data were acquired simultaneously and continuously throughout the experiments across all boiling regimes. All the experiments were repeated no less than three times to confirm the repeatability of the data. Since a large volume (5 L) of the test fluid was used in our experiments, the subcooling level did not deviate during the entire experiments.
2.3.1. Heat transfer data reduction
We performed transient experiments wherein the power was ramped up at a rate of 0.03 W/s. Hence, Fourier’s 1-D heat conduction equation cannot be applied to estimate the heat flux and surface temperature. The actual heat flux at the boiling surface is dictated by the heat transfer coefficient due to the boiling regimes and determined using the inverse heat conduction strategy. The three thermocouples embedded in the neck portion of the copper block (see inset of Fig. 1) were used to solve an inverse heat conduction problem (IHCP) to estimate the area-averaged heat flux and the boiling surface temperature [15]. We have thoroughly validated the inverse heat conduction methodology employed here in our previous work [15], wherein we performed two separate steady-state and transient experiments. A good match in heat flux and wall superheat values in nucleate boiling regime were observed for both the experiments and is also reported in our previous study [15]. We acquired the temperature data at20 Hz from the three embedded thermocouples [TC1, TC2, and TC3, see Fig. 2(a)]

2.3.2. Acoustic emission data characterization
The acoustic signals were acquired at a sampling frequency of 1[W[ kJ,zwhich enabled us to determine frequencies up to qflW– kJz (Nyquist criteria). However, we limit our investigation to fl kJz beyond which we could not find any noticeable features in AE during boiling experiments. Accordingly, all the acoustic features utilized to characterize various boiling regimes lie in the audible frequency range of 0–2 kHz [15]. The acquired acoustic signals from the hydrophone were analyzed using the Fast Fourier Transform toolbox of MATLAB® to outline the frequency content. Since the background noise was negligible in our study (similar to [24]), we neither filter the acoustic signals nor perform any additional signal conditioning to ensure that no information is lost inadvertently.
2.3.2.1. Sound pressure level. Sound pressure level (SPL) also known as acoustic intensity is defined as the power carried by sound waves per unit area in a direction perpendicular to the area. For any given pressure wave, SPL is an intensity of a sound measured relative to a reference value. SPL is expressed in decibels (dB), and defined as, SPL 8 10log (I/IO)
10 d; where, Po is reference sound intensity corresponding to 1 μPa underwater and 20 μPa in the air.
2.3.2.2. Spectral analysis. For frequency decomposition of any sound, a mathematical tool known as Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) [34] is used. A signal is alternatively represented by combinations of sines and cosines using DFT. For any time-series data points un, the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) is expressed as, 8 8

. Discrete Fourier transforms (DFTs) reveal periodicities in input data as well as the relative magnitude of any periodic component.
The DFT is therefore considered as a frequency domain representation of the temporal signal. To avoid the ambiguities and complexity in calculation of the frequency composition arising out of DFT, an efficient algorithm known as Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is used. FFT converts the time domain signal to frequency domain and hence reveals the frequency content.
2.3.2.3. Spectrogram. A spectrogram plot is a result of digital process involving FFT. A time-domain data is split into overlapping bins and FFT is performed to obtain the frequency component of the data in each bin. These spectra represent magnitude versus frequency at a particular time which corresponds to the midpoint of the data in the bin. Further, all such spectrums are stacked side by side to get a surface where each point represents the time (x-axis), frequency (y-axis), and its magnitude/intensity (color bar).

3.2. Effect of subcooling
We now discuss the effect of subcooling on the transition boiling regime, specifically, the explosive boiling regime. The pool boiling curves at different subcooling are shown in Fig. 4. As expected [34], the CHF values in our experiments increase as we increase the level of subcooling. A large increase in CHF values with increase in subcooling is well known in literature and is typically attributed to the enhanced condensation of departed bubbles [32]. Further, at all subcooling levels, the heat transfer deteriorates after CHF in the transition boiling regime. However, at higher subcoolings of T 22 °C
sub and 25 °C, the nature of boiling curve deviates from the typical monotonically decreasing trend reported at lower subcooling levels ( T 20 °C
sub ). A momentary stabilization in the heat flux and wall superheat in the transition boiling regime were observed at 22 °C (see green line, zoomed-in window of Fig. 4). This behavior was more pronounced at 25 °C subcooling wherein an increase in heat flux in the transition boiling regime even beyond the CHF value was observed (see zoomedin window of Fig. 4, t 8 5770 s 6026 s). Literature [36–43] suggest that such unique feature in the heat transfer occurring above 20 °C subcooling are typically referred to as the microbubble emission boiling (MEB). MEB was first reported by Inada et al. [44–47] in 1981. They performed experiments on a 50 μm thick platinum foil attached to a copper substrate in a subcooled pool and noted the presence of numerous microbubbles capable of dissipating heat flux in excess of the CHF value. Several pool [36–43] as well as flow [48–50] boiling studies followed this work for high heat flux applications. All of these studies suggest that a threshold subcooling T ff 20 °C
sub is required (necessary condition) for the occurrence of MEB. Since such atypical increase in heat flux in the transition boiling regime also exists in our data at 25 °C subcooling, it suggests that the explosive boiling discussed in our study under highly subcooled conditions is MEB. Please note that we could not go to higher subcoolings above 25 °C due to the limit posed by the power of the cartridge heaters ([(( Ö ) used in the heater assembly. However, the trend in our experimental data suggests that on further increasing the subcooling beyond 25 °C, a more intensive and prolonged explosive boiling regime or MEB may occur, which is also reported in literature [41,51]. To investigate this further, we characterized the audio-visual data and the results are shown in Fig. 5(a-f) and Supplementary Video SV1. These results demonstrate identical audio-visual features in the explosive boiling regime in all the experiments. Revisiting the data for
highly subcooled boiling presented in Section 3.1, we clearly see that the data at low subcooling possess all the three key features of explosive boiling discussed earlier. These are, (a) the presence of numerous small sized bubbles emanating from the heater surface [Fig. 5(a-c)], (b) increase in the loudness of the boiling sound [Fig. 5(d-f)], and (c) a sudden shift in peak frequency zone with the onset of explosive boiling [Fig. 5(d-f)]. Further, all these key features are same as that of MEB, except the atypical increase in heat flux beyond CHF at subcoolings ff20 °C. Hence, we sincerely believe that the MEB is observed at all subcoolings unlike reports in literature [36–40] wherein a threshold subcooling T ff 20 °C
sub is required (necessary condition) for the occurrence of MEB. We further show that MEB should primarily be identified by the characteristic bubble and acoustic behavior, and not only by the post-CHF increase in the heat flux. Studies [36–40] on MEB also confirm the presence of microbubbles and loud sound as observed in the explosive boiling regime in the present study. Further, the diameter of the bubbles reported in the MEB studies is typically of few hundred microns (due to the high subcooling and hence the name “MEB”), which was also observed in present study at higher subcoolings ( T ff 20 °C)
sub . However, we observed relatively bigger bubbles (diameter 8 )W( )W5 m)m at subcoolings below 20 °C in the explosive boiling regime. Based on the audio-visual features of the explosive boiling and its striking similarity with MEB under high subcooling conditions, we believe that MEB and explosive boiling are fundamentally same. However, since the bubble sizes are larger at lower subcoolings, we use a more generic term, i.e., explosive boiling over the MEB. Further, we observed that the explosive boiling is relatively short-lived ( q s) at 2 °C subcooling in comparison to prolonged L fl1( saOt 25 °C subcooling [see Fig. 5(a-f) and Table 3]. Studies in literature typically increase the power in stepwise manner and acquire steady-state data, and hence such a short-lived explosive boiling regime under low subcooling conditions may have been missed in the past. We believe that the audiovisual characterization performed in the present study was instrumental in identifying explosive boiling characteristics reported in this work. The duration/extent of explosive boiling regime increases with the increase in the level of subcooling (see Table 3). Further, the intensity ratio (explosive boiling to nucleate boiling) of AE increased / folds when the subcooling was increased from 2 °C to 25 °C (water, refer Table 3). The peak frequency (frequency with highest magnitude) of the AE in the explosive boiling regime also shows an increasing trend from 320 ± 20 Hz at 2 °C subcooling to 880 ± 20 Hz at 25 °C subcooling. Since it is well known that the frequency of acoustics emissions increases with decrease in the bubble size [52], the peak frequency data (Table 3) is clearly in line with the trend in bubble size with subcooling reported earlier.

 3.3. Effect of fluid and surface modification
Results discussed above confirm that the: (i) explosive boiling regime occurs at all subcoolings, and (ii) the extent of explosive boiling depends on the degree of subcooling. We now show that any strategy which can destabilize the vapor film in the transition boiling regime may be used to prolong the extent of the explosive boiling regime
before the eventual transition to the silent film boiling regime. Such strategies may be instrumental in providing the much needed time cushion to implement safety measures against the undesired and often catastrophic thermal runaway in boiling systems. In this regard, we now investigate the effect of boiling fluid modification, particularly the decrease in the surface tension via the addition of surfactant in water, on explosive boiling. We perform

 experiments with aqueous surfactant (SDS at CMC) solution [53,54] at various subcoolings. At higher subcoolings such as 20 °C and 25 °C, the heat flux was again found to exceed the CHF value in the explosive boiling regime (Fig. 6), i.e., MEB was observed. Conversely, at lower subcoolings ( T 7 20 °C
sub ), the heat flux decreased monotonically and a smooth transition to film boiling regime was observed. Further, the key acoustic features of the explosive boiling regime such as increase in the loudness level of sound and the blue-shift in the peak frequency were also observed in these experiments [see marked zones in temporal plots of thermal data and spectrograms in Fig. 7(a-f)]. As expected due to the relatively low surface tension, the extent of explosive boiling regime in these experiments was significantly higher than the baseline case of water (see Table 3). For example, the extent of explosive boiling increased /( folds to fl(( s in comparison to [ s for water at 5 °C subcooling (please refer to the data in Table 3).
We believe that the lower surface tension of the surfactant solutions in comparison to water delays the formation of stable vapor film postCHF. This can be explained using the framework of hydrodynamic instability theory wherein the disturbance wavelength ( d) is directly
related to the surface tension L ) as d 0.5. This relation clearly suggests that lower the surface tension, shorter will be the wavelength of disturbance. Further, it is also reported that shorter wavelength of disturbance results into faster growth rate of unstable mode [55]. Consequently, the low surface tension fluid delays the stabilization of the vapor film and hence a prolonged transition regime is observed in the present study. We next modify the heater surface and introduce nanostructures which are well known to increase CHF [29] and delaying the Leidenfrost point [56] due to the intrinsic merits of capillarity induced rewetting. We perform experiments with the superhydrophilic surface (copper oxide nanostructured surface, refer to Section 2.2) and water with a focus on the explosive boiling regime. The comparison of the results on smooth copper (Cu-F) and nanostructured copper (Cu-n) at 10 °C subcooling are shown in Fig. 8(a-d) and Supplementary Video SV2. In contrast to the results for flat surface where a smooth transition to the film boiling was observed in heat transfer data [Cu-F in Fig. 8(a) also discussed in Section 3.2 as well], the heat flux and surface temperature appears to stabilize in the explosive boiling regime on Cu-n surfaces [Fig. 8(b)]. A prolonged (4× longer in comparison to Cu-F case) and intensified [see dark red zone in Fig. 8(d)] explosive boiling was observed on these Cu-n surfaces. Nanostructured coated copper surface (Cu-n) facilitates solid–liquid contacts due to enhanced wettability/ wickability, and hence a prolonged transition boiling regime is reported in literature [57]. Moreover, nanostructure protrusions provide local cold spots, often reported as “fin effect” [58], help in easy collapse of the vapor film. Consequently, a prolonged explosive boiling regime is

3.4. Quenching: Transition from film to nucleate boiling regime
Explosive boiling regime/MEB is clearly a sub-regime of transition boiling regime as observed from the experimental results reported in previous sections. It is also well known that transition boiling regime occurs during transition from film boiling to nucleate boiling in quenching applications wherein a sufficiently hot surface initially blanketed by a vapor film cools in a liquid pool. To uncover the features of transition boiling regime during quenching, we next focus on the audio-visual and thermal features during quenching of the smooth copper surface (Cu-F) at 25 °C subcooling. We first heat the surface (t 8 0 s to 6100 s) until a stable vapor film is formed (t 8 6100 s) and discussed the results in Section 3.1 wherein explosive boiling regime
was observed during heating. We then turn off the power once a persistent vapor film was present and allow it to cool down in the liquid pool. This allowed us to mimic the scenario of a typical quenching experiment. The cooling time (from T 320 °C
s to T 100 °C
s ) was observed to be ](( s in our experiments, attributed to the large thermal inertia of the heater assembly. We next analyze the thermal [Fig. 9(a-c)], acoustic [Fig. 9(d)], and visual data [Fig. 9(e)] during quenching at 25 °C subcooling. Explosive boiling/MEB was once again observed at t 8 6400 s during the transition from film boiling to the nucleate boiling regime. Our results are in complete agreement with the typical quenching studies [59,60] which also report microbubble emission in transition boiling regime, however, they do not comment on heat transfer and acoustic emission characteristics. The audio-visual features in the transition boiling regime during boiling (increasing power, Section 3.1) and in the quenching experiment are strikingly similar in nature. For example, the key features of

Fig. 9. Results from a quenching experiment at T 25 °C
sub . (a) The temporal plot of temperatures (primary y-axis) as recorded from the thermocouples. (b) The area-averaged surface heat flux (primary y-axis) and surface temperature (secondary y-axis) calculated from solving IHCP. (c) Spectrogram showing frequency spectrum variation with time. K. Nishant Ranjan Sinha, et al. Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 118 (2020) 110162
10


 transition boiling regime: (a) highly unstable vapor film with microbubbles all over the heater surface, (b) a loud sound, (c) and blue-shift in peak frequency are essentially present in heating/boiling as well as cooling/quenching experiments. However, the boiling curve during cooling [see blue curve in Fig. 9 (d)] of the heater surface does not retrace the same path as the heating curve due to hysteresis, which is widely reported in literature [2,15]. The audio-visual and thermal characterization results of the quenching experiments once again confirm the fact that explosive boiling or MEB regime is essentially a subregime of the transition boiling regime and can be observed during heating as well as quenching experiments.
4. Conclusion
In summary, we performed pool boiling experiments at various subcoolings to simultaneously characterize the audio, visual, and thermal data during the transition boiling regime. Within the transition boiling regime, we report the presence of a unique explosive boiling regime before the transition to the silent film boiling regime. This regime is characterized by the (a) the presence of numerous small bubbles emanating from the unstable vapor film, (b) a sharp increase in AE intensity, and (c) a blue-shift in the peak frequency of AE. Audio-visual characterization results confirm the presence of this explosive boiling regime at all subcoolings investigated in the present study. We further show that any surface or fluid modification strategy, which may delay the formation of a stable vapor film on the heater surface, can be used to extend the duration of explosive boiling regime. We sincerely believe that such strategies may be instrumental in providing the much needed time cushion to implement safety measures against the sudden, undesired, and often catastrophic thermal runaway in boiling systems. We further believe that our study clarifies many aspects related to the transition boiling regime which may be important to various phasechange based heat transfer applications.

title: A critical review on measures to suppress flow boiling instabilities in microchannels
abstractNote: Due to the development of the electronic components, concentrator photovoltaic, fuel cell, etc., the cooling requirement of the devices increases rapidly. Due to the higher heat transfer and compact design of the microchannel technology, it has been widely considered and investigated to solve the increasing heat flux. Boiling heat transfer is an effective way to dissipate a mass of heat using the latent heat of phase change. Hence, comparing with the single phase liquid flow, flow boiling in microchannel utilizes both advantages of micro scale effect and phase change effect, and can realize a much higher heat flux. Therefore, it becomes a critical way for super high heat dissipation. However, the procedure of the phase change and two phase flow may lead to instability, which can seriously inhibit the heat transfer performance of coolant in microchannel. To tackle with the flow boiling instability, a number of methods were proposed and reported. Therefore, in this paper, to effectively improve the flow boiling in microchannel heat exchanger, the cutting-edge control technologies in this field were reviewed, categorized and summarized, and the future trend of the research was presented.

{
    "content": "REVIEW
A critical review on measures to suppress flow boiling instabilities in microchannels
Ning Mao1 & Jiaojiao Zhuang1 & Tianbiao He1 & Mengjie Song2
Received: 6 March 2020 / Accepted: 30 November 2020 # The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH, DE part of Springer Nature 2021
Abstract
Due to the development of the electronic components, concentrator photovoltaic, fuel cell, etc., the cooling requirement of the devices increases rapidly. Due to the higher heat transfer and compact design of the microchannel technology, it has been widely considered and investigated to solve the increasing heat flux. Boiling heat transfer is an effective way to dissipate a mass of heat using the latent heat of phase change. Hence, comparing with the single phase liquid flow, flow boiling in microchannel utilizes both advantages of micro scale effect and phase change effect, and can realize a much higher heat flux. Therefore, it becomes a critical way for super high heat dissipation. However, the procedure of the phase change and two phase flow may lead to instability, which can seriously inhibit the heat transfer performance of coolant in microchannel. To tackle with the flow boiling instability, a number of methods were proposed and reported. Therefore, in this paper, to effectively improve the flow boiling in microchannel heat exchanger, the cutting-edge control technologies in this field were reviewed, categorized and summarized, and the future trend of the research was presented.
Keywords Flow boiling instability . Microchannel . Heat transfer . Flow pattern . Pressure drop . Temperature fluctuation
Abbreviations
CHF Critical heat flux CNT Carbon nanotubes CPV Concentrator photovoltaic DWO Density wave oscillations HCPV High concentrator photovoltaics HTC Heat transfer coefficient MFB Minimum film boiling OFO Onset of flow oscillation ONB Onset of nucleate boiling OSV Onset of significant void PCI Parallel-channel Instability PDO Pressure drop oscillation ThO Thermal oscillation
1 Introduction
In recent years, microchannel flow boiling technology has been widely applied in lots of industrials, such as CPU, concentrator photovoltaic (CPV) system, fuel cells, microreactors etc. According to Moore’s law [1], the CPUs had a tremendous progress in the past decades. The high power electronic devices with nanometer size resulted in the huge heat dissipation. The highest heat dissipation on the IC chip is estimated to reach up to 107 W/m2 [2]. To realize the maximum benefit from solar energy and reduce the solar cell material, concentrator photovoltaic (CPV) systems and high concentrator photovoltaics (HCPV) (300–1000 suns) have been proposed and investigated in recent years [3]. However, the high density of radiation results in severely high temperature. To maintain the high efficiency of PV, the temperature of solar cell material must be controlled [4, 5]. To tackle with these problems in CPU, CPV and other instruments with much high heat flow density, microchannel is attracting increasing attentions recently [2], due to its three advantages: higher surface to volume ratio, higher heat transfer and small scale. Therefore, microchannel has become to one of the most effective method for thermal management of high heat dissipation devices. Comparing with the single -phase liquid flow, flow boiling
* Mengjie Song mengjie.song@gmail.com
1 Department of Gas Engineering, College of Pipeline and Civil Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, Shandong, China
2 School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China
https://doi.org/10.1007/s00231-020-03009-2
/ Published online: 2 January 2021
Heat and Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910


 in microchannel utilizes both advantages of micro scale effect and phase change effect, and can realize a much higher heat transfer coefficient. Therefore, it becomes a critical way for super high heat dissipation in many fields, such as electronics systems, batteries, photovoltaic, light-water Reactor, hydrogen storage reservoirs etc. [6–9], as shown in Fig. 1. However, the wide applications of microchannel flow boiling are facing some challenges: the high pressure drops and flow boiling instabilities which resulted in low heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux (CHF) [10]. Flow boiling instabilities can seriously inhibit the heat transfer performance of working fluid in microchannel. Therefore, it’s necessary to enhance the heat transfer and meanwhile to weaken the flow boiling instability simultaneously. Over the past decade, lots of works have been conducted to investigate the flow boiling instability in microchannels [11–13]. It was found that the pressure drop [14], wall temperature [15] and mass flux [11] in the microchannels oscillate during flow boiling. The transient flow pattern alternation may lead to reverse flow [16], and rapid bubble growth and expansion may result to backflow phenomenon [12, 17, 18]. The system pressure influences the flow boiling instabilities in micro-channels [15], leading to the temperature oscillation, which continue to influence the system pressure. The channel size, volume compressibility and mass flow rate also affected the pressure drop oscillation. The rapid bubble growth due to high heat flux will lead to pressure oscillation [19] or even reverse flow and large temperature spikes [12, 20]. To tackle with these instable phenomenon, a number of measures were also proposed to modify the stability of the system, such as modifying microchannel geometry to change the flow pattern [21–23], surface or wall treatment to modify boiling characteristics [24, 25], designing new structure of the microchannel heat exchanger [26], and applying new working fluid [27], etc. The objective of this paper is to review the numerous studies on fundamental mechanism of the various instabilities and methods to eliminate their occurrence. In Section 2, the fundamentals and classification of flow boiling instability were summarized. In Section 3, five types of measures to control or improve flow boiling
instability were detailed reported and analyzed. Finally, a conclusion and future works are presented in Section 4.
2 Fundamentals
The flow boiling curve has multiple descriptive stages beginning with single phase convection and ending at film boiling. Fig. 2 shows the variation of heat flux with channel wall superheat [28] for traditional macroscale and the microscale flow boiling. During flow boiling procedure, supplying heat to microchannel causes a series of heat transfer and phase change procedures: single phase convection, subcooled boiling, saturated boiling, transition boiling and film boiling. Initially, with the increase in temperature difference, the heat flux increases but no bubble generates, which is single phase convection. The continuous increase in temperature difference will cause formation of bubble nucleation, where is seen the onset of nucleate boiling (ONB). After the ONB point, discrete bubbles are randomly released, until bubble generation where is seen as the onset of significant void (OSV). At temperature difference higher than the OSV point, some bubbles appear on

10000
Flow boiling in microchannel Electronics
Battery
Photovoltaic
Fig. 1 Application of microchannel and its advantages [2]
Fig. 2 Illustration of flow boiling curve in a macrochannel/microchannel heat exchanger
890 Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910


 the wall changing the boiling to fully developed status, although this is still subcooled boiling. If the wall temperature rises above the saturation temperature, bubbles coalesce and merge into larger ones, and the heat flux increases till to the peak value, named critical heat flux (CHF). After CHF point, the further increase in the temperature results to intensive bubbles preventing liquid attaching the wall surface, which reduces the heat transfer. The decrease in heat transfer continues to the valley point, after which, a vapor film begins to form at the surface. This point is the minimum film boiling (MFB). Compared with the typical macro scale flow boiling, the micro channel enhanced the heat flux values. The CHF of water flow boiling is increased from the range of 100 kW/m2 to 1 MW/m2 [29–31] for macroscale flow boiling to the range of 1 to 10 MW/m2 for microscale flow boiling [32, 33]. The ONB and CHF point are also delayed for the microchannel by the reason of the higher heat flux of single phase flow due to the much higher specific area of the micro effect [29, 33].
2.1 Flow patterns and instability mechanism
During flow boiling, the liquid-vapor flow pattern varies inside the channel, which has the critical impact on the heat transfer performance including heat transfer coefficient and critical heat flux (CHF), and pressure drop properties, including amplitude of fluctuation of pressure drop and the value of pressure drop. Various types of flow patterns have been reported previously which can be summarized as bubbly flow, plug flow, slug flow, wavy and annular flow [34–37], as shown in Fig. 3. These flow pattern variation results to different heat transfer characteristics. Yin and Jia [38] studied the bubble behaviors in a microchannel during flow boiling. In the inlet region, the flow is single phase or some bubbles begin to generate. With the increasing heat supplying, the bubble continues to grow to the channel size and becomes elongated bubble. In the outlet region, the continuous elongated bubbles merge with each other to form the annular flow. Zaidi et al. [39] studied the flow boiling of HFE-7100 in microchannels, and concluded the four types of flow patterns: bubbly, slug, churn and annular flow. Near the inlet, the bubble nucleation occurred and then numerous discrete bubbles formed. The small bubbles continue to grow to a bigger one the size of which is confined by the channel. This is followed by a vapour slug, which was observed in the middle position. The continuous evaporation rate increased the vapor velocity and caused the slug bubble distorted and changed into churn flow. Near the outlet, the film stayed around the vapor to form annular flow. Kim and Mudawar [40] classified the flow pattern transmission into five procedures: bubbly flow, slug flow, annular flow, mist flow and vapor. Based on the heat transfer property, these five procedures were further classified into two types: nucleate boiling dominant heat transfer, and convective boiling dominant heat transfer, as seen in Fig. 3b and c. For former
type, the bubbly and slug flow mainly accounted for the channel. Hence, with the reduction of nucleate boiling along the flow direction, the heat transfer performance was reduced. While for the latter type, the annular flow accounted for the main part of the channel. Hence, with the development of annular flow, the thinner film leaded to increase in heat transfer coefficient. In microchannels, the main reason that causes flow boiling instability is the bubble dynamics which resulted from the bubble generation during boiling. As mentioned above, with the increase in vapor volume, the different flow patterns occur [42]: isolated bubble, elongated bubble, annular flow, and mist flow. As shown in Fig. 4, firstly, the heat supply leads to the formation of bubble nucleation, and then bubbles generate and grow to the elongated bubble [43, 44]. The continuous supply of heat flux will cause the more liquid to evaporate into vapor, and causes two types of phenomenon. (1) The growth of bubble may result to bubble clogging. When the bubble growth stops and loses the force to prevent fluid entering the channel, the fresh liquid reverses to the microchannel, which results to flow oscillation [45]. (2) On the other hand, the liquid evaporation results to the continuous growth of elongated bubble and form liquid film adjacent to the wall, and then leads to the flow pattern transmission from slug flow to annular flow. The further heat supply leads to evaporation of the thin film, resulting to dry out of film, occupation of the vapor inside channel, and then deterioration of heat transfer.
2.2 Classification of flow instability
According to the mechanism of flow boiling instabilities [46–49], it has been classified as two types: static and dynamic. The static instability is namely statically unstable or the flow status oscillates between two steady states. The widely existing static instability is Ledinegg instability. The Ledinegg instability is influenced by several flow variables: pressure, channel design, etc. Hence, increase of the pressure, reduction of the parallel channels number and the channel length can effectively weaken Ledinegg instability [50]. It was pointed out that the Ledinegg depends on the pressure drop and mass flowrate in a loop flow, which can be expressed as Eq. (1) [51]. The equation implies the static instability occurs when pressure slope of internal characteristic curve is less than the pressure supply system, which corresponds to the region between points of CHF and MFB, shown in Fig. 2.

Fig. 3 Flow patterns in microchannels (a) Flow pattern transitions in microchannel during flow boiling observed in experiments [37, 38, 41], (b) Nucleate boiling dominant heat transfer [40], (c) Convective boiling dominant heat transfer [40], (d) Typical flow patterns during flow boiling
892 Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910


 The dynamic instability is caused by the interaction between the operating parameters: pressure, mass flowrate and temperature, hence, this type of instability can be classified into four types based on the causes: pressure drop oscillation, density wave oscillation, thermal oscillation and parallel channel instability [45]. Dynamic instabilities are the main reason to cause pressure drop and wall temperature fluctuations [15, 52].
& Density wave oscillations. This phenomenon is due to a delayed response in mass flow rate to an increase in the pressure drop [53, 54]. & Pressure drop oscillation. Pressure drop oscillation is associated with the vapor generation and the compressible volume. This type of instability can be eliminated through controlling inlet flow [55]. & Thermal oscillation. Thermal oscillation is associated with the channel wall temperature fluctuations, the movement of the dry out and nucleate boiling boundaries [56–58]. & Parallel-channel Instability. This type of instability is due to the interactions between pressure at inlet/outlet of different channels.
The types of flow instabilities are summarized and shown in Fig. 5. The typical studies on the flow boiling instability including wall temperature oscillation, pressure drop variation
and reverse flow are shown in Fig. 6, which are also summarized in Table 1.
3 Measures to control or improve flow boiling instability
In order to control the flow boiling instabilities, numerous techniques have been developed [66], which are classified into five types: geometry modification [17, 62–89], surface modification [91–104], vapor venting technology [107–110], working fluid modification [111–115], and some combined methods [120–122], as shown in Fig. 7.
3.1 Geometry modifications
The channel geometry is the key component of microchannel heat exchanger that influence the its flow boiling and heat transfer performances. The geometric parameter would influence the bubble behaviors, bubble dynamics inside the microchannel, which determine the flow pattern and flow instability properties. Therefore, modifying the geometry of microchannel is an important way to control the flow instability. The modification can be classified into three types: modifying flow passage, designing reentrant configuration and adding inlet restrictors.

 3.1.1 Modifying flow passage of microchannel
The methods of modifying flow passages can be divided into five types: expanding channel, wavy channel, interconnected channel, segmented channel and auxiliary channel design, which are summarized in Fig. 8. Balasubramanian et al. [67] proposed an expanding microchannel, through removing fins at certain positions of the straight channel, as shown in Fig. 8a. It was found that pressure drop of the new designed expanding microchannel was obviously reduced. Deng et al. [21] designed a new periodic expanded-constrained microchannels. Compared with the straight channel, this new design reduced the pressure drop by 10–74%. Xia et al. [22] designed a microchannel with sinusoidal wavy sidewall and used acetone
as the working fluid. The microchannel design is shown in Fig. 8c. The corner regions in the sidewall can supply additional liquid for continuous transmission of the liquid for vapor, to maintain the annular flow in a stable heat transfer state, and to prevent dry-out. Hence, the design of the sinusoidal wavy sidewall weakened wall temperature oscillation and reduced the boiling flow instability. Xia et al. [68] compared the straight and triangular corrugated microchannels with acetone as the working fluid, and gave different conclusions. The design is shown in Fig. 8d. It was found the corrugated channels were more instable. To improve the instability, inlet throttle was additionally applied, and the operating parameters such as inlet temperature and heat transfer should be controlled at a suitable range. It was found that the transverse trapezoid microchannel affected the flow patterns and helped entrain the vapor slug. Zhang et al. The interconnected microchannel net design, as shown in Fig.8f, enhanced generation of boiling nucleation which increased the two phase interface area, and facilitate the rewet of channel wall to prevent dry-out. (20 × 250 μm) distributed from the middle location to the outlet location. The micro-slots can enhance nucleate boiling, accelerate bubble motion, and bring about periodic rewetting, which enhanced heat transfer and stabilized boiling flow. Prajapati et al. [73] compared segmented finned microchannels on a copper block with straight channels, as shown in Fig. 8h. A lower inlet pressure fluctuations was observed, indicating the fins stabilize the flow through realizing steady effects of the thin-film evaporation regime. Prajapatia et al.  microchannel. It was found that bubble elongation in secondary channels inhibited the upstream-direction moving of bubbles in the main channel. Hence, this design was effective to prevent reverse flow and to suppress the instability. Li et al. [66, 77] proposed a microchannel with auxiliary channels and multiple nozzles for connecting to the main channel, as shown in Fig. 8i, and investigated the flow instability. The designed nozzle generated jetting flow near outlet section which was able to remove the confined bubbles. Moreover, mixing flow was induced near the nozzle area which increased the heat transfer and reduced the deviation of wall temperature. Hence, this auxiliary channel with nozzle configuration reduced wall temperature and pressure drop fluctuations and suppressed the instability. Overall, designing novel types of microchannel to reduce the flow boiling instability is an effective method. However, some controversial phenomenon should be noticed. For the similar design of wavy channel, Ref. [22] reported the obvious decrease in fluctuation amplitude of wall temperatures from around 20 °C to around 4 °C for the wavy channel design, however, Ref. [68] reported corrugated microchannels caused higher pressure drop and leaded to more severe periodical backflow. This may be caused by the channel dimension and working fluid properties, which indicates the flow boiling instability should consider the channel dimension and working fluid. Moreover, the energy consumption is also an important influencing factor. It’s reported that the diverging channel and segmented channel suppressed the flow boiling instability with slightly increased the pressure drop which may consume more energy and finally cause instability [69]. Hence, the energy cost should also be considered.
3.1.2 Reentrant cavity design
Designing and building reentrant cavities in microchannel is an important way to create artificial nucleations which enhance bubble generation and reduce flow oscillation, as shown in Fig. 18.7. Kosar et al. [78] firstly proposed reentrant cavity design method, with interconnected reentrant cavities fabricated on channel sidewalls, as shown in Fig. 9a. To suppress flow instability, five orifices with width of 20 μm are designed at the channel entrance. Deng et al. [79] developed reentrant microchannels which consists of 14 parallel semiclosed Ω-shaped microchannels in copper with deionized water and ethanol as coolant, as shown in Fig. 9b. It was found that this configuration enhanced the heat transfer and mitigated the boiling flow instability. This was due to that the semiclosed Ω reentrant design provided more alternative passages for the bubbles elongation and reduced the confinement of channel on the vapor flow and eliminated the bubble slugs. Kuo and Peles [80, 81] designed two types of reentrant cavities in microchannels: nonconnected reentrant cavities and interconnected reentrant cavities, and their performance were compared with traditionally plain wall microchannel. The influence of reentrant construction of microchannel on flow boiling oscillations and instabilities were investigated. It was found that this design was helpful to weaken the instability caused by the rapid growth of bubbles. The analysis indicated that the reentrant structure reduced superheat and pressure which helped mitigate the rapid growth of bubbles and then moderate the flow oscillation. Sitar [82] developed potential nucleation cavities in the microchannel sidewalls and bottom. It was found that the cavities can stabilized flow during
Fig. 9 Configuration of reentrant (a): Ref. [78], (b): Ref. [79])
Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910 897


 boiling, due to that they can promote the transition of liquid to vapor and increase surface roughness which increases boiling superheat. Li et al. [17] designed a novel microchannel with triangular cavities and conducted flow boiling experiments. It was found that the triangular cavities inhibited the boiling instability, and uniformity of wall temperature was improved. This was due to that the triangular cavity enlarged flow area, suppressed bubble coalescence and stimulated bubble departure which results to more stable flow boiling and eliminates reversal flow. Deng et al. [83] proposed a novel pin fininterconnected reentrant microchannels and investigated the flowing heat transfer properties. It was found that the reentrant chambers provide spaces for the bubble nucleation, departure and movement, which enhanced the flow stability.
3.1.3 Inlet configuration design
Figure 10a and b show the design of inlet restrictor or orifice [41, 88], and Fig. 10c shows a typical N-shape characteristic curve of pressure-drop with flow-rate of flow boiling system. Case 3 shows the pressure curve of pump for traditional microchannel, and case 6 shows the pressure for inlet restrictor, as shown in Fig. 18.8. As seen, the inlet restrictor in the channel increases the system pressure. The modified curve eliminates the negative Δp-G region, and then stabilize the system. Some researchers have proposed different inlet restrictor designs [84, 88–92] to distribute the fluid in the inlet region and trap the bubble generation, in order to suppress flow boiling instability. Kandlikar et al. [86] have assessed the effects of an inlet pressure restrictor and fabricated nucleation sites as a means of stabilizing the two-phase flow instabilities in microchannels. Kosar et al. [87] proposed inlet orifices in parallel microchannel to reduce the boiling instability. The results indicated that the inlet orifice restrict the inlet pressure which eliminated the upstream compressible volume
instabilities. Wang et al. [41] designed three types of inlet and outlet configuration in parallel microchannels, and investigated the effects different combinations on flow boiling instabilities. One of the three types was perpendicular to the microchannels to restrict flow. It was found that the inlet restrictor helped eliminate the oscillations of temperature and pressure and inhibit the reverse flow of bubbles, and realized steady flow boiling. Szczukiewicz et al. [88] studied flow boiling of different refrigerants: R245fa, R236fa, and R1234ze, in the parallel microchannel with inlet micro-orifices. It was found that the inlet orifice can evenly distribute the flow and effectively improve the flow uniformity, prevent vapor reverse flow and suppress the flow instability. Park et al. [89] studied a multi-microchannel with the orifice insert, and found that the inlet orifice resulted in additional pressure drop and subcooling reduction, blocked reversed flow, prevented the upstream dry-out, and thus suppressed the flow instability (Table 2). It’s important to note that the reviewed reentrant cavity design indicate that this design not only modify the flow boiling stability and extend the stable boiling region, but also the heat transfer coefficient is enhanced. For passage modification, the enhancement to heat transfer performance can be obviously observed, however, the suppression of flow boiling instability depends on its design. The wavy design not only suppress the flow boiling instability through managing the bubble growth, but also increase the heat transfer and CHF obviously through improving the nucleate boiling [71, 76, 82]. Whereas, corrugated microchannels caused higher pressure drop and leaded to more severe periodical backflow [68]. The design of passage can either suppress the flow oscillation or enhance the flow instability, which depends on the specific channel dimension and working fluid. Further research is necessary to study the effect of different channel passage structure. The inlet restrictor in the channel was proposed to increases the system pressure to
Fig. 10 Design of inlet configuration and its improvement (a): Ref [41], (b): Ref [88], (c): Ref [58])
898 Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910


 modify the flow instability. Although most of the reviewed inlet restrictor performed well in both reducing the flow oscillation, surface temperature fluctuations and enhancing the heat transfer, one of the studies reported that it can not stop the reversed flow [86], which may finally lead to instability. Considering that the combination of inlet restriction and channel reentrant design gave a better performance in stable pressure, improvement in heat transfer and resistance in the backflow [68], the combination of multiple geometry modification will be effective in controlling boiling instability, enhancing heat transfer, and saving energy.
3.2 Surface modification
Surface modification is newly developed in recent years and is also a widely used method to enhance heat transfer and control flow instability in microchannels. The modification aims to improve the bubble dynamics through changing the surface morphology, which is the fundamental of heat transfer and flow. For this method, various technologies have been applied to develop different coatings, including silicon nanowires (SiNW) or metal nanowires, carbon nanotubes and porous materials, as listed in Table 3. These modified surface can bring about different roughness characteristics, wettability, porous structures and increase the active nucleation-site
density [46], which lead to the reduction of ONB and weakened instability.
3.2.1 Silicon or metal nanowires
Morshed et al. [90] investigated boiling flow of deionized water in a microchannel with copper nanowires (CuNWs) coatings which were formed on the bottom surface using electrochemical deposition technique. It was found that the nanowires reduced contact angle, as shown in Fig. 11c, and improved the surface wettability, which increased the number of bubble nucleation sites. Although the copper nanowires enhanced heat transfer coefficient to 56% with a cost of pressure drop increase of only up to 20%. However, the pressure drop fluctuation was observed in both nanowires coatings and bare surface microchannels. The nanowires coatings were found has little effect on controlling flow boiling instability. Li et al. [91] compared heat transfer performance in microchannels with plain surface and nanowire coated surface, as shown in Fig. 11a. It was observed that at a much higher range of heat flux, the flow boiling can be maintained at stable state for the nanowire coated microchannels. The nanowire coated surface enhanced the rapid coalesce of vapor bubbles to vapor slugs, and rapid transmission to annular flow, which induced much lower flow fluctuations. Hence,

It was found that the nanowire surface eliminated the large periodic oscillation in temperatures. It was found the the SiNW surface significantly reduced flow boiling instabilities in aspects of fluctuation of wall temperature and pressure drop compared to plainwall. This was due that SiNWs introduced explosive bubble nucleation and enabled the explosive boiling directly transforming to annular flow without undergoing slug flow, which reduced channel blockage, improved rewetting, and maintained thin liquid film, as shown in Fig. 11d. 3.2.2 Carbon nanotubes
Carbon nanotubes coatings (CNTs) with thermal conductivity as high as 3000 W/mK [94] have been developed to enhance boiling heat flux [95]. The applications of CNTs
in micro/mini channels enhance nucleate boiling and thus exhibit obvious enhancement of heat transfer coefficient or CHF [96–98]. Sujith et al. [96] studied the effect of CNT on flow boiling of water in a mini-channel, the CNTs coated sample shows 6.69% to 21.6% of increase in CHF at different mass flow rates, compared to other surface modification samples. In the previous studies on CNTs applicated in microchannels, limited studies have been involved in the flow boiling instability. Khanikar et al. [97] studied a carbon nanotubes coated microchannel, as shown in Fig. 12. It was found that CNT coating greatly enhanced the heat transfer, and had similar pressure drop at low mass flow rate. However, at high mass flow rate, the CNT coating was negative for the instability. Khanikar et al. [99] also compared bare wall and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) coating microchannels. It was found that both the bare
Fig. 12 Microchannel coated with carbon nanotube (a) Photograph of microchannel coated with CNTs. (b) SEM images of different test surfaces [97]
Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910 901


 and CNTs microchannels had appreciable fluctuations with increasing heat flux.
3.2.3 Porous surface
Porous coating is one of the most effective surface modification methods to enhance boiling heat transfer [100–102]. The porous coating forms a large number of cavities of different sizes, increases nucleation-site density which significantly affects bubble generation, bubble growth and departure bubble dynamics. Bai et al. [102] proposed a porous-coated parallel microchannels and compared it with bare ones. The SEM image of the porous coating is shown in Fig. 13b. It was found that the porous coating enabled to mitigate the flow boiling instability. As shown in Fig. 13c, with the increase in vapor quality near the outlet region, a rapid increase in pressure drop fluctuation was observed for the bare surface while relatively steady pressure drop for the porous-coated surface. This is due to that porous-coated wall changes the bubble dynamics, enables the bubble nucleation generating in steady and orderly process and reduces the number of big bubbles that would be elongated in downstream direction. This would eliminate the phenomenon of reverse flow which may cause flow oscillation. Jia et al. [25] proposed a novel porous wall design through fabricating micro pin fin arrays on channel sidewalls, as shown in Fig. 13a. The porous walls provided a great number of boiling nucleation sites and the pin fins along the channel wall brought about wicking effect which was helpful to maintain the rewetting of channel and notable flow boiling enhancement. Hence, this novel porous wall were found to reduce pressure drop significantly and to mitigate the flow boiling instability. Zhang et al. [103] designed three types of porous interconnected microchannel nets (PIMNs) and investigated the pressure drop and two-phase flow instability. It was reported the medium channel size presents better performance to mitigate the flow instability. However, the comparison with the plain channel was not reported.
3.2.4 Other surface modification technology
Apart from the above surface modification methods, researchers also adopted other methods to change the morphology of the microchannel surface. Kandlikar et al. [104] investigated parallel rectangular microchannels with inlet pressure restrictor and fabricated nucleation sites. Nucleation sites were created at bottom channel wall through laser engraving method. It was found that the combination of fabricated nucleation sites and 4% area pressure drop elements (PDE) completely prevented reverse flow and eliminated the related instabilities. In this section, the microchannel surface modification technologies including nanowire, carbon nanotube and porous surface, were introduced. Overall, although in most cases, the benefits of nanowire coating surface were observed in both heat transfer and flow stability in most studies [24, 91–93], however, the copper nanowires coating was reported having little effect on controlling flow boiling instability [90]. Moreover, high mass flux of HFE-7000 prevented capillarity-induced annular flow due to that low-surfacetension of HFE-7000 suppressed the stability of liquid film, and interrupted capillary flow, which limited the CHF value to 120 W/cm 2 [105]. This indicates the nanowire design in microchannel should be designed considering the material of microchannel and properties of working fluid, in order to obtain better performance. The CNTs technology also shows great performance in enhancing heat transfer due to the increase in nucleation density and enhancement in bubble growth [96–99], however, some previous research on flow boiling instability reported the negative effect CNTs on flow boiling instability [97, 99], which may prevent the application of CNTs in microchannel heat exchanger although it has excellent performance in heat transfer. Different from the nanowire and CNTs methods, the porous surface performed well in both heat transfer enhancement and boiling instability controlling. Whereas, Zong et al. designed novel porous-wall microchannel, and drawn the flow operational maps, and divided the operation into unstable and stable zones [106]. This

3.3 Vapor venting method
One of the reasons that cause flow boiling instability is the rapid bubble growth which increases the system pressure or even results to severe clogging, reverse flow and flow instability. To tackle with this problem, a direct idea is to regulate the bubble growth and bubbly moving. Hence, vapor venting method has been proposed to extract vapor, eliminate the bubble clogging and dry-out phenomenon [107–110]. Fig. 14a shows the schematic of a vapor venting design. Milnes et al. [107] developed hydrophobic Teflon membrane wall on a copper microchannel. The study on the flow and heat transfer in the microchannel indicated that vapor venting is helpful to suppress the flow instability. Meanwhile, it was suggested that increasing the transmembrane pressure and reducing gas velocity can further improve the vapor venting effectiveness. In another research,
Milnes et al. [108] designed hydrophobic PTFE membrane and separate vapor transport channels based on a parallel microchannel, and found a significant reduction of pressure drop suggesting the impact of vapor venting on significant pressure drop reduction, as shown in Fig. 14b. Pressure drop reduction is helpful to weaken the occurrence of Ledinegg instabilities. Sharma et al. [26] investigate the combination of vapor venting technology and surface modifications. It was found that the vapor can be easily removed through the hydrophobic pores in the exit plenum, which lowered pressure drop and weakened temperature fluctuations. Apreotesi et al. [109] investigated a disk shaped microchannel which was covered with a porous Nylon membrane. The study on boiling flow of water indicates that the Nylon membrane can effectively remove the water vapor. It was found that the pressure drop was reduced by as much as 34% through extraction of vapor. Apreotesi et al. further suggested that suitable value of flow rate and pressure can get a better vapor extraction and lower the pressure below the level of single-phase flow. Fang et al. [110] proposed a rectangular microchannel with a hydrophobic porous membrane for phase-separation. Instead of
