 the slug and annular flow in the conventional channel, At the high heat flux, the hydrophobic porous membrane only induces a massive vapor escaping process which prevented the conventional occurrence of slug and annular flow in channel and stopped the further increase in pressure due to the limited vapor. Results show the hydrophobic porous membrane can effectively drain the vapor out, which mitigated the vapor accumulation in channel and reduced the pressure drop, as shown in Fig. 14c, and inhibit the dry-out in the channel.
3.4 Modification of working fluid property
To improve heat transfer, nanofluid was proposed which is created by adding low concentrations of metallic or nonmetallic nanoparticles to base fluid [111]. These nanoparticles distributing in the fluid greatly enhance the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid. Considering the significant enhancement of nanofluid in heat transfer [112, 113], in recent years, attentions have been payed to the combination of microchannels and nanofluids considering the increased conductivity of nanofluid, micro-scale effect of microchannel [27], and flow properties [114, 115]. Yu et al. [114] studied boiling flow of Al2O3–water nanofluids through a minichannel experimentally, and found that adding Al2O3 nanoparticles to working fluid delayed the ONB point and mitigated the onset of flow instabilities. This was due to decreased size range and number density of nucleating sites, decreased surface wettability, and thinning of thermal boundary layer near the wall. Xu et al. [115] investigated microchannel with pure water and nanofluid consisting of Al2O3 nanoparticles as the working fluids. The results showed that the flow boiling instability was obviously suppressed by the nanofluid and deposition of nanoparticle did not occur. Firstly, the pressure drop was significantly reduced. Secondly, nanofluid inhibits the dry out development. Thirdly, nano particles results to the miniature bubbles and
prevents the bubbles to growing to elongated bubbles which may block the channel. Hence, nanofluids can maintain a stable flow, as summarized in Fig. 15. However, contradictory phenomenon was also observed. Duursma et al. [116] investigated boiling of nanofluids in rectangular high-aspect-ratio microchannels, it was found that the nanoparticle concentration did not influence the pressure drop for single phase, while boiling occurred, the nanoparticle concentration leaded the pressure drop unstable and fluctuating and affected the frequency and amplitude of these fluctuations. Overall, although the nanofluid shows good performance in heat transfer enhancement, the flow boiling instability suppression still depends on the microchannel dimension, operating mode and the material of working fluid. Besides, there is also another drawback for the nanofluid in microchannel: the deposition of nano particles, which will bring negative effects on flow and thermal properties. The nanoparticle deposition improves the surface of microchannel, influencing the heat transfer procedure and fluid adjacent to the channel surface [117]. Wu et al. [118] studied heat transfer of water-Al2O3 nanofluid in silicon microchannels. The nanoparticle deposition was observed with increasing wall temperature in boiling heat transfer procedure. The deposition and adhesion to the channel walls became severe when the boiling began, which hinder the nanofluid boiling heat transfer in microchannels, and the deposition layer will finally lead to clogging of microchannels [119].
3.5 Combined methods
Wang et al. [120] investigated microchannel with silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and three-dimensional flow path design, as shown in Fig. 16a, and compared its performance with a plain-wall microchannel. On one hand, the surface modification affects the flow properties. After the ONB occurs, SiNWcoated device shows a much lower pressure drop than plain

 wall device. On the other hand, the manifold design of microchannel presents much better performance in suppressing flow instability, which brings about uniform flow distribution, even coolant delivering and a more uniform temperature distribution. Hence, the combination of three-dimensional flow path design and silicon nanowire has an effective control on wall temperature fluctuation and flow pattern variation in order to maintain a stable flow and prevent dry-out. Deng et al. [121] combined the design of reentrant and porous wall and developed Ω-shaped reentrant porous microchannels, as shown in Fig. 16b. It was found that these porous microchannels significantly increased nucleation sites which promoted the generation of bubble nucleation, and enabled the microchannel to maintain a stable boiling even with small subcooling. Xu et al. [122] developed a porous wall microchannel with pinfins. The porous walls were fabricated through building a large number of pin fins in lines, as shown in Fig. 16c. On one hand, this design provided a lots of corners that can be seen as nucleation sites. Hence, it can enable nucleation heat transfer within
pin-fin porous channel wall. On the other hand, different from the solid channel wall, this pin-fin porous wall can prevent the continuous growth of the confined bubbles and change them to un-confined bubble flow, which eliminated channel blockage, meanwhile improve the uniform distribution of working fluid between different channels.
4 Conclusions and future works
In this paper, the flow boiling fundamentals was firstly introduced, which is followed by the mechanism description and classification of instability. As stated, the bubble dynamics which contains bubble generation, bubble growth and bubble transition, contribute to the flow boiling instability of Ledinegg instability, pressure drop fluctuation, temperature fluctuation, reverse flow, and dry out, etc. To tackle this problem, numerous researches have been carried out, focusing on controlling the phase change, regulating the bubble behaviors.
Fig. 16 Combined methods to suppress flow instability. (a): SiNW-coated manifold microchannels [120], (b): Reentrant porous microchannels [121], (c): porous pin-fin microchannels [122])
Heat Mass Transfer (2021) 57:889–910 905


 These methods are classified into five types: geometry modification, surface modification, vapor venting technology, working fluid modification, and some combined methods. Based on a though review on these methods, it can be concluded as follows:
& Modifying the microchannel passage geometry is an effective way to control the evaporation of working fluid from liquid to vapor and to further control the flow pattern to suppress the flow boiling instability. The diverging channel is helpful to reduce pressure, a further modification of periodic diverging – converging can provide additional liquid for evaporation to maintain the wetness of channel wall, the slots on channel wall or reentrant cavities can also provide additional liquid and enhance nucleate boiling, the segmented channel is helpful for prevent the elongation of bubble. Different from the above modification, the inlet orifice increases the inlet flow resistance, and increases the pressure of the system, which helps eliminate the negative slope of the pressure-mass flow rate curve and suppresses the instability. & It should be noticed that not all the passage modification has positive effect on flow boiling stability. This indicates that further research is necessary to study the effect of different channel passage structure to understand fundamental and the relations between the structure and working fluid, channel materials, etc. & Three main types of surface modification were introduced: silicon/metal nanowires, porous and carbon nanotubes. These surface modifications change the morphology of the channel wall which influences the bubble generation and bubble behavior. The nanowires, porous and carbon nanotubes structures enable an increase in nucleation-site density resulting large number of small bubbles which helps wash out slug bubble, and maintains rewetting properties. However, the limitation of nanowire should also be noticed, and the cross impact of working fluid properties and mass flow rate should be considered in the designing of nanowire. & To regulate the bubble behavior to eliminating the intensive bubble or elongated bubble, extracting the vapor phase is an easy and important method. Hence, vapor venting method through a membrane or porous media to separate the vapor from the fluid has been proposed. The previous studies prove that the vapor venting method is an effective way to reduce pressure caused by vapor and to suppress flow instability. Therein, vapor extracting efficiency is influenced by the operating mass flow rate and pressure. Therefore, it’s critical to control the operating parameters. & Due to the high conductivity of nanofluid, it was adopted as working fluid in microchannels to enhance the heat transfer properties. Meanwhile, its positive effects on
mitigating flow instability were also investigated. The nano particles not only increased the conductivity, but also prevented large size bubble which may result to clogging. Besides, the deposition of nanoparticles is the main drawback which will deteriorate the heat transfer and the boiling procedure may accelerate the deposition. This indicates the stability of nanofluid is the key element of application.
Although numerous valuable works on controlling flow boiling instabilities have been carried out in the past decades and lots of progresses have been achieved, there are still some aspects that should be further investigated.
& Phase change and the corresponding phenomenon of bubble behaviors are the fundamentals of understanding the flow boiling instability mechanism. The bubble growth results in complex liquid-vapor two phase flow. With the variation of mass flux and heat flux, the two phase flow pattern will transform to different modes which may bring about flow instability. Therefore, understanding the flow pattern and establishing a general flow pattern map in terms of mass flux and heat flux is an important further work. & In recent years, nano fluid is an effective way to enhance heat transfer in microchannel and may be an interesting method to mitigate flow boiling instability or lead to instability. To further apply this technology, the boundary of the instability and the discipline should be further investigated. & The combination of modification methods should be further studied. For example, the modification of microchannel geometry and the modification of working fluid are combined, to eliminate the deposition of nano particles and meanwhile suppress the flow boiling instability. & Current studies mainly focus on the negative effect of flow boiling instability and measures to suppress the instability. However, the positive effect of flow instability should also be considered. The critical point is to control the flow instability to a limited level and to utilize this instability to enhance heat transfer.

title: Enhanced Nucleate Pool Boiling by Coupling the Pinning Act and Cluster Bubble Nucleation of Micro-nano Composited Surfaces
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
abstractNote: A new idea by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-ﬁns and the cluster bubble nucleation of nonuniform nanowalls, was proposed to improve the boiling performance on micro-nano composited surface in the low surface tension liquid. To realize this idea, the uniform/nonuniform nanowalls were composited on the smooth surface and micro-pin-ﬁnned surfaces. The pool boiling performance of a smooth surface, the uniform/nonuniform nanowall surfaces, micro-pin-ﬁnned surface, and micro-nano composited surfaces with uniform/nonuniform nanowalls were tested and compared with each other. Both the heat transfer coeﬃcient (HTC) and critical heat ﬂux (CHF) of the micro-nano composited surface with nonuniform nanowalls (named PFNW2) are signiﬁcantly increased compared to the micro-pin-ﬁnned surface. In addition, small bubble departure diameters, high bubble departure frequency and velocity were also found on PFNW2. A unique bubble behavior, named microbubble explosive emission, was observed, which provides a strong evidence for the effectiveness of the method to enhance boiling heat transfer by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-ﬁns and the cluster bubble nucleation. It is suggested that high liquid subcooling and modulated nanostructures for bubble departure promotion are both indispensable for further enhancement of HTC and CHF in low surface tension liquid.

Enhanced Nucleate Pool Boiling by Coupling the Pinning Act and Cluster Bubble Nucleation of Micro-nano Composited Surfaces
Bin Liu a, Lingmin Yu b, Yonghai Zhang a,∗, Paolo Di Marco c, Jinjia Wei a,d,∗
a School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China b School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, No. 2 Xuefu Road, 710021, China c Department of Energy, Systems, Constructions and Territory Engineering, University of Pisa, Pisa 56122, Italy d State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow in Power Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China
article info
Article history:
Received 11 February 2020 Revised 28 April 2020 Accepted 19 May 2020
Keywords: Nanowall micro-pin-fin enhanced nucleate boiling microbubble explosive emission pinning act bubble dynamics
abstract
A new idea by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation of nonuniform nanowalls, was proposed to improve the boiling performance on micro-nano composited surface in the low surface tension liquid. To realize this idea, the uniform/nonuniform nanowalls were composited on the smooth surface and micro-pin-finned surfaces. The pool boiling performance of a smooth surface, the uniform/nonuniform nanowall surfaces, micro-pin-finned surface, and micro-nano composited surfaces with uniform/nonuniform nanowalls were tested and compared with each other. Both the heat transfer coefficient (HTC) and critical heat flux (CHF) of the micro-nano composited surface with nonuniform nanowalls (named PFNW2) are significantly increased compared to the micro-pin-finned surface. In addition, small bubble departure diameters, high bubble departure frequency and velocity were also found on PFNW2. A unique bubble behavior, named microbubble explosive emission, was observed, which provides a strong evidence for the effectiveness of the method to enhance boiling heat transfer by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation. It is suggested that high liquid subcooling and modulated nanostructures for bubble departure promotion are both indispensable for further enhancement of HTC and CHF in low surface tension liquid.
© 2020 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Boiling is widely exploited in power generation, nuclear power, thermal management system and heat dissipation of electronic devices [1]. Enhanced boiling heat transfer performance, including the increase of boiling heat transfer coefficient (HTC) and the critical heat flux (CHF), is of great benefit to the energy conversion process. On the one hand, it can reduce the working temperature of equipment effectively, thereby reducing equipment failure caused by thermal stress, thermal fatigue, thermal corrosion rate and prolonging the equipment service life. On the other hand, it can increase the heat flux limit of the equipment and prevent the equipment from burning out. Therefore, the enhancement of boiling heat transfer has been an enduring research hotspot in the past decades. Surfaces with nanostructures have been extensively studied for their great potential in enhancing boiling heat transfer due to the
∗ Corresponding authors. E-mail addresses: zyh002@mail.xjtu.edu.cn (Y. Zhang), jjwei@mail.xjtu.edu.cn (J. Wei).
improvement of the nucleation sites density and wickability [2]. Using the oblique-angle deposition scheme, Li et al. [3] fabricated nanorods on the plain surface and achieved notable enhancement of nucleate boiling heat transfer through increasing the nucleation site density. Chen et al. [4] found that the boiling heat transfer performance can be improved pronouncedly by nanowires due to their high surface tension forces offered by liquids in nanowire arrays. The heat transfer performance of the nanowires surfaces was further improved by modifying the wickability and configuration of nanowires [5–6]. Hendricks et al. [7] obtained notable pool boiling heat transfer enhancement of water by using microreactor assisted-nanomaterial-deposition technology. Under the action of high nucleation site density and strong wettability, the maximum CHF of the flower like ZnO nanostructures on bare Al surface increases up to 4 times with a wall superheat reduction of 25–38 K compared to that of bare Al surface. Nanoporous is another effective way for the enhanced boiling heat transfer, Lee et al. [8] and Tang et al. [9] both obtained significant enhancement of the nucleate boiling heat transfer by nanoporous surface. Although the nanostructured surface has remarkable advantages in improving nucleation site density and wickability, there
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2020.119979 0017-9310/© 2020 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.


 2 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979
are other factors, i.e. the bubble behavior and hydrodynamic instability, that affect the boiling heat transfer performance; nanostructured surfaces have shown little improvement in these areas. Many kinds of microstructured surface have the advantages of regulating bubble behavior and hydrodynamic instability [10–13]. Therefore, coupling the nanostructured surface and microstructured surface is a good method to further improve the heat transfer performance while preserving the strength of both [14–17]. However, for the boiling in high wetting liquid, i.e. FC-72, R113a and PF5060, compositing the uniform nanostructures on the microstructured surfaces usually can not further increase the HTC and CHF at the same time [18–21]. Zhou et al. [18] found that the HTC of micro-pinfinned surface composited with uniform nanograss is increased, while the CHF is decreased compared to micro-pin-finned surface. Kim et al. [19] suggested that the local coating nanostructures may be an optimized way to increase the HTC and CHF at the same time, because composition of the uniform nanostructured coating on the micro-pillar structured surface occupied the vacant space between the pillars and obstructed the liquid supply, and thus, deteriorated the heat transfer performance. Cao et al. [20] observed a 20%-40% improvement of CHF on the modulated nanoparticlecoating surfaces, while no enhancement in CHF can be found on the uniform nanoparticle-coating surfaces. Therefore, the existence of nanostructures on the microstructured surface can be an obstruction for liquid supply due to its higher flow resistance and lower capillary wicking, leading to a decrease of CHF [21]. There are many mechanisms for enhancing the boiling heat transfer on micro-nano composited surfaces, such as increasing the heat transfer area, increasing the nucleation site density, modifying the surface wettability, improving the surface capillary wicking, and adjusting the bubble behavior, etc. In most literature, increasing heat transfer area and increasing nucleation site density are classified as two important factors for the enhancing of boiling heat transfer [14, 15, 18, 20–22]. However, the complexity of the micro-nano composited structures makes the calculation of the actual heat transfer area and the density of nucleation sites very difficult, and there are still contradictions in the literature about the effects of actual heat transfer area on boiling heat transfer [21, 23, 24], so it is difficult to effectively strip it from many factors for indepth mechanism analysis. For the boiling on micro-nano composited surfaces in low surface tension liquids, the effect of modifying surface wettability and improving capillary wicking on boiling heat transfer is very limited [18, 20, 21, 23]. Some researchers currently believe that adjusting the bubble behavior is an important factor to improve the boiling heat transfer performance, but they only simply and qualitatively compared the size and number of bubbles through boiling images in these reports [14, 15, 20, 25, 26]. Therefore, the mechanism of boiling heat transfer by adjusting the bubble behavior should systematically and thoroughly be studied. Zhou et al. [18] found that a large amount of surface energy is converted into kinetic energy of bubble detachment, and thus, significantly accelerates the bubble detachment due to the vigorous bubble coalescence. Therefore, by controlling the bubble coalescing and making bubbles densely generate locally, it can promote the coalescence and rapid departure of bubbles locally, then effectively delay the boiling deterioration and improve the liquid replenishment ability in the high heat flux area, thereby improving CHF. In the present study, a new way to regulate bubble behavior, by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation of nonuniform nanostructures, was proposed to improve the boiling performance on micro-nano composited surface in low surface tension liquids. To realize this idea, uniform and nonuniform nanowalls were composited onto the micro-pinfinned surface. The boiling performance of a smooth surface, uniform/nonuniform nanostructured surfaces, a micro-pin-finned surface, and micro-nano composite surfaces were compared. The re
lationship between surface morphology and bubble dynamics was systematically analyzed. The effects of bubble coalescence on bubble behavior and heat transfer performance were studied comprehensively by an improved force-energy balance model.
2. Experiment system and method
2.1. Preparation of boiling surfaces
Single-sided polished P-doped silicon chips with a size of 10 mm × 10 mm × 0.5 mm (length × width × thickness) were used as the heating surfaces in the present study. A smooth silicon chip, named SS, was used for comparison. One kind of microstructured surface, named PF, was processed by the dry etching technique. Then, two types of nanostructured surfaces were fabricated by compositing uniform ZnO nanowalls (named NW1) and nonuniform ZnO nanowalls (named NW2) on smooth surfaces. Similarly, uniform nanowalls and nonuniform nanowalls were also composited on micro-pin-finned surfaces to fabricate two types of micro-nano composited surfaces, named PFNW1 and PFNW2, respectively. The samples with ZnO nanowalls were prepared by a very simple method: firstly, a thin ZnO film was coated on the silicon chip as the seed layer; secondly, ZnO nanowalls were prepared on the silicon surface with the seed layer in a growth solution [27] (Supplementary Section 1).
Fig. 1 shows the images of the micro-nanostructured surfaces token by a Field Emission-SEM (MAIA3 LMH). A thin layer of uniform ZnO nanowalls can be observed on NW1, as shown in Fig. 1(a). As for the nonuniform ZnO nanowall surface, cluster nanorods were found to bond on the uniform nanowalls (see Fig. 1(b)). The dimensions of micro-pin-fins are 50 μm × 50 μm × 60 μm (length × width × height) and the center distance of two adjacent micro-pin-fins is 100 μm, as shown in Fig. 1(c). As for the micro-nano composited surfaces (see Figs. 1(d)-(e)), the nanowalls were observed to distribute almost uniformly on PFNW1, while for PFNW2, large amounts of agglomerated nanorods were observed on the top side of micro-pin-fins and on the bottom side of the micro-channels.
2.2. Experimental set up and procedures
The experimental setup consists of a Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) boiling chamber, an auxiliary heater, a condensation system, a DC power source, a cubic PMMA support, a heating surface, a data acquisition system, a computer, a rubber bag, two thermocouples (Fig. 2), and a visualization system (Fig. 3). FC-72 was used as the working fluid with the subcooling of 0 K, 25 K, and 35 K. Pool boiling occurred in the transparent PMMA chamber, with an internal size of 136 × 136 × 120 mm3. The size of the heating surface is 10 mm × 10 mm and the ratio of the heater length (L) to the capillary length (Lc =[σ / g(ρl-ρg)]0.5) L/Lc > 12. Lu et al. [28] found that the effect of heater size is not significant when L/Lc > 8. Therefore, the effect of heater size can be neglected in the present study. The temperature of the working fluid (Tf) was maintained by the auxiliary heater and the condensation system. The condensation system consists of a mini pump, two silicone hoses, a water cooling machine, and a coil heat exchanger. The rubber bag was used to maintain the pressure in the boiling chamber at atmospheric pressure. The temperatures of the liquid and heating surface were measured by two T-type thermocouples, respectively. The thermocouple is at the upper right of the chip and has a distance of 2 cm to the center of the chip. Heating surfaces were parallelly glued both on the top of the cubie PMMA support and on the side of the support for experiments in horizontal and vertical orientations. (Supplementary Section 2). The bubble behaviors of the boiling surfaces both in the


 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979 3
Fig. 1. FE-SEM images of the micro-nanostructured surfaces (a: NW1; b: NW2; c: PF; d: PFNW1; e: PFNW2)
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the pool boiling experimental setup.


 4 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979
Fig. 3. Schematic diagram of the visualization system (a: at horizontal orientation; and b: at vertical orientation).
horizontal and vertical orientations were captured by a high-speed camera (NAC Memrecam HX-6E) with a micro-lens (Fig. 3). The recording speed was set at 5000 fps with a resolution of 896 × 896 for horizontal oriented experiments, and a LED light source was used (Fig. 3(a)). While a recording speed of 10000 fps and a resolution of 649 × 672 were used for experiments in the vertical orientation, with a coaxial light source adopted (Fig. 3(b)). The pool boiling heat transfer performance, including the wall superheat ( Tsat), CHF and HTC were measured and compared. The bubble behavior parameters, including bubble departure diameter D, bubble departure frequency f, bubble departure velocity V, and nucleation site density N, were captured at q = 6.5 W/cm2 at the liquid subcooling ( Tsub) of 25 K for each testing surface (Supplementary Section 3).
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Boling heat transfer performance
To ensure the reliability of the experimental setup, boiling curves of SS and PF were compared with those of Honda et al. [29]. The boiling curves in this study are very close to those in Ref. [29] (Fig. S5). In addition, the boiling experiment for each condition was repeated at least three times and good repeatability was observed (Fig. S5). The SEM images of NW1 before and after 20 completed pool boiling tests were compared, and no significant change could be found in surface morphology (Fig. S6). Therefore, these experiments were reliable and repeatable, and the surfaces are durable for practical applications (Supplementary Section 4). The boiling heat transfer performances of testing surfaces were tested at the liquid subcooling of 0 K, 25 K, and 35 K in horizontal and vertical orientations, as shown in Fig. 4. As we can see, a slight increase in the wall superheat on SS and PF is observed at vertical orientation under some conditions. However, in most conditions, the boiling curves for each surface at horizontal orientation are very close to that at the vertical orientation in the low and moderate heat flux regions. While in the high heat flux region (when the heat flux approaching CHF), the tested surfaces show lower wall superheat and higher CHF value at horizontal orientation compared to that at vertical orientation under their corresponding conditions. The effects of liquid subcooling on pool boiling performance are different on different surfaces. The heat transfer enhancement is not remarkable on SS. While, the CHF of PF, NW1, and NW2 are strongly increased, although no notable decrease in the wall superheat is observed. As for PFNW1 and PFNW2, the heat transfer performance deteriorates in the high heat flux region when Tsub = 0 K, but the wall superheat decreases pronouncedly when Tsub = 25 K and 35 K, and the CHF values are also increased dra
matically. The increased subcooling enhances natural convection, but the subcooled liquid confines the departure of nucleated bubbles, which decreases the nucleate boiling heat transfer. Therefore, the effects of subcooling on heat transfer depend on the combined action of these two opposite effects [30]. In low and moderate heat flux regions, with limited nucleation sites, although an increased subcooling enhances natural convection, subcooled working fluids confines the departure of nucleated bubbles. Therefore, the effects of liquid subcooling on the boiling heat transfer coefficient are not very significant. While in the fully developed nucleate boiling region, evaporation dominates the boiling heat transfer, and the effects of natural convection on boiling heat transfer can be neglected. On the other hand, large bubbles and vapor patches formed due to the vigorous bubble coalescence under this condition. The large bubbles and vapor patches covering the heating surface reduces the evaporation area, which deteriorates the heat transfer performance and the boiling curve becomes flat. As the liquid subcooling increases, bubbles tend to depart with a smaller diameter, which causes the probability of lateral bubble coalescence decreases. At this time, the high boiling heat transfer coefficient can still be maintained at a higher heat flux. This indicates that the increase of subcooling is more beneficial for the enhanced boiling heat transfer for surfaces with micro/nanostructures, micronano composited surfaces especially. The pool boiling performance was compared in Fig. 5. Compared to SS, NW1 has no distinct improvement in CHF (11%) in the saturated pool boiling, but the maximum HTC value increases by 131% and a low wall superheat (10.0 K) occurs at the onset of nucleate boiling (ONB). Different from NW1, a remarkable increase in both CHF (81%) and HTC (93%) was observed from PF. However, the wall superheat of PF at ONB is very high (20.5 K, which is 4.5 K higher than that of SS). Compared to PF, PFNW1 also has a notable increase in HTC, but almost no difference in CHF. Therefore, it can be inferred that the existence of uniform ZnO nanowalls is helpful to increase HTC, instead of CHF. While for NW2, a much larger increment in CHF (41%) can be observed compared to NW1. On the contrary, the enhancement of NW2 in the maximum HTC is not as high as that of NW1 compared to SS (67%). Similarly, PFNW2 has a higher CHF than that of PFNW1, but maximum HTC of PFNW2 is lower than that of PFNW1. The increased enhancement of surface area ratio (r) is a significant factor that improves the HTC. The increase of r helped to increase the nucleation site density and thus the HTC. In addition, the increase of r enlarged the heat transfer area, thereby increasing the HTC. However, in some cases, the effect of r on HTC is not clear. For example, the r of PFNW1 and PFNW2 is 2.2 times that of NW1 and NW2, but at low and moderate heat fluxes, the difference in HTCs between these four samples is very small (as shown in Fig. 5 (c)). In addition, the r of NW2/PFNW2 and


 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979 5
Fig. 4. Comparison of boiling curves under different liquid subcoolings and orientations (a: SS; b: PF; c: NW1; d: PFNW1; e: NW2; f: PFNW2).
NW1/PFNW1 is almost the same, but the HTC of NW2/PFNW2 is slightly smaller than that of NW1/PFNW1. Therefore, the difference in HTC between the samples can not be attributed to the difference in surface area enhancement ratio. The remarkable distinctions in pool boiling heat transfer performance between NW1 and NW2 as well as PFNW1 and PFNW2 indicate that the mechanisms for enhanced boiling heat transfer by uniform nanowalls and nonuniform nanowalls are completely different although they have similar morphology. As the liquid subcooling increases to 35 K, the heat transfer performance on PFNW1 and PFNW2 is significantly improved, with a remarkable decrease of wall superheat at CHF and a remarkable increase of CHF: the wall superheat of PFNW1 and PFNW2 at CHF when Tsub = 35 K decreased by 13.5 K and 16.2 K compared to those under Tsub = 0 K, respectively. As a comparison, the decrements of SS, NW1, NW2, and PF in wall superheat at CHF when Tsub = 35 K are −1.5 K, 3.3 K, 7.9 K, and 4.1 K compared to those under Tsub = 0 K, respectively. In addition, the increment of NW1, NW2, PFNW1 and PFNW2 in CHF when Tsub = 35 K are 106%, 102%, 91% and 100% compared to those under Tsub = 0 K, respectively. On the contrary, the CHF of SS and PF are only increased by 59% and 70% compared to those un
der Tsub = 0 K, respectively. Furthermore, the maximum HTC of PFNW1 and PFNW2 at Tsub = 35 K is increased by 414% and 417% compared to those under Tsub = 0 K, respectively. These results indicate that compared to SS and PF, the surfaces with ZnO nanowalls, especially for the micro-nano composited surfaces (PFNW1 and PFNW2), have greater advantages at high liquid subcoolings. In our previous study [30], we investigated the subcooled pool boiling heat transfer on micro-nano composited surfaces processed by femtosecond laser, and we found that the sensitivity of the micro-nano composited surfaces’s CHFs of to liquid subcooling increased with the increase of nanostructure covered area. Lee et al. [31] found that the CHF on nanowire-forested surfaces is much more sensitive to the liquid subcooling than that on smooth surfaces. Therefore, it is suggested that micro-nano composited surfaces have great application potential in subcooled boiling heat transfer.
3.2. Bubble behaviors
It can be found in previous section that significant distinctions in boiling heat transfer performance exist among the tested surfaces. There is a close relationship between bubble behavior and


 6 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979
Fig. 5. Comparison of boiling heat transfer performance: (a) Boiling curves: 0 K-horizontal; (b) Boiling curves: 35 K-horizontal; (c) HTC: 0 K-horizontal; (d) HTC: 35 Khorizontal.
boiling heat transfer performance. Therefore, the origins of the differences in boiling heat transfer performance can be explained by studying the difference in bubble behaviors.
3.2.1. Comparison of bubble behavior parameters
The average values of bubble departure diameter D, bubble departure period τ , bubble departure velocity V, and nucleation site density N are illustrated in Fig. 6. Bubbles grew slowly, with large bubble departure diameters due to the low nucleation site density on SS. On the contrary, the nucleation site density of NW1 and NW2 significantly increases compared to SS, and the bubble departure diameter decreases significantly due to vigorous bubble lateral coalescence (Figs. S7(b-c) and Figs. S8(b-c)). Although the increase in the nucleation site density of PF is not remarkable compared to that of SS, the bubble departure frequency and average bubble departure velocity increase dramatically, accompanied by a much smaller bubble departure diameter due to the pinning act on the sidewalls of the micro-pin-fins during the growth ((Fig. S7(d) and Fig. S8(d))). The contact line of a growing bubble is pinned, and the contact angle is decreased with the increase of bubble diameter under this condition. Therefore, the pinning act of the micropin-fins lead to a significant decrease of bubble contact diameter and contact angle, resulting in a decrease in the surface tension force and viscous drag force of bubbles and then the promotion of bubble departure [32]. Therefore, although the nucleation site density of PF is less than that of NW1 and NW2, its bubble departure diameter is smaller, while its bubble departure frequency and bubble departure velocity are larger. This indicates that micropin-finned structures have a very significant effect on bubble behaviors, especially on promoting bubble departure. Compared to PF, with the composition of nanowalls, the nucleation site density of PFNW1 and PFNW2 is increased significantly, resulting in more dramatical bubble coalescence. In addition, the coupling of the in
creased nucleation site density by nanowalls and the pinning act of the micro-pin-fines lead to a significant decrease of the bubble departure diameter, a clear increase of the bubble departure frequency and velocity (Figs. S7(e-f) and Figs. S8(e-f)). As we discussed above, the difference in surface area enhancement ratio between samples is not the only reason for the difference in HTC. Nucleation site density also plays an important role in nucleate boiling performance. It can be observed that NW1, NW2, PFNW1, and PFNW2 have a similar nucleation site density. Therefore, their HTCs are close to each other in the low and moderate heat flux regions. In addition, for surfaces with the same surface area enhancement ratio (NW1 versus NW2 and PFNW1 versus PFNW2), the slight difference in HTC between them can be attributed to the slight difference in the nucleation site density. The deviations of D, τ , and V of single bubbles from the corresponding average value on SS are small, as shown in Fig. 6. Conversely, the points of NW1 are much more dispersed than the average value. Although NW2 has no increase in the nucleation site density compared to NW1, the diameter and the period of a large number of bubbles are significantly lower than the average value (Fig. S9). As for NW2, a large number of effective cavities can be formed due to the interweaving of nanorods (Fig. 1b). In addition, the bubble embryos in the same cluster can be captured by the nanorods and merged into a larger bubble. Therefore, the bubble coalescence on NW2 is more concentrated, and a part of bubbles can departure more easily than those on NW1 due to the nonuniform of cluster nanorods (Supplementary Section 4). Similar to NW1, under the synergistic effect of high nucleation site density of nanowall surface and pinning act of micro-pin-fins, the bubbles on PFNW1 tend to detach at smaller diameters. However, the increase of nucleation site density also improves the bubble lateral coalescence, and some of the bubbles merge with each other to form a larger size before they detach from the surface. On the contrary,


 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979 7
Fig. 6. The average values of bubble behavior parameters (a: the bubble departure diameter; b: bubble period; c: bubble departure velocity; and d: nucleation site density).
Fig. 7. A time series of microbubble explosive emission on PFNW2 at vertical orientation (scale bar: 0.1 mm).
the existence of cluster nanorods inhibits the merging of bubbles between different clusters effectively. Therefore, much more bubbles of PFNW2 grow and detach at a faster speed with a smaller diameter compared to that of PFNW1.
3.2.2. Microbubble explosive emission phenomenon
A unique phenomenon, named microbubble explosive emission, can be observed on PFNW2: a large number of microbubbles with the diameter of approximately 100 μm are ejected in strings from the surface at such a fast speed (~0.5 m/s) that a high-speed camera cannot capture the clear interface of these bubbles (Fig. 7). It should be noted that the bubbles in Fig. 7 are ejected at a very rapid speed which is perpendicular to the focal plane but the microscopic lenses have a very small depth of field. Therefore, it looks much larger and the outline becomes very blurry on the next frame after departure. It can be observed from Fig. 7, the 1st bubble was generated in the micro-channel between two adjacent micro-pin-fins when t = 0 ms, then grows and departs within 0.3
ms. No clear bubble waiting time was observed after the previous bubble departure, and three entire bubble periods were experienced within 1 ms. Similar phenomena have been reported in literatures at high liquid subcoolings in transition boiling [33–35] and on micro-nano composited structured surfaces [36] (microbubble emission boiling), and in the nucleate boiling region in the surfactant solution (bubble explosion) [37]. The main reason for the occurrence of the microbubble emission boiling is the collapse of large bubbles under strong condensation by the liquid [33–35], while the origin of bubble explosions is the failure of large bubble coalescence due to the mutual exclusion between hydrophilic groups on the interface of adjacent bubbles [37]. However, the microbubble explosive emission can be observed in FC-72 at low heat fluxes under the subcooling of 25 K in the present study, and it occurs under coupling of the pin-effects of micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation in nanorods, as shown in Fig. 8. For PFNW2, bubbles can intensively generate in the clustered nanorods, and quickly co


 8 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979
Fig. 8. Schematic diagram of microbubble explosive emission on PFNW2.
alescence with each other. However, due to the existence of the micro-pin-fins, the contact line of bubbles is pinned, leading to these bubbles depart with small diameters. In addition, the vigorous coalescence of bubbles in the clustered nanorods causes a large amount of surface energy to be converted into kinetic energy, which provides additional help for bubbles to depart with a smaller size. Therefore, the behavior and triggering mechanism of the microbubble explosive emission is very different from those of the microbubble emission boiling and the bubble explosion, which is a unique phenomenon that has not been reported in the literature. The microbubble explosive emission phenomenon promotes bubble departure and prevents bubbles from coalescing into a large size. Therefore, it is of great significance for enhancing boiling heat transfer by preventing the dryout in the high heat flux region.
3.3. Effects of surface morphology on the bubble behaviors
3.3.1. Bubble dynamics analysis: force-energy balance model of bubbles
To investigate the effects of surface morphology on the bubble behaviors, the bubble departure diameter of tested surfaces was verified based on the bubble force-energy balance model of bubbles proposed by Zhou et al. [38]. The resultant force perpendicular to the heating surface for a single bubble can be expressed as [39]:
Fnet = Fb + Fi + Fp − Fs − Fd − Fm (1)
Until bubble departure, the resultant force on the bubble is 0. Note that Eq. (1) only works for a single bubble. For SS at q = 6.5
W/cm2, there is nearly no bubble coalescence when bubbles depart due to the low nucleation site density. Therefore, the bubble departure diameter can be predicted by the force balance model for a single bubble. In the nucleate boiling heat transfer region, the coalescence of bubbles is very common. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the effect of bubble coalescence on bubble departure. During the process of bubble coalescence, there is a conversion process of surface energy to kinetic energy, resulting in the bubble jumping [18, 38]. The conversion of energy mainly includes the change in surface energy Es, the viscosity dissipation work Evis caused by the bubble disturbance, the interfacial adhesion between the bubble and the surface Eadh, the energy generated by the net force Fnet acting on the bubble during the oscillation Ef, and the kinetic energy Ek. According to energy conservation, the energy conversion during bubble coalescence can be expressed as:
Ek =
n ∑
i=1
(Es,i + Evis,i + Eadh,i
) − Es,c + Ef (2)
Only when Ek > 0, the bubble departure velocity V > 0, the bubble can detach from the heating surface (Supplementary Section 5).
3.3.2. Effects of bubble coalescence and surface morphology on bubble departure
According to the data of bubble behavior in Fig. 5, the relationship between the kinetic energy of each surface, Ek, and the number of bubbles, n, participating in merging when detached, can be


 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979 9
Fig. 9. Variation of the kinetic energy of the merged bubbles with the number of bubbles participating in the merger (a: under the assumption of θ = 39.5°; b: under the pinning act of the micro-pin-fins).
Fig. 10. Schematic diagram of the pinning act (a: on the sidewalls; b: on the top side).
obtained, as shown in Fig. 9. It is worth noting that the dynamic behavior of bubbles at different positions on each surface is different, and it is very complicated to analyze all the bubbles separately. Therefore, only one bubble on each surface which is closer to its average value is selected in Fig. 6. In addition, for structured surfaces, the diameter of many bubbles is very small, so the diameter of each participating bubble cannot be counted in detail due to the vigorous bubble coalescence. Therefore, it is assumed in this study that bubbles participating in the coalescence have an equal diameter. It can be seen from Fig. 9(a) that the kinetic energy of the bubbles after coalescence increases when n > 2, and this trend is more significant with the increase of n. For NW1 and NW2, bubbles can depart from the heating surface when n ≥ 4. Although the volume of small bubbles participated in the coalescence is much smaller than that of the main bubble, the number of small bubbles participated in the coalescence can be larger than 4 (Fig. S7). Therefore, the coalescence of many small bubbles around the main bubble makes it easier to depart. While for PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2, it can be seen from Fig. 9(a) that even if n increases to 20, the kinetic energy of the bubbles after the coalescence is still less than 0, suggesting that the bubbles are difficult to depart under the contact angle of 39.5° (the contact angle of the bubble on the plate surface is assumed as 39.5° [40, 41]). Actually, the value of θ for bubbles on the surfaces with micropin-fins is not kept at 39.5° due to the existence of the pinning act, which can be shown by the following two possible ways during the growth process: 1) the bubbles are confined by the sidewalls of micro-pin-fins when they are generated at the bottom or sidewalls of the surface, as shown in Fig. 10(a); 2) when the
bubbles are nucleated on the top of the micro-pin-fins, the contact line of the bubble is limited by the top surface of the micropin-fins, as shown in Fig. 10(b). The maximum contact radius for these two cases can be inferred as approximately 50 μm. The contact angle of a bubble under the pinning act can be calculated by θ = sin−1(Db/D). Therefore, the contact angles of bubbles on PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2 in Fig. 9(a) can be estimated as 11°, 11° and 16°, respectively, as shown in Fig. 9(b). It can be found that under the pinning act, the bubble on PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2 can detach when n > 4 (Fig. 9(b)), which is a more reasonable result. Some bubbles with very small diameter (D < 0.2 mm) departed on PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2, and the proportion relationship of these bubbles is PF <PFNW1 <PFNW2 (Fig. S9(a)). As mentioned above, the contact diameter of the bubble is approximately 50 μm under the pinning act. When the bubble contact line is pinned, the contact angle of a bubble increases with decreasing bubble departure diameter, which makes it more difficult for bubble departure. At this time, more bubbles need to participate in the coalescence to make the bubbles depart. Taking a small bubble with a diameter of 0.124 mm on PFNW2 as an example, the condition for the bubble departure is n ≥ 8, as shown in Fig. 9(b). The bubble coalescence on PFNW2 is very fierce due to the cluster bubble nucleation, so it is possible for bubbles to depart from the heating surface with a very small diameter (Fig. S8(f)). Figure 11 shows the relationship between the bubble departure velocity and the number of bubbles involved in the coalescence. It can be found that only bubbles on the surface with micro-pinfins can depart at a high velocity, which indicates that the pinning act is an important reason for the microbubble explosive emission. The microbubble explosive emission was observed only on PFNW2,


 10 B. Liu, L. Yu and Y. Zhang et al. / International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 157 (2020) 119979
Fig. 11. The relationship between the bubble departure velocity and the number of bubbles participated in the coalescence.
but not on PF and PFNW1. This is because the nucleation site density of PF is low, so it is almost impossible for tens of microbubbles with similar sizes to coalesce with each other. The nucleation site density of PFNW1 is high, but no remarkable difference exists in the nucleation of bubbles at different positions due to its uniform nano-wall structure, resulting in a relatively uniform distribution of microbubbles on the surface, so it is difficult to gather a large number of bubbles in a small area. The existence of nanorod clusters on PFNW2 leads to the concentrate generation of bubbles in nanorod clusters so that a large number of microbubbles can be coalesced easily. Under the coupling effects of the pinning act of micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation in cluster nanorods, the bubbles on PFNW2 can depart rapidly with a very smaller size.
3.4. Mechanisms of CHF
3.4.1. Effects of wickability on CHF
The capillary wicking of the heating surface plays a very important role in CHF improvement. Ahn et al. [42] and Rahman et al. [13] both found that the increased wickability is the only origin for CHF improvement for superhydrophilic surfaces and then proposed correlations to predict the increase of CHF. Cao et al. [25] also proposed a similar correlation to predict CHF improvement due to the increased wickability for FC-72. However, some investigations have proved that the increased wickability is not the only reason for CHF enhancement of micro-nano composited surfaces for low surface tension liquid [20, 21], although it is important to the CHF improvement. In the present study, the wickabilities of testing surfaces were tested using a simple setup [21], and capillary velocities were compared in Fig. 12. h is the height dropping of FC-72 in the capillary tube, and a surface with a higher value of h means a stronger wickability. The CHF of PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2 is significantly higher than that of SS, NW1, and NW2, which is consistent with the trend of capillary wicking velocity. This suggests that the increase of surface capillary wicking is a significant factor to the increased CHF. However, the trend of surface wickability is not always consistent with that of the CHF. For instance, the highest capillary wicking velocity is observed on PFNW1, while the lowest capillary wicking velocity is obtained on PFNW2. However, the relationship of CHF under Tsub = 0 K is PFNW2> PF> PFNW1, while the relationship of CHF when Tsub = 35 K is PFNW2> PFNW1> PF, and the CHF of PFNW2 is significantly higher than those of PF and PFNW1. The capillary wicking tests were performed at room temperature under free wetting state. Therefore, although these test results can qualitatively explain the reason for the increase in CHF, it cannot completely represent the ability of
Fig. 12. The height dropping of FC-72 in the capillary tube with the wicking time.
liquid supply at CHF. When approaching CHF, a large number of bubbles covered the heating surface and caused the liquid supply to become very complicated. In addition, the sharp lateral coalescence of bubbles significantly weakens the liquid supply. Therefore, when the capillary wicking velocities of surfaces are close to each other, it is very important to modulate the bubble behavior to increase the CHF.
3.4.2. Effects of bubble behaviors on CHF
As mentioned above, strong wettability is an important reason that PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2 can obtain very high CHF, but it cannot explain why the CHF of PFNW2 is greatly improved compared to those of PF and PFNW1. Kim et al. [24] and Liu et al. [21] pointed out that for micro/nanostructured surfaces, in addition to wettability, the coalesced mushroom bubble departure frequency fc and the wall superheat Tsat also have significant effects on CHF. In the CHF model proposed by Liu et al. [21], the CHF can be calculated as follows:
qCHF = qV0 + qwickqV0 = 1.57 × 10−6 · ρlhfg fc
√2σ /g(ρl − ρg)Ja0(.342)
where, qV0 is the contribution of the pre-existed liquid in the small bubble layer, qwick is the contribution of liquid replenished by capillary wicking and can be obtained by the correlation developed by Cao et al. [25], fc is the frequency of coalesced mushroom bubble departure frequency, and Ja=ρlCpl Tsat/ρvhfg is the Jacob number. Then, it can be found qV0 ∝ fc Tsat0.32 from Eq. (4). Kim et al. [24] found that the departure frequency of the coalesced mushroom bubble is directly proportional to Ge0.25, where Ge is the vapor mass flow rate of the surface. In addition, Ge is proportional to the surface area enhancement ratio r, then we can infer that fc∝r0.25. Furthermore, it can be inferred that qV0 ∝ r0.25 Tsat0.32. In this study, the surface area enhancement ratios of SS and PF are 1 and 2.2, respectively. And the r of NW1 can be estimated to be 8.1 (Supplementary Section 1). The clustered nanorods on NW2 have a relatively small increment in area enhancement ratio relative to a uniform nanowall, then it can be approximated rNW2 = rNW1. Next, the surface area enhancement ratio of the micro-nano composited surfaces (PFNW1 and PFNW2) can be obtained to be 17.8 [43]. The values of r, Tsat and r0.25 Tsat0.32 at the liquid subcooling of 0 K and 35 K are listed in Table 1. As for PF, PFNW1, and PFNW2, whose capillary wicking velocities are close to each other, we can find that the highest CHF can be obtained from PFNW2 (Table 1). Similarly, it can be obtained from Table 1 that NW2 has a higher CHF than that of NW1. Both the CHF and r0.25 Tsat0.32 of PFNW1 are higher than those of PF under Tsub = 35 K, indicating the CHF mechanisms can be explained well by the model of Kim et al. [24] and Liu et al. [21]. However, the r0.25 Tsat0.32 of PFNW1 is higher than PF, but its CHF is lower than PF when Tsub = 0 K, suggesting that the CHF mechanisms for this case

Table 1
Comparison of surface area enhancement ratio, wall superheat and r0.25 Tsat0.32.
Sample r Tsat, 0 K (K) r0.25 Tsat0.32 CHF0 K (W/cm2) Tsat, 35 K (K) r0.25 Tsat0.32 CHF35 K (W/cm2)
SS 1.0 29.5 2.95 15.8 30.5 2.99 25.2 NW1 8.1 14.0 3.93 17.5 10.7 3.60 36.0 NW2 8.1 38.0 5.40 22.3 31.1 5.07 45.0 PF 2.2 27.4 3.51 28.6 23.3 3.34 48.7 PFNW1 17.8 19.9 5.34 27.8 6.4 3.72 53.0 PFNW2 17.8 31.5 6. 20 33.2 15.3 4.92 66.5
cannot be explained well by the model of Kim et al. [24] and Liu et al. [21]. The possible reason is that the nucleation site density of PFNW1 is too high, and the bubble coalescence is too fierce, the effect of increased r on PFNW1is weakened due to the blocking of vapor patches. While for PF, the attenuation of this effect is not as clear as that of PFNW1 due to is significant smaller r. The probability of bubble merger decreases, and the blocking of vapor patches is weakened. Therefore, the advantages of high nucleation site density on PFNW1 can be realized, so its CHF value is higher than that of PF in this condition. In addition, it can be found in Fig. 6 that the bubble departure diameter, bubble departure period and bubble departure velocity of PFNW1 are all smaller than that of PFNW2, which indicates that PFNW2 is more effective to prevent the coalescence of vapor patches, so as to delay the deterioration of heat transfer and obtain a higher CHF. This is also consistent with the experimental results. Therefore, the difference in bubble behavior between PFNW1 and PFNW2 is the main reason for their different CHF. In summary, in the saturated boiling heat transfer of well wetting liquids, the improvement of capillary wicking capacity of nanostructures is limited, whereas the high nucleation site density of nanostructures intensifies the bubble coalescence, and obstructed the liquid supply. Therefore, the HTC of a microstructured surface can be further significantly improved by simply compositing the uniform nanostructures, but its CHF is difficult to be further increased. However, in the boiling heat transfer with high liquid subcooling, the advantages of nanostructures can be realized because the strong condensation effect of liquid on bubbles reduces the bubble coalescence significantly, and the blockage of the liquid supply by vapor blanket is relieved. Similarly, by modifying the nanostructures to modulate the nucleation and coalescence of bubbles, and to promote bubble detachment, both the HTC and CHF can be further significantly increased. In this study, it is through coupling the pinning act of micro-pin-fins and the modulation of the nucleation and coalescence of bubbles by non-uniform nanowalls that help achieve a further pronounced increase in HTC and CHF simultaneously based on the micro-pin-fined surface. Wei and Honda [44] found that some parameters, such as the height and surface area enhancement ratio of micro-pin-fins, have a great influence on the boiling heat transfer performance. The height and surface area enhancement ratio of the micro-pin-fin in this study are much smaller than that of Wei and Honda [44], so there is a clear gap from the optimal value. In future research, we will further optimize the parameters of micro-pin-fins and the distribution of nanowalls to obtain a more remarkable heat transfer enhancement in low surface tension liquid.
4. Conclusions
In the present study, a new way, by coupling the pinning act of micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation of nonuniform nanowalls, was proposed to improve the boiling performance in the low surface tension liquid. The pool boiling performance of a smooth surface, uniform/nonuniform nanowall surfaces, a micropin-finned surface, and micro-nano composited surfaces with uni
form/nonuniform nanowalls were compared. Both HTCs and CHFs of the micro-nano composited surfaces with nonuniform nanowalls are significantly increased compared to the micro-pin-finned surface. In addition, small bubble departure diameters, high bubble departure frequency and velocity were also found on PFNW2. A unique bubble behavior named microbubble explosive emission was observed, which provides strong evidence for the effectiveness of the method to improve boiling performance by coupling the pinning act of the micro-pin-fins and the cluster bubble nucleation. The nanowalls, with a very high surface area enhancement ratio, can provide large amounts of nucleation sites. However, bubble coalescence on the surfaces with nanowalls in saturated boiling is very vigorous, and the coalesced vapor patches not only obstruct the liquid supply but also hinder the vaporization. Therefore, the advantages of micro-nano composited surfaces for improving boiling performance is significantly masked under this condition. It is suggested that high liquid subcoolings and modulated nanostructures for bubble departure promotion are both indispensable for further enhancement of HTC and CHF in low surface tension liquid.

Diagnostics of coolant boiling onset based on the analysis of fluctuations of thermohydraulic parameters
Y E Litvintsova1, S Laouar1,2, M I Delov1, D M Kuzmenkov1, A A Lavrukhin1 and K V Kutsenko1
1National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), Moscow, Russia. 2Nuclear Research Centre of Birine, Birine, Algeria.
E-mail: YELitvintsova@mephi.ru
Abstract. When operating heat-exchange facilities, it is of interest to develop methods of diagnostic and prediction for coolant boiling onset. It is known that the surface temperature of a heating element always has stochastic oscillations around an average value, the analysis of such fluctuations will make it possible to predict the change in the heat transfer regimes. This paper presents the results of an experimental study of temperature fluctuations for an inertial heater and using of method of diagnostic are based on statistical and frequency analysis of temperature fluctuations. It is shown that there is a correlation between the frequency characteristics of heater temperature fluctuations and oscillations in the thermohydraulic parameters of the coolant (temperature, flow, pressure). The obtained results will find application in the development of an automated system for diagnostics the coolant boiling onset in power facilities.
1. Introduction
Nucleate boiling heat transfer is an effective heat transfer mode and it is widely used in various industrial and commercial systems: nuclear and renewable energy, water desalination systems, heat exchangers, chemical industry, cooling systems of microelectronics, etc. The importance of nucleate boiling arises from its ability to remove more quantities of heat per unit time and area from a hot surface than during the other heat transfer modes. However, the boiling heat transfer has an upper limit, i.e. the critical heat flux (CHF). As soon as the critical heat flow is reached, the nucleate boiling is changed to the film boiling mode, overheating the boiling surface. This may lead to premature failure of the equipment. Therefore, diagnostics the heat transfer regimes are interesting in the design and operating heat exchange equipment. Boiling modes were mostly investigated through visualization methods using high-speed video cameras. The optical images of vapor bubble dynamics were obtained for entire boiling regimes including natural convection, nucleate boiling, transition boiling, and film boiling during the one of the earliest study in this field by Gaertner [1]. More recent studies have used laser interferometry and infrared (IR) thermometry as other visualization techniques. For example, Gao [2] has used the laser interferometric method and high-speed camera techniques to study dynamic characteristics of the microlayer beneath an ethanol vapor bubble during nucleation process. Surtaev at al. [3] have demonstrated the efficiency of using the high-speed IR thermography technique to obtain the


 ICNRP Volga 2020
Journal of Physics: Conference Series 1689 (2020) 012042
IOP Publishing
doi:10.1088/1742-6596/1689/1/012042
2
nonstationary temperature field and integral characteristics of heat transfer at boiling on a thin-film heater. The results of developing a machine learning methodology for investigating boiling processes in real time are presented in [4]. The paper proposes to use neural networks to measure bubble growth time, bubble period, and nucleation site density directly from the radiation recorded by the high-speed infrared camera. However, identification of boiling modes based on visualization data is associated with high costs for organizing optical access to the boiling system and is not applicable in complex industrial installations. Boiling processes are always accompanied by an intense noise sound that can be heard and easily detected by invasive and non-invasive methods. The advantage of these methods lies in the absence of the necessity to provide optical access to the system during measurements. For example, Tang et al. [5] analyzed the acoustic signals of sound emission in subcooled pool boiling with a small plate heater using sound pressure level (SPL), fast Fourier transform and discrete wavelet transform (DWT). It is shown that during the transition from the nucleate boiling to the microbubble emission boiling or transition boiling, the SPL exhibited a step increase, which, combined with a DWT analysis, could be used to identify boiling modes. In the study [6], the acoustic emission measurements were analyzed for each boiling regime and characterized by their statistical and spectral parameters. It is shown that acoustic emission signals have low-frequency components in nucleate boiling and high-frequency components in film boiling mode. The paper also notes a unique peak in the spectrum of acoustic emission signal at boiling crisis. The paper [7] presents the identical result. A sudden shift in peak frequency of acoustic emissions when the system transitions from stable nucleate boiling regime to CHF at boiling crisis is observed. It is known that operating parameters such as the surface temperature of the heating element, heat flux, pressure and flow rate, regardless of the heat exchange mode, always have stochastic fluctuations near the average value in a stationary mode. The analysis of these parameters is of interest in identifying heat transfer regimes. Wojtasik, et al. [8] performed a statistical analysis of the time evolution of heat flux based on probability density functions (PDFs) to investigate the influence of various operating parameters on the boiling behavior. This analysis is shown to represent an effective tool to characterize the type of boiling by defining the shape of the curve. In work by Lu et al. [9] was noted that during the transition from the convective heat transfer regime to nucleate boiling, the dispersion of temperature fluctuations reaches a local maximum. The authors of the work [10 - 12] showed that in various liquids before the onset of the transition process, the low-frequency spectrum of temperature fluctuations becomes close to the flicker noise spectrum. These results suggest that statistical and frequency analyses of fluctuations of the coolant thermohydraulic parameters will allow us to diagnose the onset of boiling. Diagnostics methods for changing heat transfer regimes based on the analysis of temperature fluctuations of the heat transfer surface of heaters with small thermal inertia (thin platinum wires) are described in previous works of the authors [13, 14]. The methods were tested experimentally for various fluids (liquid nitrogen, saturated water, subcooling water) both under pool boiling conditions and during forced movement. This work is devoted to testing the applicability of diagnostic methods for an inertial heater.
2. Statistical and frequency analysis methods
During statistical analysis, it was found that there is always an asymmetry in the histograms of the temperature distribution. As a characteristic criterion of asymmetry As has chosen an absolute value of the ratio of the third central moment μ3 to the third power of the standard deviation:
As = |μ3
σ3| (1)
In the transition regime, which is accompanied by a significant increase in the heat transfer coefficient, there is a local maximum in this criterion. For the frequency analysis, the amplitude spectra of fluctuations ψ(ν) were calculated by the fast Fourier transform of the temperature fluctuations of the heat transfer surface. All amplitude spectra were approximated by a power function with an exponent α:

ψ(ν) = C |β|

Detailed information about the methods can be found in the previous works of these authors [13, 14].
3. Experimental setup
The experimental setup was designed to conduct an analysis of fluctuations in the surface temperature of an inertial heater under pool boiling conditions. The setup scheme is shown in figure 1. The setup consists of a LOIP LT-424B thermostat, an automated control system and working area. The liquid temperature in the working volume of the thermostat (24 liters) was set by the control unit 4 and maintained with an accuracy of 0.1 K. The working area was a cylindrical heater with a length of 160 mm and a diameter of 6.5 mm. The shell of the working area is made of stainless steel. The working area was positioned horizontally relative to the gravity field and heated by Joule heating during the passage of alternating electric current through it. All experiments were conducted in saturated and subcooled water at atmospheric pressure. Micro thermocouples K-type was attached to the heater surface. During the experiments, the value of the transport current, the voltage drop across the heater, and the surface temperature of the heater were measured.
Figure 1. Experimental setup: 1 – thermostat, 2 – heater, 3 – temperature sensor, 4 – thermostat control unit, 5 – working area, 6,7 – thermocouples, 8 – measuring unit, 9 – computer.
The obtained data were used to calculate the heat flux density q and superheat of the heater surface relative to the liquid temperature in the working volume ΔT. The heat flux density determination error did not exceed 10%, and the absolute error of ΔT was less than 0.5 K.
4. Results
Initially, the boiling curves based on the values of the working area superheat and heat flux averaged for each series of experiments were constructed. Figure 2 shows boiling curves during the pool boiling conditions obtained from the time-average values of superheat and heat flux for two cases: working area was performed as a thin wire and an inertial heater. Here and further, all results for boiling on a thin wire are taken from the previous studies of the authors [14].

The lines in figures depict: 1 – convective heat transfer given by equations (4); 2 – boiling heat transfer given by equations (5).
Figure 2 presents the result of calculating the convection heat transfer coefficient according to Kutateladze [15]:
Nu d = 0.54Ra0.25, (4)
where d is the diameter of the platinum wire or inertial heater and it’s used as the characteristic scale of the process. For the case of saturated water boiling, the heat transfer coefficient was determined as follows:
h d = A ∙ qamv , (5)
where the coefficients are A = 0.6 (a) and A = 1.15 (b), m = 0.8 [16]. Detailed calculations are given in [14]. As it can be seen from the figure 2, the results of the calculations are in good agreement with the experimental data. The change in the slope of the approximating lines is associated with an increase in the heat transfer coefficient and the development of boiling. The points are numbered for easy interpretation of the analysis results. The results of determining the asymmetry coefficient of the distribution of heat-transfer surface fluctuations are shown in figure 3.

Both dependencies in figure 3 have a local maximum ((a): point 7; (b): point 8) in the region of the onset of the transition process from convection to nucleate boiling. The results of statistical analysis are presented below. Figure 4 shows the dependencies of the exponent α in the approximation function (2) of the temperature fluctuation spectra on the heat flux.
Figure 4. The dependence of the exponent α in approximation function (2) on the heat flux: (a) – thin wire [14]; (b) – inertial heater.
In this case, the dependencies again have a local maximum ((a): points 5–6; (b): points 6–7) in the region of the onset of the transition process from convection to nucleate boiling. Figure 5 shows the dependencies of the parameter β in the approximation function (3) of the temperature fluctuation spectra on the heat flux.

Figure 5. The dependencies of the parameter β in approximation function (3) on the heat flux: (a) – thin wire [14]; (b) – inertial heater.
As can be seen from figure 5 (a), under the convection and nucleate boiling (points 1–4 and 9–11), the values of the parameter β are several orders of magnitude higher than the values in the transition region (points 5–8). It is connected with high sensitivity to small perturbations for a non-inertial heater, which is expressed in a sharp dip at points 5–8, which belong to the unstable transition mode. This effect is not observed on an inertial heater. In addition, the expression (3) is derived in the approximation of a small number of Bio, but it is not correct for an inertial heater. Thus, it is relevant to apply techniques based on frequency analysis to diagnose boiling onset on the surface of non-inertial heaters. The parameters α and As are appropriate for diagnosing the onset of boiling on the surface of inertial heaters. In addition, the paper [17] shows the possibility of performing the Fourier transform using software and hardware package “UMIKON” in real time. Thus, the obtained results can be used in the development of an automated system for diagnostics of the onset of coolant boiling in heat exchange systems.
5. An experimental study using flow loop
It is worth to investigate not only temperature fluctuations for diagnostics of transients but also other related parameters. For example, in [18] it is shown that there is a correlation between temperature fluctuations and sound noise that attend the process of subcooled boiling. To search for a correlation between the characteristics of fluctuations in the heater temperature and the coolant thermohydraulic parameters (pressure and flow), an experimental flow loop was created. The flow loop has two circulation lines for conducting experiments with forced (Reynolds numbers up to 5·104) and natural circulation. The total volume of distilled water in the loop is 800 liters. The pressure in the loop is atmospheric. In the transparent working volume are removable test sections with heaters. In this study heaters with a diameter of 6.5 mm and a length of 160 mm were used. The heat flux from the surface of the heaters varied in the range from 0 to 5·105 W/m2, which made it possible to achieve the nucleate boiling of the coolant. The flow loop is equipped with various types of temperature, pressure and flow sensors. The arrangement of the sensors is shown in figure 6. To control the temperatures of the heaters and coolant in the working volume, thermocouples were used. More detailed information about the experimental flow loop is presented in work [19].

In the experiments, fluctuations of superheat of the heater surface relative to the coolant temperature in the working volume were measured. An example of the amplitude spectrum of such fluctuations is shown in figure 7(a). As can be seen from the figure, in the transition regime the Lorentz spectrum is achieved. Data were obtained in experiments with forced water circulation.
Figure 7. The amplitude spectrum: (a) - temperature fluctuations; (b) - pressure fluctuations.
Also, in this study, a statistical and frequency analyzes of fluctuations of pressure, flow, and temperature of the coolant were carried out. An example of the amplitude spectra of the pressure of the coolant is shown in figure 7(b). The obtained amplitude spectra are qualitatively similar to the spectrum of temperature fluctuations of a heater surface. At present, there are a number of works devoted to the study of the correlation between thermohydraulic regime parameters and neutron fluxes. For example, [20] presents the results of a thermohydraulics surveillance of pressurized water reactors by experimental and theoretical investigations of the low frequency noise field. The paper shows that the space dependent neutron noise effects caused by the coolant boiling were observed in experiments and declares the possibility to detect subcooled boiling in a PWR via neutron noise signals. Further investigations of the effect of coolant fluctuations on neutron fluxes are presented in [21,22]. The papers identify the characteristic features of

8
neutron flux noise caused by fluctuations in the parameters of the coolant in the VVER core and select the simplest method for monitoring the state of the coolant based on the analysis of neutron flux fluctuations. The combination of various diagnostic methods will improve the efficiency of boiling detection and the safety of equipment operation.
6. Conclusion
The applicability of the frequency and statistical methods of analyses for the diagnostics of coolant boiling onset on the surface of inertial heaters is tested. A correlation was found between the frequency characteristics of fluctuations in the temperature of the heater and fluctuations in the coolant thermohydraulic parameters. The obtained results will find application in the development of an automated system for diagnosing the coolant boiling onset in heat-exchange facilities.

title: Acoustic emission monitoring of water boiling on fuel cladding surface at 1 bar and 130 bar
abstractNote: This paper aimed to observe the acoustic emission (AE) signals of water boiling occurring on fuel cladding surfaces under ﬂow conditions at 325 °C and 130 bar. Visualization test was ﬁrst performed to identify a correlation between the water boiling phenomenon and AE signals in a transparent glass cell at 1 bar. Based on the correlations obtained at 1 bar, the boiling AE signals were obtained at 325 °C and 130 bar and successfully analyzed through various AE parameters, such as frequency, energy and hit number. Relatively small bubbles were formed at 130 bar, and the bubble density increased with the increase of the internal heater temperature.

Seung Heon Baek a,b, Kaige Wu a, Hee-Sang Shim a, Deok Hyun Lee a, Jung Gu Kim b, Do Haeng Hur a,⇑
a Nuclear Materials Safety Research Division, Korea Atomic Energy Research Institute, 989-111 Daedeok-daero, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-353, Republic of Korea b Department of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University, 300 Chunchun-dong, Jangan-gu, Suwon 440-746, Republic of Korea
