Keywords:
Acoustic emission Water boiling Bubble Visualization High pressurized water
abstract
This paper aimed to observe the acoustic emission (AE) signals of water boiling occurring on fuel cladding surfaces under flow conditions at 325 °C and 130 bar. Visualization test was first performed to identify a correlation between the water boiling phenomenon and AE signals in a transparent glass cell at 1 bar. Based on the correlations obtained at 1 bar, the boiling AE signals were obtained at 325 °C and 130 bar and successfully analyzed through various AE parameters, such as frequency, energy and hit number. Relatively small bubbles were formed at 130 bar, and the bubble density increased with the increase of the internal heater temperature. Ó 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
General corrosion occurs on primary circuit materials in contact with the primary coolant of pressurized water reactors (PWRs) [1,2]. During the general corrosion process, corrosion products such as metal oxide particles and metal ions are released from the inner surface of steam generator tubes and piping in the coolant. The released corrosion products are then transported into a reactor, activated by a neutron flux, and then deposited on the surfaces of the primary system materials. Activated corrosion products are the major source of radiation build-up and occupational radiation exposure, especially, the corrosion products deposited on the surface of the fuel cladding. These products are referred to as crud (an acronym for chalk river unidentified deposits) [3,4]. Crud is porous in nature and as the crud thickens, boron may become concentrated within the crud. Because boron is added into the primary coolant for a neutron absorber, the concentrated boron within the crud absorbs neutrons locally. Consequently, the thick crud can shift the core neutron flux from the top of the core to the lower half of the core along the axis of the fuel assemblies. This is, called a crud induced power shift (CIPS) or axial offset anomaly (AOA) [5–9]. This phenomenon can decrease core shutdown margin, and lead to a down-rating of the plant. The crud may also accelerate the corrosion rate of the cladding materials, which is referred to as
crud induced localized corrosion (CILC) [10]. This occurs because crud hinders heat transfer from the fuel cladding to the coolant, resulting in an increase in the temperatures of the cladding. Crud deposition is accelerated on the cladding surfaces under a heat transfer condition of subcooled nucleate boiling (SNB). The SNB process occurs when the outer cladding wall temperature is higher than the saturated coolant temperature and the bulk coolant temperature remains below the saturation temperature. In this condition, the superheated coolant adjacent to the wall surface begins to nucleate bubbles at preferential sites on the cladding surface. Crud deposition increases through a sufficient corrosion product supply around the steam-liquid interface of a boiling bubble [11–14]. Therefore, the understanding of this SNB phenomenon is important for effective and safe operation of nuclear plants. Experimental SNB studies have been performed in visible conditions at a low pressure [15–17]. This is because it is easy to correlate bubble dynamics to thermal hydraulics through in-situ visualization tests using a high-speed video camera or infrared camera. However, visualization tests are hard to apply to the SNB phenomena when they occur under conditions involving high temperature and highly pressurized water, such as within the primary coolant system. Meanwhile, an acoustic emission (AE) method is an on-line non-destructive evaluation method used to sense transient elastic wave resulting from a rapid release of energy within a dynamic process. This method is widely used in metal corrosion [18–21], plastic deformation [22,23], crack growth [24], and various
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.measurement.2017.05.042 0263-2241/Ó 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

 chemical engineering processes including bubble dynamics [25,26]. Some researchers have investigated the boiling phenomena using the AE method [27–29]. However, their works were performed at atmospheric pressure conditions. Therefore, the objective of this study is, for the first time, to detect and monitor boiling behavior on the fuel cladding surface using an AE technique in simulated PWR primary water at 325 °C
and 130 bar. These are typical operation conditions for the primary system in PWRs. To achieve this goal, a series of visualization tests were performed first to identify a correlation between the water boiling phenomenon and AE signals in a transparent glass cell at 1 bar. Based on the visualized tests at 1 bar, the AE signals obtained at 325 °C and 130 bar were analyzed in terms of various AE parameters.

 2. Experimental method
2.1. Specimen and solution preparation
A ZIRLOTM cladding (Westinghouse, USA), which is commonly used as a fuel cladding material, was chosen as the test tube. The cladding tube has a dimension of a 9.5 mm outer diameter (OD) and a 8.3 mm inner diameter (ID) with a 550 mm length. The tube was heated with a rod-type internal heater (heated zone = 250 mm, maximum power = 80 W/cm2), and the gap between the cladding tube (ID = 8.3 mm) and the heater (OD = 8.2 mm) was filled with MgO paste. This cladding tube was degreased in acetone and washed with distilled water before testing. The test solution was 3.5 ppm Li as LiOH and 1500 ppm B as H3BO3. This solution was used to simulate a typical primary water environment in PWRs.
2.2. Boiling test at 1 bar
The experimental apparatus used for the boiling test and the AE data acquisition at 1 bar is schematically shown in Fig. 1. The
boiling test was carried out in a transparent glass cell so that the boiling phenomenon could be observed visibly. The water boiling was realized by stepwise heating of the cladding tube using the internal heater. Detailed control of the water boiling process is as follows: First, the bulk water was heated to 95 °C using a hot plate, i.e., 5 °C lower than the saturation temperature at 1 bar. When the temperature of the bulk water was stabilized at 95 °C, the power of the hot plate was switched off. Afterwards, the internal heater was quickly powered on and the cladding tube was heated until the temperature of the internal heater (TIH) was stabilized at 120 °C. At this stage, the boiling behavior on the cladding surface was observed using a digital camera and the AE data was simultaneously acquired for 500 s. After these measurements were done, the cladding tube was heated through a stepwise increase of the TIH from 120 °C to 180 °C with an increment of 20 °C per step. At each step of temperature, observation of the boiling behavior and acquisition of the corresponding AE data were made for 500 s.
2.3. Boiling test at 130 bars
The boiling test in a flow water-condition was performed in a circulation loop with a mass flow rate of approximately 280 ml/min at 130 bar. Fig. 2 shows the schematic of the test equipment. The circulating loop system consisted of the following main components: solution tank, high pressure pump (HP pump), preheater for inlet temperature control, back pressure regulator (BPR) for pressure control, heat exchanger, and test section equipped with a cladding tube. The inlet water into the test section was pre-heated and the temperature of the flowing water adjacent to the cladding tube was maintained at 325 °C. The pressure of the test section was maintained at 130 bar. The saturation point of water at 130 bar is 330 °C. Similar to the tests at 1 bar, the cladding tube was heated using an internal heater by stepwise increasing the TIH from 340 °C to 400 °C with an increment of 20 °C. At each temperature, AE data were measured for 500 s.
2.4. AE measurements during boiling tests
While attempting to investigate the boiling behavior on the fuel cladding surface, an AE sensor was coupled to the fuel cladding tube, as shown in Figs. 1 and 2. To minimize the variation of the AE signal attenuation, we used the cladding tube with the same dimension and geometry equipped to an autoclave lid in all test conditions at both 1 bar and 130 bar. The same internal heater was also used. The cladding tube with a length of 550 mm was equipped to the autoclave lid via a tube fitting, and the AE sensor was coupled with the tube at a distance of 65 mm away from the upper end of the tube. The distance between the AE sensor and the autoclave lid surface was 35 mm. The AE sensor was connected to a preamplifier (2/4/6 Preamplifier, Physical Acoustic Corporation, USA), then the preamplifier was connected to the AE signal acquisition system. The AE sensor is available to use in the temperature range of 65 °C to 175 °C. The heat shield of a disk-type was installed between the sensor and autoclave lid to protect a radiant heat from the heat sources. During the measurements, the sensor temperature was maintained below 60 °C in all experiments. The obtained AE signals can be analyzed by various AE parameters such as average frequency, absolute energy, count and amplitude in AE-win software (Model: PCI-2, Physical Acoustic Corporation, USA). According to our previous experience and other reports [27,28], the boiling signals were specialized in the frequency range from 1 kHz to 100 kHz. Thus, a low frequency AE sensor (type R3a, 30–75 kHz resonant frequency, MISTRAS from Physical Acoustic Corporation, USA) was chosen to collect the AEboiling signals, which were filtered using a low pass of 100 kHz.

20 S.H. Baek et al. / Measurement 109 (2017) 18–26


 In this test, the AE signals were amplified with a gain of 40 dB, and the threshold value set at 48 dB to eliminate the background noise. Because the AE sensor was directly coupled to the cladding tube with the inserted internal heater, the obtained raw data were mixed with signals from both the boiling process and internal heater noise. As a result, the extraction of boiling-AE signals from the raw data would be of primary concern. During the post-analysis of the AE data, we tried to filter the heater noise signals through analysis of various AE parameters.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. AE signals at 1 bar
The visualization test at 1 bar was performed to identify a correlation between the water boiling behavior and the corresponding AE signals. Fig. 3 shows the visualization images of the water boil
ing and the raw AE data in the average frequency domain obtained during the boiling test at 1 bar. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the boiling process was divided into four zones, i.e., A, B, C and D. No water bubble was observed in zone A, and single-phase natural convection from the heated surface to the bulk water without formation of bubbles was observed. Water boiling, which means nucleate boiling in this test condition, was observed in zone B, C, and D. In case of the zone B, bubbles began to form on the cladding surface, and collapsed immediately near the cladding surface. With the increase of TIH, bubble size, density, and nucleation sites in the zone C were increased. During water boiling in this zone, bubbles were formed on the cladding surface and departed from the surface, and then finally collapsed in the solution. In the boiling zone D, bubbles of large size and containing nucleation sites of high density were observed on the cladding surface. Then, water temperature (TW) was increased to 100 °C, which is equal to saturation temperature at 1 bar. In addition, bubbles were observed to rise to the water surface and burst, which means that saturation
Table 1
Observation of results water boiling in different zones in Fig. 3 at 1 bar.
Zone TIH (°C) Boiling process Bubble dynamics Bubbles size Bubbles density
A 120 Natural convection – – B 140 Onset of nucleate boiling Growth and condensation Small Low C 160 Subcooled nucleate boiling Coalescence, departure and condensation Small and big High D 180 Saturation boiling Departure and burst Bigger Higher
Fig. 4. Relationship between amplitude and count of raw AE data obtained during water boiling test at 1 bar: (a) internal heater temperature = 120 °C; (b) internal heater temperature = 140 °C; (c) internal heater temperature = 160 °C; and (d) internal heater temperature = 180 °C.
S.H. Baek et al. / Measurement 109 (2017) 18–26 21


 boiling was reached. Consequently, the boiling process in four zones can be indicated as natural convection, onset of nucleate boiling (ONB), subcooled nucleate boiling (SNB), and saturation boiling from the Nukiyama’s boiling curve [29]. The detailed observation of the water boiling process in each zone is represented in Table 1. Combined with the above observation, the raw AE signals obtained during the test were analyzed. Fig. 3(b) shows the raw AE signals in the average frequency domain, which was also divided into four zones (i.e., A, B, C, and D) corresponding to Fig. 3(a). When the use of the internal heater did not start, no AE signal was detected. However, when the internal heater was operated in zone A, the AE signals were densely detected in a narrow band ranging from 15 kHz to 30 kHz. Considering that water boiling had not yet occurred, the AE signals of zone A was attributed to the internal heater noise. As the bubbling zone moved from B to C and D, the distribution of the AE signals was expanded to a wider frequency range. The AE signal density was also apparently increased. These changes are in good agreement with the boiling behavior observed in Fig. 3(a). However, considering that the internal heater signals were detected continuously in the frequency range of 15 kHz to 30 kHz in the zones A to D, the raw AE signals in zone B, C and D is a mixture of water boiling and heater signals. Therefore, additional work was performed to extract real boiling signals from the raw AE signals.
3.2. Analysis of water boiling-AE signals at 1 bar
In order to separate water boiling and internal heater signals, a domain analysis of count and amplitude of the AE signals was employed. Fig. 4 shows the relationship of count versus amplitude of the raw AE data obtained during water boiling test at 1 bar. In zone A where only the heater signals were detected, the heater
Fig. 5. Frequency distribution of internal heater and water boiling signals separated by the relationship between AE amplitude and count at 1 bar: (a) real internal heater signals and (b) real boiling signals.
Fig. 6. Variation of the real boiling-AE signals in each zone with an increase of the internal heater temperature at 1 bar: (a) absolute energy and (b) hit number.
Fig. 7. Raw AE data in the average frequency domain versus the internal heater temperature during the test at 130 bar.
22 S.H. Baek et al. / Measurement 109 (2017) 18–26


 signals were located into a single group in the amplitude range of above 60 dB, as shown in Fig. 4(a). However, in zones B, C, and D where both the boiling and heater signals were detected, the AE signals were divided into two different groups as shown in Fig. 4 (b)–(d). The new group showed relatively lower amplitude than the heater signal group. Because boiling started to occur in zone B, the new group must have come from the boiling signals. As the temperature of the internal heater was raised, the amplitude and count of the boiling signals increased. The amplitude and count of the heater signals also increased. However, the two groups have kept distinct. From this analysis, it is clear that the real boiling signals can be successfully extracted from the raw AE data mixed with the heater signals. Generally, because AE frequency contains much information in relation to an AE source, AE frequency has been used as an important AE parameter for analyzing the AE source [30–32]. Therefore, to obtain more information for the water boiling signals in the raw frequency data, real boiling and heater signals separated by the above extracting process were reconverted to the AE frequency. Fig. 5(a) shows the heater signals separated from the raw AE frequency data. The heater signals in the heated zone were detected in a homogeneous narrow band in the frequency range between 10 kHz and 35 kHz. On the other hand, Fig. 5(b) shows the real boiling signals separated from the raw AE frequency data. With increase of TIH, the distribution of the frequency in the water boiling zones showed the water boiling signals to be expanded from 70 kHz to 100 kHz. In addition, the density of the boiling signals increased both in the high and low frequency ranges. This
increase of boiling signals is in good agreement with increasing boiling density with the increase of TIH as the visualized boiling behavior. However, their distribution was concentrated more in the low frequency range than the high frequency range. This seems to be attributed to increasing bubbles size with the increase of TIH. According to an equation between AE frequency and bubble diameter [33–35], the AE frequency of a bubble is inversely related to the bubble diameter. In addition, from the above visualized result, the bubble size confirmed to increase when the boiling zones moved from B to D. This means that low frequency signals are generated from big bubbles, while high frequency signals are emitted from relative small bubbles, in this frequency data. Consequently, this frequency data showed that the boiling signals were detected as successfully corresponding to visualized boiling behavior. Another important AE parameter for the boiling study is the AE energy, which is related to the AE amplitude and AE count. It was reported that the AE energy, AE amplitude and AE count increased with increasing the bubble size [36–38]. Therefore, to analyze the bubble size and boiling density more clearly, the boiling signals was converted to the AE energy and the AE hit number. Fig. 6 shows the evolutions of AE energy level and the AE hit number of the boiling signals, respectively. As the bubbling zone moved from B to C and D, the distribution of the AE energy was spread in the AE energy range of 0 aJ to 17,000 aJ and the AE signals of high energy level was increased. In addition, the number of boiling events was increased with increasing in TIH. A higher AE energy is emitted from a larger bubble. Therefore, it indicated that the boiling signals in a high AE energy are detected from big bubbles, and
Fig. 8. Relationship between amplitude and count of raw AE data obtained during water boiling test at 130 bar: (a) internal heater temperature = 340 °C; (b) internal heater temperature = 360 °C; (c) internal heater temperature = 380 °C; and (d) internal heater temperature = 400 °C.
S.H. Baek et al. / Measurement 109 (2017) 18–26 23


 the boiling signals in a low AE energy are detected from small bubbles. Consequently, the AE energy also provides effective information for the bubble size corresponding to the visualized boiling behavior. It should be noted that in Fig. 5(b), zone D, most of the AE signals are in low frequency. However, in Fig. 6(a), zone D, most of the AE signals are in low energy. That is, all signals of the low frequency range have not a high energy. At first glance, these may be misinterpreted to mean that low frequency signals correspond to low energy signals. However, this may be because the AE signals obtained in these tests include all the boiling dynamics of bubbles such as formation, growth, departure, collision, and collapse. Another important result is that the bubble density increased as the TIH increased. It was possible to analyze these bubbles densities quantitatively through the boiling hit number, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). The boiling hit number in zone B, C and D was increased with an increase of TIH. In particular, the boiling hit number increased significantly more in zone D than zone B and C. This can be related to heat flux of the heater and water temperature. Generally, bubbles begin to form actively on the surface with an increase in heat flux, and boiling density increases more when the water temperature reaches the saturation point [39]. From the above visualization test, the saturation boiling was observed in zone D, where the boiling density is remarkably increased. Therefore, the boiling hit number in zone D was increased significantly, and their density was analyzed effectively through the AE hit number. Consequently, the AE signals from the water boiling were successfully analyzed through the visualized boiling behavior, and the bubble size and boiling density were analyzed by the AE energy and AE hit number more clearly. These results can be important criteria to observe the water boiling in non-visualized high pressure condition. Therefore, the AE signals obtained at 130 bar were analyzed, based on the above results at 1 bar.
3.3. Analysis of water boiling-AE signals at 130 bar
Fig. 7 shows the raw AE data of the average frequency domain obtained at 130 bar. The AE signals having two different characteristics were detected as the raw frequency data at 1 bar. One was densely detected in a narrow band ranging from 10 kHz to 30 kHz, and the other was relatively widely distributed in the frequency range of 1 kHz to 72 kHz. According to previous results, it is known that the signals concentrated in the range of 10 kHz to 30 kHz are the heater signals, and the signals distributed in the range 1 kHz to 72 kHz are the boiling signals. However, this means that the boiling and heater signals overlapped in the heater frequency range of 10 kHz to 30 kHz as the test at 1 bar. Therefore, the AE signals were classified to the boiling signals and heater signals by using the count and amplitude parameters. Fig. 8 shows the relationship of count versus amplitude for raw AE data obtained during the 130 bar water boiling test. When only the heater signals were detected, the heater signals were located into a single group in the amplitude range of above 54 dB, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). However, when both the boiling and heater signals were detected, the AE signals were divided into two different groups, as shown in Fig. 8(b)–(d). Considering that the boiling signals group was detected in a lower amplitude than the heater signal group as a result of count versus amplitude at 1 bar, the lower amplitude group must be from the boiling signals. Therefore, the signals detected in the amplitude value of above 54 dB are from the heater noise, whereas the signals detected in the amplitude value of below 54 dB are from the water boiling. In addition, because the boiling and heater groups were clearly distinguished, the two groups can be converted to the real boiling and heater signals in the raw frequency data, respectively.
Fig. 9(a) shows the heater signals separated from the raw AE frequency data. The heater signals in the heated zone were detected in the form of a narrow band in the frequency range between 10 kHz and 30 kHz. On the other hand, the boiling signals were detected widely in the frequency range between 1 kHz and 72 kHz, as shown in Fig. 9(b). In addition, the boiling signals were distributed evenly in the range of each TIH. The boiling density was also increased slightly with the increase of TIH. Considering that the bubble size in a low frequency range is relatively small, the change of bubbles size with the increase of TIH at the 130 bar condition showed no significant difference. Furthermore, boiling density also seems to not change significantly as TIH increase. These changes in bubble size and boiling density were analyzed more clearly through the boiling AE signals converted to the AE energy and AE hit number. Fig. 10 shows the evolutions of the AE energy level and the AE hit number of the boiling signals, respectively. With the increase of TIH, the distribution of AE energy was detected consistently in the range of below 3,000 aJ, as shown in Fig. 10(a). However, the bubble density increased remarkably in the low range between 0 aJ and 300 aJ when TIH was 400 °C. This means that the boiling density of small bubbles was increased at 400 °C. This boiling density was analyzed quantitatively by the AE hit number, as shown in Fig. 10(b). The AE hit number of boiling signals at 360 °C and 380 °C was slightly increased with the increase of TIH, but there
Fig. 9. Frequency distribution of internal heater and water boiling signals separated by the relationship between AE amplitude and count at 130 bar: (a) real internal heater signals and (b) real boiling signals.
24 S.H. Baek et al. / Measurement 109 (2017) 18–26


 was no significant difference. However, it increased significantly at 400 °C. This is because the water temperature at TIH of 400 °C reached 330 °C, which is the corresponding saturation point of water at 130 bar. Therefore, the bubble density at 400 °C can be increased significantly by saturation boiling. Consequently, it is concluded that boiling AE signals were successfully detected at 130 bar, and the AE technique can be effectively implemented in non-visualized conditions at high temperatures. Therefore, this technique can be utilized to evaluate boiling behaviors and crud deposition behaviors on heated surfaces under high temperatures and pressures.
4. Conclusions
A visualization test combined with an AE measurement was first performed to identify a correlation between the water boiling phenomenon and the AE signals in a transparent glass cell at 1 bar. Afterwards, based on such obtained correlations, the boiling AE signals in the non-visualized condition at 325 °C and 130 bar were successfully detected. The main conclusions are drawn as follows.
(1) AE parameters of count and amplitude were proved to be effective indicators in separating water boiling signals from the raw AE data mixed with heater signals. The analysis of the relationship between count and amplitude shows a consistent result with the observed boiling phenomenon.
(2) In the visualized 1 bar condition, AE frequency from water boiling was distributed in the range of 1–100 kHz. In the 130 bar non-visualized condition, AE frequency from water boiling was distributed in the range of 1–72 kHz. (3) In the visualized 1 bar condition, the AE energy from water boiling was distributed in the range of 0–17,000 aJ. In the 130 bar non-visualized condition, AE energy from water boiling was distributed in the range of 0–3,000 aJ. This decrease in AE energy means that the bubble size becomes smaller at 130 bar. (4) In both conditions, the boiling density was analyzed quantitatively through the AE hit number, and increased with the increase in heat flux. In addition, the boiling hit number was significantly increased when saturation boiling was present.

title: Spatial–temporal thermal‐fluid behaviors of microbubble emission boiling (MEB)
abstractNote: We investigate non-uniform behaviors in space of the microbubble emission boiling (MEB). Special attention is paid to radial temperature difference on the heated surface before/after the onset of the MEB. Correlation among the regimes of the MEBs, the radial temperature difference in the heated surface and the vapor bubble behaviors is indicated experimentally. We find that the surface temperature near the center becomes higher in the transition to the MEB, and then it becomes lower in the developed MEB. It is found that a linear correlation between the power input to the heaters and the resultant heat flux on the heated surface breaks in the transition of the MEB. The difference of the MEB regimes, S-MEB and C-MEB, is apparently distinguishable by monitoring the radial temperature difference of the heated surface.

{
    "content": "TRANSPORT PHENOMENA AND FLUID MECHANICS
Spatial–temporal thermal-fluid behaviors of microbubble
emission boiling (MEB)
Kazuna Horiuchi1 | Hotaka Kobayashi1 | Shu Miyashita1 | Takuma Hori2 |
Ichiro Ueno3,4
1Division of Mechanical Engineering, Graduate School of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, Chiba, Japan
2Division of Advanced Mechanical Systems Engineering, Institute of Engineering Division, Tokyo University of Agriculture and Technology, Tokyo, Japan
3Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, Chiba, Japan
4Research Institute for Science and Technology (RIST), Tokyo University of Science, Chiba, Japan
Correspondence
Ichiro Ueno, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, Chiba, Japan. Email: ich@rs.tus.ac.jp
Funding information
the Japan Boiler Association, Grant/Award Number: JFY2016; the Mitsubishi Foundation
Abstract
We investigate non-uniform behaviors in space of the microbubble emission boiling
(MEB). Special attention is paid to radial temperature difference on the heated sur
face before/after the onset of the MEB. Correlation among the regimes of the MEBs,
the radial temperature difference in the heated surface and the vapor bubble behav
iors is indicated experimentally. We find that the surface temperature near the center
becomes higher in the transition to the MEB, and then it becomes lower in the devel
oped MEB. It is found that a linear correlation between the power input to the
heaters and the resultant heat flux on the heated surface breaks in the transition of
the MEB. The difference of the MEB regimes, S-MEB and C-MEB, is apparently dis
tinguishable by monitoring the radial temperature difference of the heated surface.
KEYWORDS
microbubble emission boiling, subcooled pool boiling, temperature distribution, transition
1 | INTRODUCTION
Microbubble emission boiling, known as MEB, is a new regime of the
boiling phenomena found by Inada et al1-4 in 1980's. It has been
known that this new regime is realized in subcooled conditions
accompanying with higher heat flux than the critical heat flux (CHF).
Inada et al1 indicated the heat transfer characteristics of the MEB
with circular heated surface under various subcooled conditions. They
discussed correlation among the heat transfer characteristics, the tem
perature distributions in the subcooled pool and the temperature dis
tribution inside the heated surface. Then Inada et al2 indicated that
the aged and contaminated surface affects the heat transfer charac
teristics of the MEB through monitoring the heated-rod temperature
near the heated surface at different radial positions, the heat flux and
the vapor bubble behaviors. They illustrated that the MEB takes place
accompanying emission of tiny bubbles around the heated surface.
This is the origin of the name of this unique phenomenon. After these
milestones, a number of researches have been conducted to investi
gate the heat transfer characteristics and occurrence conditions of
this phenomenon in order to apply to, for example, cooling devices for
highly effective heat removals.
It has been indicated that stable MEB is realized under relatively
high degree of subcooling ΔTsub ≔ Tsat − T∞, where Tsat is the satura
tion temperature under the pressure concerned, and T∞ is the ambi
ent temperature of the bulk. Effect of ΔTsub on the heat transfer
characteristics were examined intensively.5-9 Not only the effect of
the temperature of the bulk liquid, but also the characteristics of the
heated surfaces and the geometries has been discussed; such as their
wettability10,11 and shape and/or size (on squared surface10; in
micro/mini channels12,13; on wires12,14).
The previous investigations have indicated two major regimes of
the MEB. Kubo and Kumagai15 conducted a series of experiments by
employing a system of ambient liquid sucking above the heated sur
face, and measured the variations of pressure and boiling sounds to
Received: 16 February 2020 Revised: 31 October 2020 Accepted: 8 January 2021
DOI: 10.1002/aic.17193
1 of 12 © 2021 American Institute of Chemical Engineers wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/aic AIChE J. 2021;67:e17193. https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.17193


 categorize the MEB into two regimes; (a) Stormy MEB (S-MEB)
accompanying with vigorous variations of the pressure and the boiling
sounds, and with abrupt increase of the heat flux in the boiling curve,
and (b) Calm MEB (C-MEB) accompanying with gentle variations of
the pressure and the boiling sounds, and with gradual increase of heat
flux. Kumagai et al16 measured the pressure fluctuation in the liquid
near the heated surface and observed the bubble motions using a
high-speed camera simultaneously. They illustrated that the amplitude
of the pressure fluctuation in the MEB was much larger than that in
the nucleate boiling, and the collapse of the vapor bubbles on the
heated surface resulted in maximum pressure fluctuation. They fur
ther categorized the S-MEB into I and II by monitoring the wall super
heat ΔTsat ≔ Tw − T∞, where Tw denotes the wall temperature of the
heated surface. Kumagai et al17 described the vapor bubble behaviors
in the S-MEBs and the C-MEB. The correlation between boiling noises
and vapor bubble behaviors in the MEB under various degree of sub
cooling were investigated by Tang et al.18 Variation of spatial behav
iors of the vapor bubbles over the heated surface were indicated as a
function of intensity of the MEB.9
In addition to categorizing regimes of the MEB, their onset condi
tions were also discussed. Tang et al7 visualized the bouncing behav
ior of vapor bubbles over the heated surface through a series of
experiments, and they proposed that the Marangoni effect over the
vapor-bubble surface would trigger the MEB via numerical simulation.
Ando et al8 detected the transition process from the nucleate boiling
regime to the MEB, and they indicated that ambient liquid flows
toward the heated surface after the transition.
Besides those researches on the occurring conditions and heat
transfer characteristics, we have not reached any concluding ideas to
explain how the MEB occurs. Saiki et al6 proposed “core-periphery”
model, in which non-uniform temperature distribution on the heated
surface would be the key to result in higher heat flux. Inada et al3 mea
sured the temperature with thermocouples at different positions (at the
center and 3R/5 from the center, where R is the heated-surface radius
of 5 mm) near the heated surface, and illustrated the correlation among
the boiling curve, the vapor behaviors and the temporal variations of
the temperatures. They also indicated that the averaged departure
period of the coalesced bubbles becomes shorter as ΔTsat increases. In
the present study, we revisit the problems of the MEB by monitoring
the radial temperature difference on the heated surface before/after
the onset of the MEB in the pool by employing the heated surface of
15 mm in diameter, which is larger than those employed in the previous
studies. Through the experiments with larger heated surface, it
becomes possible to monitor the radial temperature difference easier,
as well as to examine the size effect of the heated surface on the MEB.
In order to illustrate correlation among the heat transfer characteristics,
the vapor bubble behaviors and the radial temperature differences for
different regimes of the MEB, we conduct high-speed observations of
the vapor bubbles over the heated surface.
2 | EXPERIMENT
2.1 | Apparatus
We employ two types of apparatuses in the present study. For both
apparatus, we prepare cylindrical rods of 10 and 15 mm in end
surface diameters ds, machined from the copper block for the heating
area. We install the heating block in the bottom plate of the vessel
filled with the distilled water as the test fluid. The end surface of the
rod is exposed to the test fluid. In order to equalize the roughness of
the heating surface, the end surface of the copper block is carefully
polished with #1000 emery paper, whose averaged particle diameter
is of 24.5 μm (see Figure 1 [left]). Then the end surface is rinsed by
acetone. These treatments are conducted before each experimental
run. We conduct a series of experiments by applying designated

 degree of subcooling ΔTsub. The degree of subcooling is controlled by
cooling channel made of copper connected to a constant temperature
bath and by a U-shaped heater immersed in the pool. The tempera
ture of the bulk liquid, T∞, is measured with sheathed K-type thermo
couple at 20 mm apart from the edge of the heated surface; the
position is decided by referring Inada et al.2 The outer diameter of the
sheath is of 1 mm.
One of the apparatuses is the same as used in Ando et al,8 which
was validated by checking the reproducibility of the experiments and
the CHF under different ΔTsub. The other apparatus is shown sche
matically in Figure 1 (right). The most different part between the
apparatuses is the design of the heating block. The details of the
apparatus, which is prepared for the present study, are described in
the following.
The test vessel is made of stainless steel, whose inner sizes are
of 145, 195 and 225 mm in depth, width and height, respectively.
Glass windows are installed to all side walls for visualizations and
light supply. The copper block is surrounded by thermal insulation
materials. The cylindrical copper rod is stuck slightly into the bottom
of the test vessel. The top- and bottom halves of the block are wrap
ped in insulator made of MIOLEX (PGX-595, Ryoden-Kasei Co., Ltd.,
Japan), and that of LUMIBOARDTM (L-14Z, NICHIAS Corporation.,
Japan), respectively. The bottom of the block is covered by glass
wool material. In order to prevent any leakage of the test fluid, the
area between the rod and the insulator is covered by Aron Ceramic®
(Toagosei Co., Ltd., Japan). The copper block is heated by six car
tridge heaters, whose diameter and length are of 10 and 50 mm,
respectively. The heaters are inserted to the block from the under
side. The maximum heating power of each heater is 270 W at
200 V. In order to monitor the temperature of the test surface, three
K-type sheathed thermocouples (Nippon NetsuDenki SeisakuSho
Co., Japan), each 0.5 mm in diameter, are inserted into the rod at
points 1, 3, and 5 mm from the test surface along the center line.
Each thermocouple is calibrated to have uncertainty of ±0.6 K by
making comparisons with the data by the thermistor. The tempera
ture and its gradient at the end surface are evaluated by assuming a
one-dimensional temperature distribution. We call the evaluated
heat flux at the heated surface by applying the Fourier's law to the
measured temperature gradient as Ando et al8

In addition to those thermocouples along the center of the rod,
we insert another K-type thermocouple of the same size as the others
at 1 mm from the end surface and 1 mm from the side wall of the test
rod. This thermocouple is installed to monitor the temperature differ
ence near the heated surface from that at the center.
The experimental procedure is almost the same as described in
Ando et al.8 After the test fluid is poured into the vessel, the bulk liq
uid temperature is increased using the U-shaped heater and the
heated copper block. Then, under saturated boiling condition, the fluid
is degassed for more than 60 min. The cooled water prepared by the
temperature control bath is pumped through the pipes to establish
the designated ΔTsub of the pooled liquid after the degassing process.
In the present study, we conduct a series of experiments under
ΔTsub = 0, 10, 20, 30 and 40 K under atmospheric pressure.
We start the experiment when the designated ΔTsub is reached in
the pool. The output of the cartridge heaters is controlled by changing
the applied input voltage in order to vary q0. The surface temperature
increases in step with the output of the heaters as observed by mea
suring the temperature of the heated block in the steady state, as in
the previous study.8 The behaviors of the vapor bubbles over the
heated surface are captured by a high-speed video camera (FASTCAM
APX-RS, Photron, Japan) at a frame rate of 0.1 million frames per sec
ond (fps) with a back-lighting system.

3 | RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
3.1 | Boiling curves and vapor bubble behaviors
Figure 3 illustrates typical transition processes from the nucleate boil
ing to the MEB in the boiling curves with ds = 15 mm for (a) ΔTsub = 20
and (b) 30 K. One can track the transition, for instance, from the final
stage of the nucleate boiling (i) to the early stage of the MEB (i0) for
the experimental run 1. In the case of (a) ΔTsub = 20 K, the transition
to the MEB takes place rather unstably. The MEB is successfully real
ized after the CHF in the experimental runs 1 and 2, but does not take
place in the run 3. Note that the transition processes after the CHF
are different among those runs; the S-MEB and the C-MEB are inter
mittently emerge in the run 1, the S-MEB emerges after the emer
gence of the C-MEB in the run 2, and the MEB never takes place but
the transition to the transient boiling is realized in the run 3. In the
case of the run 2, one can find a single plot after the transition from
the CHF to the MEB. This reflects that fully developed value of the

that one hardly distinguish the MEBs from the boiling curves because
one plots the data averaged spatially and temporally, although Kumagai
et al17 illustrated apparent differences among the MEBs in the forced
flow conditions. The previous researches5,8 showed that the MEB
steadily emerges even under ΔTsub = 20 K for ds = 5 mm and 10 mm.
Ando et al8 illustrated that the S-MEB steadily emerges for any sub
cooled conditions, and the C-MEB seldom occurs in the system with
the heated surface of ds ≤ 10 mm. If one employs heated surface of
ds = 15 mm, it is indicated that the threshold in terms of ΔTsub for the
MEB onset becomes higher, and that one can firmly realize the C-MEB
in addition to the S-MEB. We find that not only ΔTsub but also the
heated surface size, ds, affect the transition condition of the MEB.
Figure 4 shows typical examples of vapor bubble behaviors in
various boiling regimes with the heated surface of ds = 15 mm. Rows
(1)–(3) illustrate the behaviors of the vapor bubbles during (1) the
transient boiling, (2) the S-MEB, and (3) the C-MEB under ΔTsub = 20
K. Rows (4) and (5) illustrate those during (4) the S-MEB and (5) the C

the C-MEB as shown in the rows (3) and (5), on the contrary, the
vapor bubbles exhibit quite different behaviors on the heated surface.
The heated surface is covered by the vapor bubbles as the S-MEB,
but their averaged height is much smaller than that of the S-MEB.

3.2 | Temporal variations of heat flux in onset of MEB
Figures 5 and 6 illustrate typical examples of the transition from the
nucleate boiling to the MEB with the heated surface of ds = 15 mm
under ΔTsub = 20 K and 30 K, respectively. Columns (a) and
(b) indicate the temporal variation of q0 and the “pseudo-” boiling
curve, respectively. Note again that the vertical axes for both of
(a) and (b) express the “quasi-” heat flux; this q0 corresponds to the
transient heat flux obtained from the temperature in transient condi
tion in the rod.11 The pseudo-boiling curves in column (b) are illus
trated by plotting all data of q0 against the wall superheat for any
instant. We draw these figures even though we recognize that the
characteristic time of the thermocouples is not short enough to per
fectly follow the phenomenon on the heated surface. Despite of the
insufficient temporal resolution, these figures bring fruitful informa
tion to us to distinguish the transition process of the boiling regimes.8
In each frame, one finds the marks ξi (ξ = a or b or c, and i is non
negative integer) accompanying dashed arrows. In the frame in the
column (a), ξi denotes the instant when the ith condition of the experi
ments is started after varying to fix the power input to the heaters. In
the frame in (b), ith denotes the data under developed ith condition
shown in (a). This is why ξi in the frame in (b) is drawn with a slightly
vertically offset from that in (a), and is drawn at the same vertical posi
tion as ξi + 1 in (a).
In the case of ΔTsub = 20 K (Figure 5), we illustrate three major
examples of the transition processes from the nucleate boiling; (1) to
the C-MEB and then to the S-MEB, (2) to the intermittent variation
between S-MEB and C-MEB, and (3) to the transient boiling without
transition to the MEB. In the frames, the regimes of the C-MEB after
the transition to the MEB are indicated by solid arrows. In the regimes
of the nucleate boiling (upto a6 in (1), b4 in (2) and c4 in (3)), the quasi
heat flux exhibits smooth increase and then reaches a converged
value after the variation of the power input to the heaters as time
elapses as shown in (a). In the saturated conditions, one obtains q0 in
stably constant. Even in (b) the pseudo-boiling curves, q0 increases
monotonically, thus the variation in q0 between ξi and ξi + 1 is smoothly
connected. When the boiling regimes exhibit transition to the MEB as
seen after a6 in (1) and b4 in (2), one obtains an abrupt drop of q0 as
shown by Ando et al8 with the heated surface of ds = 10 mm. This is a
typical sign in the MEB transition. It is found that the similar transition
in q0 emerges in the system with larger heated surface. In the case of
(1) the transition to the C-MEB, we find a sharp rise in q0 is realized
after its abrupt drop as shown in (a). The pseudo-boiling curve indi
cates a vigorous variation in q0 as well as ΔTsat. Under the C-MEB, q0
(MW/m2) and ΔTsat (K) vary as a function of time in ranges of
2.2 ≲ q0 ≲ 3.2 and 40 ≲ ΔTsat ≲ 80. In this case, the boiling regime
exhibits another transition to the S-MEB spontaneously (or, without
any change in the power input to the heaters), then it stably remains.
Once the S-MEB is stably established, it keeps taking place after the
increment of the power input to the heaters. In the regime of the
S-MEB, one finds oscillation of the amplitude of q0 as a function of
time, which is larger than that for the nucleate boiling as indicated in
the previous researches.8,9 Note that the variation in ΔTsat becomes
smaller than that for the C-MEB, and thus one can realize fully devel
oped S-MEB with the convergence of the averaged q0.8

 In the case of (2) the intermittent transitions to the S-MEB and
C-MEB, one obtains the S-MEB first, then the C-MEB followed by
another S-MEB for the condition b4 in this example case. After the
variation to the condition to b5, another C-MEB emerges and remains
till the end of the run. As one sees in (a) the temporal variation of q0
and (b) pseudo-boiling curve, q0 scatters as a function of time. The
S-MEB as well as C-MEB exhibits a similar behavior against ΔTsat as
seen in the case of (1). That is, the variation of the amplitude of q0 in
the S-MEB becomes larger than that for the nucleate boiling but
smaller than that for the C-MEB. In addition, the averaged value of q0
in time converges against time for the S-MEB. The amplitude of q0 for
the C-MEB, on the contrary, scatters and never converges. In this
case, ΔTsat fluctuates from 40 to 100 K under a constant power input
to the heaters. It is quite seldom to realize a stable state for the
C-MEB. The high-speed observation of the vapor bubble behaviors as
depicted in Figure 4 as well as the description by Kumagai et al16 indi
cates that the heated surface is partially covered by thinner vapor
layers than the case of the S-MEB. Such vigorous variations of q0 and
ΔTsat reflect unsteadily exchanges of the liquid and the vapor on the
heated surface. Under =20 K, one cannot always realize any MEBs
even though one increases the power input to the heater as shown in
column (3). After the nucleate boiling regimes (until c4), we further
increase the power input under q 2 MW/m2 as we conduct in the
case of (2), but the boiling regime exhibits a transition to the transient
state without a transition to the MEB. After the transition, q0 drasti
cally drops accompanying a jump of ΔTsat to the value around 150 K.
Under such situation, we stop the experimental operation for the sake
of safety. To conclude, the occurring condition of the MEB becomes
unstable with the heated surface of ds = 15 mm under ΔTsub = 20 K,
which is different from the cases with smaller-size heated surface.8
The C-MEB or the transient boiling emerges in spite of the S-MEB in
such transient situation.
When one increases ΔTsub at 30 K, the occurring condition for
the MEB becomes more mild even in the system with the heated sur
face of ds = 15 mm. We seldom observe transitions to the transient
boiling, and we have two major patterns in the transition from the
nucleate boiling by increasing the power input to the heaters; (1) to
the C-MEB first, and then to the S-MEB, and (2) to the S-MEB
steadily. Figures 6 (1) and (2) illustrate typical examples of such transi
tions. In the case (1), the C-MEB soon emerges after varying the con
dition at a6 from the nucleate boiling, and q0 exhibits a vigorous
oscillation in time with larger amplitude (as seen in the temporal varia
tion of q0) than those in the nucleate boiling as seen under ΔTsub = 20 K
(Figure 6). In (b) the pseudo-boiling curve, an abrupt jump of ΔTsat
upto 140 K. Then the S-MEB takes over the boiling regime on the
heated surface, and stably exists in further increasing the power input
to the heaters. Note that the oscillation of q0 in the S-MEB has larger
amplitude ( 1 MW/m2) than those aforementioned. Under such con
dition, ΔTsat ranges between 50 and 80 K. It is found that the range of
ΔTsat shifts lower as increasing the power input to the heaters. For
instance, ΔTsat varies between 60 and 80 K under the condition a6,
but between 50 and 75 under a9. It can be explained that the supply
of the ambient liquid to the heated surface becomes more effective as
the state of the S-MEB develops as the power input to the heaters,
thus the range of the variation of ΔTsat shift lower as q0 increases. We
do not reach, however, any comprehensive conclusions for such varia
tions of q0 and ΔTsat in time for the S-MEB, and need further research
to explain the mechanism. In the case (2), one obtains stable S-MEB
right after the transition from the nucleate boiling by showing an
abrupt decrease of q0 (under b5 in the figure). Such transition process
is firmly realized in the system with the heated surfaces of ds = 10
mm and 5 mm.8 Stable variation of q0 is realized under fully developed
condition as also seen in the nucleate boiling. It is emphasized that
the variation of ΔTsat becomes smaller even under higher q0 as illus
trated between the conditions of b5 and b8 in the pseudo-boiling
curve. This is a typical and unique behavior of ΔTsat and q0 in the sta
ble S-MEB regimes. Ordered oscillation of the surface temperature
was also indicated by Inada et al,4 which corresponds to the variation
of ΔTsat in the present study. From these present results in addition to
those in the previous researches,8,9,18 we find that we need higher
ΔTsub to realize the S-MEB stably in the system with larger heated
surface. We also find that the C-MEB emerges even under higher
ΔTsub, which seldom emerges for the smaller heated surface. Such
tendency is reasonably explained by considering the results with the
forced convection,15 and is qualitatively matched; one obtains the
C-MEB under lower ΔTsub and/or lower intensity of the forced con
vection. In the present system with larger size of heated surface, it is
considered that efficient supply of the ambient liquid becomes less
because of the larger scale of the heated surface.

 3.3 | Non-uniform behaviors of q0, surface temperature, and vapor bubble behavior
If one pays their attention to the region from the CHF to the onset of
MEB, the slope of the quasi-heat flux becomes zero or negative in the
boiling curve even though the heat input to the heater is increased.
Such trend was detected in the experiments with heating surfaces of
different sizes in the pool1-4,7-9,18,22 and in the forced-convection sys
tems.16,17 We consider this reflects the unique characteristics in terms
of heat transfer of the MEB especially on the verge of its onset.
Figure 7 illustrates the correlations between the evaluated q0 and the
total power input to the heaters in the copper block under different
ΔTsub. Solid and null marks correspond to the results with the heated
surface of 10 mm and 15 mm in diameter, respectively. One finds that
the quasi-heat flux linearly increases as the power input to the heaters
in the regime of the nucleate boiling (for instance, q0 ≲ 3.2 × 106
W/m2 for 10-mm heated surface and of q0 ≲ 3.3 × 106 W/m2 for
15-mm heated surface under ΔTsub = 50 K). Such variation reflects
that the assumption of one-dimensional heat conduction works well.
Then, one finds that q0 bents even though the power input to the
heaters increases. This range of nonlinear variation of q0 corresponds
to the region for the onset of MEB for both heated surfaces of differ
ent size. By further increasing the power input to the heaters, where
the MEB stably emerges on the heating surface, q0 increases linearly
against the power input to the heaters (for instance again,
q0 ≳ 6 × 106 W/m2 for 10-mm heated surface and of q0 ≳ 4 × 106
W/m2 for 15-mm heated surface for ΔTsub = 50 K). It is noted that
the slope in the range of fully developed MEB becomes almost the
same as that in the range of nucleate boiling. One has to remember
the assumption of one-dimensional conduction to evaluate q0. In case
the temperature gradient in the z direction, ∂T/∂z, is the same at any
radial position r, one would obtain linear variation of the heat flux as
the power input increases. On the other hand, in case ∂T/∂z would
vary in r, the assumption of one-dimensional heat flux would collapse.
There might exist non-uniform temperature distribution in the r direc
tion in the regime of the MEB onset after the results of nonuniform
variation in q0 against the power input to the heaters.
In order to confirm our expectations, we monitor the temperature
near the heated surface at different radial positions. We refer the
temperature monitored at the center of the heated surface as TðcÞ
s, and that near the periphery of the heated surface as TðpÞ

find that ΔTðcpÞ
s remains negative when the S-MEB becomes domi
nant, and that the amplitude of ΔTðcpÞ
s becomes much larger than that
for the nucleate boiling but remains almost the same (3, 600 ≲ t[s] ≲ 4,
300, and 4, 700 ≲ t ≲ 5, 100 in the frame). An apparent temperature
difference TðcÞ
s < TðpÞ
s is found to exist for the S-MEB. One can expect
that vigorous oscillation of the vapor bubbles brings ambient liquid to
the heated surface from above as found by Ando et al8 to “cool” the
center area of the heated surface comparing to the periphery. Once
the C-MEB takes over the S-MEB (4, 300 ≲ t[s] ≲ 4, 700, and 5,
100 ≲ t ≲ 5, 800), ΔTðcpÞ
s exhibits much more vigorous variation than
that for the S-MEB, although its averaged value still remains negative.
These characteristics of the oscillations for the S- and C-MEBs corre
spond to the variation of q0 and ΔTsat as illustrated in the pseudo
boiling curves (see Figures 5 and 6). In the case of the developed
S-MEB, although ΔTðcpÞ
s exhibits an oscillation as a function of time,
the averaged value of ΔTðcpÞ
s remains almost constant. On the con
trary, the transient regime from the nucleate boiling to the MEB
and/or the C-MEB, ΔTðcpÞ
s exhibits a vigorous oscillation as a function
F I G U R E 7 Correlation between q0 and power input to heaters [Color figure can be viewed at wileyonlinelibrary.com]

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License


 of time. It is considered that unsteady oscillations of ΔTðcpÞ
s breaks
one-dimensional heat transfer along the heated rod, which supports
the correlation between q0 and the power input to the heaters as illus
trated in Figure 7. Such would be explained by considering the vapor
bubble behaviors. In the case of C-MEB, the average value of the
vapor bubble thickness on the heated surface becomes much smaller
than those for the S-MEB. The route of the ambient-liquid supply
becomes spread covering the heated surface non-uniformly, so that
synchronized behaviors of the vapor bubbles as seen in the S-MEB
never be realized. Note again that the sampling rate by the thermo
couples is far beyond the characteristic time of vapor bubble behav
iors for the C-MEB, thus we recognize that our ΔTðcpÞ
s does not reflect
the phenomenon itself on the heated surface. Despite the uncertainty,
however, we emphasize that the regimes of the MEB are apparently
distinguishable by monitoring ΔTðcpÞ
s. Here we consider an adequacy of monitoring ΔTðcpÞ
s . Because of
the thermal diffusivity of the heated rod made of copper, we
recognize that such ΔTðcpÞ
s does not indicate the accurate temperature
difference under the present characteristic time of the thermocouples
τTC. The distance between two thermocouples near the heated sur
face is L(cp) = 6.5 mm, so that the characteristic thermal diffusion time
at T = 450 K, for instance, in L(cp) is τ0 = (L(cp))2/κ 0.28 s < τTC. The
distance between the heated surface and the thermocouples for TðcÞ
s
and TðpÞ
s is L(s) = 1 mm. So that one finds the characteristic thermal dif
fusion time in L(s) as τ1 = (L(s))2/ν τ0. Thus ΔTðcpÞ
s rather reflects
thermo-fluid behaviors including the phase change above the heated
surface than the thermal variation in the heated rod.
In order to confirm spatially and temporally non-uniform boiling
on the heated surface illustrated by unsteady oscillations of ΔTðcpÞ
s, we monitor the vapor bubble behaviors in different regimes on the
boiling curve (Figure 9a). Three characteristic status are chosen;
A: nucleate boiling regime close to the CHF, B: transient state from
the nucleate boiling to the MEB, and C: fully developed S-MEB.
These experimental data are obtained with the heated surface of

Frames (a) and (b) illustrate the results without and with transition to the microbubble emission boiling (MEB), respectively. The results shown in (b) are obtained in the same experimental run as shown in Figure 5 (2) [Color figure can be viewed at wileyonlinelibrary.com]

 ds = 10 mm, because of the positions of the windows installed to the
vessel for better observation path. For clear visualization of the vapor
bubble behaviors in the S-MEB regimes, we conduct this series of
runs at ΔTsub = 40 K. Note that we do not apply this analysis to the
C-MEB; the averaged thickness and its amplitude of the oscillation on
the heated surface are too small as shown in Figure 4 to detect the
vapor-bubble behaviors observed from the side because of the spatial
resolution of the high-speed camera in the present system. Top
frames of Figure 9b indicate the snapshots of the vapor bubbles
observed from the side. We define a horizontal line at 1 mm above
the heated surface as the monitoring line of the vapor bubbles. We
extract such line image from each frame of the successive data, and
accumulate downward as time elapses. Bottom frames of Figure 9b
are obtained by the way described above to illustrate spatio–temporal
variations of the vapor bubbles passing the monitoring line. In the
case A, the vapor bubbles coalesce above the heated surface to form
large coalesced bubbles. Those bubbles depart due to the buoyancy
cyclically, whose sizes are almost uniform. One finds such a behavior
clearly in the spatio–temporal variation; the growing vapor bubbles
always pass the monitoring line, so one can detect their lateral growth
and then shrink indicating the necking and departing processes. From
this spatio–temporal diagram, life cycle of the vapor bubble is clearly
detected. The spatio–temporal diagram indicates the generation fre
quency of the vapor bubbles of about 40 Hz. In the case B, the vapor
bubble behaviors become non-uniform spatially and temporarily. In
this frame, one detects intermittent dark area in time, which locate
covering the region corresponding to the heated surface. It must be
emphasized that the longitudinal and lateral sizes the passing vapor is
not uniform; such can be translated that vapor bubble passes the
monitoring line faster than the case of the nucleate boiling but not
constantly, and the lateral size of the vapor bubble becomes smaller
than that of the heated surface. Those behaviors correspond to fast
but non-uniform oscillation of the vapor bubbles as Tang et al9
reported. That is, such fast oscillation of vapor bubbles, which is one
of the essential features of the MEB,8 arises after the transition to the
S-MEB. This regime, however, is not fully developed, thus the S-MEB
regime is not realized on the thorough heated surface. As aforemen
tioned, the thermal diffusion time between the heated surface and
the positions of the thermocouples nearest to the heated surface, τ1,
is much smaller than that between the two thermocouples near the
heated surface, τ0. Thus ΔTðcpÞ
s illustrated in Figure 8 rather reflects
thermo-fluid behaviors including the phase change above the heated
surface than the thermal variation in the heated rod. In the case C,
fully developed S-MEB, one clearly finds uniform pattern in space and
in time. No transient states with large coalesced vapor bubbles over
the heated surface emerge. This indicates that the vapor bubble oscil
lation takes place uniformly over the heated surface with an almost
uniform interval. The interval, corresponding to the width in time of
the passage of the vapor bubbles, becomes much thinner and con
stant comparing to that in the case B. It is evaluated as 400 440 Hz,
which corresponds to the fundamental frequency of the boiling sound
(see Appendix). By making a comparison between q0-power input
(Figure 7) and those spatio–temporal diagram of the vapor bubble
behavior, we conclude as follows; in the transition state between the
nucleate boiling and the MEB, non-uniform behaviors of the vapor bub
bles in space result in non-uniform temperature field in the heated rod.
Thus a linear correlation between q0 and power input to the heaters
breaks out. But once fully developed S-MEB is realized, vigorous oscilla
tion of the vapor bubbles as well as ΔTðcpÞ
s becomes steadily uniform in
time and space and emerges over the thorough heated surface. This
corresponds to one-dimensional thermal field in the heated rod, which
results in recovery of linear correlation between q0 and power input.
4 | CONCLUDING REMARKS
We investigated transition processes from the nucleate boiling to the
MEB on the heated surface of 15 mm in diameter, which is larger than
those in the previous studies,7-9,18 to examine the heated-surface size
effect on the phenomenon. We also investigated non-uniform behav
iors in time and space of the MEB by monitoring the correlation
between the quasi-heat flux q0 and the power input to the heaters,
the difference of the temperature at the center and at the periphery
of the heated surface, and the vapor bubble behaviors.
In the transition processes with a larger heated surface, it was
found that we need higher degree of subcooling (ΔTsub) to realize
steady MEBs, and that the C-MEB often emerges even under
ΔTsub = 20 K, which had been observed under low ΔTsub and low
intensity of the forced convection around the heater.16,17 Through
monitoring the vapor bubble behaviors on the heated surface and the
temporal variation of q0 in the pseudo-boiling curves, the characteris
tics of the C-MEB as well as the S-MEB were extracted by making a
comparison with those of the nucleate boiling. That is, we have vigor
ous oscillations in q0 and the wall superheat ΔTsat in time in the case
of the C-MEB. In the case of the S-MEB, we still have their oscilla
tions, but their amplitudes become much smaller than those of the
C-MEB.
As for the transition processes to the MEBs, we found that a lin
ear correlation between the power input to the heaters and the resul
tant heat flux on the heated surface breaks in the transition of the
MEB. Special attention was paid to radial temperature difference on
the heated surface before/after the onset of the MEB. Correlation
among the regimes of the MEB, the radial temperature difference in
the heated surface, and the vapor bubble behaviors were indicated
experimentally. We found that the surface temperature at the center
once becomes higher than that near the periphery in the transition to
the MEB, and then it becomes lower in the developed MEB. The dif
ference of the MEB regimes, the S-MEB and the C-MEB, is apparently
distinguishable by monitoring the radial temperature difference of the
heated surface. It was found that such non-uniform temperature dis
tribution accompanies with non-uniform behaviors of the vapor bub
bles on the heated surface. From the nucleate boiling to the transient
regime of the S-MEB, the spatio–temporal behaviors of the vapor
bubbles exhibit a transition from the steady growth and departures of
the coalesced bubbles to the non-uniform behaviors in space and
time. In the fully developed regime of the S-MEB, we found that the
HORIUCHI ET AL. 10 of 12
15475905, 2021, 8, Downloaded from https://aiche.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aic.17193 by University Of Arkansas Library, Wiley Online Library on [26/04/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License


 vapor bubbles grow and depart from the heated surface uniformly in
space and time, whose temporal frequency becomes about 10 times
higher than that for the nucleate boiling.
Further researches would be needed to uncover the physical pro
cesses of the transition to the MEB; we have not understood why
such high heat flux is realized, why such high-frequency oscillation of
the vapor bubbles is realized, why unstable condition of C-MEB is
realized under small ΔTsub for larger heated surface, and so on. It
would be indispensable to apply precise measurement system for
temporal variations of the surface temperature and heat flux with,
such as high-speed infrared cameras23,24 and/or MEMS tech
niques25,26 under high-temperature and high-heat flux conditions.

title: Experimental investigation of transition process from nucleate boiling to microbubble emission boiling under transient heating modes
abstractNote: This study investigated the transition from nucleate boiling to microbubble emission boiling (MEB) on a small heating surface under transient heating modes. During the short unstable boiling process after critical heat flux with a rapid increase in wall temperature, capillary waves were observed over the vapor film at subcooling of 30–40 K. The visualization results in this process demonstrated that the partial collapse of vapor film induced strong jet toward the heating surface and therefore avoided complete dryout thereon. This mechanism certainly plays an important role in the transition to stable MEB. Further comparison between the wall temperature and boiling states indicates that the wall temperature at onset point of stable MEB is determined by the predominance of MEB mode over film boiling in the transition process. Effects of liquid subcooling and surface oxidation on the transition process were also investigated at the same time.

fulltexts.text:
{
    "content": "TRANSPORT PHENOMENA AND FLUID MECHANICS
Experimental investigation of transition process from
nucleate boiling to microbubble emission boiling under
transient heating modes
Jiguo Tang | Licheng Sun | Min Du | Hongtao Liu | Zhengyu Mo | Jingjing Bao
State Key Laboratory of Hydraulics and Mountain River Engineering, College of Water Resource & Hydropower, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China
Correspondence
Licheng Sun, State Key Laboratory of Hydraulics and Mountain River Engineering, College of Water Resource & Hydropower, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China. Email: leechengsun@sohu.com and Min Du, State Key Laboratory of Hydraulics and Mountain River Engineering, College of Water Resource & Hydropower, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China. Email: dmscu_2009@scu.edu.cn
Funding information
National Natural Science Foundation of China, Grant/Award Number: 51706149; Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation, Grant/Award Number: 2018M643474; National Natural Science Foundation of China, Grant/Award Number: 51606130; Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University, Grant/Award Number: YJ201658
Abstract
This study investigated the transition from nucleate boiling to microbubble emission
boiling (MEB) on a small heating surface under transient heating modes. During the
short unstable boiling process after critical heat flux with a rapid increase in wall tem
perature, capillary waves were observed over the vapor film at subcooling of
30–40 K. The visualization results in this process demonstrated that the partial col
lapse of vapor film induced strong jet toward the heating surface and therefore
avoided complete dryout thereon. This mechanism certainly plays an important role
in the transition to stable MEB. Further comparison between the wall temperature
and boiling states indicates that the wall temperature at onset point of stable MEB is
determined by the predominance of MEB mode over film boiling in the transition
process. Effects of liquid subcooling and surface oxidation on the transition process
were also investigated at the same time.
KEYWORDS
dryout, evaporation and condensation, microbubble emission boiling, rewetting, transient
boiling
1 | INTRODUCTION
Problems arising from rapid increase in the heat release of advanced
devices and systems, such as electronic chips, inverters of electric vehi
cles, advanced lasers, and fusion reactors, prompt a search of enhanced
heat transfer technologies to meet the increasingly cooling demand.1–3
Nucleate boiling is one of the attractive methods because it allows
accommodation of high heat flux at relatively low wall superheat,4–6
while its application is still limited due to the risk of boiling crisis. In
1981, Inada et al.7 observed an extremely intensive boiling process with
heat flux much higher than critical heat flux (CHF) under high subcooled
conditions. It was termed as microbubble emission boiling (MEB) due to
that many microbubbles were emitted over the heating surface in the
boiling process. In MEB, a process of vapor bubble/film being con
densed and collapsed repeats rapidly, accompanied by violent motion
of microbubbles and loud boiling sound. Therefore, MEB has great
potential in meeting the demand of not only cooling high heat release
equipment but also the enhancement of mixing fluids.8
Inada et al.7 first confirmed that a threshold liquid subcooling was
required for triggering MEB. Later works9–11 in experiments of sub
cooled pool boiling proved this critical value was about 20 K for water
as working fluid. Then, Kumagai et al.12 and Suzuki et al.13,14 found that
increasing inlet liquid velocity or decreasing inlet pressure could reduce
the threshold subcooling in flow boiling experiments. Inspired by
Inada's pioneering work, a number of experiments were conducted to
investigate heat transfer performance and bubble behaviors in MEB
covering a wider experimental range. Suzuki et al.15 achieved MEB for
aqueous solutions of ethanol, propanol, and butanol but failed for pure
ethanol at different liquid subcooling. They also investigated the effect
of heater size on MEB for subcooled flow boiling of water.16 Both the
Received: 29 May 2018 Revised: 14 January 2019 Accepted: 19 January 2019
DOI: 10.1002/aic.16555
AIChE Journal. 2019;65:e16555. wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/aic © 2019 American Institute of Chemical Engineers 1 of 13 https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.16555


 maximum heat flux of MEB and CHF was reduced with an increase in
the heater size due to higher downstream liquid temperature. Nomura
et al.17 investigated MEB in a copper multimicrochannels with the same
subchannel hydraulic diameter of 0.15 mm. Visualization results
showed that the microchannels were almost occupied by coalesced
bubbles and MEB did not appear even at a liquid subcooling as high as
80 K. However, Wang and Cheng18 realized MEB in a microchannel
with hydraulic diameter of 0.155 mm. Periodical growth, coalescence,
and collapse of vapor bubble were observed in the microchannel. In
experiments of Nomura et al., subcooled fluid was heated from both
the bottom and two sides of the microchannel. Vapor bubble grew rap
idly and hindered the supply of cold liquid to the heating surface, lead
ing to a transition to film boiling earlier.
Tange et al.,19 Hattori and Ueno20 performed statistical investiga
tion on microbubbles in MEB, showed that increasing liquid subcooling
would decrease the microbubble size and intensify the jet process. Zei
garnik et al.21 analyzed the components of the microbubbles from col
lapsed vapor films in MEB and deemed that they were the mixture of
vapor and noncondensable gases. Recently, Inada et al.22 experimentally
studied boiling of a liquid droplet on a small heating surface to clarify
the process of subcooled liquid rushing to the heating surface. They
found that the microbubbles were resulted from cavitation caused by
collapse of vapor bubbles. Condensation process of a single vapor bub
ble in MEB was investigated by Ueno et al.23,24 through injection vapor
bubbles into a subcooled pool. It was found that the formation of fine
surface waves caused by strong condensation across the interface could
accelerate instability of bubble surface and cause the collapse of the
vapor bubble. Numerical simulation works by Tang et al.10 showed that
Marangoni convection around the vapor–liquid interface could also
intensify the instability of the vapor film, which might be one of the
mechanisms triggering collapse of vapor film in MEB.
In most cases, the temperature of heating surface always experi
enced a jump increase associated with the transition from nucleate
boiling to MEB,7,11,15 as shown in Figure 1. In our previous work,10 it
was suspected that the heating surface might be almost covered by a
vapor film for a very short time after CHF, which deteriorated the
heat transfer and led to rapid increase in wall temperature. Ando
et al.11 further investigated this transition process and observed that
there indeed existed a vapor bubble thoroughly covering the heating
surface just prior to MEB. They found that the heat flux in stable MEB
region was quasi-stable and oscillated with a frequency of 0.8–2 kHz.
Yagov et al.25 studied microbubble boiling phenomenon occurred in
cooling of hot mental spheres in a subcooled pool. Many tiny bubbles
were observed near the vapor film around the sphere in this boiling
process. However, it mainly arose at a quite higher heating surface
temperature (over the minimal temperature of film boiling) than that
in MEB. They deemed that the microbubble boiling was triggered in
cooling process when heat supply of a heating surface was controlled
with the metal thermal effusivity. Besides, according to the analysis of
experimental results by Ando et al.,11 Yagov et al.25 also made a sup
position that the oscillation of heat flux meant the potential influence
of the thermal effusivity of the heater on the transition to MEB.
At present, the mechanism for the occurrence of MEB is still not
quite clear. Researchers paid more attention to the nucleate boiling
and MEB under steady heating mode in previous works and less to
the transition process between them. Investigations on the transient
process to MEB can help ones to illustrate the mechanisms triggering
MEB and boiling crisis. In this study, experiments of subcooled pool
boiling were conducted under transient heating modes to capture the
transition process to MEB. Transient interfacial behavior and wall
temperature were recorded, with a focus on the effect of liquid sub
cooling and surface conditions. Based on visualization results, mecha
nism of transition from nucleate boiling to MEB was analyzed.
2 | EXPERIMENTAL SYSTEM
The experimental apparatus to study MEB is shown in Figure 2, which
has been introduced in our previous work in detail.26,27 It is mainly
composed of a water tank and a copper block. The water tank with
three observation windows has a size of 150 mm × 200 mm × 200
mm. The copper block has two cylindrical sections of 10 mm and
30 mm in diameter and a cubic base of 60 mm × 80 mm × 35 mm.
The top surface of the smaller cylinder was taken as the heating sur
face. The block was heated by seven cartridge heaters inserted in the
copper block, each of which has the size of 6.35 mm in diameter and
76 mm in length and has a maximum heating power of 255 W at
220 V. A ceramic thimble was mounted around the upper copper cyl
inder to thermally insulate it from the water tank. A copper cooler and
two auxiliary heaters of 1,000 W capacity were used for adjusting
and maintaining the liquid temperature in experiments. Three K-type

It should be emphasized that the obtained transient heat flux is not
exactly accurate because of the inadequate response time of the ther
mocouples, but their variation trend is similar to the actual ones. Water
temperature was measured by another K-type thermocouple located at
5 mm upper of the heating surface and 45 mm horizontally apart from
its center. In some experimental runs, the temperature distribution of liq
uid in a direction along vertical axis of the heating surface was also mea
sured with five K-type sheathed thermocouples which were placed at
about 3, 5, 12, 30, and 45 mm apart from the heating surface. The data
were recorded by an acquisition system (PXIe-1082, National Instru
ments, USA) with a thermocouple input module (PXIe-4353, National
Instruments, USA). All boiling experiments were conducted at liquid sub
cooling 15–60 K and atmospheric pressure. Distilled water was used as
working fluid and further degassed for more than 1 hr before experi
ments. The specific experimental runs are listed in Table. 1.
To capture the transition process from nucleate boiling to MEB, a
large heating power was set for the cartridge heaters at the beginning
of each transient experiment. While a too large heating power would
make the wall temperature increase too fast, leading to poor measure
ment in the heat flux and wall temperature. On the other hand, the
heating power had to be larger than that for CHF because MEB was
always observed thereupon. The heating power was increased step
by step to confirm this critical value for triggering the transition to
MEB before experiments. After repeated tests, the heating power
0.7–1.1 kW was specified in present transient experiments. The heaters
would be shut off before the wall temperature exceeded 200 C.
Boiling phenomena were recorded by a high-speed video camera
(Fastcam SA5 model 1000 K-M3, Photron, Japan), with an aid of a
backlight system. Due to the limited memory of camera, only transient
process from nucleate boiling to MEB was captured in most experi
ments. It is known that MEB always occurs accompanied by the explo
sive boiling sound.7,11 Therefore, the acoustic signals were monitored
by a hydrophone to help affirm the occurrence of MEB in the experi
ments. Typical result at liquid subcooling of 30 K is shown in Figure 3.
Once the vapor film oscillated and collapsed violently, the boiling sound
would have a sudden increase in its amplitude, which could be used to
distinguish the boiling mode. The snapshots were taken immediately
using the pretrigger function of the high-speed video camera after
detecting this sudden increase in boiling sound intensity. Obtained
high-speed videos were processed with an image processing procedure
programmed by MATLAB. The bubble volume was estimated based on
the processed images with the method of discrete integral,28
