3 | EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
3.1 | Transitions of wall temperature and bubble behaviors
Figure 4 shows the variations of wall temperature and typical bubble
behaviors in transition from nucleate boiling to MEB under transient
heating mode. Corresponding quasi-heat flux, heat transfer coeffi
cient, and temperature distribution in the copper block are illus
trated in Figure 5. A transient process was observed after CHF,
characterizing by a sharp increase in wall temperature and substan
tial drop in quasi-heat flux and heat transfer coefficient, which is
similar with that reported by Ando et al.11 The dTw/dt in this process
outgrew dozens of times of that in conventional boiling modes. As
shown in Figure 5, during the transition process, the temperature
increment in the block increases with the decrease in distance
between the heating surface and the measurement position. Differ
ent from fully developed nucleate boiling and MEB, a vapor film
covers most of the heating surface during the transition process and
restrains the surrounding liquid reaching the heating surface, as
shown in Figure 4. This behavior is much more similar to that in film
boiling on a small heater under saturation29 and low liquid subcool
ing conditions.30,31 Ono and Sakashita31 deemed that the rapid
increase in wall temperature for subcooled boiling on a small copper
heater was arisen from the depletion of the liquid macrolayer under
neath the vapor film. More discussions on mechanism and dynamic
of the liquid macrolayer could be found in studies by Kumada and
Sakashita32 and Sadasivan et al.33
Figures 6 and 7 show the two different transition behaviors
observed during the process to MEB. For the first one, as indicated
in Figure 6a, the vapor film on the heating surface experiences a
short-time repeated process of expanding, necking, and departing
and then collapses suddenly in the end, which is similar to boiling
phenomenon in stable MEB. Meanwhile, the vapor film volume
changes significantly during the process, as shown in Figure 7a. For
the second one, a vapor film covers the heating surface without
detachment and collapse, as shown in Figure 6b. Its volume remains
almost unchanged all the time till the occurrence of MEB, after
which the film becomes very unstable and its volume starts to fluc
tuate violently, as shown in Figure 7b. This process was also
observed by Ando et al.11 on a circular heating surface of 5 mm in

 diameter at liquid subcooling of 20 K. In experiments, the first-type
behavior was rarely observed at liquid subcooling higher than 50 K,
while it dominated the bubble behaviors for low subcooling cases. It
is associated that the two different transition behaviors may arise
from the combined process of condensation and evaporation across
the vapor–liquid interface. The type-I transition is the result of the
evaporation of the liquid around the vapor film surpassing the con
densation of the vapor itself. In this case, the vapor film gradually
expands and new bubbles depart from the film periodically, as
shown in Figure 6(a). The type-II transition is due to an approximate
balance state of the evaporation and condensation across the inter
face, resulting probably from the enhanced condensation by increas
ing liquid subcooling or the deterioration of heat transfer around the
edge of the heater. Variation in the vapor volume shown in Figure 7
presents a certain support to the conjecture.
3.2 | Collapse and oscillation of vapor film in transition to MEB
Instead of periodic detachment of vapor bubbles from the heating sur
face, an oscillated vapor film consistently covered the heating surface
accompanied by collapse in its local region in MEB. Many
researchers11,23,34 believed that the collapse and oscillation of the
vapor film were of significance in realizing MEB with high heat trans
fer ability. Because film boiling and MEB occurred under the similar
subcooling and wall superheat condition during the transition process,
observations in this process could help to further understanding the
bubble dynamics and heat transfer process in MEB.
First, it was found by Ueno et al.23,24 that the sudden collapse of a
vapor bubble always took place after emergence of capillary waves
through experiments of vapor injection into subcooled liquid. The capil
lary waves could accelerate the condensation process and contribute to
the collapse of vapor bubble and emission of microbubbles, which is
one of important factors in MEB generation.34 However, in most previ
ous works pertinent to MEB, capillary wave was not observed directly
in boiling experiments. It was also not detected in current observation
from a side view, as shown in Figure 6. A new approach of diagonal
view similar to that by Bang et al.35 for boiling is thus employed in cur
rent work to detect the clearer vapor–liquid interface. Figure 8 shows
the concept of this visualization approach. A smooth vapor film will
look very bright due to the mirror reflection. While a wavy vapor film
will become darker by diffuse reflection that reflects incident lights
scattering in all directions. Consequently, the grey level of the vapor
film is able to use for estimation its surface roughness. Typical interfa
cial behaviors in the transition process are presented in Figure 9. An
example of probability distributions of grey value of snapshots in
Figure 9a is given in Figure 10. At liquid subcooling of 30–40 K, the for
mation of the capillary surface waves and its effect on vapor collapse
were observed successfully in the transition process. At first, the vapor
film was stable and its surface was bright and relatively smooth. Then,

 the capillary waves came into being on the vapor film, the surface
roughness increased gradually and the distribution of grey value shifted
to darker grey levels. After that many tiny bubbles were emitted from
the vapor film rapidly. This process is visually similar to the fission pro
cess of a collapsing bubble caused by Rayleigh–Taylor instability or re
entrant jet collapse mechanism.36 However, Ueno et al.24 found that
the second derivative of bubble radius was negative for that of a bub
ble collapsing in highly subcooled liquid, which did not meet the general
criterion of Rayleigh–Taylor instability.36 If increasing the liquid sub
cooling to 50 K, no obvious capillary surface waves could be detected
any more from any viewing angles, as shown in Figure 9c. The vapor
film commonly collapsed directly due to the intensive condensation at
the vapor–liquid interface in this case. Current visualization results indi
cate that there indeed exists the capillary wavy over the vapor film in
MEB under certain conditions, but it is not the only mechanism to trig
ger collapse of the vapor film in MEB.
Liquid supply to the heating surface after the collapse of the vapor
film is also important in heat transportation in MEB.34 It is known that
surrounding liquid could rewet the heating surface easily due to the
gravity and capillary forces in nucleate boiling.37 As boiling crisis comes
into being, an increasing evaporation recoil force would inhibit the liq
uid supplying into the heating surface, resulting in the dryout of liquid
macrolayer. MEB is triggered at the wall temperature over that of boil
ing crisis, but the resulted heat flux can exceed CHF and even reach
10 MW/m2 in some cases. This indicates that there probably exists
some other mechanisms driving the surrounding liquid to wet the heat
ing surface again at high wall temperature. The experiments by Ono
and Sakashita31 could help to demonstrate the existence of the liquid
layer beneath the vapor film. They measured the time-averaged void
fraction adjacent to the heating surface with a conductance probe
located apart from the surface 10 μm. Their experimental data are
replotted and presented in Figure 11. The time-averaged void fraction
in the transition process was much larger than that in nucleate boiling,
but it decreased sharply with the increase in wall temperature in MEB
again. This implies that locally rewetted region do exist under the vapor
film in MEB. Next, evaporation processes across the vapor film surface
in film boiling and MEB states are shown in Figure 12 and compared to
have an insight into the transition process. For the former, the evapora
tion was mainly found at the edge of the heater, and the generated
vapor kept the vapor film in a slowly growing state. For the latter, the
expansion of the vapor film was much more violent than that in film
boiling state even though both of them were observed under similar
conditions. This could also support the supplying of liquid into the

 heating surface again in MEB because only strong evaporation from
the liquid layer is able to replenish this rapid growth of vapor film at
high liquid subcooling. It is further found that the explosive growth of
the vapor film commonly occurred after partial collapse of the film
itself. The remained part still covered the heating surface after that
(at t = t0 + 0.8 ms in Figure 12), which is different from the expected
scenario. At this moment, it may be difficult for the liquid to rewet the
heating surface. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that the remained part
of the vapor film did not expand immediately but contracted first right
after the collapse. It is likely that an impact jet is introduced due to the
local collapse of the vapor film near the heating surface. By simulating
the motion of liquid near a rigid wall, Wang et al.38 found that a jet flow
would form toward the wall as a gas bubble was collapsed. Thus, it is
deemed that the impact jet caused by the collapse may drive the sur
rounding liquid to wet the heating surface again and contribute to the
explosive expanding of the vapor film in MEB.
Different from film boiling state, the vapor film would shrink signifi
cantly after a while of rapid growth in MEB, which can be explained by
the following two reasons. First, it is believed that the continuous bub
ble expansion would be hindered by the condensation across the film
surface. The liquid temperature profile along vertical axis of the heating
surface was measured to verify this assumption. Typical result at liquid
subcooling of 30 K is illustrated in Figure 13. At a measurement height

 ht lower than 12 mm, there existed a large temperature gradient above
the vapor film. The liquid temperature decreased rapidly with the eleva
tion of the measurement position. Nevertheless, if the height ht
exceeds 30 mm, the liquid temperature was almost unvaried. There
fore, the vapor film would tend to contract due to the growing conden
sation as it expanded continuously. Another possible reason for
shrinkage of the vapor film may be the dryout of new formed liquid
layer (macrolayer) beneath the vapor film. As shown in Figure 11, in sta
ble MEB region, the void fraction is still much larger than that in nucle
ate boiling even though it has started to decrease rapidly. This indicates
that the rewetting and dryout alternately dominates the heat transfer
in process of vapor film oscillation. Therefore, at the later stage of
expansion of vapor film, the liquid layer beneath the vapor film would
dry out again, leading to a substantial reduction in evaporation of liquid
and reversing the expansion.
Ahn and Kim29 deemed that the liquid layer beneath the vapor
bubble on a small heater may take a concave shape. Based on their
CHF model and present visualization results, a phenomenological
model of vapor film oscillation is proposed and its schematic is given
in Figure 14. At first, the vapor film is relatively stable with a large dry
patch region under its bottom in film boiling state. Once the vapor
film collapses suddenly, an impact jet toward the heater is introduced
and drives the surrounding liquid to rewet the heating surface. Then,
the remained part of the vapor film after collapse expands explosively
due to the rapid evaporation. After a while of growth, the vapor film
starts to contract again due to increasing condensation and dryout of
the newly formed liquid layer. In MEB state, the frequent collapses
lead to a continuous oscillation of the vapor film. In one bubble cycle,
evaporation of liquid carries off the heat from the heating surface
after the local collapse of the vapor film, which is then released by
condensation across the vapor film surface and expelled from periph
eral region of the vapor film by convection of liquid over the film.
3.3 | Wall temperature at onset of stable MEB
In transition from nucleate boiling to MEB, the heat flux generally
exhibits a minimum where the corresponding wall temperature repre
sents the onset of stable MEB. This temperature is an important
threshold in design of heat exchangers with MEB as the manner of
heat transfer. To observe bubble behaviors near this critical point,
complete transition process from nucleate boiling to MEB was cap
tured with the frame rate of 5,000 fps. Figure 15 shows typical trans
formation of boiling modes and variations in wall temperature and
quasi-heat flux in the transition process at liquid subcooling of 30 K.
In Figure 15a, the FB is an acronym for film boiling state. It is found
that not only the wall temperature but also the bubble dynamics are
unsteady in the transition process. At initial period of this process, film
boiling was the dominant mode on the heating surface and no bubble
collapse was observed. However, local collapse and oscillation
occurred to the vapor film periodically at the later period even though
the wall temperature continued to rise rapidly. This can explain why
the time-averaged void fraction measured by Ono and Sakashita31
increased first and then decreased with the increase in wall superheat

 or time in the transition process. Just after CHF, the liquid layer was
consumed and the void fraction near the heating surface thus
increased constantly due to the vaporization by incoming heat flux
and the absence of liquid supply in film boiling state. As MEB state
was triggered and occurred more frequently with the increase in wall
temperature, the heating surface would be rewetted more frequently,
leading to a decrease in the time-averaged void fraction. The above
phenomenon was not detected in cases without the occurrence of
MEB at low liquid subcooling.
As shown in Figure 15, the film boiling and MEB alternately
occurred not only at the later period of transition process but also at
the initial period of stable MEB. Meanwhile, the proportion of MEB in
unit time was increased from 0 to 1 gradually with the increase in wall
temperature. Therefore, the trigger of stable MEB may be the result
of the competition between MEB and FB states in the transition pro
cess. It is deemed that the minimum heat flux in the transition to MEB
would occur at a wall temperature by which a certain predominance
of the mode of MEB over film boiling is realized.
3.4 | Effects of liquid subcooling and surface oxidation
It is well known that liquid subcooling and surface conditions have a sig
nificant effect on boiling heat transfer. Figure 16 illustrates the variation
of wall temperature in the transition process from nucleate boiling to
MEB at liquid subcooling of 15–60 K. For case at subcooling of 15 K,
no MEB occurred and the wall temperature kept rising all along after
CHF. At liquid subcooling over 20 K, the sharp increase of heating sur
face temperature was ended up by the occurrence of MEB. The wall
temperature increment in the transition process is decreased by increas
ing the liquid subcooling, as shown in Figure 17. A similar trend was also
found in steady or quasi-steady boiling experiments by Inada et al.,7
Suzuki et al.,9,39 and Ando et al.11 Figure 18 shows the wall temperature
at CHF and onset of stable MEB at subcooling of 25–60 K. A higher liq
uid subcooling led to a lower onset temperature of stable MEB due to
the faster collapse frequency of vapor bubble/film.28 This would create
a higher probability for liquid to contact with the heating surface.

 For a heating surface on which MEB occurred, surface oxidation
and erosion are inevitable,40,41 which would influence its roughness
and wettability in return. In addition, an oxide layer developed on a
heating surface in liquid environment is different from that in the air,
leading to a different effect on boiling process.42 Figure 19 shows
conditions of a fresh heating surface polished with a #500 emery
paper and an oxidized heating surface boiled in water about 1.5 hr.
The static contact angle of water on the surface was measured and
presented in Figure 19a. Microscopic image of the heating surface
and surface roughness Ra were obtained using a digital microscope
(KEYENCE VHX5000) and shown in Figure 19b,c. Ra is defined as the
arithmetic average of the absolute values of profile deviations from
the center line.43 It can be found that repeated boiling experiments
could improve the wettability slightly compared with that of the fresh
heating surface. The static contact angle is reduced from about 64.1
to 57.4 , as shown in Figure 19a. The oxidized heating surface has a
roughness Ra about 16.6 μm, larger than that of the fresh surface
(about 11.9 μm). Kubo et al.41 found that the heating surface would
be eroded during MEB test. Many craters were observed on the heat
ing surface with the help of a scanning electron microscope. They
deemed that it was resulted from frequent collapse of vapor bubble/
film in MEB. Similar results are also found in present experiments as
shown in Figure 19c; the polished line on the fresh surface is replaced
by many small craters, and a darker oxide layer comes into being, as
shown in Figure 19b.
Figure 20 illustrates the variation of wall temperature in transition
from nucleate boiling to MEB on a fresh heating surface and that after
several times of repetitive boiling experiments at liquid subcooling of
30 K. With the increase in oxidation time, the heating surface was oxi
dized more seriously and a higher wall temperature for MEB was
required. It is probably the oxide layer on the heating surface that leads

 to the increase in this threshold temperature. In present experiments,
contact angle of water on oxidized surface has a change just about
10% compared to the fresh surface. In addition, Kurihara and Myers44
found that surface roughness in the range from 0.76 to 50 μm has no
obvious effect on boiling heat transfer. As shown in Figure 19, the
roughness of the used heating surfaces is in this range. Therefore, the
changes of surface wettability and roughness for the oxidized surface
may have no obvious effect on boiling in present experiments. Actually,
the obtained wall temperature is that beneath the oxide layer due to
without considering its thickness in Equation 2. An oxide layer is com
monly consisted of cupric oxide and cuprous oxide. Their thermal con
ductivities are about 20 and 5 W/mK, respectively, much lower than
that of metallic copper. Thus, the oxide layer provides a relatively large
additional thermal resistance. Furthermore, if the heating surface is
heated to a very high temperature and oxidized in boiling water for a
longer time, its contact angle with water will be reduced dramatically.
The obvious change of surface wettability may also have an effect on
the transition process to MEB. As shown in Figure 20, the temperature
of the fresh heating surface for triggering MEB would be increased by
around 20 K after about 1.5 hr oxidation in boiling water. This does not
benefit the applications of MEB, especially for devices with a limit of
running at low operating temperature. Nevertheless, the surface oxida
tion is inevitable, special treatment or coating layer for the heating sur
face is required for the application of MEB to mitigate the negative
influence arising from surface oxidation and erosion.
Although great efforts have been made over half a century, boiling
crisis is still one of the least understood thermal and hydraulic
instabilities,45 and its triggering mechanisms remain largely unclear. In
most proposed CHF models, boiling crisis was always triggered by a
critical phenomenon, such as irreversible spreading of a vapor bubble
due to the increasing vapor recoil force,46 formation of irreversible
dry spot on a heating surface,47 and dryout of liquid macrolayer
underneath the vapor bubbles.48 In the present boiling experiments
under transient heating modes, it is found that the critical phenomena
of boiling crisis can be breached by occurrence of MEB. Further works
on dynamics of microlayer/macrolayer and liquid–vapor phase distri
bution near the heater in the transition process to MEB will help to
understand mechanism of CHF and reveal the effects of microlayer/
macrolayer on its occurrence more clearly. In addition, to further illus
trate mechanism of MEB, more works on impact of bubble collapse
effect on rewetting of the heating surface are still required.
4 | CONCLUSIONS
Experiments of subcooled pool boiling were performed under tran
sient heating modes on a copper heating surface with a diameter of
10 mm. The heat transfer performance and bubble behaviors in the
transition from nucleate boiling to MEB were focused in this study.
Major conclusions can be summarized as follows:
1. During the transition process to MEB, capillary waves were
detected at liquid subcooling of 30–40 K, but it was not observed
at subcooling exceeding 50 K.
2. Observations of the evaporation process in the transition indicate
that the impact jet caused by local collapse of the vapor film could
drive the liquid to rewet the heating surface after CHF. On this
basis, it is deemed that the oscillation of vapor film in MEB may
be resulted from the intermittent rewetting and dryout on the
heating surface as well as intensified condensation across the
vapor film surface. A phenomenological model for oscillation pro
cess was given accordingly.
3. The complete transition process from nucleate boiling to MEB
was captured in experiments. Both the film boiling and MEB states
were found alternatively in the transition process and initial period
of stable MEB. According to the observations, it is associated that
the wall temperature at onset point of stable MEB is related to
competition between MEB and film boiling states in the transition
process. This critical temperature was surely affected by both liq
uid subcooling and surface oxidation, while the effect of the latter
became less obvious after repeating the experiments several
times.

title: Acoustic State Sensing of Subcooled Boiling Heat Transfer on a CuCrZr Surface Using a Deep Neural Network for Divertor Application
abstractNote: In the cooling systems of a nuclear fusion reactor (NFR), anomaly events may occur where the flow of coolant unexpectedly decreases, such as in a loss-of-flow accident (LOFA). From the viewpoint of reactor safety design, ITER should be capable of handling subcooled boiling in coolant duct lines due to a sudden LOFA under severe operating conditions where the reactor’s divertor is under a steady-state massive heat load of 10 MW=m2. To detect anomaly events, we performed a proof-of-concept (PoC) experiment and propose a novel method of state sensing of the coolant duct line based on sound emitted from boiling. In the PoC pool boiling experiment, using a hydrophone, we acquired sound from water boiling where the heat transfer surface was made of a divertor alloy [copper-chromium-zirconium (CuCrZr)] or copper (Cu) as the reference material. Notably, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) occurred at the highest heat flux in the case of CuCrZr alloy as well as Cu. This indicates that MEB can also occur in an actual NFR. In this study, we developed deep neural network (DNN) models to identify boiling states from acquired sound. Our well-trained DNN models could distinguish between the boiling sound of Cu and the CuCrZr alloy of the heat transfer surface material. The regression DNN models achieved a coefficient of determination R2 of approximately 99%. Based on the PoC study, we have demonstrated that DNN models of boiling acoustics have the potential to identify boiling states inside coolant duct lines in the NFR, where the heat flux ranges from the low region to the high region with MEB occurrence.

 Acoustic State Sensing of Subcooled Boiling Heat Transfer on a CuCrZr Surface Using a Deep Neural Network for Divertor Application
Yoshitaka Ueki, a* Yuta Aoki,a Naoya Maeda,a Noriyuki Unno,a Kazuhisa Yuki,b Yohji Seki,c and Shin-ichi Satakea
aTokyo University of Science, Faculty of Advanced Engineering, Department of Applied Electronics, Niijuku 6-3-1, Katsushika, Tokyo 125-8585, Japan bSanyo-Onoda City University, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Daigakudori 1-1-1, Sanyo-Onoda, Yamaguchi 756-0884, Japan cNational Institutes for Quantum and Radiological Science and Technology, Naka Institute for Fusion Science and Technology, 801-1 Mukoyama, Naka-shi, Ibaraki 311-0193, Japan
Received July 23, 2024 Accepted for Publication November 21, 2024
Abstract — In the cooling systems of a nuclear fusion reactor (NFR), anomaly events may occur where the flow of coolant unexpectedly decreases, such as in a loss-of-flow accident (LOFA). From the viewpoint of reactor safety design, ITER should be capable of handling subcooled boiling in coolant duct lines due to a sudden LOFA under severe operating conditions where the reactor’s divertor is under a steady-state massive heat load of 10 MW=m2. To detect anomaly events, we performed a proof-of-concept (PoC) experiment and propose a novel method of state sensing of the coolant duct line based on sound emitted from boiling. In the PoC pool boiling experiment, using a hydrophone, we acquired sound from water boiling where the heat transfer surface was made of a divertor alloy [copper-chromium-zirconium (CuCrZr)] or copper (Cu) as the reference material. Notably, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) occurred at the highest heat flux in the case of CuCrZr alloy as well as Cu. This indicates that MEB can also occur in an actual NFR. In this study, we developed deep neural network (DNN) models to identify boiling states from acquired sound. Our well-trained DNN models could distinguish between the boiling sound of Cu and the CuCrZr alloy of the heat transfer surface material. The regression DNN models achieved a coefficient of determination R2 of approximately 99%. Based on the PoC study, we have demonstrated that DNN models of boiling acoustics have the potential to identify boiling states inside coolant duct lines in the NFR, where the heat flux ranges from the low region to the high region with MEB occurrence.
Keywords — Acoustic detection, deep learning, subcooled pool boiling, copper-chromium-zirconium, lossof-flow accident.
Note — Some figures may be in color only in the electronic version.
I. INTRODUCTION
The cooling technology used in a divertor and in the blanket of a nuclear fusion reactor (NFR) is important,
especially in the case of the ITER divertor subjected to a steady-state heat load of 10 MW=m2,[1] where a material that can remove such high heat flux is required. Copper-chromium-zirconium (CuCrZr), which is a copper (Cu) alloy,[2–4] is considered a suitable cooling plumbing material for use in a divertor that operates in
*E-mail: ueki@rs.tus.ac.jp
FUSION SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY © 2025 American Nuclear Society DOI: https://doi.org/10.1080/15361055.2024.2435193
1


 such an environment.[5–8] In a previous study, the heat load and material properties of CuCrZr were analyzed and compared with those of tungsten-based materials.[9] In another study, these Cu alloy materials were found to not be susceptible to the boiling phenomenon with respect to a heating block made of Cu.[10] Systems with CuCrZr plumbing have, however, been observed to exhibit a phenomenon where coolant unexpectedly stops flowing, resulting in a loss-of-flow accident. The literature also includes a series of studies in which the boiling aspect in flow was checked using supersonic wave sound sensing to predict such a case.[11,12] In addition, in recent years, we developed a method to use underwater boiling sounds in conjunction with machine learning to detect nucleate boiling[13] and the microbubble emission boiling (MEB) phenomenon accompanied by corrosion of the heat transfer side from the low-heat-flux region.[14] In the present study, we identify the boiling state by applying deep learning to the sound data of the subcooling phenomenon of the Cu alloy. In addition, we evaluate deep learning performance using single-species datasets for Cu and CuCrZr and investigate machine learning classification of the differences in acoustic features.
II. EXPERIMENTAL APPARATUS
Pool boiling is a fundamental phenomenon that is extensively investigated and characterized. The sound pressure of the boiling is generated on the heat transfer surface. To detect anomaly events, we performed a proofof-concept (PoC) experiment by using acoustic datasets of pool boiling. The experimental apparatus with a data acquisition system for the pool boiling test is depicted in Fig. 1. In this study, deionized water was used for the boiling test. Before the test, water was boiled to degas
noncondensable gases. The pool vessel consisted of glass walls and stainless steel plates. The width, length, and height were 150, 150, and 200 mm, respectively. In the stainless steel top plate, there was an opening port to air. Therefore, the system pressure was constant at atmospheric pressure during the boiling test. Heater blocks made of CuCrZr and oxygen-free copper (hereinafter, Cu) were fabricated and installed in the bottom stainless steel plate (Figs. 2a and 2b). The CuCrZr block was fabricated in a manner identical to that reported previously.[15] The shape of the heating surface was circular (diameter of 9 mm). Cartridge heaters were placed in the bottom of the heater block, and the heat flux was controlled by varying the voltage applied to the cartridge heaters. To reduce the heat loss from the block, we wrapped a thermal insulator around the heater block. The temperature of the water was controlled at 50°C (specifically, the liquid subcooling was maintained at 50 K) using auxiliary heaters and a heat exchanger made of stainless steel tubing. Cooling water flowed between the heat exchanger and a chiller. To obtain a uniform liquid subcooling field, we used a stirrer as weakly as possible. The temperature of the water was measured using a thermocouple (Class 1, K-type). In this study, the heat flux q on the heating surface was calculated using Fourier’s law, which assumes a linear distribution of temperature at the top of the heater block:
where λ = thermal conductivity [W/(m·K)]; dT
dx = temperature
gradient in the heater block. The heat flux of q is determined by the input power amount of the cartridge heaters installed at the bottom of the heater block. The least-squares method was used to obtain the temperature gradient with the temperatures measured at 3-mm intervals from the heat transfer surface. Three thermocouples (Class 1, K-type, 0.5-mm diameter) were used. The temperature data were averaged for 1 min. The thermal conductivity of the CuCrZr block was previously reported.[10] Moreover, the wall superheat ΔTsub was calculated by subtracting the water saturation temperature from the wall temperature obtained with the intercept of the temperature distribution line. A hydrophone with a preamplifier system (sampling rate of 48 kHz) was immersed in the water to obtain the boiling sounds. To record the boiling bubble behavior during subcooled pool boiling, an LED light and a high-speed camera were used. The LED light was synchronized with the camera at 10 kHz via a signal generator.
Fig. 1. Experimental setup for subcooled pool boiling.
2 UEKI et al. · ACOUSTIC STATE SENSING OF SUBCOOLED BOILING HEAT TRANSFER
FUSION SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY · VOLUME 00 · XXXX 2025


 III. METHODOLOGY
Cepstrum analysis[15] is widely employed to detect complicated acoustic signals such as human voices and sound emitted from mechanical machines.[16] Logarithmic processing can effectively extract acoustic features from their original dataset. Figure 3 illustrates the framework of the acoustic feature extraction and deep neural network (DNN) models used in this study. The neural network architecture was identical to that in a previous study.[14] Raw sound data acquired with the hydrophone were equally divided into segments. Before a discrete Fourier transform (DFT) was applied to the sound segments, a Hamming window was utilized. We then took the common logarithm of the DFT data, resulting in the spectra in the form of decibels. We then took the inverse discrete Fourier trans
form (IDFT) of the spectra and obtained the cepstrum. The cepstrum dataset was used as a training or testing dataset. The Python libraries scipy.fft.fft and scipy.fft.ifft were used for DFT and IDFT; the scipy version was 1.7.1. Figure 4 illustrates the dataset for training and testing the DNN. The training dataset includes the cepstrum data of the CuCrZr and Cu cases in proportion. The training and testing datasets were randomly split in a 7:3 ratio. The CuCrZr and Cu datasets consist of the cepstrum data corresponding to at an applied voltage to the cartridge heaters of 20 V (the corresponding heat flux: 0.3 MW=m2) to 70 V (4.7 MW=m2), which includes the MEB cases. The mixed training dataset was used to train the present DNN models. Another mixed dataset with an identical number of datapoints as the training dataset was used as a testing dataset (Case 1). In addition, single-species datasets for Cu (Case 2)
Fig. 3. Illustration of the acoustic feature extraction and the DNN model.
Fig. 2. Photographs of the heater blocks: (a) Cu and (b) CuCrZr.
ACOUSTIC STATE SENSING OF SUBCOOLED BOILING HEAT TRANSFER · UEKI et al. 3
FUSION SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY · VOLUME 00 · XXXX 2025


 and CuCrZr (Case 3) were used for testing the trained DNN models.
IV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The boiling curves obtained using CuCrZr and Cu heating surfaces are shown in Fig. 5. The Kutateladze correlation[17] and the Rohsenow correlation,[18] which predict the nucleate boiling heat transfer, are also depicted. The Ivey-Morris prediction[19] is typically used to estimate the critical heat flux (CHF) in subcooled pool boiling. The Ivey-Morris line was calculated for a degree of subcooling of 50 K and for the CHF value of the Zuber prediction.[20] As shown in Fig. 5, when the heat flux exceeded the Ivey-Morris prediction line, the wall superheat rapidly increased because of the onset of
MEB. MEB was observed on both the CuCrZr and the Cu heating surfaces. This indicates that MEB can also occur in an actual NFR. Moreover, the boiling curves of Cu and CuCrZr well correspond to the Rohsenow correlation with the surface parameter Csf of Cu.[21] Figure 6 shows the spectral profiles at the MEB state as well as a typical nucleate boiling state, as evaluated by DFT, for the Cu and CuCrZr surfaces. The most significant difference between them is observed in the spectral range of approximately 700 to 1300 Hz. In the case of the CuCrZr surface, the most characteristic intense peak is located at 1250 Hz. The difference in the spectral profiles is attributable to the acoustic features for the DNN classification and regression analyses. We speculate that the difference in the acoustic spectral profiles is attributable to the difference in the heat transfer surface states.
Fig. 5. Comparison of the boiling heat transfer between Cu and CuCrZr at 50 K liquid subcooling.
Fig. 4. Datasets for training and testing the DNN models.
4 UEKI et al. · ACOUSTIC STATE SENSING OF SUBCOOLED BOILING HEAT TRANSFER
FUSION SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY · VOLUME 00 · XXXX 2025


 Figure 7 depicts the confusion matrices of the DNN classification that show the test results for the mixed dataset of Cu and CuCrZr and the single-species datasets of Cu and CuCrZr. In the case of Fig. 7a, note that the present DNN model can predict the boiling states of the mixed dataset with an accuracy of 100%. In addition, Figs. 7b and 7c show that the DNN model trained by the mixed dataset can accurately classify the MEB state, where the heat flux is 4.7 MW=m2; with an accuracy of 100% even in the cases of single-species datasets. Figure 8 shows the DNN regression analysis results used to predict the heat flux. In all of the cases, the DNN model accurately predicted the heat flux. The mean absolute error (MAE), loss in the DNN training, and the coefficient of determination R2 are shown in Table I. Figure 9 shows the regression analysis results used to predict the wall superheat. The MAE, loss, and R2 in the case of the superheat are shown in Table II. These results indicate that the regression DNN model can also
predict these values accurately, with a sufficiently high R2 value of 99%. In the previous study,[14] accuracy degradation of the DNN models against white noise was evaluated in the case of the Cu heater block, which showed that the DNN model with cepstrum analysis for the acoustic feature extraction was superior to that with DFT analysis. Future work should characterize the performance of DNNs under the influence of superimposed noise.
V. CONCLUSION
In this study, we characterized the acoustic features of subcooled boiling on a heat transfer surface made of the divertor alloy (CuCrZr) as well as the reference material of Cu, and we performed a PoC experiment of acoustic sensing of boiling states up to MEB occurrence. Our main findings are as follows:
Fig. 6. Spectral profiles of boiling acoustics. (a) 1.4 MW=m2 of nucleate boiling heat flux in the case of Cu. (b) 4.7 MW=m2 of MEB heat flux in the case of Cu. (c) 1.4 MW=m2 nucleate boiling heat flux in the case of CuCrZr. (d) 4.7 MW=m2 of MEB heat flux in the case of CuCrZr.
ACOUSTIC STATE SENSING OF SUBCOOLED BOILING HEAT TRANSFER · UEKI et al. 5
FUSION SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY · VOLUME 00 · XXXX 2025


 1. MEB occurred at the highest heat flux in the case of CuCrZr alloy as well as Cu. This indicates that MEB can also occur in an actual NFR.
2. The DNN models enabled identification of the MEB transition.
3. The regression DNN models of heat flux and wall superheat achieved a coefficient of determination
R2 of approximately 99%.
4. Based on the present PoC study, we have demonstrated that DNN models of boiling acoustics have the potential to identify boiling states inside coolant duct lines in the NFR, where the heat flux ranges from the low region to the high region with MEB occurrence, which is accompanied by erosion of the heat transfer surface. Using the proposed method, we can create an acoustic sensing system that detects heating states at the time an abnormality occurs inside the duct line in actual NFRs.
Fig. 7. Confusion matrixes of the DNN classification. (a) Case 1: the test result for the mixed dataset of Cu and CuCrZr. (b) Case 2: the test result for the single-species dataset of Cu. (c) Case 3: the test result for the single-species dataset of CuCrZr.

title: A visualized study of micro-bubble emission boiling
publicationTitle: International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer

fulltexts.text:
{
    "content": "A visualized study of micro-bubble emission boiling☆
Guangyu Zhu a, Licheng Sun b,⁎, Jiguo Tang a, Zhengyu Mo b, Hongtao Liu b
a National Defense Key Subject Laboratory for Nuclear Safety and Simulation Technology, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150001, China b State Key Laboratory of Hydraulics and Mountain River Engineering, College of Water Resource & Hydropower, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
article info abstract
Available online 31 October 2014
Keywords:
Micro-bubble emission boiling Bubble behavior High heat flux Vapor film
Experiments were designed, and experimental equipment was built, to study the characteristics of micro-bubble emission boiling (MEB) in water contacting a copper heating surface 10 mm in diameter. The behavior of bubbles on the heating surface was captured by a high-speed video camera. The results of these experiments indicated that after subcooling exceeded 25 K, MEB occurred and was accompanied by the emission of numerous extremely small bubbles. During the initial stage of MEB, two different bubble behaviors were observed: a film of vapor on the heating surface expanded and shrank periodically, emitting micro-bubbles, and the film of vapor expanded unevenly before condensing or collapsing into many micro-bubbles. During fully developed MEB, the film of vapor exhibited irregular changes at its surface and partially collapsed in several milliseconds. Nearly simultaneously, a new vapor film layer formed on the heating surface. MEB never occurred during water subcooling when the heating surface was embedded 0.5 mm within a ceramic thimble. © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
In subcooled boiling regions, heat fluxes higher than the critical heat flux (CHF) have been observed, accompanied by the emission of micro-bubbles formed from coalescing bubbles on the heating surface. This phenomenon is referred to as micro-bubble emission boiling (MEB), and it was first studied by Inada and coworkers in 1981 [1–4]. Inada et al. conducted a series of pool boiling experiments on a copper heating surface attached to a thin platinum foil delivering subcooling of 15–85 K. After the first report of MEB, many researchers have studied the occurrence conditions, formation mechanisms and prospective applications of MEB. Shoji and Yoshihara (1991) [5] observed MEB on a thin wire with liquid subcooling over 40 K. However, Tange et al. (2004) measured 30–40 K as the incipient level of subcooling at which MEB occurs [6]. Tange's experimental results showed, that in a pool boiling experiment, bubble diameters were less than 30 μm. The power spectrum of the sound of boiling was found to have a clear peak at approximately 2700 Hz with subcooling greater than 20 K. Suzuki et al. (2011) preformed a series of experiments on a flat heating surface 10 mm in diameter, heating water, ethanol and aqueous solutions of ethanol at 10 wt.% and 50 wt.% in an ultrasonic
field of 20 kHz [7]. These researchers concluded that ultrasonic vibration could accelerate MEB and that subcooling of at least 20 K was required to trigger MEB in water under ambient pressure. Furthermore, their experimental results from water and aqueous solutions indicated that, as the concentration of ethanol rises, the oscillation period of coalescing bubbles decreases and the maximum heat flux decreases. In pure ethanol, film boiling occurs instead of MEB. Forced convective heat transfer has been of interest to many researchers. Suzuki et al. (2001, 2002) [8,9] conducted a series of experiments on MEB in circular and rectangular minichannels. They concluded that both subcooling and fluid velocity were key factors that triggered MEB in flow boiling. MEB tends to be more easily generated more easily on a short heating surface than a long heating surface with the same width. Tange et al. (2004, 2005) [6,10] performed numerous experiments on minichannels 200–1000 μm high. In their experiments, the maximum heat flux in the 200 μm channel was 8 MW/m2. However, for a rectangular minichannel 2 mm × 2 mm with subcooling of 30 K,the maximum heat flux barely exceeded 1 MW/m2 before the heating surface burned out. P. Cheng et al. (2009) studied MEB in a horizontal semi-rectangular channel with a hydraulic diameter of 155 μm [11]. With the help of a platinum film microheater, the heat fluxes for water can be increased significantly, with a maximum of 14.41 MW/m2 at a mass velocity of 883.8 kg/m2 s and an inlet temperature of 20 °C. In micro-channels of nearly the same size, MEB was not observed in experiments conducted by Suzuki et al.
International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157
☆ Communicated by: W.J. Minkowycz. ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: leechengsun@sohu.com (L. Sun).
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.icheatmasstransfer.2014.10.003 0735-1933/© 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ichmt


 on a copper heating surface. This result may be because the large heat capacity of the heating element weakened the liquid–vapor heat exchange rate [12]. In this paper, we focused on water undergoing MEB and ordinary boiling on a small heating surface that can be embedded into a ceramic thimble. The effects of subcooling on MEB were also examined.
2. Experimental apparatus
Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the experimental apparatus, which includes a heating element, a water tank and associated control and data acquisition instruments. The heating element is a copper block heated from below by four Si–C heaters. The upper section of the copper block is a cylinder with a diameter and height of
Fig. 1. Experimental apparatus for subcooled pool boiling.
Fig. 2. Elastic connection in the experimental apparatus.
Fig. 3. Illustration of the heating surface embedded in the ceramic thimble.
G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157 149


 10 mm each. A ceramic thimble is used to insulate the copper block from the water and reduce heat dissipation. The copper block is elastically fastened to the cover of the electrical furnace wall by
four bolts and springs, as shown in Fig. 2. The use of an elastic connection can offset the difference in swelling between the heated copper block and the other components of the setup, thereby avoiding the cracking of the ceramic thimble during the experiments. The water tank is stainless steel and has observation windows on three of its walls. Its inner width, depth and height are 200 mm, 200 mm and 300 mm, respectively. Two aspects of the heating surface are taken into consideration in the experiments described herein. First, the heating surface is flush with the tank bottom, as shown in Fig. 3a. Second, the heating surface is embedded approximately 0.5 mm into the ceramic thimble by the adjustment of the asbestos washer shown in Fig. 3b. Consequently, the effects on MEB of circumferential convection around the heating surface can be illustrated. Three T-type sheathed thermocouples 1 mm in diameter are inserted into the three blind holes approximately 3 mm, 5.75 mm and 8 mm beneath the heating surface, as shown in Fig. 1. The tips of the sheathed thermocouples are oriented along the axis of the copper block. To reduce the influence of the thermocouples on the uniformity of the temperature field inside the heating block, the thermocouples are horizontally separated by 30°. Temperature measurements are recorded by a NI acquisition system with a maximum error of approximately 0.6 K. Both the temperature and the heat flux of the heating surface can be estimated from the measured temperature by Fourier's equation with the relative errors no more than 1.5%. A thermocouple is placed directly above the heating surface in the tank to monitor the subcooling of the water. Subcooling is controlled by an electric heater combined with a cooler in the tank with temperature settings ranging from 10 K to 70 K. The electric heater has 2 kW of power. The flow rate of cooling water in the cooler is adjusted by a control valve. Bubble behavior is captured by a high-speed video camera (Photron Fastcam SA5) with a frame rate of 2000–10,000 fps. Light sources are placed above and on two sides of the water tank. A convex lens is also used to enhance the brightness of the image during high frame rate shoots.
3. Experimental results and discussion
3.1. Heat transfer characteristics of MEB
Fig. 4 shows a comparison of our results for the boiling curves of water during MEB at subcooling temperatures of 30 K and 50 K with those of Inada et al. (1981) [1]. Our experimental data exhibit
Fig. 4. Comparison of boiling curves derived from our experimental results and those of Inada et al.
Fig. 5. Effect of subcooling on MEB.
Fig. 6. Bubble behaviors at subcooling of 30 K and a heat flux of 2.3 MW/m2.
150 G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157


 similar tendencies to that of Inada et al. Both data sets exhibit a sudden increase in the wall superheating between the nucleate boiling regime and the MEB regime, but the increments of this increase in the former are smaller than in the latter. This difference could be a result of the increase in thermal resistance caused by the thin platinum film attached to the heating surface in Inada's experimental apparatus, resulting in an increase in the degree of superheating required to trigger MEB.
The effects of water subcooling on MEB and nucleate boiling are shown in Fig. 5. When subcooling exceeded 25 K, MEB occurred immediately after the water reached the CHF. After this point, the heat flux increased rapidly and superheating increased as it does during typical nucleate boiling. The increments of superheating can be decreased by increasing water subcooling. As shown in the experimental results, water subcooling plays an important role in MEB and affects its heat transfer characteristics. At subcooling of
Fig. 7. Bubble behaviors at subcooling of 30 K and a heat flux of 2.9 MW/m2.
Fig. 8. Bubble behaviors at subcooling of 15 K and a heat flux of 2.4 MW/m2.
G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157 151


 Fig. 9. Bubble behaviors observed during micro-bubble emission boiling at subcooling of 30 K and a heat flux of 3.1 MW/m2.
Fig. 10. Bubble behaviors during micro-bubble emission boiling at subcooling of 40 K and a heat flux of 4.5 MW/m2. (a) Expansion and shrinkage of the vapor film. (b) Periodic collapse of the vapor film.
152 G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157


 15 K, MEB did not occur and traditional boiling became film boiling after the CHF was reached.
3.2. High-speed camera visualization
The bubble behavior observed in these experiments is similar to that observed during typical pool boiling when the heat flux is below CHF. A number of isolated small bubbles generated on the heating surface quickly coalesced to form a large bubble covering most of the heating surface. After the bubble grew for a period of several milliseconds, a necking process occurred at the bottom of the coalesced bubble and the bubble broke off and departed. The bubble promptly condensed in the subcooled water and a new coalescence process commenced. Fig. 6 shows typical bubble behavior at subcooling of 30 K with a relatively low heat flux of 2.3 MW/m2. Isolated tiny bubbles generated on the heating surface persisted in attaching to the heating surface until they coalesced into a large bubble. However, when the heat flux increased to 2.9 MW/m2, just below the CHF, with the same degree of subcooling, a new coalesced bubble was formed nearly simultaneous to the condensation of the previous bubble. Two or three coalesced bubbles may even exist simultaneously and connect end to end, forming an unsteady bubble column, as shown in Fig. 7. Another bubble behavior observed at the lower
subcooling of 15 K and a heat flux of 2.4 MW/m2 is shown in Fig. 8. The decrease in subcooling resulted in an increase in the volume of the coalesced bubble before the CHF increased, and unsteady bubble columns formed more frequently. In addition, under these conditions bubble surfaces became smooth and stable, and their oscillations appeared to be weakened as a result of the reduction in micro-convection around the bubbles' surfaces. When the heat flux surpasses the CHF, large bubbles can no longer connect as shown in Fig. 7, and instead an unsteady vapor film forms on the heating surface. After this point, the boiling process passes through a short transitional boiling regime, which may result in a sudden decrease in the heat flux accompanied by a jump in the superheating of the heating surface. Bubble behavior during transition boiling at subcooling of 30 K and a heat flux of 3.1 MW/ m2 is shown in Fig. 9. After the vapor bubble detached from the heating surface, at 36 ms, a significant disturbance emerged at the liquid–vapor interface and MEB occurred. The new, coalesced bubble collapsed into many micro-bubbles, bursting into subcooled water above the heating surface extremely rapidly. Meanwhile, cold water was supplied to the heating surface. Because the collapsing process spreads across the heating surface, micro-bubbles were emitted in all directions. Several modes of MEB can exist under the same experimental conditions. Two different boiling modes are shown in Figs. 10 and
Fig. 11. Bubble behaviors during micro-bubble emission boiling at liquid subcooling of 50 K and a heat flux of 4.6 MW/m2. (a) Expansion and shrinkage of the vapor film. (b) Periodic collapse of the vapor film.
G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157 153


 Fig. 13. Bubble behaviors during micro-bubble emission boiling at liquid subcooling of 50 K and a heat flux of 5.7 MW/m2.
Fig. 12. Bubble behaviors during micro-bubble emission boiling at liquid subcooling of 30 K and a heat flux of 6.2 MW/m2.
154 G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157


 11. Fig. 10 shows two randomly occurring behaviors at subcooling of 40 K and a heat flux of 4.5 MW/m2. In Fig. 10a, the vapor film continuously expands and shrinks before collapsing, a process which may last over a period of ten milliseconds. In Fig. 10b, the vapor film collapses into many extremely small bubbles after its expansion, a process that occurred over a period of approximately 2.5 ms. Fig. 11 presents a similar situation with approximately equal heat flux but with higher subcooling of 50 K. Figs. 12 and 13 show a comparison of bubble behavior during fully developed MEB at liquid subcooling of 30 K and 50 K and heat fluxes of 6.2 MW/m2 and 5.7 MW/m2, respectively. Under conditions of higher heat flux, the vapor film was found to expand irregularly and partially collapse. The expansion of the vapor film was magnified by decreased subcooling, similar to that shown in Fig. 10a. This may be because condensation at the interface was weakened due to the decreased water subcooling. At subcooling of 15 K, no MEB was observed. The heating surface dried out and the superheating rose rapidly, after which boiling became film boiling. Bubble behavior at liquid subcooling of 15 K
Fig. 14. Film boiling at subcooling of 15 K and a heat flux of 0.62 MW/m2 with a wall superheating of 112 K.
Fig. 15. Effect of the relative position of the heating surface on heat transfer during boiling.
G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157 155


 and a heat flux of 0.62 MW/m2 with a wall superheating of 112 K is shown in Fig. 14. A large film of vapor with a stable interface covered the entire heating surface and expanded into a spherical
bubble with a diameter similar to that of the heating surface. This vapor bubble departed, and beneath the bubble a new vapor film appeared nearly simultaneously.
Fig. 16. Bubble behaviors under different conditions involving a heating surface embedded into a ceramic thimble (a) Subcooling of 50 K, heat flux of 1 MW/m2, wall superheating of 10 K. (b) Subcooling of 50 K, heat flux of 0.64 MW/m2, wall superheating of 27 K. (c) Subcooling of 50 K, heat flux of 0.65 MW/m2, wall superheating of 62 K.
156 G. Zhu et al. / International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer 59 (2014) 148–157


 In the current experiments, the collapse of the vapor bubble or the vapor film usually occurred near the edge of the heating surface, resulting in a higher heat flux therein than in the central region of the heating element, a finding that was discussed previously by Inada et al. (1981) [1]. This may be due to the strong Marangoni convection caused by the steep temperature gradient across the superheated liquid film on the periphery of the vapor bubble. The high heat transfer performance of MEB must also be influenced by the oscillation of the surface of the vapor film. Thus, the oscillation frequency and the volume of the film play important roles in the heat transfer process.
3.3. Bubble behavior on an embedded heating surface
Fig. 15 compares the boiling curves observed with a heating surface that is flush with the bottom of the tank and a heating surface that is embedded approximately 0.5 m into a ceramic thimble at subcooling of 50 K. Experimental data from the conventional pool boiling of water is also included in Fig. 15. With an embedded heating surface, nucleate boiling and CHF occurred earlier than with an un-embedded heating surface, and MEB did not occur at any degree of water subcooling. After reaching the CHF, the heat flux remained nearly invariant as the superheating increased. Fig. 16a–c shows bubble behaviors on the embedded heating surface under different conditions of subcooling, heat fluxes and wall superheating. A large bubble covered nearly the entire heating surface and continuously merged with nearby small bubbles during its initial expansion. During the bubble's expansion, its surface remained smooth for subcooling as high as 50 K. Complete expansion of the bubble lasted more than 20 s, after which the entire vapor bubble departed and a new thin film of vapor formed on the heating surface. After the formation of the new vapor film, the process began again. The above-mentioned boiling behavior may be the result of the embedded heating surface, which inhibits the supply of cold water. The temperature of the water around the bubble on the embedded heating surface is higher than on a heating surface that is flush with the bottom of the water tank. Therefore, micro-convection near the bubble is weakened, which benefits the expansion of the superheated vapor bubble by encouraging continuous evaporation at its interface. Accordingly, small temperature gradients in the liquid film greatly weakened the Marangoni convection around the bubble. Therefore, no MEB could occur under this condition.
4. Conclusion
The micro-bubble emission boiling of water was experimentally studied under pool boiling conditions. The conclusions of these studies can be summarized as follows.
1. Liquid subcooling is greatly important to the occurrence of MEB. When the heating surface is flush with the bottom surface of the water tank, a remarkable degree of MEB will occur when subcooling exceeds 25 K.
2. The periodic formation and collapse (into many extremely small bubbles) of a vapor film on the heating surface is part of the typical process of MEB and leads to a notably high heat flux. 3. When the heating surface is embedded approximately 0.5 mm into a ceramic thimble, MEB does not occur. This indicates that, alongside subcooling, the boundary conditions around the heating surface may play a key role in the formation of MEB.
Acknowledgments
The authors are profoundly grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51376052) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Sichuan University (YJ201432).

title: Effects of surface properties on wall superheat at the onset of microbubble emission boiling
abstractNote: Boiling heat transfer (BHT) is a promising technology for cooling the high heat flux emitted from next generation electronic devices. Pool boiling represents an eco-friendly cooling technology because its pumps do not require an external electronic power source. To increase the maximum heat flux of pool boiling beyond the critical heat flux, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) can be implemented with highly subcooled water. However, conventional MEB experiments use a copper heating surface, which can easily be oxidized in air. Thus, copper heating surfaces do not retain constant surface properties, especially when the BHT boiling pool is open to air. Applying a coating to the copper surface can enable further investigations of the MEB phenomenon and improve the reliability of MEB-based cooling devices. However, it is unclear which surfaces are suitable for promoting the onset of MEB. Herein, we experimentally investigated a stable hydrophilic black chrome plating on copper by performing subcooled pool boiling with water at atmospheric pressure. The results confirmed that the black chrome plating did not interrupt the onset of MEB, but rather, it enabled constant wall superheat during repeated experiments spanning the transition from nucleate boiling to MEB. Moreover, the threshold of liquid subcooling for the onset of MEB with the black chrome plating (20 K) was similar to that with a copper heat transfer surface.

Effects of surface properties on wall superheat at the onset of microbubble emission boiling
Noriyuki Unno a,*, Ryotaro Noma a, Kazuhisa Yuki a, Shin-ichi Satake b, Koichi Suzuki a
a Department of Mechanical Engineering, Sanyo-Onoda City University, 1-1-1 Daigaku-dori, Sanyo-Onoda, Yamaguchi, 756-0884, Japan
b Department of Applied Electronics, Tokyo University of Science, 6-3-1 Niijuku-ku, Katsushika, Tokyo, 125-8585 Japan
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Boiling heat transfer Subcooled pool boiling Microbubble emission boiling Metal plating Black chrome
ABSTRACT
Boiling heat transfer (BHT) is a promising technology for cooling the high heat flux emitted from next generation electronic devices. Pool boiling represents an eco-friendly cooling technology because its pumps do not require an external electronic power source. To increase the maximum heat flux of pool boiling beyond the critical heat flux, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) can be implemented with highly subcooled water. However, conventional MEB experiments use a copper heating surface, which can easily be oxidized in air. Thus, copper heating surfaces do not retain constant surface properties, especially when the BHT boiling pool is open to air. Applying a coating to the copper surface can enable further investigations of the MEB phenomenon and improve the reliability of MEB-based cooling devices. However, it is unclear which surfaces are suitable for promoting the onset of MEB. Herein, we experimentally investigated a stable hydrophilic black chrome plating on copper by performing subcooled pool boiling with water at atmospheric pressure. The results confirmed that the black chrome plating did not interrupt the onset of MEB, but rather, it enabled constant wall superheat during repeated experiments spanning the transition from nucleate boiling to MEB. Moreover, the threshold of liquid subcooling for the onset of MEB with the black chrome plating (20 K) was similar to that with a copper heat transfer surface.
1. Introduction
Next generation electronic devices, such as power semiconductors, are expected to be much smaller and have greater output power than currently available options. This effectively reduces fabrication costs because smaller semiconductor chips increase the yield of the desired product. As a result, the heat flux emitted from the electronic devices also increases. For example, the heat flux emitted from a next generation
inverter could reach 500 W/cm2 (5 MW/m2) (Abebe et al., 2016). When
the heat flux exceeds 100 W/cm2, it is very challenging to remove the desired product using conventional single-phase cooling technology (i. e., a combination of heatsinks and fans) (Refai-Ahmed et al., 2020). Therefore, cooling strategies employing latent heat via boiling heat transfer (BHT) technology have garnered considerable research attention. Pool boiling represents a particularly promising approach because it does not require a large pump to generate forced convection. Thus, the external electronic power needed for pool boiling is lower than that required for flow boiling. The main challenges in terms of cooling electronics via pool boiling are related to the critical heat flux (CHF) and the heat transfer
coefficient (HTC). For example, when water is used, the CHF of the
saturated pool boiling system is only ~1.1 MW/m2 at atmospheric pressure (Zuber, 1959). As noted above, this CHF is insufficient for next generation power semiconductors, and therefore, considerable research efforts have been directed toward increasing the CHF of pool boiling (Xie et al., 2018). Subcooled pool boiling is one promising way to increase the CHF without applying an additive process to the heat transfer surfaces (Inoue et al., 1998). Ivey and Morris studied the CHF of water at atmospheric pressure (Ivey and Morris, 1966), and reported a CHF of 5
MW/m2 when the liquid subcooling exceeded 60 K (i.e., the water temperature was reduced by 40◦C). In this case, a large radiator would be needed to transfer heat from the water in the pool into the air. Refrigerators, which waste external electronic power, are typically used to keep the temperature of the water pool lower than the atmospheric temperature. Currently, it is difficult to correctly predict the CHF, and experimental results have indicated that the variation in CHF values can be greater than 100% (Moˇze et al., 2020). Thus, the CHF of pool boiling
should exceed 10 MW/m2 to attain a safety factor of two, which would be sufficient for a cooling device based on BHT for next generation power electronics. Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) has long been considered a
* Corresponding author at: Mechanical Engineering, Sanyo-Onoda City University: Sanyoonoda Shiritsu Yamaguchi Tokyo Rika Daigaku, Japan. E-mail address: unno@rs.socu.ac.jp (N. Unno).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
International Journal of Multiphase Flow
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ijmulflow
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijmultiphaseflow.2022.104196 Received 24 February 2022; Received in revised form 9 June 2022; Accepted 15 July 2022


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
2
candidate for cooling in the presence of a very high heat flux (i.e., >10
MW/m2) (Inada et al., 1981). MEB is typically observed at high liquid subcooling (i.e., more than a 20–30 K temperature reduction from the saturation temperature of water at atmospheric pressure), and when MEB occurred, the maximum heat flux exceeded the ordinary CHF. There is no transition to film boiling at the CHF during MEB; instead, numerous microbubbles are emitted from coalescent bubbles on relatively small heat transfer surfaces (<20 mm). Water and copper heat transfer surfaces are typically used for MEB experiments. The onset of MEB is observed during both pool boiling and forced convective boiling (Wang and Cheng, 2009). However, Suzuki et al. (2005) reported that the high concentrations of certain organic solvents prevent the onset of MEB. Tang et al. (2018) studied the boiling sound during MEB, and Ueno et al. (2011) investigated the condensation of vapor bubbles in a subcooled water pool. Kawakami et al. (2021) demonstrated the transition to MEB in limited spaces, and Unno et al. (2018a) evaluated the effect of the water level on MEB. To illuminate the mechanism governing MEB, conventional studies have mainly focused on the physical properties of the working fluid and the growth and condensation of
boiling bubbles under subcooled conditions. In addition, ultrasonic

Fig. 1. Application of the black chrome plating on the oxygen-free copper surface: (a) before plating; (b) after plating, (c) scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the black chrome plating.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
3
waves have been shown to induce the onset of MEB (Suzuki et al., 2011; Tang et al., 2019). Although MEB can cool very high heat fluxes, the main concern for practical applications of MEB is the increase in the wall temperature. For example, the wall temperature in the MEB regime can reach 140–200◦C or higher with water (Kumagai et al., 2003). When MEB is used to cool silicon carbide (SiC) semiconductors, a wall temperature below 200◦C is generally acceptable. In contrast, silicon (Si) semiconductors are usually difficult to operate at such a high temperature. Therefore, the mechanism dictating the variation in wall superheat during MEB must be determined so that it can be controlled within a certain temperature range. In contrast to conventional studies that have focused on the working fluid and boiling bubbles, Unno et al. (2018b) reported that MEB was not observed on the surface of a chrome plating layer, even at 40 K liquid subcooling at atmospheric pressure. The CHF of the chrome plating was substantially lower than that of on an oxygen-free copper surface. Thus, coalescent bubbles tended to adhere to the chromium surface, which prevented their collapse because the wettability of the chromium surface was lower than that of the copper surface. This difference in wettability was enhanced after subcooled boiling experiments. If the onset of MEB can be triggered by the boiling bubble behavior (i.e., growth and condensation), then the MEB transition would also be expected on the chrome plating surface. Ultimately, the experimental results indicated that the surface properties must be considered when investigating the mechanism of MEB. Another crucial issue to account for in terms of the MEB mechanism involves changes in the surface properties. As mentioned above, conventional MEB studies have evaluated copper heat transfer surfaces. In air, a copper surface is easy to oxidize, which changes its surface wettability (Yuki et al., 2015). For example, Kubo et al. (1999) reported
Fig. 2. Experimental setup for subcooled pool boiling experiments under atmospheric conditions using a copper heating block plated with black chrome.
Fig. 3. (Top) Details of the cylindrical part of the copper heating block containing three thermocouples; (Bottom) typical temperature distributions measured by the thermocouples.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
4
that MEB caused surface erosion. Unno et al. (2017a) found that the wall superheat increased by 15 K after a 6-hour MEB test, at which time, the copper surface was completely corroded. Degassing the water in the pool is an effective way to prevent surface oxidation. However, for subcooled boiling under atmospheric conditions, it is difficult to remove the dissolved gas in the working fluid because air can easily become re-dissolved. Thus, in practice, it is challenging to completely degas a
cooling device using BHT and retain the degassed state for a long time. To overcome this limitation, a coating method must be applied to maintain constant surface properties. For example, subcooled pool boiling has been investigated using oxygen-free copper surfaces coated with nickel, platinum, or chrome platings; however, these plated surfaces also tended to interrupt the onset of MEB (Unno et al., 2018b). The present work used a surface coating method to apply a black
Fig. 4. Boiling curves obtained using the black chrome-plated copper surface at atmospheric pressure. The liquid subcooling ΔTsub was varied between 45–15 K; at ΔTsub = 15 K, no MEB was observed.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
5
chrome plating on copper materials. The black chrome was introduced to take advantage of its durability at high temperatures. Additionally, its chemical durability is high (as indicated by salt spray tests), and its wettability is superior to that of normal chrome surfaces. A previous report confirmed that MEB can be observed on the black chrome surface (Unno et al., 2020); however, the detailed MEB characteristics were not determined (e.g., the threshold of the liquid subcooling needed for the
onset of MEB). Therefore, the present study investigated subcooled pool boiling at the black chrome surface by systematically varying the liquid subcooling level. As a result, the MEB mechanism could be evaluated by eliminating the effect of surface property changes as much as possible.
Fig. 5. Typical coalescing bubbles at the CHF at varying degrees of liquid subcooling ΔTsub: (a) 45 K, (b) 40 K, (c) 35 K, (d) 30 K, (e) 25 K, (f) 20 K, and (g) 15 K.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
6
2. Experimental apparatus and methodology
Fig. 1a,b shows the heat transfer surfaces comprising oxygen-free copper with or without the black chrome plating, respectively. A scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the black chrome plating is shown in Fig. 1c, which reveals nanostructures on the black chrome surface. Fig. 2 shows the setup for subcooled pool boiling experiments at atmospheric pressure. The pool vessel comprised four side plates (glass) and a bottom plate (stainless steel), which formed a cube (length = width = height = 200 mm) where the top of the pool was open to the air. In this study, 4 L of deionized water were used as the working fluid.
Before each boiling experiment, the water was degassed by boiling. Because the boiling experiments were performed at atmospheric pressure, the saturated temperature Tsat was 100◦C. The liquid subcooling of the pool during each experiment was controlled using a heat exchanger and an auxiliary heater. Cooling water was circulated between the heat exchanger and a chiller. The degree of liquid subcooling was varied between 15–45 K in this study (i.e., the water temperature was reduced to between 85 and 55◦C), and a stirrer (100 rpm) was used to obtain a uniform temperature field throughout the pool. The water temperature in the pool was measured using a K-type thermocouple (class 1), which was positioned 5 cm away from the heat transfer surface. A high-speed camera (500 fps) was used to record the behavior of boiling bubbles (with lighting from the back). An oxygen-free copper block was used as the heating block. The top part of the heating block had a cylindrical shape (height = 15 mm), where the diameter of the heat transfer surface was 9 mm. The heat transfer surface was polished with #500 polishing papers, and then, the copper surface was coated with black chrome (target thickness = 2 μm). Cartridge heaters were installed in the base of the heating block, and the input power of the heaters was controlled by a voltage converter with a 100-V alternating current (AC) source. Each experiment was carried out by increasing the voltage until either MEB or film boiling was observed. In this study, the CHF was determined by measuring the heat flux just before the transition from nucleate boiling to either MEB or film boiling. The heating block was covered with thermal insulators to prevent heat leakage. Three holes (diameter = 0.6 mm) for inserting K-type thermocouples (class 1, diameter = 0.5 mm) were located 3, 6, and 9 mm away from the heat transfer surface. The bottoms of the holes were aligned at the center of the cylinder to measure the temperature distribution along the central axis. The temperatures were recorded using a data logger at steady state with sample measurements every 0.5 s, and the measured data were averaged over 1 min. Fig. 3a,b shows the details of the cylindrical heating block with three thermocouples. Typical steady-state temperature data indicated heat
fluxes of 0.3, 2.3, and 3.7 MW/m2. The coefficient of determination (R2) was greater than 0.999; therefore, the temperature distribution in the cylindrical element was assumed to be linear under steady-state condi
tions. The heat flux q (W/m2) was calculated using Fourier’s law, as shown in Eq. (1),
q = k dT
dx, (1)
where k is the thermal conductivity (W/mK), and ddTx is the slope of the temperature distribution in the cylindrical heating block. In this study, ddTx was computed by applying a least squares method to analyze the temperature data obtained from the three thermocouples. Additionally,

In this study, the saturation temperature of the water was assumed to be 100◦C because the pool was opened to the air, and the thermal conductivity of copper was estimated by fitting previously reported data (White and Minges, 1997). The resulting maximum uncertainties of the
heat flux and wall superheat were 0.3 MW/m2 and 3 K, respectively, at the onset of MEB. Hereafter, the calculated uncertainties are indicated in the boiling curves as error bars. The thickness of the black chrome plating applied in this study was only 2 μm, and therefore, the temperature difference along the depth of the black chrome layer was not
