3. Results and discussion
3.1. Boiling curve characteristics with a black chrome surface upon varying the liquid subcooling level
Fig. 4a-g shows the boiling curves at liquid subcooling levels of 45, 40, 35, 30, 25, 20, and 15 K. The solid and dotted lines in each plot represent the predicted nucleate boiling regime, as proposed by Kutateladze (1952) and Stephan and Abdelsalam (1980), respectively. Each experiment was repeated without modifying or replacing the heat transfer surface. As mentioned above, when a copper heat transfer surface is used, it is difficult to maintain consistent surface conditions over multiple experiments because of changes in the surface properties (e.g., wettability) (Maracy and Winterton, 1988). It is also well known that the cavity size and nucleation site density are critical factors influencing BHT (Basu et al., 2002). Thus, it was necessary to carefully polish the copper surfaces before each experiment to ensure consistent surface conditions and uniform active cavities. However, it is impossible to obtain an identical surface each time after polishing. In contrast, when a black chrome plating was applied, subcooled pool boiling experiments
could be repeated using the same surface. The boiling curves confirmed this repeatability, i.e., even if the degree of liquid subcooling was varied, the wall superheats were constant in the nucleate boiling regime within the acceptable range of uncertainty. These findings are consistent with reported subcooled pool boiling results (Ono and Sakashita, 2007). Thus, the threshold of liquid subcooling for the onset of MEB was 20 K, which is similar to that for a copper heat transfer surface. The effects of the surface properties on the MEB threshold should be investigated further in future studies by varying the surface wettability using another stable surface. The CHF values obtained with the black chrome surface were higher than those predicted by Ivey and Morris (1966). The CHF typically increases when the surface wettability increases (Hsu and Chen, 2012). The wettability (with water) on the black chrome plating was greater than that on the copper surface owing to the nanostructures on the black chrome surface, which are not observed with a normal chrome plating. In this study, the transition to MEB was observed when the liquid subcooling was in the range of 20–45 K. Although no MEB occurred under 15 K liquid subcooling, film boiling was observed at a heat flux of 2.3
MW/m2. Previous studies have shown that 20–30 K was the lowest degree of liquid subcooling that could promote MEB using a copper surface (Inada et al., 1981; Tang et al., 2017). Therefore, the experimental results of this study confirmed that the liquid subcooling should be greater than 20 K for the onset of MEB, even when black chrome plating was applied, i.e., the black chrome plating did not increase the minimum liquid subcooling level for MEB compared with the un-coated copper surface.
3.2. Boiling bubble behavior on the black chrome surface
Fig. 5 shows the boiling bubbles at various liquid subcooling levels in the steady state before the onset of MEB or during the transition to MEB.
In this study, the heat flux was increased by approximately 0.5 MW/m2 close the transition, and the CHF was defined as the heat flux just before the transition. Similar to the previously reported vapor injection tests (Ueno et al., 2011; Tang et al., 2015), the surfaces of boiling bubbles appeared rough when the liquid subcooling was greater than 25 K. When a normal chrome layer was applied, this roughness was not observed
close to the CHF (~1.4 MW/m2) with liquid subcooling of 40 K (Unno et al., 2020). Therefore, the vapor generation rate, which is determined by the heat flux, induces the instability of the water-vapor interface, thereby triggering the onset of MEB. In contrast, the bubble surface appeared smooth under liquid subcooling of 15 K, and film boiling was observed at this time. Next, we explored the coalescing boiling bubble behavior over time. Fig. 6a-c shows the coalescing bubbles on the black chrome plating at various levels of liquid subcooling (between 25–15 K). When the liquid subcooling was 25 K, the coalescing bubbles were separated, as indicated by white arrows in Fig. 6a. In terms of the bubble shape (highlighted by red circles), the buoyancy of the bubble generated an updraft flow, allowing the subcooled liquid to be introduced in the vicinity of the heat transfer surface. After the coalescing bubbles collapsed, isolated bubbles were observed on the heat transfer surface. In contrast, the first coalescing bubble observed under 20 K liquid subcooling had difficulty condensing to water because of the lesser degree of liquid subcooling. As a result, a second coalescing bubble connected to and combined with the first bubble (indicated by the yellow circle in Fig. 6b). After the first bubble collapsed, the second bubble was pulled upward and deformed in the vertical direction. Moreover, it was clear that the number of separate coalescing bubbles decreased at 20 K liquid subcooling. Finally, it was difficult to observe the separation under 15 K liquid subcooling (white circle in Fig. 6c). By the time the first bubble detached, the next coalescent bubble had appeared, i.e., it was difficult to observe isolated bubbles. Similar to the case with 20 K subcooling, the second bubble was pulled upward and deformed vertically at 15 K subcooling. These experimental results suggested that the subcooled liquid flow close to
Fig. 9. Wall superheat at the onset of MEB as a function of the liquid subcooling. The red squares indicate the average wall superheat at each liquid subcooling level.
Fig. 10. The obtained CHF values as a function of the liquid subcooling ΔTsub.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
9
the heat transfer surface is a key factor contributing to MEB. To evaluate the coalescing bubbles’ behaviors, the maximum widths of at least 45 coalescing bubbles were measured in the recorded images in each case (Fig. 7). The symbols in Fig. 7 indicate the average width, and the error bars show the standard deviations. When the degree of liquid subcooling was reduced from 50 K to 30 K, the average bubble width increased because a lower degree of liquid subcooling caused slower bubble condensation. At a liquid subcooling of 15 K, the average width decreased because the coalescing bubbles were pulled vertically following the collapse of the previous coalescing bubble. Moreover, the CHF decreased as the liquid subcooling level decreased, i.e., the vapor generation ratio at the CHF was slower at lower liquid subcooling. Based on the experimental results, MEB was not observed at a liquid subcooling level of 15 K. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that the onset of MEB might be interrupted by the movements of coalescing bubbles. To further examine the relationship between the coalescing bubble behavior and the onset of MEB, future studies should monitor the bubble frequency with a higher speed camera. Fig. 8 shows the typical boiling bubbles during MEB with the black chrome plating when varying the degree of liquid subcooling from 20 K to 45 K. Similar to the results with the un-coated copper surface, many microbubbles were emitted from the coalescing bubbles on the heat transfer surface, and the wall superheat increased rapidly. Therefore, the onset of MEB was clearly observed on the black chrome plating. The bubbles appearing during MEB at the black chrome surface exhibited the same behavior as those on the copper surface. However, the wall superheat in the black chrome case was greater than 80 K, which was higher than that on copper surfaces (typically above 40 K). As mentioned previously, a corroded copper surface, whose water contact
angle would be lower than that on the initial copper surface, tends to exhibit a higher wall superheat in the MEB regime (Unno et al., 2017a). Because the black chrome plating surface has a higher wettability than the copper surface, this might account for the increased wall superheat. Unno et al. (2017b) studied the MEB transition on a hydrophobic surface fabricated using a fluorinated silane coupling agent and found that the hydrophobic surface reduced the wall superheat at the onset of MEB. However, this coating became unstable after 3 hours. To evaluate the effect of the surface wettability on MEB, other stable hydrophobic surfaces that can promote MEB must be explored in the future.
3.3. Wall superheat and CHF on the black chrome surface at the onset of MEB
Fig. 9 shows the wall superheat values at the onset of MEB at various degrees of liquid subcooling between 20–45 K (black circles and red squares indicate experimental data and averages, respectively). Assuming that the black chrome surface did not change during the entire experiment, these results suggest that the wall superheat at the onset of MEB could increase as the degree of liquid subcooling decreased. Considering the results presented in Fig. 6, it is reasonable to expect that the collapse of coalescing bubbles during MEB was mitigated by the lower degree of liquid subcooling, which reduced the contact frequency between the heat transfer surface and the water. Furthermore, the heat flux required for the onset of MEB decreased as the degree of liquid subcooling decreased. Suzuki et al. reported that the pressure fluctuation during MEB was proportional to the heat flux in the MEB regime, which further indicated the liquid-solid contact frequency (Suzuki and Inagaki, 2005). Therefore, the frequency of liquid-solid contact would
Fig. 11. SEM images of the black chrome plating on the copper surface before and after all experiments.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
10
be lower with a lesser degree of liquid subcooling. Fig. 10 shows the CHF values as a function of liquid subcooling, with the correlations proposed by Kutateladze (1953) (blue line) and Ivey and Morris (1966) (red line) included for comparison. The CHF of the saturated boiling water was calculated using Zuber’s prediction (Zuber, 1959). The black dotted line shows the linear fit of the experimental results. The slope of the fitting line was 0.052, which was consistent with Kutateladze’s value (0.0493) and Ivey and Morris’s value (0.0525). This result supports the observation that the black chrome surface remained stable during the experiments conducted in this study. It is well established that the CHF is influenced by surface wettability (Chu et al., 2013). Thus, if the wettability changed notably during the experiments, the CHF would show more variation, and the slope would differ. Fig. 11 presents SEM images of the black chrome surfaces before and after all experiments. Despite a certain degree of scaling after the experiments, the observed nanostructures were the same as those on the initial surface. The Ra on the black chrome surface after the test was 0.45 μm (SD = 0.07), while that on the initial surface of the same lot was 0.47 μm (SD = 0.11). These results indicate that the surface roughness of the black chrome plating was not altered during the boiling tests. This was because the wall superheats in the nucleate boiling regime were almost constant, even when the liquid subcooling level was varied. These experimental results confirmed that the wall superheat at the onset of MEB can be controlled within the range of uncertainty by maintaining
consistent surface conditions.
3.4. MEB mechanistic insights based on the experimental results
Previous studies have shown that MEB is not observed before CHF, i. e., coalescing bubbles are necessary for the onset of MEB. Fig. 12 shows a schematic of coalescing bubbles close to the CHF with the chrome and black chrome surfaces. It is known that the temperature is not uniform over a heat transfer surface during MEB; specifically, the edge regions of the heat transfer surface have lower temperatures. As a result, the phase change is mainly initiated at the edges during the MEB regime because the temperature at the center of the heat transfer surface is close to or above the wetting limit temperature (Furusho et al., 2018). With the chrome plating surface, MEB was not observed, even at a liquid subcooling level of 40 K. The wettability of the chrome plating surface is lower than that of the copper surface. Therefore, it is difficult to re-wet the edges, and the vapor supply rate to the coalescing bubbles (red arrows) is low. In this case, the growth rate of the coalescing bubble (black arrows) is slow or almost stable, and there is no instability induced at the vapor/liquid interface (Fig. 12a,b) (Ueno et al., 2011). In this case, the solid-liquid contact (Suzuki and Inagaki, 2005) was incomplete at the center, and film boiling occurred without MEB. When using a black chrome plating, which has higher surface wettability than a chrome plating surface, it is easy to re-wet the edge of
Fig. 12. (a) Schematic diagram of a coalescing bubble with the chrome plating surface; (b) photo at the CHF; (c) schematic diagram of a coalescing bubble with the black chrome plating surface; (b) photo at the MEB regime. The liquid subcooling level was 40 K in both photos.
N. Unno et al.


 International Journal of Multiphase Flow 155 (2022) 104196
11
the heat transfer surface despite the high temperature at a high heat flux (Fig. 12c,d). As a result, the vapor supply rate to the coalescing bubble (red arrows) is high, and the growth and condensation cycle is short, thus inducing vapor-liquid interface instability. The intense motion of the vapor-liquid interface (black arrows) increases solid-liquid contact and promotes the transition to MEB. Overall, the experimental results suggest that the surface wettability and the coalescing bubble motion must both be considered when exploring the onset of MEB because of the high vapor generation rate associated with the MEB phenomenon.
4. Conclusions
To elucidate the mechanism governing wall superheat variations during MEB, this study applied a black chrome plating on an oxygen-free copper surface. Previous investigations typically used copper surfaces as the heat transfer surfaces for BHT in subcooled pool boiling; however, it was difficult to ensure constant surface properties because of oxidation or erosion effects caused by MEB. The present study confirmed that it was possible to maintain a consistent surface before and after the onset of MEB by introducing a black chrome plating. Thus, subcooled pool boiling was investigated under atmospheric conditions, while varying the degree of liquid subcooling and eliminating the effect of surface property changes. The experimental results led to the following conclusions:
1) The threshold of liquid subcooling needed for the onset of MEB was 20 K with the hydrophilic black chrome-plated surface. This value is similar to that observed with a copper heat transfer surface. 2) The arithmetic average roughness on the black chrome plating was not deteriorated after conducting 19 experiments involving MEB. The reliability of the wall superheat was within the range of ±5 K at the onset of MEB.
In this study, each boiling experiment was stopped just after the onset of MEB. Therefore, the durability of the black chrome plating
should be investigated at a higher heat flux (e.g., 10 MW/m2) to evaluate the potential applicability for cooling electronics. Additionally, to better understand the mechanism and to enable the fabrication of an MEBbased cooling device with a lower wall temperature, another stable surface must be developed to further reduce the wall superheat. The results presented herein will be helpful to guide the design of BHT cooling devices using MEB.

title: High-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles and resultant ambient liquid motion in microbubble emission boiling in subcooled pool
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
abstractNote: High-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles and resultant ambient liquid motion in the microbubble emission boiling (MEB) is experimentally investigated. A new approach is introduced to detect the occurrence of MEB by monitoring the temperature fluctuations of the heating surface. Frequency and temporal irregularity of corresponding vapor bubble oscillation are evaluated by high-speed imaging over a designated range of the degree of subcooling. The resultant ambient liquid behavior around the heating surface is measured via the particle image velocimetry by detecting the emitted tiny bubbles as tracers. Through the spatial–temporal variation of the velocity field of the ambient liquid, the macroscopic liquid path around the heating surface is unveiled against the variations of MEB regime.

International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/hmt
High-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles and resultant ambient liquid motion in microbubble emission boiling in subcooled pool
Akira Otake a, Hotaka Kobayashi a,1, Kizuku Kurose b, Ichiro Ueno c,∗
a Division of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Graduate School of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda, Chiba 278-8510, Japan b Division of Systems Research, Faculty of Engineering, Yokohama National University, 79-5 Tokiwadai, Hodogaya, Yokohama, Kanagawa 240-8501, Japan c Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Faculty of Science and Technology, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda, Chiba 278-8510, Japan
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) Ambient liquid flow Subcooled pool boiling Vapor bubbles Frequency Temporal irregularity
ABSTRACT
High-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles and resultant ambient liquid motion in the microbubble emission boiling (MEB) is experimentally investigated. A new approach is introduced to detect the occurrence of MEB by monitoring the temperature fluctuations of the heating surface. Frequency and temporal irregularity of corresponding vapor bubble oscillation are evaluated by high-speed imaging over a designated range of the degree of subcooling. The resultant ambient liquid behavior around the heating surface is measured via the particle image velocimetry by detecting the emitted tiny bubbles as tracers. Through the spatial–temporal variation of the velocity field of the ambient liquid, the macroscopic liquid path around the heating surface is unveiled against the variations of MEB regime.
1. Introduction
Vapor bubble behavior and resultant ambient liquid motion are the key issues in boiling phenomena. Supply of the ambient liquid to the heating surface would dominate their heat transfer characteristics. The supply would be categorized into two depending on their scale: The microscopic supply and the macroscopic one. The former corresponds to the direct delivery of the ambient liquid to the heating surface, which results in the phase-change heat transfer via latent heat. The variation of wet area on the heating surface has been monitored optically [13], thermally [4–8], or electrically [9–12]. The latter, on the other hand, corresponds to the convective motion of the ambient liquid around the heating surface. Especially in the subcooled pool boiling, this type of supply prevails on delivery cold ambient liquid to the heating surface. Zuber [13], for instance, modeled the flow path to predict the critical heat flux (CHF). Preceding research has illustrated the ambient liquid motion in the nucleate boiling regime [14–18]. It is indispensable and of great importance to comprehend both of supplies to predict and control the boiling heat transfer characteristics. It has been indicated there exists a new boiling regime, so-called microbubble emission boiling (MEB) [19–22]. This new regime brings stably higher heat flux than the CHF remaining rather small wall superheat ΔTsat under conditions with rather high degree of subcooling ΔTsub
∗ Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: 7523513@ed.tus.ac.jp (A. Otake), 7520532@alumni.tus.ac.jp (H. Kobayashi), kurose-kizuku-dw@ynu.ac.jp (K. Kurose), ich@rs.tus.ac.jp (I. Ueno). 1 Original affiliation: Division of Mechanical Engineering (affiliation name was changed after the graduation).
[23–31]. Here the wall superheat and the degree of subcooling are defined as ΔTsat = Tw −Tsat and ΔTsub = Tsat −T∞, respectively, where Tw is the temperature of the heating surface or wall temperature, Tsat is the saturation temperature under the system pressure, and T∞ is the bulk temperature of the ambient liquid. The MEB arises accompanying with high-frequency oscillation of the vapor bubbles resultant in significant boiling sound: The frequency of the vapor bubble oscillation reaches up to several-hundred Hz in systems of pool boiling of water on the heating surface of about 10 mm in diameter [32–36]. Vigorous boiling sound has been confirmed not only in the pool [34–39] but also in the flow boilings [23–26,40]. The vapor bubbles behavior causing such a sound field is significantly different in spatial and temporal characteristics compared to that in nucleate boiling [33,35–37]. It is remarked that characteristic time of the thermal flow field in the MEB is of the order of 10−2 s deduced by the rapid growth and collapse of the vapor bubbles [35,41,42]. Notwithstanding the preceding research, the physical mechanisms have not been unveiled for the substantially high heat flux accompanying with the high-frequency oscillation of the vapor bubbles on the heating surface. The microscopic and macroscopic supply paths of the ambient liquid to the heating surface must be the essential and
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2025.126832 Received 8 November 2024; Received in revised form 16 January 2025; Accepted 13 February 2025
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 242 (2025) 126832
Available online 2 March 2025
0017-9310/© 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.


 A. Otake et al.
critical features to be uncovered. It is also indispensable to elucidate the characteristics of the vapor bubbles’ high-frequency behavior inducing the significant ambient liquid motion. Ando et al. [32] unraveled that the ambient liquid flows toward the heating surface from above, which is different from that in the nucleate boiling regime [18]. They also depicted the variation of vapor bubbles behavior in the transition to the MEB. Horiuchi et al. [34] then illustrated the correlation of the surface temperature difference in the radial direction of heating surface for the nucleate boiling and the MEB. These results on the vapor bubbles behavior and the surface-temperature difference insinuate the drastic change of the ambient liquid supply to the heating surface on the onset of MEB. By tracking the tiny bubbles emitted in the MEB as Ando et al. [32], more detailed flow field near the heating surface is examined [41,43]. These works showed asymmetric distribution of the averaged velocity field about the normal vector of the heating surface. By temporally resolving the ambient liquid behavior, Li et al. [41] indicated that the ambient liquid moves synchronously with the vapor bubbles oscillation on the heating surface. In the present study, we especially focus on (1) the temporal variation of the heating surface temperature, (2) the kinetic properties of the high-frequency vapor bubbles oscillation, including frequency and temporal irregularity, and (3) the spatial–temporal variation of the macroscopic motion of the ambient liquid in the MEB realized in the pool. A series of experiments are conducted to evince the correlation between the heat transfer characteristics and both of vapor bubble behavior and resultant macroscopic paths of the ambient liquid at different stages of the MEB for various ΔTsub.
2. Experiments
Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the experimental apparatus. The apparatus is almost the same as introduced in Refs. [32,34,35]. The validity of this system has been verified by comparing the reproducibility of CHF, heating surface size dependence, and MEB occurrence conditions with those of previous studies [32,34]. The top surface of a cylindrical copper block surrounded by the insulation material is used as the heating surface to achieve the boiling phenomenon. The diameter of the heating surface is of 10 mm. Prior to a series of experiments, the heating surface is polished with #500 sandpaper and washed with acetone. The averaged surface roughness is of 30.2 μm. The static contact angle of distilled water on the fresh heating surface before the experiment is 65.8◦ ± 1.3◦ at a room temperature of 24 ◦C. The contact angle on the oxidized heating surface after the experiment becomes 52.6◦±2.2◦ at the same room temperature of 24 ◦C, which are obtained by averaging tentime measurements and their standard deviation. These contact angle values are in good agreement with Ref. [33]. The copper block is placed beneath a test chamber filled with distilled water as the test fluid: The heating surface is located at the same level of the bottom of the chamber. The test fluid is degassed at saturation temperature for at least 30 min in prior to each experimental run. To control ΔTsub, copper cooling channels connected to the constant temperature bath (NCB2510B, TOKYO RIKAKIKAI CO., LTD., Japan) are installed in the test
Fig. 1. Overview of experimental apparatus.
chamber. The test chamber is a cuboid shape constructed of stainless steel of 10 mm thickness, with internal dimensions of 300 mm in length, 290 mm in width, and 300 mm in height. A rectangular shaped glass window is installed on each side of the chamber to observe the boiling behavior. We install electric cartridge heaters (HI-SD ROD 110V-200 W, SAKAGUCHI ELECTRIC HEATERS CO., LTD., Japan) at the bottom part of the copper block to raise the block temperature by DC power supply (PRK200-18, Matsusada Precision Inc., Japan). To evaluate temperature and heat flux at the heating surface, Tw and q, respectively, the temperature is measured at different points along the centerline of the rod. The positions of the temperature measurement are at 1 mm, 3 mm, and 5 mm from the surface of the rod. In each point, a K-type sheathed thermocouple with an outer diameter of 0.5 mm (LOLEN-350K SUS316 #9 Nippon NetsuDenki SeisakuSho Co., Japan) is inserted. Temperature data at three different locations are applied to the one-dimensional Fourier’s law to estimate q:

Here, λ is the thermal conductivity of the copper block, x is the distance from the heating surface along the central axis of the block, i is an index of temperature measurement position (i = 1, 2, 3), x and T are averaged values of the measurement points and averaged temperature, respectively. Considering the inherent temperature uncertainty of the thermocouple, ±1.5 K, and the positional uncertainty of its installation, ±0.05 mm, the uncertainties in q and Tw are evaluated to be ±0.27 MW∕m2 and ±4.4 K, respectively. The measures for uncertainty propagation refer to Ref. [44]. The coefficient of determination R2 [45, 46] associated with the approximation of the temperature gradient within the heating block for all steady-state points, averaged over each experimental run, is measured at R2 = 0.994. This indicates that the temperature distribution is validly approximated. In contrast to the characteristic time scale of the MEB phenomenon of the order of 10−2 s, the temperature diffusion time depending on the physical properties of the copper block is sufficiently long to maintain a nonstationary temperature distribution during short-time periods. In the case of plotting the time variation of q without a fully developed temperature distribution in the rod, this value is called ‘‘quasi-heat flux’’ q′′ [32], and is distinguished from q. Note that q′′ = q when the temperature in the rod is in a steady state. Quasi-heat flux q′′ is decomposed as q′′ = q′′ + ̂q′′, where q′′ is the time-averaged component, and ̂q′′ is the time-varying component. If the temperature is monitored over sufficiently long-time spans after the change of power input to the cartridge heaters up to the designated value, variation of q′′ becomes saturated to realize a steady state. Averaged value of q′′ over the period of 3 min , determined by preliminary experiment, is plotted as a ‘‘steadystate point’’ on the boiling curve. The variation ̂q′′ is strongly dependent on aliasing effect due to the difference between the frequency of the temperature variation caused by the MEB and the inherent time constant of the thermocouples. After the applied voltage is changed to a designated value, we wait until Tw and q are fully developed, then the vapor bubble behavior is captured by a high-speed camera (FASTCAM APX-RS, Photron Ltd., Japan) through the observation window using backward illumination via a metal halide lamp (HVC-SL, Photoron Ltd., Japan). The conditions for high-speed imaging are as follows: the shutter speed is ranged between 1/50000–1/25000 s, the frame rate is set constant at 10 000 fps (frames per second), and the spatial resolution is 30–40 μm∕pixel. In this study, we examine the tracking of bubbles as tracers for particle image velocimetry (PIV) as seen in Refs. [41,43]. The PIV analysis is performed with PIVlab [47], a plug-in software for MATLAB. The size of surrounding numerous tiny-bubbles generated in the MEB produced by the present experiment is about 100–500 μm in diameter. The Stokes
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 242 (2025) 126832
2


 A. Otake et al.
number of the bubbles, St = ρd2U ∕18μL [48], is estimated to be in the range of 9.3 × 10−5–2.3 × 10−3, which ensures sufficient tracking ability. Here ρ and μ are the density and viscosity of vapor, respectively, d is the averaged diameter of emitted bubbles, U is the characteristic velocity, and L is the characteristic length (L = 10 × 10−3 m as the diameter of the heating surface). As for U , we apply U = 1 m∕s, which is about ten times larger than the measured maximum value in this series of experiments not to underestimate the traceability of bubbles. The present spatial resolution of high-speed imaging (30–40 μm∕pixel), as indicated, is fine enough relative to the bubble diameter. In experimental analysis with PIV, the uncertainty in particle displacement is ±0.1 pixel [49]. Accordingly, the measured velocity in this experimental system includes an uncertainty of ±0.036 m∕s. It is remarked that the method of PIV in this study is based on normal back-light system instead of sheet-light system due to the limitation of the light intensity.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Boiling curve
Fig. 2 shows the typical example of boiling curves obtained in this series of experiments under ΔTsub = 40 K, 50 K, and 60 K. The reproducibility of boiling curves is rigidly convinced, as has been verified in Ref. [36]. Each symbol in the figure represents a steady-state point of temperature for a designated heater input. Symbols of different shapes correspond to different subcooling conditions. Results obtained in different runs are distinguishable by filled and empty marks. The arrows in the figure indicate the steady-state points before and after the MEB transition: The dotted and solid arrows indicate steady-state points corresponding to the end of nucleate boiling and the beginning of the MEB, respectively. As in previous studies [19,32–35,37], for the targeted ΔTsub in this study, high heat flux exceeding CHF is stably achieved during the development of the MEB at relatively low ΔTsat compared to that of film boiling regime. With the increase in ΔTsub, the heat flux at the end point of nucleate boiling increase [13,31,33,5052]. Heat flux at the MEB onset point also tends to increase, whereas ΔTsat in the MEB regime tends to decrease, both corresponding to the increase in ΔTsub, which reproduce well the trends in Refs. [31,35–37].
Fig. 2. Boiling curve under ΔTsub = 40 K (circle mark), 50 K (upward triangle), and 60 K (square). Solid and dotted arrows indicate the steady-state points at which the MEB firstly emerges and the end of the nucleate boiling region, respectively. Filled and empty marks indicate the data obtained in the 1st and the 2nd runs, respectively.
3.2. Wall temperature fluctuation
3.2.1. Evaluation method of temperature fluctuation ΔTw
Fig. 3-(a) shows an extracted data of ‘run2’ under ΔTsub = 50 K from the boiling curves in Fig. 2. The boiling curve is plotted with transient data obtained at a sampling rate of fs = 10 Hz in addition to the steady-state points. The quasi-boiling curve shows that the fluctuation of Tw is relatively small in the nucleate boiling regime, whereas Tw varies vigorously in the MEB [32,34,35]. This difference in Tw variation is due to the spatial–temporal behavior of vapor bubbles under each boiling regime. In this study, we quantitatively discuss the magnitude of this Tw fluctuation: Fig. 3-(b) shows an example of the evaluation of ΔTw at three typical steady-state points. The time series data of Tw are extracted by a time section of 180 s from the signal in the well-developed region: The average of Tw in time becomes constant. It is noted again that a sampling rate of temperature
Fig. 3. Typical examples of (a) boiling curve consisting of steady-state points and transient data, and (b) temperature fluctuation ΔTw at each steady-state point ((I)–(III) in Frame (a)) via temporal variation of Tw (experimental conditions: ‘run 2’ under ΔTsub = 50 K in Fig. 2). For all steady-state points (I)–(III) in Frame (b), the vertical axis is displayed in the same range (max–min: 12 K).
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 242 (2025) 126832
3


 A. Otake et al.
Fig. 4. Correlation between ΔTw and (a) absolute heat flux q and (b) normalized one q∕qCHF. Here qCHF is the critical heat flux obtained in each experimental run. Arrows in dotted and solid lines indicate the points at the end of the nucleate boiling and where the MEB firstly emerged, respectively, as Fig. 2.
Fig. 5. Correlation between normalized ΔTw and Nub in MEB region.
measurement is limited to 10 Hz due to the characteristic time of the thermocouples, although the temperature fluctuation in the MEB occurs in (100 Hz) [21,32,35,37]. Therefore, the temperature variation in the MEB is not completely tracked in this experimental system [35]. To evaluate such rapid temporal variation, we define ΔTw by a statistical method based on the sample survey. The inability to measure the wall temperature with precise temporal accuracy can be attributed not only to the insufficient sampling rate of the thermocouple but also to the influence of unsteady heat conduction on the evaluation method of this study, which assumes a one-dimensional temperature distribution. In this study, we consider only the issue of the lack of sampling rate and define temperature data ideally measured at a sufficiently high sampling rate relative to the fundamental frequency of MEB, (100 Hz), as true temperature data. We first consider the true temperature data as the statistical population, and deem the temperature measurement data acquired at 10 Hz as the sample. From this sample, 3-sigma limits (i.e., the range in which the 99.7 % of the population is statistically included [45]) are derived to estimate the true range of temperature data, which corresponds to the range of temperature fluctuation. Here, ‘sigma’ corresponds to the standard deviation. The temperature fluctuation range is thus defined as ΔTw = 3σTw × 2, where σTw is the standard deviation of the temperature measurement data derived
by σTw =
√ [
∑n
i (Tw,i − Tw)2
]
∕n. Here Tw,i is ith detected value of the
successive data set of Tw in the specific steady-state point. The number of data within the targeted period (180.0 s) is described as n. In Fig. 3, the validity of this evaluation method is confirmed from the range of the derived temperature fluctuation, indicated as dashed horizontal lines drawn at the position ±3σTw from time-averaged value of Tw.
3.2.2. Correlation between ΔTw and heat flux
Fig. 4 illustrates the correlation between estimated ΔTw and the heat flux. The value of ΔTw in the nucleate boiling maintains nearly constant, which is sufficiently small compared to that in the MEB regime. This
is attributed to rather uniform liquid supply to the heating surface with respect to time in the nucleate boiling region [2,3,53–55], where the heat flux is relatively low and the wall surface temperature is maintained almost constant. Further, as illustrated in Fig. 3 as well as in Refs. [32,34,35,43,56], the fluctuation of q is significantly small in the nucleate boiling, that is, q ∼ q′′. The nucleate boiling approaches its end point as increasing q, ΔTw sharply rises, and eventually reaches a peak in the regime of the MEB transition, corresponding to the area between the dotted and solid arrows. The rapid increase in ΔTw in the transition region is in agreement with the results shown in the previous studies [32–34]. Intermittent dryout caused by destabilized state of the solid–liquid contact is expected to cause this larger ΔTw in the regimes of the nucleate boiling near CHF [3,54,57,58] and the early stage of the MEB [32]. Once the MEB regime is realized, ΔTw turns small and remains lower than the peak value even though the MEB develops due to the increase in the heat flux. This implies, as the MEB develops, that the oscillation behavior of vapor bubbles becomes more spatially and temporally uniform [35], enhancing stable liquid supply to the heating surface and resulting in homogenization of Tw over time. In Fig. 4-(a), it is demonstrated that qCHF and the range of the heat flux itself after the MEB onset are different for each ΔTsub against q. This reflects the trend as shown in the boiling curve in Fig. 2, where the boiling curve appears to shift toward the higher heat flux region as ΔTsub increases. To eliminate such an effect of ΔTsub, nondimensional heat flux q∕qCHF is applied in Fig. 4-(b). The condition at q∕qCHF ∼ 1 indicates the transition point from the nucleate boiling to the MEB for each ΔTsub. Variation in ΔTw exhibits a sharp peak around the transition point under each ΔTsub, whereas it is emphasized that the peak value is not always at the onset point of the MEB. This comes due to the development process of ΔTsat and q: After changing the power input to the heaters, the waiting period is prepared with monitoring the evaluated ΔTw and q as well as the temperatures by the thermocouples. The onset of the MEB is realized by partially collapsing of coalesced vapor bubbles over the heating surface, which reflects the abrupt rise after the abrupt drop of q [32]. The vigorous fluctuations of vapor bubbles and corresponding ΔTsat and q lead the MEB not only in the fully developed state but also in the developing one [35,36]. The magnitude of ΔTw reaches its maximum value at the transition from the nucleate boiling to the MEB during the series of processes on the boiling curve. The above fact suggests that not only ΔTsat or q but also ΔTw is the important measure to detect the transition state. In the previous studies, the MEB transition has typically been defined by monitoring boiling noise and vapor bubbles behavior, but in the present study, introducing ΔTw enables a quantitative detection based only on monitored temperature. On the other hand, since ΔTw is not necessarily maximum at the onset of the MEB, it is quite difficult to definitively detect the end point of the nucleate boiling or the onset point of the MEB in the quasi-boiling curve. A specific region with a certain span around the peak values indicates the transition region to the MEB.

This implies that ΔTw is affected by the sensible heat depending on the temperature of heating surface and surrounding liquid: The spatialtemporal state of direct contact between the subcooled liquid and the heating surface is a key to determine ΔTw∕ΔTsub in the MEB. In the present series of experimental runs, the heat flux rises with increasing the power input to the heaters, and the temperature difference between Tw and T∞ becomes larger. In other words, a so-called driving force of temperature fluctuation at the heating surface intensifies. Nevertheless, since ΔTw remains smaller after the MEB transition compared to that in the transition regime, it is considered that the state of the solidliquid contact changes throughout the development process, leading to a temporal homogenization of Tw. This is also inferred from the results shown in Fig. 7, where the frequency of vapor bubble oscillation increases as q increases. It is remarked, while the frequency of vapor bubbles oscillation increases monotonically, that ΔTw tends to slightly increase at particularly high Nub: The vapor bubbles oscillation behavior does not thoroughly govern the variation of ΔTw. To explore the dominant parameters, in addition to macroscopic observation of vapor bubble behavior, a microscopic investigation on the solid–liquid interaction near the heating surface, similar to prior studies on the nucleate boiling [2,3,9,10,54], is indispensable.
3.3. Vapor bubbles oscillation
To evaluate vapor bubbles behavior in the MEB, we analyze a series of high-speed images taken at each steady-state point as illustrated in Fig. 5. Three representative points ((1), (4), and (6): low, medium, and high heat flux regions, respectively) are extracted from the MEB regime (as labeled in Panel (a) in Fig. 6 (1)–(6)) in the boiling curve
as examples to illustrate the analysis method. To measure the temporal behavior of vapor bubbles oscillation, an inspection line with a width of 1 pixel perpendicular to the heating surface is placed at the center of the surface as a representative section (left image of right panel in Fig. 6). The pixel row data along the inspection line are extracted from each image of successive image set, and are arranged in a time series, resulting in the so-called ‘‘resliced images’’ shown on the right in Panel (b) in Fig. 6. Such images benefit in accumulating spatiotemporal information in vapor bubbles behavior qualitatively [36,59] and quantitatively [35,60]. As an example, the resliced data only for 0.1 s are presented here. These images enable us to detect the oscillation behavior of vapor bubbles, which corresponds to the temporal variation of the liquid-vapor interface position. First, the y-coordinate values of liquid-vapor interface are obtained from binarized data of the resliced images. Particularly under the high heat flux condition (as shown as (6)), performing the fast Fourier transform (FFT) [35] on the timeseries data of this coordinate value reveals a distinct peak at the highest spectrum. This peak is then identified as the representative frequency of the vapor bubbles oscillation. This agrees well with the result shown in a previous study [35]. Under the low heat flux condition, on the other hand, the oscillatory behavior is temporally irregular, making it difficult to obtain a distinct peak in the spectrum through the FFT analysis. In this study, the representative frequency of vapor bubbles oscillation is evaluated by extracting the local maxima from the waveform of the liquid-vapor interface behavior and counting the number of peaks per unit time. The positions of the detected peaks are overlaid as vertical lines on the resliced images. The successive image data used for the analysis consists of data captured at 10 000 fps over a duration of 1.0 s, i.e., a total of 10 000 frames. This dataset provides a sufficiently long timescale against the vapor bubble oscillation in the MEB with the fundamental frequency of (100 Hz). The number of detected peaks per unit time increases as q increases, which directly reflect a trend of increasing frequency [35–37,61]. When paying attention to the distance between peaks, or the peak-to-peak period Δti = ti+1 − ti, its variation depends on each steady-state condition. Here, ti indicates the ith interval between the peaks detected in the resliced image. We introduce the ‘‘irregularity’’ CVΔt = σΔt∕Δtmean = σΔt∕Δti to evaluate the regularity of high-frequency oscillation of vapor bubbles, where σΔt and Δtmean denote the standard deviation and the mean value of Δti, respectively. Panels (a) and (b) in Fig. 7 illustrate the evaluated frequency from the peak-to-peak periods, f = 1∕Δti, and its irregularity CVΔt against q. The MEB is a phenomenon with a quite short time scale

The frequency f increases in the MEB monotonically with its development with increasing q within the range of ΔTsub in this study. Similar trends to the above have been reported in several previous studies [36,37,41]. When paying attention to the dependency on ΔTsub, one finds that the data shifts toward the higher heat flux region as ΔTsub increases. The frequency f tends to decrease as ΔTsub increases by considering a fixed q after the onset of the MEB, which is consistent with similar results in Ref. [42]. Regarding the heat transfer mechanism of the MEB, it has been reported that, in addition to latent heat transfer similar to that in the nucleate boiling region, sensible heat transfer due to direct solid–liquid contact between the heating surface and the subcooled liquid is also effective [39]. The temperature of the liquid directly supplied to the heating surface by vapor bubble oscillation depends on ΔTsub. Thus, the waiting time for vapor bubbles formation on the heating surface becomes longer as ΔTsub increases [9], indicating that the growth rate of the vapor bubbles become smaller during the expansion phase with one cycle of vapor bubbles oscillation. It has been reported by Inada et al. [21] that the shrinking and growing processes of vapor bubble is highly correlated. That is, the reduction rate of vapor bubble volume becomes high when the growing rate of vapor bubble is high. Thus the decrease in the bubble expansion rate with increasing ΔTsub under a constant q is attributed to the decrease of f . Whereas focusing on the irregularity CVΔt, it exhibits a negative correlation with q for each ΔTsub. That is, the temporal uniformity of vapor bubbles oscillation increases with q. According to Ref. [35], it has been reported that the spatial homogeneity of vapor bubbles oscillation increases with the development of the MEB as q increases. The results of this study indicate that the development of the MEB improves the homogeneity of vapor bubbles oscillation, not only spatially but also temporally. The mechanism of heat transfer by the sensible heat is based on Zuber’s model [13], which considers transient heat conduction during the contact period between two bodies with a temperature difference. It has been further formulated in Ohkura et al. [62]. Additionally, heat transfer models for droplet impact [63,64] have also proposed by considering heat transfer via the sensible heat. In the MEB, a similar phenomenon may occur as subcooled liquid is frequently supplied to the heating surface, leading to a pronounced effect of the sensible heat transfer. In the present study, the effects of ΔTsub on the frequency of vapor bubble oscillation and its irregularity are examined. The assumption we made is supported by the consistency of trends across these multiple experimental results by considering Nub, which is a parameter accounting for both of ΔTsat and

3.4. Ambient liquid motion
Fig. 9 shows an overview of the evaluation of the ambient liquid behavior via PIV, with the emitted tiny bubbles as tracers. In the MEB, the coalesced vapor bubbles generated on the heating surface and the surrounding liquid are known to move radially with respect to the surface [26,42,65]. Therefore, in this study, an arc-shaped inspection line is placed with the center of the heating surface as the origin, and this radial motion of ambient liquid is evaluated by extracting the radial and tangential velocity components on the inspection line, vr(θ, t) and vφ(θ, t), respectively. The radius of the inspection line is set at 7 mm, which is determined to avoid overlapping with the area of vapor bubbles motion on the heating surface. As demonstrated in Panel (b) in Fig. 9, the radial motion dominates the ambient liquid behavior in the thorough MEB. Here in this Panel, the temporal variations of

Focusing on the temporal characteristics, vr(θ, t) alternates between positive and negative values at the frequency of (100 Hz) in each state. In the states of relatively low heat flux, e.g., (1) and (2) in Fig. 10, there are mixed distributions of the time domains of localized high-frequency oscillation and of low-frequency one. High positive velocity is detected at θ ∼ 0◦ and ∼ 180◦, which corresponds to radial emission of tiny bubbles in almost parallel to the heating surface as observed by experiments [20,24,26,29,32,33,42,43]. of the ambient liquid. Here, we select θ = 5◦, 45◦, 90◦, 135◦, and 175◦ as representative coordinates for visualizing the spatial distribution. To reduce the influence of velocity near the wall, a margin of ±5◦ from the wall is provided at two points in the horizontal direction from the heating surface: This is because the wall surface on the same level as the heating surface is set as a region excluded from the analysis for PIV. In rather low (conditions (1) and (2)) or medium (conditions (3) and (4)) heat flux states, relatively large positive values appear in the parallel direction to the heating surface. While the ambient liquid exhibits oscillatory behavior due to vapor bubbles oscillation above the heating surface, a strong ejection occurs in the horizontal direction constantly over the long-enough observation period as discussed in the previous research [43]. It is remarked that there are no negative values in vr(θ) in this heat flux range. In other words, it is rather difficult to identify a steady inflow of liquid from a specific direction, nevertheless the liquid weakly heads to the heating surface in relatively all directions during the shrinkage of vapor bubbles as seen in Fig. 10. In the high heat flux states in (5) and (6), with regard to the positive averaged velocity component, stronger injection is observed in the normal direction to the heating surface (θ ∼ 90◦) compared to that in the low heat flux region: There steadily exists a vigorous injection from the heating surface in the normal direction (θ ∼ 90◦) in addition to the parallel direction (θ ∼ 0◦, 180◦), which is consistent with the results in Ref. [43]. Meanwhile, the negative velocities appear in the oblique direction (θ ∼ 45◦, 135◦). It consequently indicates that a steady liquid supply occurs from these diagonal directions above the heating surface. The causes of these supply paths include the dynamics of vapor bubbles oscillation or the spatial–temporal distribution of solid–liquid contact near the heating surface, but the specific mechanisms are still uncertain. It is extremely important to clarify the liquid supply path to the heating surface, not only on the macroscopic point of view but also on the microscopic one, to elucidate the heat transfer mechanism in the MEB, and more detailed investigations focusing on the aforementioned aspects are essential and indispensable.
4. Concluding remarks
We investigate the high-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles and the resultant ambient liquid motion in the MEB with respect to its heat transfer characteristics. The wall temperature fluctuation ΔTw is introduced to quantify the temporal variation of the heating surface temperature induced by changes in the boiling state. The correlation between ΔTw and wall heat flux q shows that ΔTw increases sharply during the transition regime from the nucleate boiling to the MEB. After the onset of the MEB, ΔTw decreases and converges to a relatively small value. This trend over the entire boiling curve is consistent for each ΔTsub by considering local Nusselt number Nub as a function of ΔTsub and ΔTsat . Furthermore, by monitoring ΔTw via the temperature measurements, the onset of the MEB becomes definitely detectable. High-frequency oscillation of coalesced vapor bubbles is measured by monitoring along an inspection line at the center of the heating surface. With introducing an index of irregularity CVΔt, it is discussed not only the representative frequency of vapor bubbles oscillation, f , but also the temporal variation of frequency. The irregularity CVΔt tends to decrease with the development of the MEB with increasing q. It is also demonstrated that f and CVΔt are consistent with Nub as a function of ΔTsub and ΔTsat . These results support the argument that sensible heat transport plays an important role in the MEB [39]. To comprehend the ambient liquid motion caused by vapor bubbles oscillation, PIV is applied by using emitted tiny bubbles as tracers. Radial velocity vr(θ, t) and tangential one vφ(θ, t) are extracted by the arc-shaped inspection line to evaluate the characteristic motion of the ambient liquid in the MEB. Throughout the MEB, the radial component of ambient liquid motion is dominant. By monitoring the spatial–temporal characteristics of vr(θ, t), we quantify that the parallel injection to the heating surface is dominant in low and medium q, whereas the normal injection in addition to the parallel one becomes dominant in high q. We indicate the existence of a steady liquid supply path to the heating surface from diagonal directions. To elucidate the causes of these spatial characteristics, it is indispensable to unveil factors such as the dynamics of vapor bubbles oscillation and the solid–liquid interactions in the vicinity of the heating surface.

title: Onset of microbubble emission boiling at reduced pressure using a confined vessel for subcooled pool boiling
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer

fulltexts.title: PDF
fulltexts.text:
{
    "content": "Onset of microbubble emission boiling at reduced pressure using a confined vessel for subcooled pool boiling
Noriyuki Unno a,*, Kazuhisa Yuki b, Koichi Suzuki b
a Department of Applied Electronics, Tokyo University of Science, 6-3-1 Niijuku, Katsushika-ku, Tokyo 125-8585, Japan
b Department of Mechanical Engineering, Sanyo-Onoda City University, 1-1-1 Daigakudo-ri, Sanyo-Onoda, Yamaguchi 756-0884, Japan
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Boiling heat transfer Subcooled pool boiling Microbubble emission boiling Confined space Reduced pressure Liquid chamber
ABSTRACT
Boiling heat transfer (BHT) attracts attention for application to next-generation electronics cooling owing to its high heat transfer coefficient. In practical use, the shrinking of cooling devices using BHT is needed. Therefore, BHT in a confined space is widely studied. A liquid chamber (LC), like a vapor chamber, is a cooling device that uses BHT within a compact vessel and operates at reduced system pressure. Typically, LCs are operated at temperatures near the saturated temperature of a working fluid. Subcooled pool boiling, however, is a method to increase the critical heat flux (CHF). Moreover, microbubble emission boiling (MEB), which is observed at a high liquid subcooling, is a promising technology for achieving high heat flux cooling, exceeding the ordinary CHF. There are many studies of subcooled pool boiling with MEB in large vessels at atmospheric pressure. However, the MEB characteristics in a confined space at reduced pressure are also important to realization of nextgeneration cooling devices. To reveal the BHT characteristics at high liquid subcooling and in a confined space, subcooled pool boiling is investigated with water at reduced pressure using a confined vessel. Three watercontacting heat transfer surface configurations are investigated: a small-diameter flat surface, a thin and solid heat-spreader plate, and a finned surface. It is shown that the onset of MEB in subcooled pool boiling is not observed using a heat spreader at reduced pressure. The fabricated LC with the 9-mm-diameter heat transfer
surface connected to the finned copper surface has succeeded in removing a maximum heat flux of 311 W/cm2 at the maximum temperature of 94 ◦C
1. Introduction
There is a strong demand for high-performance computers (HPCs) to realize machine-learning, big-data based artificial intelligence. As nextgeneration electronics need to be more powerful to further increase the performance of HPCs, semiconductor devices and integrated technology are developing year by year. The break-through monolithic threedimensional integrated circuit (M3D-IC) [1,2], which is a mounting technology that involves stacking IC chips, one above another, aims to achieve the goal of “More Moore” [3]. However, thermal management will be a major problem when many IC chips are stacked vertically. Because heat flux is simply increased by stacking chips, successfully applying conventional air-cooling technology to M3D-ICs will be challenging [4]. To construct economical HPCs and reduce carbon emissions, the next generation of electronic devices must be smaller, reduce the electric power consumption of cooling equipment, and reduce the electric power
required for computing. Phase-change cooling devices, which use the latent heat transport in the vaporization of a liquid, are a promising technology that may reduce or eliminate external input energy. For instance, heat pipes [5] and vapor chambers [6] are widely used as electronics cooling devices because these devices need no external electric power to transport heat. Boiling heat transfer (BHT), which generates boiling bubbles on a heating surface, removes a higher maximum heat flux than that of conventional single-phase cooling technologies [7]. While heat pipes and vapor chambers have a capillary limit in the wicks [8], electronics cooling devices using BHT also have a maximum removable heat flux called the critical heat flux (CHF). If the heat flux in a BHT cooling device exceeded the CHF, the boiling state transitions from nucleate boiling to film boiling, which results in rapid increase of temperature above the electronic device burn out temperature (Fig. 1). The liquid chamber (LC) [9] is a promising candidate for the next generation of electronics cooling devices using BHT. An LC is different from a vapor chamber: the main mechanism of heat transfer in an LC is
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: n.unno@rs.tus.ac.jp (N. Unno).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ijhmt
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2024.126600 Received 19 July 2024; Received in revised form 7 September 2024; Accepted 15 December 2024
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
Available online 20 December 2024
0017-9310/© 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).


 boiling while that in a vapor chamber is evaporation. The filling ratio of working fluid in an LC is typically much higher than in a vapor chamber, so an LC does not need a wick structure. As a result, an LC has no capillary transport limit (Fig. 2). Although cooling devices using a similar concept have been recently reported [10], the improvement of CHF is still a concern. To adopt BHT for electronic cooling devices, there are some challenges to be addressed. For instance, the maximum junction temperature must be <100 ◦C—or much lower—when the M3D-IC is made of silicon chips [11]. This makes it impossible to use water, which has a saturation temperature of 100 ◦C at atmospheric pressure, as a working fluid for M3D-ICs cooling via BHT without a reduced ambient pressure. However,
the CHF of water is <50 W/cm2 at reduced pressure in saturated pool boiling with a planar heat transfer surface made of copper [12]. This value is not adequate to deal with the expected heat flux of above 100
W/cm2 in future M3D-ICs. Dielectric liquids are also attracting attention for use with immersed boiling cooling technology, which realize a high heat transfer coefficient (HTC). However, the CHF of dielectric liquids in
saturated pool boiling with a planar surface is approximately 20 W/cm2 [13], which is of no use for future M3D-ICs. Therefore, there are many
avenues of research to improve the CHF in BHT without external input power, such as surface modification [14], wettability control [15], thermal conductivity distribution [16], porous body [17], porous coating [18], lotus-type porous metals [19], the additive of nanofluid or surfactant [20], free-particles [21], and boiling bubble resonator [22]. In contrast, subcooled pool boiling is a promising technology for the increase of CHF. For example, based on the Ivey-Morris prediction [23], the CHF of water at atmospheric pressure is increased from approxi
mately 110 W/cm2 at saturated boiling to 370 W/cm2 at 50 K of liquid subcooling. Furthermore, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) [24] is observed (red line in Fig. 1) under high liquid subcooling conditions. When MEB occurs, many microbubbles are emitted from the coalescent boiling bubble on the heat transfer surface, and the maximum remov
able heat flux exceeds the ordinary CHF value, usually 1000 W/cm2 [25]. However, the detailed mechanism of MEB onset is not revealed. Thus, there has been some research to investigate the MEB characteristics in terms of pressurized system [26], working fluid property [27], surface wettability [28], vapor-liquid interface behavior [29,30], ultrasonic field [31], boiling sound [32], confined space [33] and boiling bubble motion [34,35]. To shrink the BHT cooling devices using MEB, the limitation of water level is also important, and it is reported that the onset of MEB needs a water level of above 8 mm in atmospheric pressure [36]. To understand the onset of MEB under circumstances similar to those of an LC, such as pressure or device size, investigation of subcooled pool boiling in a confined vessel at reduced pressure is needed. In this study, the mechanism of MEB onset was examined with water in subcooled boiling using an LC with three water-contacting heat transfer surface configurations: a small-diameter flat surface, a thin and solid heat-spreader plate, and a finned surface.
2. Experimental apparatus and methodology including data reduction
Fig. 3 depicts the experimental setup for subcooled pool boiling in a fabricated LC at reduced pressure. The confined vessel was made from polycarbonate and the inner dimensions of the vessel were a width of 60 mm, a length of 60 mm, and a total height of 22 mm. The volume of the

q heat flux (W/m2) T temperature (◦C) Tsat saturation temperature (◦C) Tw wall temperature (◦C) Tf liquid bulk temperature (◦C) ΔTsub liquid subcooling (K) U uncertainty
Subscripts
w wall sat saturation sub subcooling
Fig. 1. Boiling curve with microbubble emission boiling (MEB) regime.
Fig. 2. Schematics of (a) a conventional vapor chamber and (b) a liquid chamber.
N. Unno et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
2


 vessel was 79.2 mL. The vessel was designed to have a height less than half the that of a 1 U rack server. In this study, deionized water was used as a working fluid and the filling ratio for the total vessel volume was 100 %. The water used to fill the LC was boiled, to be degassed, before being used to fill the LC. Inside the vessel, a copper cooling pipe was installed (inner diameter (ID) 4 mm, outer diameter (OD) 6 mm), which was used to maintain the subcooled state of the liquid water. An external chiller (HRS024-A-20, SMC Co., Japan) provided cooling water with constant temperatures of 15, 25, and 35 ± 0.1 ◦C. In this study, the difference between the inlet and outlet temperatures of the cooling water was <1 ◦C, which approximates a uniform temperature distribution in the heat exchanger. These temperatures are typically employed in conventional data centers or super computers [37]. To maintain the system pressure at lower than the atmospheric pressure, a vacuum pump (DTC-41, ULVAC Co., Japan) was connected to the port at the top of the vessel. Here, the initial system pressure was varied from 100 kPaG to 85 kPaG.using a pressure regulator (IRV20, SMC Co., Japan) which was placed between the vacuum pump and the vessel through a vacuum trap. The initial pressures were adjusted at room temperature. Note that the valve installed in the front of the vacuum trap was closed while the boiling tests were conducted. Therefore, the actual system pressure and the liquid temperature in the LC were varied using the heat input from a heating block during the boiling tests because the temperature of the cooling water supplied from the chiller remained constant. This situation is similar that of an LC in practical use. The system pressure was monitored using a pressure transducer (GP-M001, ±100 kPaG (±1.0 % of F.S.), KEYENCE Co., Japan). To input heat flux, a heating block made of oxygen-free copper was used. The heating block consisted of the two parts: a cylindrical top part designed to measure the heat flux, and a bottom base part with cartridge heaters. The input power for the heaters was controlled using a stabilized DC power source (PWR1201MH, KIKUSUI ELECTRONICS Co., Japan). The applied DC voltage was increased incrementally, and each temperature or pressure datum was recorded by a data logger (NR-500
and NR-TH08, KEYENCE Co., Japan) at 0.5 s intervals under steady state conditions. The diameter of the top of the heating block was 9 mm. The container was placed so that the normal of the heat transfer surface was opposite the direction of gravity, as in conventional HPC rack servers. Figs. 4(a) and (b) are the photos of the assembled LC. To seal the LC
Fig. 3. Experiment setup for subcooled pool boiling in confined space at reduced pressure.
Fig. 4. Photo of the assembled liquid chamber from (a) the side-view and (b) bird-view.
N. Unno et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
3


 and allow for reduced system pressure, a bottom plate was fastened to the vessel, with an O-ring between, using bolts. The bottom plate was made of a 0.5-mm-thick stainless-steel plate or a 1-mm-thick oxygenfree copper plate. Three water-contacting heat transfer surface configurations were investigated: a 9-mm-diameter copper (Fig. 5(a)), a solidcopper heat-spreader plate connected to the heater (Fig. 5(b)), and a solid-copper heat-spreader with 5.5-mm-high, 12-mm-long, and 14-mmlong copper fins (Fig. 5(c) and (d)). In the case of Fig. 5(b) and (c), the top of the copper heating block (the same 9-mm-diameter copper surface from Fig. 5(a)) was attached to the heat spreader via a solder (SAC305) thermal interface material (TIM). The heat transfer surface was polished with #240 polishing papers, resulting in an arithmetic mean roughness (Ra) of 0.7–0.8 μm. The test parameters used for the subcooled pool boiling experiments with the LC are listed in Table 1. Fig. 6 depicts the data reduction process for subcooled pool boiling at reduced pressure. Three K-type thermocouples (class 1, diameter 0.5 mm) were placed in the cylindrical part of the heating block at distances, from the top of the block, of 3, 6, and 9 mm. The insertion depth of the thermocouples was 4.5 mm, so that the tips of the thermocouples were arranged along the central axis of the cylindrical part. The 45◦ angle assumption and the surface area ratio are generally used to perform thermal analyses of heat spreaders and finned surfaces, respectively. However, it has been reported that the angle assumption is difficult to apply under high heat flux conditions. In addition, calculation of the heat flux based on the surface area of the finned surface also tends to be problematic because no linearity is observed between the CHF and the surface area [38]. Therefore, in this study, the data reduction process was performed as follows: the heat flux is calculated based on the top of the cylindrical part (diameter: 9 mm) to ensure that the total heat input to the LC remains the same for each heat transfer surface configuration. The obtained data were averaged for 1 min. In this study, the coef
ficient of determination (R2) of the measured temperatures was above 0.999, which means that a linear distribution may be assumed. There
fore, the heat flux q (W/m2) was calculated using Fourier’s law:
q = k dT
dx, (1)
where ddTx is the slope of the temperature distribution and k (W/mK) is the thermal conductivity of copper, which was obtained by fitting the reported data [39]. Here, the slope was calculated using the least-squares method on the averaged data of each thermocouple. The wall temperature Tw (◦C) was estimated by extrapolating the linear temperature distribution. Although the Nukiyama curve is typically used to report boiling data for a constant parameter, both the actual system pressure and the liquid subcooling varied with increasing heat flux in this study. Therefore, the data obtained from the LC are organized using the wall temperature as a function of the heat flux. This method also provides a convenient way to focus on the relationship between the wall temperature and the onset of MEB. The uncertainty analysis was carried out in as in the previous study [40]. Here, it is assumed that the heat flux and wall superheat were expressed as follows:
