3. Results and discussion
3.1. Small-diameter surface
BHT with the small-diameter surface was investigated by varying the initial system pressure (Fig. 7(a)(b)). The cooling water temperature supplied from the chiller was constant at 15 ◦C. The open and closed symbols in Fig. 7(a) represent the nucleate boiling regime and the MEB regime, respectively. The onset of MEB was observed when the initial pressure was between 95 and 87.5 kPaG and the liquid subcooling was >14 K. When
the heat flux was 100 W/cm2, the lower initial system pressure tended to result in lower wall temperature. This is because the saturation temperature decreased with pressure and the nucleate boiling became more active. In contrast, the coolant water temperature supplied from the chiller was constant, which decreased the liquid subcooling at lower initial system pressure. Note that liquid subcooling locally increased just before the onset of MEB. This is because a large coalescent bubble was formed on the heat transfer surface, stirring the internal liquid, and resulting in enhanced convective heat transfer on the heat exchanger in the confined space. As a result, the onset of MEB was observed at 116 W/
cm2 and film boiling was then observed at above 188 W/cm2, at 95 kPaG. In the other case, the experiments were stopped owing to the heater temperature limitation. In this study, the wall temperature was 93 ◦C when MEB occurred at 90 kPaG for the small-diameter flat surface. It was reported previously that the Leidenfrost temperature on a silicon plate was approximately 450 K (177 ◦C) at 1.0 bar with water and that this temperature was reduced to approximately 380 K (107 ◦C) at 0.1 bar [41]. Moreover, the direct solid-liquid contact is key for
Fig. 6. Data reduction process in this study.
Fig. 7. (a) Wall temperature and (b) liquid subcooling as a function of heat flux with the small-diameter surface by varying the initial system pressure. The temperature of coolant supplied from the chiller was constant at 15 ◦C.
N. Unno et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
5


 triggering of the MEB transition: the MEB onset temperature was within the transition boiling region under ambient air conditions [33]. These results support the idea that direct solid-liquid contact with consideration of the wetting limit temperature [42] will be essential for the occurrence of MEB, even under reduced pressure conditions. In fact, the surface temperature at the onset of MEB was reported to be approximately 150–170 ◦C under atmospheric pressure [43]; therefore, the surface temperature difference between the temperature at 90 kPaG and that at atmospheric pressure was 57–77 ◦C at the onset of MEB, which is close to the Leidenfrost temperature reduction of approximately 70 ◦C. As a subject for further discussion, the transition boiling region and the wetting limit temperature under the reduced pressure conditions should be investigated in detail. Fig. 8 depicts the observed CHF as a function of the initial pressure. CHF was determined by the measured heat flux at steady state just before the onset of MEB. As a result, there was a positive correlation between the CHF and the initial system pressure. At atmospheric pressure, the typical MEB transition is only observed at liquid subcooling values above 20–25 K, and it directly transitioned to film boiling at liquid subcooling <20 K [31]. By contrast, at reduced pressure, it was confirmed that the onset of MEB was possible even when the liquid subcooling was 14.5 K. Previously, it was reported that higher system pressure needs higher liquid subcooling [26]. When the system pressure is reduced, the saturation temperature will also be reduced. As a result, the liquid temperature in the LC will be different even if the degree of liquid subcooling remains the same. This result suggests that liquid properties that vary with temperature, such as viscosity and surface tension, are important factors for the onset of MEB. At the constant initial pressure of 90 kPaG, the temperature of the cooling water that was supplied from the chiller was varied to be at temperatures of 15, 25 and 35 ◦C (Fig. 9(a) and (b)). The averaged actual pressure over a period of 1 min was also plotted as shown in Fig. 9(c). The actual pressure varied with the heat flux because the generated boiling bubbles that stir the water in the LC changed their form depending on the heat flux: the convective heat transfer that occurred on the heat exchanger affected the liquid temperature in the LC. As noted above, the onset of MEB was confirmed even with liquid subcooling at 13 K and the cooling water supplied from the chiller at 35 ◦C. Moreover,
the onset of MEB was observed at above 100 W/cm2, under each cooling water temperature condition. Fig. 10 depicts the CHF value, obtained from Fig. 9(a), as a function of the liquid subcooling. Although the actual pressures at the onset of MEB were not equal, there was a positive correlation between the CHF and the liquid subcooling; the experimental result found that liquid subcooling is also an important factor for the onset of MEB, not only at atmospheric pressure but also at reduced
pressure.
3.2. Heat spreader
BHT with the heat spreader was investigated by varying the initial system pressure (Fig. 11(a)(b)). The cooling water temperature was constant at 15 ◦C. In this case, the vertical axis of Fig. 11 is the
Fig. 8. Relationship between the initial system pressure and CHF value with the small-diameter surface, in addition to the degree of liquid subcooling at CHF.
Fig. 9. (a) Wall temperature, (b) liquid subcooling, and (c) actual pressure as a function of the heat flux acquired by varying the temperature of the coolant supplied from the chiller. The initial system pressure was constant at 90 kPaG and the small-diameter surface was used.
N. Unno et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
6


 temperature of the heater top, which is extrapolated from the data of the three thermocouples, and is not the temperature of the heat transfer surface. Similar to the small-diameter surface case, the heat flux was calculated at the top of the cylindrical part using the temperature data from the three thermocouples. The onset of MEB was not observed by varying the initial pressure from 100 to 85 kPaG. In contrast to the
small-diameter case, boiling did not occur at <100 W/cm2. Furthermore, temperature overshoot (see Fig. 1) at the onset of nucleate boiling (ONB) was observed in each case. In the worst case, the temperature
reached 102 ◦C at 130 W/cm2 when the initial pressure was 85 kPaG. If the size of the active cavity was changed while varying the initial system pressure, the same result could be observed in the small-diameter case, but it did not depend on the initial system pressure. Another possible reason is the change in the net heat flux on the heat-spreader surface. Unfortunately, the net heat flux was difficult to measure in this study. To design the active cavity and adopt the LC with the heat spreader for M3D-ICs, it will be necessary to estimate the net heat flux. After ONB, inflection points were observed at each initial system pressure, which was due to local dry out, as explained in Fig. 12. The onset of MEB is caused by the subcooled liquid approaching the smalldiameter surface, which enhances the condensation of coalescing bubbles (Fig. 12(a)). However, with the heat spreader (Fig. 12(b)), subcooled liquid could not approach the center of the heating point due to surrounding bubbles, resulting in deterioration of the liquid subcooling. As a result, the onset of MEB was inhibited. Consequently, in subcooled pool boiling with the confined vessel, the onset of MEB will be difficult using a heat spreader at reduced pressure.
3.3. Finned heat-spreader
The BHT with the copper-finned heat-spreader was investigated by varying both the initial system pressure and the cooling water temperature (Fig. 13(a)(b)). Similar to the heat spreader case, the heat flux was calculated at the top of the cylindrical part using the temperature data from the three thermocouples. Although the MEB regime was observed with the small-diameter surface (planar), there was no sign of the onset of MEB with the finned heat-spreader, even under identical heat flux conditions. In this study, it was difficult to recognize the CHF with coalescing bubble on the finned surface even at the maximum heat flux
of 323 W/cm2 and the experiment was stopped owing to the limits of the heater temperature. It has been reported that the liquid-vapor interface instability, which is observed during the condensation of coalescent boiling bubbles, will be an important factor in the onset of MEB [29–31]. If this instability were the only reason for the onset of MEB, then regardless of the heat flux, the MEB transition would be observed in the finned surface case at the same heat input at which the transition was observed in the small-diameter flat surface case. However, the onset of MEB was not observed for the finned surface at heat fluxes below 323
W/cm2. Ultimately, the experiment result showed that a coalesced bubble on a heat transfer surface, which is observed at the ordinary CHF, might be necessary for the onset of MEB. Though the onset of MEB was not observed, the fabricated LC has succeeded in removing a maximum heat
flux of 311 W/cm2 at a maximum heater top temperature of 94 ◦C with
15 ◦C cooling water supplied from the chiller. Moreover, at 200 W/cm2, the maximum heater top temperatures were 72 and 82 ◦C, with 15 and 35 ◦C cooling water, respectively. This means that connecting the cooling pipes between LCs in series, with lower mass flux of the cooling water, could be possible; as a result, both external pumping energy and pipe cost in HPCs would be reduced. We believe this cooling potential of the LC will be helpful to address the thermal management problem of the future M3D-ICs in HPCs.
4. Conclusions
In this study, subcooled pool boiling was performed with a confined vessel at reduced pressure to investigate the mechanism of the onset of MEB. Three water-contacting heat transfer surface configurations—small-diameter, flat solid-copper heat-spreader, and finned copper heat-spreader—were compared by varying the initial system pressure and the cooling water temperature. The experimental results led to the following conclusions:
Fig. 10. CHF values for the small-diameter surface as a function of the liquid subcooling.
Fig. 11. (a) Wall temperature and (b) liquid subcooling as a function of heat flux with the heat spreader by varying the initial system pressure. The cooling water temperature supplied from the chiller was constant at 15 ◦C.
N. Unno et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 239 (2025) 126600
7


 1) The onset of MEB was observed in the LC at 95 to 87.5 kPaG while the liquid subcooling was >14 K. The liquid subcooling needed for the onset of MEB at reduced pressure is less than that at atmospheric pressure. 2) In the confined vessel, the convective heat transfer on the heat exchanger was enhanced by the boiling bubble. As a result, the liquid subcooling in the LC was locally increased near CHF, owing to the large coalescent bubbles. 3) A positive correlation between the CHF and the initial system pressure was obtained. This means that the initial pressure should be precisely controlled for the investigation of subcooled pool boiling in LCs. 4) At reduced pressure, no onset of MEB was observed with the heatspreader surface while varying the pressure from 100 to 85 kPaG. Subcooled liquid could not approach the center of the heating point owing to surrounding bubbles, resulting in deterioration of the liquid subcooling for the onset of MEB. 5) The onset of MEB was not observed using the finned heat-spreader, even though the heat flux was the same as that for MEB onset with the bare heat-spreader surface. This suggests that the coalesced bubble generated at the ordinary CHF might be necessary for the onset of MEB. 6) The fabricated LC with the 9-mm-diameter heat transfer surface connected to the finned heat spreader has successfully removed a
maximum heat flux of 311 W/cm2 at the maximum heater surface temperature of 94 ◦C.

title: Flow microbubble emission boiling (MEB) in open microchannels for durable and efficient heat dissipation
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
abstractNote: Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) has been reported to be an advanced heat transfer mechanism due to its significant heat dissipation capacity at high heat flux. Microchannel heat sinks are considered to be effective heat transfer carriers, but MEB is difficult to be triggered in conventional microchannels due to insufficient mainflow subcooling and restriction of narrow channel walls. In this work, we conducted flow MEB experiments in plain and lasered open microchannels with various inlet temperatures (Tinlet) and open gap height (Hg). The open configuration can provide adequate mainflow subcooling and extra flow area to trigger MEB. The results showed that MEB occurred in plain open microchannels as a transition flow pattern between bubbly flow and flow reversal at Tinlet ≤ 25 ◦C and Hg = 0.3 mm, with a significant temperature drop after a one-time flow reversal, providing abundant vapor composition as MEB evaporation cores; MEB did not happen at higher Tinlet and larger Hg (1 mm). However, in lasered open microchannels, MEB was triggered at the beginning of flow boiling at Tinlet ≤ 65 ◦C with Hg = 0.3 and 1 mm, without temperature drop or flow reversal. This demonstrated that the required inlet subcooling, heat flux and temperature gradient in vertical direction for MEB initiation were simultaneously reduced in lasered microchannels. The two-phase heat transfer coefficient (htp) of MEB was significantly increased (up to 77.8 %) compared to conventional bubbly flow, due to the faster bubble nucleation frequency and rapid impact from the subcooled mainflow to channel walls, and was further enhanced in lasered microchannels. The durability of MEB in plain microchannel was unsatisfactory, as the persistent flow reversal dominated the flow pattern after ~3750 s at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff ~1450 kW/ m2. However, in lasered microchannels, MEB ran steadily for 22500 s at the same working condition. This study provided an effective and accessible method to achieve durable MEB in microchannels with excellent heat dissipation capacity, offering valuable insights for further thermal management engineering applications.

{
    "content": "Flow microbubble emission boiling (MEB) in open microchannels for durable and efficient heat dissipation
Qi Zhao , Mingxiang Lu , Yuanle Zhang , Qiang Li *, Xuemei Chen *
MIIT Key Laboratory of Thermal Control of Electronic Equipment, School of Energy and Power Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China
ARTICLE INFO
Keywords:
Microbubble emission boiling Open microchannel Lasered microchannel Two-phase flow boiling Heat transfer enhancement
ABSTRACT
Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) has been reported to be an advanced heat transfer mechanism due to its significant heat dissipation capacity at high heat flux. Microchannel heat sinks are considered to be effective heat transfer carriers, but MEB is difficult to be triggered in conventional microchannels due to insufficient mainflow subcooling and restriction of narrow channel walls. In this work, we conducted flow MEB experiments in plain and lasered open microchannels with various inlet temperatures (Tinlet) and open gap height (Hg). The open configuration can provide adequate mainflow subcooling and extra flow area to trigger MEB. The results showed that MEB occurred in plain open microchannels as a transition flow pattern between bubbly flow and flow reversal at Tinlet ≤ 25 ◦C and Hg = 0.3 mm, with a significant temperature drop after a one-time flow reversal, providing abundant vapor composition as MEB evaporation cores; MEB did not happen at higher Tinlet and larger Hg (1 mm). However, in lasered open microchannels, MEB was triggered at the beginning of flow boiling at Tinlet ≤ 65 ◦C with Hg = 0.3 and 1 mm, without temperature drop or flow reversal. This demonstrated that the required inlet subcooling, heat flux and temperature gradient in vertical direction for MEB initiation were simultaneously reduced in lasered microchannels. The two-phase heat transfer coefficient (htp) of MEB was significantly increased (up to 77.8 %) compared to conventional bubbly flow, due to the faster bubble nucleation frequency and rapid impact from the subcooled mainflow to channel walls, and was further enhanced in lasered microchannels. The durability of MEB in plain microchannel was unsatisfactory, as the persistent flow reversal dominated the flow pattern after ~3750 s at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff ~1450 kW/
m2. However, in lasered microchannels, MEB ran steadily for 22500 s at the same working condition. This study provided an effective and accessible method to achieve durable MEB in microchannels with excellent heat dissipation capacity, offering valuable insights for further thermal management engineering applications.
1. Introduction
The conception of microbubble emission boiling (MEB) was first proposed by Inada et al. [1–4] in 1981, where they observed a great amount of microbubbles emitted from the collapsing vapor bubbles during subcooled pool boiling experiments on a circular copper-base plate. This phenomenon, occurring during the process of transition boiling after reaching critical heat flux (CHF), showed that the wall superheat increased slightly with a significant rise in heat flux, indicating MEB possessed high heat transfer efficiency and great potential in two-phase heat transfer. They also stated that the rapid condensation and collapse of the vapor bubbles enhanced the solid-liquid convection by frequently impacting the heating surface with subcooled fluid, which
substantially increased the heat dissipation capacity. These findings prompted further research into the performance and characteristics of MEB in both pool boiling and flow boiling. In pooling boiling, Tange et al. [5,6] conducted tests on a thin wire under various subcooling conditions, and concluded that the minimum required subcooling for MEB was 30–40 K, with emitted bubble size of 30 μm diameter. Ando et al. [7] investigated the MEB performance on horizontal heating surfaces (5 mm and 10 mm in diameter), and observed that the vapor collapse drove the nearby subcooled working fluid towards the heating surface, which significantly enhanced the convection field around the heating area. This heat transfer mechanism is distinct from the convection in nucleate boiling regime. Tang et al. [8, 9] studied the effects of noncondensable gas on heat transfer
* Corresponding authors. E-mail addresses: liqiang@njust.edu.cn (Q. Li), xuemeichen@njust.edu.cn (X. Chen).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ijhmt
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2024.126506 Received 22 August 2024; Received in revised form 11 November 2024; Accepted 25 November 2024
International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
Available online 30 November 2024
0017-9310/© 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.


 characteristics and bubble/vapor behaviors of MEB, and found that it can not only elongate the transition from nucleate boiling to MEB and increase the volume of emitted microbubbles during MEB, but also mitigate the liquid-vapor interface oscillation and thus weakened the heat transfer capacity of MEB. They also conducted numerical simulation and showed that the intense Marangoni convection near the liquid-vapor interface could cause interfacial waves and further led to the condensation and collapse of the vapors. Later on, Tang et al. [10] identified three typical interfacial phenomena during MEB: partial collapse at vapor edges, partial collapse at the vapor top and intense interface shock without collapse, stating that the minimum subcooling for initiating MEB with water as working fluid was 20 K, and the frequency of evaporation-collapse cycle of vapors was higher than the departure frequency of nucleated bubbles, significantly enhancing the heat dissipation. Unno et al. [11] found that a layer of hydrophilic black chrome plating on copper maintained the properties of heating surface without affecting MEB characteristics. Kobayashi et al. [12] analyzed the vapor behaviors during MEB, noting the synchronous oscillation of vapor over the heating surface after initiation of MEB even with increasing heat flux. Tang et al. [13] visualized the instantaneous velocity of the liquid around the vapors in MEB using high-speed two-phase particle image velocimetry, demonstrating that the oscillating flow over the heating area triggered by bubble oscillation played a significant role in the heat transfer process. In terms of flow boiling, Hirono et al. [14] investigated the MEB
characteristics on a copper plate with the flow area of 20 × 10 × 2 mm3 (length × width × height) under various subcooling and flow velocities conditions. They found that MEB did not occur at subcooling lower than 40 K, was unstable at 50 K, but firmly established over 70 K. Additionally, MEB was absent at very low flow velocity, even with ultra-high subcooling (0.1 m/s at 80 K). Suzuki et al. [15,16] performed flow
boiling tests on a 20 × 50 mm2 heating surface in a rectangular channel (24 mm wide, 5 mm high).They reported that MEB was easier to be triggered at higher flow velocity and larger subcooling, and recorded one cycle of bubble nucleation, growth, collapse and disappearance at 300 fps during the MEB regime. Tange et al. [5] conducted flow boiling experiments with various channel height (0.2, 0.5 and 1 mm) in a rectangular channel (50 mm long, 1 mm wide). They captured images of rippling bubbles surrounding with tiny bubbles (diameter less than 50 μm), indicating the occurrence of MEB. Kumagai et al. [17] tested flow
MEB in a flow area of 10 × 5 × 6 mm3 (length × width × height) in both horizontal and vertical arrangement, classifying the MEB into three
types: stormy MEB I (low wall superheat), stormy MEB II (high wall superheat) and calm MEB (observed only in vertical placement of heating surface). Wang et al. [18] reported MEB in a silicon microchannel with a hydraulic diameter of 155 μm, showing bubble collapse without oscillation. This phenomenon was widely observed in conventional subcooled boiling in microchannels [19–21], where the nucleated bubbles broke into small bubbles due to uneven interfacial stress on upper and lower surfaces [22]. Based on the previous studies above, it can be concluded that high liquid subcooling and high heat flux are two necessary conditions for initiating MEB; flow velocity is additionally required for flow boiling as the liquid subcooling would decrease rapidly at low flow velocity. The critical heat transfer mechanism of MEB is the violent thermal convection from the heating surface to subcooled liquid caused by highfrequency vapor oscillation, with microbubble emission being more of an accompanying phenomenon. Therefore, MEB is promising to enhance two-phase heat dissipation as an advanced heat transfer mechanism; while microchannel cooling technology [23,24] is effective to meet the ever-increasing heat dissipation demand of miniaturized electronic devices as an advanced heat transfer carrier. Therefore, integrating microchannel cooling technology with MEB is expected to significantly improve heat transfer performance. However, investigations on MEB in microchannels are rare, as most previous studies on MEB during flow boiling were conducted in macro-sized channels, observing bubble collapse rather than frequent bubble oscillation. This might be due to two main reasons: (i) achieving the required subcooling of MEB in the mainflow of microchannels is difficult as the flow area is surrounded by the heating walls, unlike the pool boiling or flow boiling in macro-size channels with only one heated wall; (ii) the violent oscillation of vapor-liquid interface can easily lead to channel dryout and terminate MEB at the initial stage due to the restriction effect of the microchannel walls. In this regard, MEB might be triggered in microchannels if these two concerns are properly addressed. “Open microchannel” refers to a new type of microchannel with an open gap on the top of fins to interconnect all channels [25,26]. The extra space provided by the open gap allows the mainflow to increase subcooling by moving away from the heating surfaces, and the generated bubbles and vapors tended to migrate into the open gap due to buoyancy, preventing channel blockage and local dryout [20,27–29]. Thus, MEB might be successfully triggered in open microchannels. In this work, a series of subcooled flow boiling experiments were carried out in cooper-based plain and lasered open microchannels with various

 flow fluxes, inlet subcooling and open gap heights using DI water as the working fluid. The aim was to explore the triggering conditions, bubble behaviors, wall temperature characteristics, heat transfer mechanisms and durability of MEB in microchannels.
2. Experimental setup and procedures
2.1. Experimental flow loop
The schematic diagram of the closed experimental flow loop is shown in Fig. 1. DI water was used as working fluid and was degassed through a 2-hour continuous boiling process before the experiments. The entire flow loop was sealed after filling the storage tank with degassed DI water to prevent the influence of the solvable gas on the experimental and visualization results. Note that the three ports of gas outlet, liquid supply and liquid discharge were kept closed during the experiments. A 90-μm filter was placed at the front of the centrifugal
pump to prevent tiny particles from entering the pump, and an electromagnetic flowmeter was arranged at the downstream of the pump. A plate heat exchanger serving as a preheater which accessed to a thermostatic water bath was used at the inlet position of the test section to obtain the setting inlet temperatures; likewise, another plate heat exchanger working as a condenser which connected to a water chiller was used at the outlet position to cool the heated water flowing out of the test section. A direct current power supply was applied to provide heating power to the test section. Two pairs of thermocouples and pressure transducers were positioned at the inlet and outlet locations, respectively. A high-speed camera assisted with a cold light source was used to capture the bubble/vapor behaviors and flow pattern transitions during two-phase flow. All data (temperatures, pressures, flow fluxes, input heating powers) were collected by data acquisition equipment and recorded on a computer, and the visualization videos were directly gathered by the computer. All 316 stainless-steel tubes in the flow loop were covered with thermal insulation cotton to reduce heat loss. In addition, Table 1 gave the specific details of the manufacturers/models of the instruments and equipment utilized in the experiments.
2.2. Test section
Fig. 2 gives the 3D explosive picture of the test section, including the cross-section view, top view and side view of the rectangular open microchannels. The copper-made microchannel heat sink was connected to the copper heating block through an extended area with length (Lhs) ×
width (Whs) of 28 × 28 mm2, which was deemed as the heating area as well. The heat sink had 46 rectangular channels with height (Hf) × width
(Wmc) of 1 × 0.3 mm2 and the fins width (Wf) of 0.3 mm. The detailed geometric dimensions of the microchannels and thermocouple hole distribution were provided in Table 2. It was worth mention that there

Note that the microchannel heat sink and the heating block were manufactured as a single unit instead of being welded together. Nine cartridge heaters were inserted inside the heating block to generate heating power, with each heater capable of a maximum heating power of 500 W at 220 V. The output power of the heaters could be regulated by adjusting the voltage. A stainless-steel cover and a Pyrex glass cover (a type of heat-resistant glass) were placed over the microchannel heat sink, two O-type fluorine rubber rings (not shown in the figure) were positioned around the inlet and outlet holes of the glass cover under the stainless-steel cover to ensure a sealing status, and a fluorine-rubber-made sealing gasket was arranged around the microchannels to prevent liquid leakage. Seven mica plates were used to cover the heating block in order to reduce the heat loss and two pieces of Teflon bricks were arranged outside the mica plates for fixation and thermal insulation. The whole section was fixed by four horizontal and eight vertical bolts, as shown in the Fig. 2 (a). The high light transmittance of the Pyrex glass cover enabled visualization, with the visualization area located in the center downstream of the microchannels, as indicated in Fig. 2 (c). Seven thermocouples were inserted into the heat sink, reaching the middle central plane of the flow area from the holes on the side. The optical microscopic images of the top fin walls and bottom microchannel walls at various magnifications were illustrated in Fig. 3. It was observed that the surfaces of both the top and bottom walls were smooth with only slight traces from the milling process. The colored altitudinal images further demonstrated the flatness of the microchannels and the precision of the geometric dimensions.
2.3. Experimental procedure
All thermocouples and pressure sensors were calibrated prior to the experiments; the electromagnetic flowmeter and centrifugal pump were calibrated by the manufactures. Degassed DI water was used as the working fluid. The pump was firstly switched on and regulated to the setting flow fluxes (G = 200, 300 and 400 ml/min) according to the flowmeter readings. In the meanwhile, the thermostatic water bath connected to the preheater was turned on and set to specific temperatures until the inlet thermocouple readings reached the expected ones (Tinlet of 5, 25, 45, 65 and 85 ◦C). Note that the flowmeter readings might decline slightly during the experimental process due to the increased flow resistance with the increase of heating power, and the pump was thus adjusted manually to maintain constant flow fluxes. The provided heating power was added stepwise by gradually increasing the input voltage (10 V at a time during single-phase region and 2 V a time during two-phase region), along with the corresponding current. All data were recorded at 0.5 s intervals for over 120 s when steady state was reached, which was defined as the maximum transient temperature fluctuations of less than 0.2 ◦C and pressure fluctuations of less than 0.2 kPa within 300 s following each increase in input heating power. The average values of the recorded data were used as valid data of certain working conditions for further analysis. The high-speed camera was used to capture the bubble/vapor behaviors and flow pattern transitions at 10,000 frames per second (fps) during the two-phase flow region. After completing a set of tests, the pump was kept running for 1 h to cool the heated microchannel heat sink until it reached the room temperature to prevent potential accidents. Note that the plain and lasered open microchannels went through exactly the same experimental procedures.

 3. Data reduction
3.1. Heat transfer ratio
The total input power given by the DC power (Qinput) was calculated by the setting voltage (U) and consequent current (I):
Qinput = UI (1)
During the flow boiling experiments, the input power was consumed in two forms: heating power provided to the microchannel heat sink and subsequently absorbed by the flowing fluid, and heat loss caused by thermal conduction or radiation to the Teflon housings, tubes, stainlesssteel cover or other fittings and eventually dissipated to the room environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to estimate the heat loss of the test section during the experimental process. In this work, a commonly used method named heat transfer ratio (r) [30–33] was applied to compute the heat loss of the experiments during two-phase flow boiling, based on the heat loss obtained during the single-phase flow under certain working conditions by the energy conservation law. This approach was necessary as directly computing the energy conversion during two-phase region was extremely complicated. In
other words, the single-phase heat transfer ratio was firstly calculated and then the ratio was applied to the two-phase region for further data reduction, as heat transfer efficiency remained steady under the same working conditions. Note that the heat loss during single-phase flow was directly obtained by the energy conservation law. The effective heating power absorbed by the fluid during single-phase flow (Qeff,s) was calculated by:
Qeff,s = MCp(Toutlet Tinlet) (2)
where M is mass flow rate, Cp is specific heat of DI water, Tinlet and Toulet are temperatures at inlet and outlet positions. Therefore, the heat transfer ratio (r) during single-phase region under certain working condition was calculated by:
r = Qeff,s
Qinput,s (3)
and the effective heating power absorbed by the fluid during two-phase flow (Qeff,t) could be obtained by the following formula:
Qeff,t = rQinput (4)
Note that the heat transfer ratios under each working condition was calculated and used independently. The estimated heat transfer ratios of the two tested microchannel heat sinks in this work with all working conditions have been given in Table 3 below.
3.2. Heat transfer characteristics and pressure drop
The effective heat flux (qeff) of the microchannel heat sink was given by:
qeff = Qeff
WhsLhs (5)
where Whs × Lhs is the heat transfer area of the heat sink (28 × 28 mm2). The bottom wall temperatures of the microchannels (Tw,i, i =1, 2, 3, 4, 5) at corresponding locations (right above the probes of the thermocouples) were calculated by:
Tw,i = Ti qeff
λc x (6)
where Ti (i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) is the reading values of the thermocouples, λc refers to the thermal conductivity of copper and x represents the distance from the thermocouples’ probes to the bottom wall of the microchannels, which is 1.2 mm. The two-phase heat transfer coefficient (htp) at the most downstream location was obtained by the following formula:
htp = qeff
Tw,5 Tsat (7)
where Tsat is the saturation temperature of the working fluid, which varies with the absolute pressure. The temperature data at the most downstream location, Tw,5, was used because boiling primarily occurred at the downstream area of the microchannels in subcooled flow boiling, and such method has been widely applied in previous studies [28, 34–36]. In this work, the saturation temperatures of DI water are independently determined by consulting the absolute pressure-saturation temperature table based on the average outlet absolute pressures under each working condition, as provided in Table 4. In addition, the wall superheat (ΔTsat) of the most downstream position of the microchannel heat sink was acquired by:
ΔTsat = Tw,5 Tsat (8)
The pressure drop (ΔP) of the microchannel heat sink was obtained by the difference of inlet (Pin) and outlet (Pout) measured pressure:
ΔP = Pin Pout (9)

 3.3. Uncertainties

4. Results of plain open microchannels
4.1. Triggering criterion of MEB
Establishing the triggering criterion of MEB during flow boiling is of crucial importance due to the lack of prior research on MEB in open microchannels. Although MEB was primarily characterized by microbubble emission, its occurrence should not omit the underlying mechanisms. In this work, the criterions for the occurrence of MEB are defined by the simultaneous presence of the following three conditions: (i) working conditions: high subcooling and high heat flux; (ii) symbolic phenomena: abundant microbubbles; (iii) heat transfer mechanism: rapid and periodical oscillation of nucleated bubbles or vapors that cause the subcooled mainflow to impact the heating surface.
4.2. Boiling curves and flow maps
Flow boiling experiments were first performed in plain open microchannels at three inlet temperatures (Tinlet = 5, 25 and 45 ◦C) with
an open gap height (Hg) of 0.3 mm (Fig. 4 (a-c)), and at Tinlet = 5 ◦C with Hg of 1 mm (Fig. 4 (d)) under various flow fluxes. The boiling curves were shown in Fig. 4. In general, all curves rose gently at the beginning,

Such characteristic of wall temperature drop with the initiation of MEB has been widely reported, not only in studies of flow boiling in microchannel [18] but also in experimental results of pool boiling on heated planar surfaces [3,10,12,38,39], as well as the phenomenon of barely increased wall superheat despite a continuous rise in heat flux during the MEB phase. With the further increase of heat flux, the wall superheat became more sensitive to heat flux, indicating the occurrence of flow reversal and an unstable two-phase flow state. Note that the inlet temperature of 25 ◦C was considered as the normal working condition as the industrial coolants were usually utilized in normal temperature (~25 ◦C). Therefore, MEB was able to be triggered by increasing the inlet subcooling compared to Tinlet = 25 ◦C. However,
MEB was absent with the decrease of inlet subcooling (Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm in Fig. 4 (c)), revealing insufficient subcooling; MEB was likewise absent with the increase of open gap height (Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm in Fig. 4 (d)), which might be ascribed to the weaker condensation effect from a reduced liquid temperature gradient in the vertical flow area [39], as the triggering of MEB required a significantly greater condensation effect on the top of the bubbles/vapors compared to the evaporation effect on the heating surface. As a result, both condensation and evaporation proceeded simultaneously to cause violent oscillation of the liquid-vapor interfaces with lots of tiny emission bubbles. Under these two working conditions, the flow pattern in open microchannel
directly transitioned to reverse and rewet flow after bubbly flow, as shown in the figures. A slight drop of wall superheat at the onset of flow reversal which was attributed to the contribution of thin liquid film evaporation to the overall heat transfer process at the beginning of reverse and rewet flow [29,40] besides nucleate boiling. As the heat flux further increased, nucleate boiling was suppressed and the wall superheat was thus increased rapidly thereafter. To clearly analyze the effect of open gap heights (vacant space above the heating surface) of microchannels to the flow boiling process, boiling curves of plain microchannels at Tinlet = 25 ◦C under various flow fluxes with two open gap heights (Hg = 0.3 and 1 mm) were drawn respectively in Fig. 5. The CHF points, defined as the condition at which nucleate boiling can no longer be maintained and steady flow reversal occurred in microchannels, were indicated in the figures. During single-phase flow and early stage of flow boiling, the wall superheats were higher for Hg = 1 mm compared to Hg = 0.3 mm at the same heat flux and certain flow fluxes. This was because the larger gap height decreased the flow velocity and therefore reduced heat transfer efficiency. After the onset of nucleate boiling, the curves at certain flow fluxes became closer as the phase-change heat transfer dominated the heat dissipation instead of liquid convection. As stated in previous paragraph, MEB was triggered with Hg = 0.3 mm but absent with Hg = 1 mm, and the wall superheats of Hg = 0.3 mm were greatly lower than Hg = 1 mm during MEB. [39] that the stronger condensation effect within restricted space played an important role to the initiation of MEB and the spatial restriction above heating area (Hg = 0.3 mm) could trigger MEB prior to CHF. The flow pattern maps of the plain open microchannels under various working conditions were provided in Fig. 6 for clarity. The flow patterns were divided into four stages: single-phase flow, conventional bubbly flow, MEB flow as well as reverse and rewet flow. Note that the flow pattern of reverse and rewet flow was characterized by periodic and alternate flow reversal and liquid rewetting, as described in previous studies [29,32,40], which was an unstable stage during flow boiling. It can be indicated from the flow pattern maps that the heat flux interval between single-phase flow and reverse and rewet flow was narrowed
with the increase of inlet temperatures, as the required heat flux to induce flow reversal decreased at lower liquid subcooling with a given open gap height. Oppositely, at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, increasing the open gap height widened the heat flux interval because the additional space at higher Hg alleviated the blocking effect of large vapor in microchannels, delaying the occurrence of flow reversal.
4.3. Characteristics of MEB flow
To further understand the characteristics of MEB during flow boiling in plain open microchannels, sequential images at the MEB initiation moment at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, G = 300 ml/min, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff = 1436
kW/m2 were given in Fig. 7, with a corresponding video provided in supplementary material (video 1, played at 100 fps). Initially (at 0 ms), conventional bubbly flow dominated the flow pattern with several
Fig. 10. Sequential images of MEB flow in plain microchannels at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff = 1436 kW/m2.
Q. Zhao et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
10


 nucleating bubbles. Subsequently, bubbles tended to coalesce into larger bubbles and vapor films (at 57.7 ms), indicating that the flow driving force could no longer surpass the evaporation driving force and thus leading to an unstable bubbly flow. At 204.8 ms, the vapor film primarily flowed on the top of fins, pushing the liquid to flow backward or temporally flow at bottom of the channels to form flow stratification (a phenomenon reported in previous studies [27,29]). The occasional bubble nucleation in the bottom liquid can also be observed in this stage. The residual liquid in the channels evaporated completely after ~300 ms, leading to the dryout in the microchannels. The increase in evaporation driving force was then terminated, allowing for the accumulation of the inlet flow driving force. Eventually, the balance was broken at 612.5 ms, with the liquid flowing into the microchannels and rewetting the heating surfaces. The MEB was then initiated with a large number of nucleated bubbles generating simultaneously. It can be obviously observed that compared to the snapshots at 0 ms and 816.6 ms, the density of nucleating bubbles during MEB was greatly higher than that during conventional bubbly flow, significantly enhancing two-phase heat transfer efficiency and distinctly dropping the wall superheat, as described in the boiling curves. Note that this kind of flow reversal occurred only once at the initiation moment of MEB, making a transition from conventional bubbly flow to MEB flow. The absence of continued flow reversal was attributed to the suppression of bubble coalescence, with smaller nucleated bubbles condensing before forming a vapor film. In addition, the reason for the non-triggered MEB before flow reversal was that the one-time reversal brought a large amount of fragmented bubbles/vapors, which provided dense nucleating bubbles. This is in contrast to conventional bubbly flow, which lacked sufficient vapor
composition to create extensive phase-change sites. The instantaneous variations of inlet/outlet temperatures, bottom wall temperatures and inlet/outlet pressures at the MEB initiation moment in plain open microchannels were also provided in Fig. 8. In can be seen that there was a sudden increase of the inlet temperature (about 0.48 ◦C) at the MEB initiation moment, which then became stabilized soon after, corresponding to the one-time flow reversal described in Fig. 7. Similarly, a rapid rise of outlet temperature was recorded at the MEB initiation moment; however, the temperature was increased ~1.25 ◦C after the occurrence of MEB, which proved the enhancement of heat transfer capacity of the microchannels, i.e., more heating power was able to be absorbed by the flowing fluid under the same working condition. The bottom wall temperatures (Tw,1-Tw,5) of the microchannel
simultaneously dropped within 1 s (2.02 ◦C for Tw,1, 2.65 ◦C for Tw,2,
4.45 ◦C for Tw,3, 5.72 ◦C for Tw,4 and 3.46 ◦C for Tw,5) after MEB occurred, due to the enhanced heat transfer performance. However, sudden increases of Tw,1 and Tw,2 were also recorded prior to the onset of MEB as shown in Fig. 8 (c), which correspond to the transient dryout of the microchannels during the one-time flow reversal. Such phenomena of temperature increase have been commonly observed in pool boiling MEB experiments on heated planar surfaces in previous studies [2,5,7, 41] during the stage of transition process before MEB initiation. Moreover, a peak was also observed in the inlet pressure data; while the average values of inlet and outlet pressures were steady, the fluctuation of these pressures were alleviated after MEB, as evidenced by the decreased standard deviations (σ) of the data, demonstrating that the flow instability was weakened (from a state that flow reversal was about to transition to stable MEB flow).

 Fig. 9 illustrated the variation in bottom wall temperatures distribution of plain microchannels at the onset of MEB with G = 300 ml/min,
Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm, qeff = 1436 kW/m2 and G = 300 ml/min,
Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm, qeff = 1834 kW/m2. The abscissa axis represented the distance from the inlet to the locations of Tw,i (i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5). It was shown in the figures that before the initiation of MEB, the wall temperatures showed a noticeable increase from Tw,1 to Tw,3, followed by a slight increase from Tw,3 to Tw,5. This was attributed to the boiling process, which achieved higher heat dissipation primarily in the downstream of the microchannels rather than the upstream. However, after the initiation of MEB, the values of Tw,4 were significantly lower than neighboring Tw,3 and Tw,5. This reduction was due to that the
location of Tw,4 was at the center of MEB area, where the violent and periodical impact of subcooled mainflow driven by frequent bubble growth and condensation cycles greatly enhanced the heat transfer process. Such similar temperatures distribution was also reported by Inada et al. [1] and Horiuchi et al. [38] during MEB in pool boiling experiments. The sequential images of MEB flow in plain open microchannels after initiation were shown in Fig. 10, the corresponding video was provided in supplementary materials (video 2, played at 50 fps) as well. It can be indicated that the bubbles grew and condensed periodically at a frequency of 20–30 Hz. For example, at 0 ms, dense tiny bubbles were observed in the channels. These tiny bubbles grew and oscillated simultaneously (at 23.2 ms), inducing the subcooled mainflow to hit the heating surfaces (can be seen more clearly in the video). During this process, many tiny bubbles were generated through rapid condensation. At 38.7 ms, the grown bubbles had condensed, coexisting with the tiny bubble. This periodic process of dense bubble growth and condensation was a prominent feature of MEB in the open microchannels, and the rapid and sustained impact of the subcooled liquid on the channel walls was the key mechanism for the enhancement of heat transfer capacity.

 Fig. 11. The MEB states during the flow boiling process were circled and the transitions to reverse and rewet flow were noted as well. For all curves, the htp was high at the beginning of the flow boiling as the wall superheat was relatively low. A slight drop in htp was observed with the increase of heat flux because the generated bubbles and vapors temporarily blocked the nucleation sites and suppressed the phase-change process. It can be seen in Fig. 11 (a) and (b) that the heat transfer coefficient sharply increased after the initiation of MEB (with a maximum increase of 77.8 % at G = 200 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm), which was ascribed to the denser nucleation sites, faster nucleation rate of bubbles, and the rapid impact of the subcooled mainflow on the channel walls. Similarly, Wang and Cheng [18] reported that the heat transfer coefficient of microchannel increased with the initiation of MEB and remained at its maximum during MEB region. In contrary, other studies [1,10,12] declared the heat transfer coefficients at the onset of MEB were lower than those observed in nucleate boiling phase during pool boiling on heated planar surfaces, due to the greatly increased wall superheat during the transition process. This highlighted the distinct characteristics of the MEB process in pool boiling and flow boiling. However, after the onset of flow reversal in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), the curves showed a significant downward trend due to the fact that MEB with ultra-high heat dissipating capacity was terminated, and alternate bubble nucleation and thin liquid film evaporation dominated the heat transfer. On the contrary, in Fig. 11 (c) and (d), where MEB was not triggered, htp exhibited a slight increase after the occurrence of flow reversal. This was due to the combined effects of bubble nucleation and liquid film evaporation contributing to the heat transfer, as well as the short duration of dryout during the initial stage of reverse and rewet flow. Note that the htp values at the onset of flow reversal in Fig. 11 (a) and (b) were generally greater than those in the last working conditions before MEB occurred, which was consistent with the slight increase of
htp in Fig. 11 (c) and (d). In addition, as shown in Fig. 11 (a), (c) and (d), the htp curves exhibited the tendency to shift rightward with the increase of flow flux, which indicated that higher flow flux primarily elevated the required heat flux for specific flow pattern and the flow patterns dominated the htp; in other words, htp was insensitive to the variation of flow flux under these working conditions. However, at ultra-high inlet subcooling (Fig. 11 (b)) during MEB flow, htp was significantly increased with increasing flow flux, demonstrating that the htp of MEB was more sensitive to flow flux at lower triggering temperature.
4.5. Pressure drop
The pressure drop (ΔP) versus effective heat flux (qeff) of plain open microchannels at various working conditions was shown in Fig. 12. It was well-known that the total pressure drop in microchannels consisted of the frictional pressure loss and vapor acceleration pressure drop. In general, the pressure drop remained stable before the onset of flow reversal. This is because the vapor acceleration loss caused by the generated bubbles/vapors increased with heat flux, while the frictional loss decreased as the viscosity of DI water decreased with rising temperature. Sharp increases of ΔP were recorded after the flow pattern transitioned to reverse and rewet flow as illustrated in all figures, which was subjected to the significant increase of vapor acceleration pressure loss required to flush out the vapors. Fig. 12 (a) and (b) indicated that the occurrence of MEB had little effect on the total pressure drop, demonstrating that MEB flow in open microchannels did not require extra pumping power compared to conventional nucleate bubbly flow, which was consistent with the pressure drop results of Tange et al. [5,6] in the minichannel. Additionally, the pressure drop typically increased with the increase of flow flux (i.e., addition of liquid-solid friction) and inlet subcooling (i.e., increase of liquid viscosity), but decreased with the rise of open gap height.
4.6. Durability
It can be concluded from above sections that MEB flow in open microchannels occurred after a one-time flow reversal (as bubbly flow can no longer be maintained) and appeared to be a transition stage between bubbly flow and reverse and rewet flow. Therefore, the investigation of durability and stability of MEB was of essential importance. In this work, the wall temperatures of the microchannels were persistently recorded when MEB was first initiated at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet =
25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff = 1436 kW/m2. This working condition remained unchanged during the durability test, and the variation of Tw,5 with time for 4000 s was plotted in Fig. 13. It can be seen the temperature increased slightly (0.6–0.7 ◦C) within ~3600 s after MEB was triggered, during which a one-time flow reversal was recorded as circled in the figure. However, the stable stage of MEB was terminated at ~3750 s when the flow pattern suddenly switched to reverse and rewet flow, in which regime the wall temperatures fluctuated significantly. These results indicated that the MEB flow exhibited weak durability over a longterm flow boiling experiment in plain open microchannels. The realization of long-term durability of MEB in microchannels would be more promising for the real applications.
5. Results of lasered open microchannels
5.1. Laser process and characterization
As discussed in Section 4, MEB was initiated as a transition flow pattern after a one-time flow reversal at high liquid subcooling and relatively high heat flux in plain open microchannels, but its durability was insufficient for long-term applications. A key problem was the lack of vapor composition during conventional bubbly flow to provide sufficient phase-change sites. In other words, MEB required to be triggered with a considerable dispersive vapor in channels, which was introduced
Fig. 14. (a) Laser process, (b) optical microscopic images and (c) colored altitudinal images of partial lasered open microchannel.
Q. Zhao et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
13


 through the flow reversal. One common method to increase vapor composition in two-phase flow was creating extra artificial nucleation sites, and the laser ablation method has been proven to be an effective way to achieve this goal [42]. Therefore, in this part, the bottom wall of the plain open microchannel in middle downstream area was laser-treated using a laser machine (PLS6MW, Universal Laser System, Scottsdale, AZ, USA; 40 W, laser wavelength = 1.06 μm) with applied power of 40 W and scanning speed of 0.2 m/s for 5 passes. The grid-like
laser area (9.9 × 9.9 mm2) was shown in Fig. 14 (a), in which the black regions represented the scanning area (width of 0.3 mm each) and the
white ones were the intervals (width of 0.3 mm). The optical microscopic images and colored altitudinal images were given in Fig. 14 (b) and (c), respectively. These images showed that lattice-like pits (depth of ~100 μm) were created on the bottom walls of channels, while the top wall of fins were unaffected by the laser process, as viewed by adjusting the laser focus position. Flow boiling experiments would be conducted in the lasered open microchannels and the results would be compared to the plain one under the same working conditions. Note that the only difference between the lasered and plain microchannels was the laser treatment.
Fig. 15. Boiling curves of lasered microchannels with G = 300 ml/min at (a) Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (b) Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (c) Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (d) Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm; (e) Tinlet = 65 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (f) Tinlet = 85 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm.
Q. Zhao et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
14


 5.2. Boiling curves
The boiling curves of lasered open microchannels (in green) at various working conditions were plotted in Fig. 15 and compared with the plain open microchannel (in red). Using a flow flux of 300 ml/min as a representative example, the curves illustrated that the wall superheat of lasered microchannels was slightly lower than that of the plain one under the same effective heat flux during single-phase stage, which indicated a slight enhancement in convective heat transfer capacity due to the lasered microstructures. Moreover, as circled in Fig. 15 (a-d), MEB was initiated at the beginning of two-phase boiling not only at Tinlet = 25
◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm (Fig. 15 (a)) and Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm (Fig. 15 (b)),
but also at Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm (Fig. 15 (c)) and Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm (Fig. 15 (d)), where MEB was absent in the plain microchannels. Furthermore, MEB also occurred at Tinlet = 65 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm but not
observed at Tinlet = 85 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm as drawn in Fig. 15 (e) and (f). This demonstrated that the required liquid subcooling and temperature gradient in the vertical direction for MEB initiation were both reduced in the lasered microchannels. Consequently, the initiation heat fluxes for MEB were lower and the heat flux interval of MEB was extended as well.
Therefore, the wall superheats of lasered microchannels were greatly reduced (maximumly 5.4 ◦C ~ 10.4 ◦C at various working conditions) in the two-phase regime under the same heat flux. Note that MEB can be triggered with the initiation of flow boiling, as sufficient artificial nucleation sites created by laser process were able to provide sufficient dispersive vapor to activate MEB. In contrast to plain open microchannels, the nucleate boiling and one-time flow reversal were absent, i. e., the transformation process did not occur and therefore the temperature drop was not observed. The flow pattern of lasered microchannel transitioned to reverse and rewet flow with the further increase of heat flux, which was similar to the plain microchannels.
5.3. Characteristics of MEB flow
Sequential images of MEB flow in lasered open microchannels under the conditions of G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff =
1488 kW/m2 (almost identical to those shown in Section 4.3) were provided in Fig. 16 for an example. The corresponding video for this operating condition was provided as supplementary materials (video 3, played at 50 fps) for further clarity. The images revealed that MEB
Fig. 16. Sequential images of MEB flow in lasered microchannels at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff = 1488 kW/m2.
Q. Zhao et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
15


 mainly occurred within the lasered area, while conventional bubble nucleation was observed outside the region. Similar to plain microchannels, bubbles grew and condensed periodically, with dense tiny bubbles present frequently throughout all stages. This indicated that the artificial laser-treated pits successfully provided potential evaporation sites, enabling the artificial triggering of MEB in the designed area.
5.4. Two-phase heat transfer coefficient
The two-phase heat transfer coefficient (htp) of lasered open microchannels (in green) under various working conditions were plotted in
Fig. 17, alongside the results of plain microchannels under similar conditions (in red) for comparison. The MEB regions were circled in the figure. The heat transfer coefficient of laser microchannels significantly outperformed that of the plain one for all working conditions (the average htp were increased by 164 % at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; 260
% at Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; 155 % at Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and
167 % at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm), which was due to the earlier initiation of MEB with lower required wall superheat and effective heat flux, i.e., MEB in the lasered microchannels was activated at lower heat flux with lower wall temperature. Additionally, the artificial micro pits concentrated more heat flux, creating plenty of nucleation sites and thus
Fig. 17. Two-phase heat transfer coefficient versus effective heat flux of lasered microchannels with G = 300 ml/min at (a) Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (b) Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (c) Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (d) Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm; (e) Tinlet = 65 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; (f) Tinlet = 85 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm.
Q. Zhao et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 238 (2025) 126506
16


 enhancing the two-phase heat transfer. It was noticed that the htp curves of lasered microchannel generally showed a slight increase before the flow pattern transitioned to reverse and rewet flow, which was ascribed to the combined effects of thin liquid film evaporation and bubble nucleation. In this stage, the dense growth of bubbles within the lasered area tended to coalesce into large vapors, and the flow pattern was about to transition to reverse and rewet flow.
5.5. Durability
The durability of MEB in lasered open microchannels was investigated at G = 300 ml/min, Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and qeff = 1488
kW/m2, a working condition as same as Section 4.6. The variation of Tw,5 over a duration in 22,500 s (more than 6 h) was shown in Fig. 18. It can be observed that initially, the temperature increased obviously as
the input heating power was increased from 1440 kW/m2 to 1488 kW/
m2, but then stabilized with a relatively gentle rise. The temperature at Tw,5 increased by ~ 1 ◦C within ~10,000 s (~2.8 h) after the initial temperature increase, and remained stable from ~10,000 s to 22,500 s without any significant rise. The results indicated that flow MEB was able to function steadily in lasered open microchannels over an extended period without flow reversal, demonstrating the solid durability of MEB operation in microchannels after laser treatment.
6. Conclusion
In this work, subcooled flow boiling experiments were conducted in both copper-based plain open microchannels and lasered open microchannels by using DI water as working fluid. The investigation focused on the triggering conditions, flow characteristics, heat transfer enhancement mechanisms and durability of microbubble emission boiling (MEB). The main conclusions were listed below. (1) MEB triggering conditions:
• In plain microchannels, MEB occurred with a significant wall temperature drop at Tinlet ≤ 25 ◦C with Hg = 0.3 mm after a one-time flow reversal, which provided sufficient vapor composition to trigger MEB; however, MEB did not occurred with Hg = 1 mm, due to the weakened condensation effect from a diminished temperature gradient in vertical direction. • In lasered microchannels, MEB was triggered without flow reversal or temperature drop at Tinlet ≤ 65 ◦C with Hg = 0.3 mm, and Tinlet =
25 ◦C with Hg = 1 mm. The artificial pits created by laser ablation provided abundant nucleation sites to serve as evaporation cores, reducing the required inlet subcooling and vertical temperature gradient for MEB initiation.
(2) Heat transfer enhancement:
• The two-phase heat transfer coefficient (htp) was greatly enhanced (up to 77.8 %) during MEB regime compared to the conventional bubbly flow in plain microchannels. This enhancement was ascribed to the rapid and periodical impact of subcooled mainflow on the heating surfaces, driven by frequent bubble growth and condensation cycles. • In lasered microchannels, the htp of MEB flow was also improved compared to the plain one (average values were increased by 164 % at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm; 260 % at Tinlet = 5 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm;
155 % at Tinlet = 45 ◦C, Hg = 0.3 mm and 167 % at Tinlet = 25 ◦C, Hg = 1 mm), due to the activation of MEB at lower wall superheat and lower effective heat flux, as well as the increased density of nucleation sites provided by the laser-induced micro pits.
(3) Pressure drop:
• The occurrence of MEB had minimal effect on the inlet/outlet pressures or the total pressure drop during the flow boiling process, demonstrating that the MEB in open microchannels did not require extra driving energy to be triggered or maintained compared to conventional nucleate bubbly flow.
(4) Durability:
• In plain microchannels, MEB was maintained for only ~3750 s before transitioning to reverse and rewet flow, accompanied by dramatic temperature fluctuation. • In contrast, MEB in lasered microchannels demonstrated improved stability, which ran steadily for 22,500 s (over 6 h) without flow reversal or substantial temperature variations. The enhanced MEB stability and durability of MEB in lasered microchannels originated from the earlier activation of MEB at the onset of flow boiling, rather than as a transitional stage in plain microchannels.

title: On homogeneity of vapor bubbles’ oscillation and corresponding heat transfer characteristics and boiling sound in microbubble emission boiling (MEB)
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
abstractNote: We investigate so-called “microbubble emission boiling (MEB)” on a horizontal circular heat transfer surface in a pool via experimental approach. We indicate the correlation among the temporal and spatial behaviors of the vapor bubbles, the averaged heat transfer characteristics, and the accompanying boiling sound. We show that the vapor bubbles oscillate in a non-uniform manner in the radial direction immediately after the MEB transition. As the MEB develops by increasing the power input to the heaters, the vapor bubbles exhibit synchronous oscillation on the heat transfer surface as if they oscillate homogeneously. The homogeneity of the vapor bubble oscillation in the radial direction is quantiﬁed by the cross-correlation between the local oscillations. We illustrate signiﬁcant variations of the heat transfer characteristics and the boiling sound driven by the homogeneous vapor bubble oscillation after the transition to the fully developed MEB.

abstract
We investigate so-called “microbubble emission boiling (MEB)” on a horizontal circular heat transfer surface in a pool via experimental approach. We indicate the correlation among the temporal and spatial behaviors of the vapor bubbles, the averaged heat transfer characteristics, and the accompanying boiling sound. We show that the vapor bubbles oscillate in a non-uniform manner in the radial direction immediately after the MEB transition. As the MEB develops by increasing the power input to the heaters, the vapor bubbles exhibit synchronous oscillation on the heat transfer surface as if they oscillate homogeneously. The homogeneity of the vapor bubble oscillation in the radial direction is quantified by the cross-correlation between the local oscillations. We illustrate significant variations of the heat transfer characteristics and the boiling sound driven by the homogeneous vapor bubble oscillation after the transition to the fully developed MEB.

1. Introduction
It has been known that so-called “microbubble emission boiling (MEB)” is able to achieve higher heat flux than the critical heat flux (CHF) under subcooled boiling conditions. It is thus expected to be applied to cooling technology of high-density heating elements such as high-power electric devices. This unique phenomenon is one of the boiling regimes discovered in the early 1980’s [1–4]. The heat transfer characteristics of pool boiling on a horizontal cylindrical heat transfer surface was illustrated by varying the subcooled conditions [1]. In this new regime, it was indicated that micrometer-scale bubbles are vigorously scattered from the thin film of vapor bubbles on the heat transfer surface [2,3]. The corresponding heat transfer characteristic model was proposed by considering the mass transfer in coalesced vapor bubbles [4]. After the discovery, a number of research have been conducted to investigate their occurring condition and heat transfer characteristics with heat transfer surface of various shapes and the ambient flow conditions. This phenomenon was named as the microbubble emission boiling (MEB) by [5] due to the fact that the microbubbles are released.

It has been indicated that the onset of the MEB is accompanied by a boiling sound of much more intense than that of nucleate boiling [5–12]. Kubo and Kumagai [5] conducted a series of experiments in a forced flow parallel to the horizontal cylindrical heat transfer surface under subcooled conditions, and indicated two distinguishable types of the MEB by monitoring the boiling sound pressure: Stormy-MEB (S-MEB), which accompanies with a strong sound pressure, and Calm-MEB (C-MEB), which accompanies with a comparatively small sound pressure. It was indicated later with the same system that the S-MEB is further classified into the S-MEBs I and II; the former occurs under higher degree of subcooling Tsub with high reproducibility and the latter occurs rather unstably under lower Tsub [6]. Tsub := Tsat − T∞, where Tsat and T∞ are the saturation temperature under the system pressure and the ambient-liquid temperature, respectively. The difference of the vapor bubble behaviors in the S-MEBs and the C-MEB were illustrated by Kumagai et al. [9]. The boiling sound in the MEBs is caused by the vigorous oscillation of the vapor bubbles induced by rapid growth and condensation [10–12]. It has been also known that the subcooled boiling on a thin wire results in the emission of the microbubbles with a boiling sound of high intensity; it was indicated that the MEB does not occur in the on-wire boiling [1316] because the wall superheat Tsat := Tw − Tsat does not exhibit any significant jump as indicated in the experiments of on-plate

boiling [1–4,9–12,17]. Here Tw and Tsat denote the wall temperature (or the temperature of the heat transfer surface) and the saturation temperature in the system pressure, respectively. The investigation on the MEB has then been conducted by employing the on-plate boiling systems. One of the main characteristics of the MEB is the highfrequency oscillation of vapor bubbles on the heat transfer surface. In other words, the vapor bubbles generated on the surface do not form any large coalesced vapor bubbles as seen in the nucleate boiling regimes e.g., [18–26], but exhibit rapid growth, shrinkage due to the condensation, and collapse at several hundred Hz on the surface [10,12,17,27–30]. Ando et al. [17] illustrated in detail the vapor-bubble behaviors during the transition from the nucleate boiling to the MEB. Kawakami et al. [30] showed the correlation between the vapor bubble behaviors and the MEB transition by restricting the space on the horizontal heat transfer surface in the MEB transition regime; they indicated that the transition to the MEB is substantially suppressed and the transition to the film boiling is realized instead when the space above the heat transfer surface is limited. As for the effect on the heat transfer characteristics by the vapor bubble behaviors, Horiuchi et al. [12] measured the difference of the surface temperature in the radial direction before and after the occurrence of the MEB on a horizontal circular heat transfer surface, and depicted the correlation between the radial temperature difference and the MEB regimes; they found that the homogeneity in time and space of the vapor bubble behaviors directly correlated to the corresponding heat transfer characteristics and the induced boiling sounds. It must be noted, however, that such correlation has not been quantified yet. In this study, we pay our special attention to the spatial and temporal behaviors of the vapor bubbles on a heat transfer surface in different boiling regimes of the nucleate boiling and the MEB, and indicate the correlation concerned qualitatively and quantitatively. In particular, we measure the homogeneity of the vapor-bubble behaviors through the high-speed imaging, and monitor the accompanying boiling sounds. We then illustrate such correlations in the regimes of the nucleate boiling, the MEB transition, and the MEBs in the developing and fully developed states.
2. Experiments
The schematic of the experimental apparatus is shown in Fig. 1. This apparatus is almost the same as the one used by Horiuchi et al. [12] except the temperature measurement system for the heat transfer block. The validation of the present apparatus is conducted through the comparison of the CHF and the correlation between the heat flux on the heat transfer surface and Tsat as
Fig. 2. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of heat transfer surface after finishing with sandpaper observed in different magnification.
Fig. 3. Boiling curves under Tsub = 40 K and 60 K.
shown in Figs. 3 and 4 in Section 3.1 to the previous research [12,17]. A heat transfer block made of copper is installed at the bottom of the test vessel. The upper part of the heat transfer block is a cylinder of 10 mm in diameter, and the lower part is a rectangular. The upper end of the cylinder is used as the boiling heat transfer surface (heat transfer surface, hereafter). The heat transfer surface is settled at the same level as the bottom surface of the vessel. Before each experimental run, the heat transfer surface is polished with # 500 sandpaper (average particle size: 30.2 μm) and cleaned with acetone. The SEM (scanning electron microscope) images of the heat transfer surface are shown in Fig. 2. To measure the temperature of the heat transfer surface, K-type sheath thermocouples of 0.5 mm in outer diameter (Nippon NetsuDenki SeisakuSho Co., Japan) are inserted at the positions of 1, 3, and 5 mm from the heat transfer surface along the central axis of the rod. The measurement error of the thermocouples is less than ±1.5 K. The heat transfer block is heated by eight cartridge heaters (110V-200W, SAKAGUCHI E.H VOC CORP., Japan) inserted in the bottom block. The cylindrical part of the heat transfer block is covered by the insulator MIOLEXTM (PGX-595, Ryoden-Kasei Co., Ltd., Japan), and the bottom block and the connecting part between

The inner scales of the vessel are of 300 mm in length, 290 mm in width, and 300 mm in depth. The top of the vessel is open to the atmosphere. Windows for visualization are installed on all

The ambient temperature of the test liquid, T∞, is measured by a K-type sheath thermocouple of 1 mm in outer diameter placed at the position of 30 mm radially apart from the center of the heat transfer surface in the test vessel, which was

determined based on Inada et al. [2]. The liquid temperature is controlled using a cooling channel connected to a constant temperature bath (NCB-2510B, EYELA, Japan) and a U-shaped heater (AWA1510, HAKKO ELECTRIC CO., LTD., Japan). The experimental method is the same as in the previous paper [12], except for the measurement of the boiling sounds. Before each experimental run, the test liquid is degassed and the pool in the vessel is pre-heated by heating the heat transfer block and the U-shaped heater for more than 3 hours. After that, the temperature of the liquid, or the degree of subcooling Tsub := Tsat − T∞ is controlled to a designated value. In this study, we conduct the whole experiments under the condition of high degree of subcooling, Tsub = 40 K and 60 K, where the S-MEB is stably observed [17,28]. The heat flux at the heat transfer surface is varied by changing the voltage applied to the cartridge heaters step by step. The temperature and the heat flux at the heat transfer surface, Tw and q, respectively, are evaluated by assuming a one-dimensional heat conduction in the heat transfer block. By use of the data of the temperature at three different points, q is evaluated by applying one-dimensional Fourier’s law [12,17] as q =
