where λ is the thermal conductivity of the heat transfer block, x is the distance along the central axis from the heat transfer surface in the heat transfer block, the subscript i is the position indicator where the thermocouple is inserted (x1 = 1 mm, x2 = 3 mm, and x3 = 5 mm), N is the total number of measurement points (N = 3 in this experiment), and x and T are the averages of the measuring positions and the temperature, respectively. When we plot temporal variations of q nevertheless the temperature distribution in the rod would not be fully developed, this is referred as ‘quasi-heat flux’ q′′ [17], and is distinguished from q. Note that we obtain q′′ = q when the temperature distribution in the rod be fully developed, and the one-dimensional Fourier’s law is applicable. Suppose q′′ is decomposed to the averaged component and the deviation as q′′ = q′′ + ̂q′′; q′′ involves the error of ±5 % reflecting the error of the thermocouples. The deviation
̂q′′ is severely affected by the temporal aliasing effect due to inevitable difference between the fundamental frequency of the phenomenon of MEB and the time constant of the thermocouples. In case the periodic thermal boundary condition be applied at the heated surface with an angular frequency ω, the characteristic
penetration depth of its fluctuation δp is evaluated as δp ∼ √2κ/ω [31], where κ is the thermal diffusivity. It would be impossible to detect the precise variations of the surface temperature and heat flux because the fluctuation of high frequency of O(102 Hz) due to the vapor bubbles’ oscillation in the MEB [17] would decay within approximately 1mm. We also evaluate the quasi heat flux q′′ regardless of the development of the heat flux q [12,17,32,33]. Note that the records of the vapor bubbles’ behaviors and the boiling sound are conducted under the fully developed state for each designated condition. The behaviors of the vapor bubbles are visualized using a high-speed camera (FASTCAM APX-RS, Photron Ltd., Japan) at 10000 frames per second (fps) with back-lighting system. The boiling sound is measured by an acrylonitrile-covered microphone (ECM-CG60, Sony Corporation, Japan) immersed in the pool at 10 mm radially apart from the center of the heat transfer surface and at 50 mm above the surface. The sampling rate for the boiling sound is fixed at 44100 Hz in the present study. The boiling sound is recorded for more than 60 s. It is noted that the attenuation effect is not avoidable because of the acrylonitrile cover on the microphone; we measure the boiling sound under the same condition through the present study, and thus we limit ourselves to discuss the sound power by relative comparison.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Boiling curve
Boiling curves obtained in the present study are illustrated in Fig. 3. The figure consists of the results of multiple runs under fully developed states for Tsub = 40 K and 60 K. Each mark indicates the fully developed state under a fixed condition. One finds good reproducibility in the present system. The boiling curves for the nucleate boiling regime match despite the difference in Tsub [34,35]. The critical heat flux for Tsub = 60 K becomes greater than that for Tsub = 40 K [10,34,35]. The present experimental system is validated in terms of the CHF by comparing to those obtained in the previous research [17,28]. In the transition to the MEB, the evaluated heat flux slightly decreases despite Tsat increases. This is due to the non-uniform temperature distribution in the r direction of the heat transfer surface as indicated by Horiuchi et al. [12]. Once the MEB is realized, the heat flux significantly increases whereas Tsat almost remains under high Tsub. Such trends were surely observed in the previous studies [1–4,10,12,17,36]. Under Tsub = 40 K, Tsat for the MEB is ranged from 60 K to 80 K, which well reproduces the previous results [10,17]. By increasing Tsub, the occurrence condition in Tsat becomes lower [10], and that the fluctuation of Tsat becomes smaller than that under Tsub = 40 K. The variation of Tsat ranges less than ±5 K around Tsat = 40 K under Tsub = 60 K. Through the general features of the boiling curves, we convince ourselves our apparatus is well built and reproduces the boiling phenomena including the MEB under high degree of subcooling. In the following, we discuss the features of the MEB. Typical examples of the pseudo-‘boiling curve’ and the corresponding heat transfer coefficient h′′ = q′′/(Tw − T∞ ) =
q′′/( Tsub + Tsat ) against Tsat are illustrated in Fig. 4 (i) and (ii), respectively, under (I) Tsub = 40 K and (II) 60 K. Square mark indicates the fully developed state under a fixed condition of ‘ξi’ (ξ : a and b, i: integer): from ‘a1’ to ‘a13’ for (I), and from ‘b1’ to ‘b14’ for (II). Each developed state is evaluated under the condition that Tw becomes almost constant after varying the voltage applied
to the heaters [12,17], where Tw is the averaged value of Tw for 3 min. In this study, we wait for more than 20 min until the boiling state is sufficiently developed under the condition designated. The states for ‘a1’ to ‘a5’ correspond to the nucleate boiling regimes, and those for ‘a7’ to ‘a13’ correspond to the MEB regimes for (I) Tsub = 40 K. Whereas, the states for ‘b1’ to ‘b7’ correspond to the nucleate boiling regimes, and those for ‘b9’ to ‘b14’ correspond to the MEB regimes for (II) Tsub = 60 K. The states for ‘a6’ and ‘b8’ correspond to the transition regime between the nucleate boiling and the MEB under the degree of subcooling concerned. In this figure, the data between each developed state are plotted in different colors alternately to distinguish the variation easier. In each run, the experiment starts from the condition i = 1 for ‘ξi’, and q increases as the power input to the cartridge heaters (power input, hereafter) increases in the nucleate boiling regimes. When the power input is increased from each condition of ‘a5’ or ‘b7’, the
transition regime of ‘a6’ or ‘b8’ are achieved, where q′′ decreases or remains almost constant and Tsat increases as the power input increases [12,17,32]. After the developed state under the condition of ‘a7’ or ‘b9’, the MEB occurs, and q′′ increases again as the power input increases. The boiling curves for both conditions show that a typical S-MEB [12,17,27] is steadily realized. It is noted that the slope of q′′ against Tsat, or ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat, changes between the conditions of ‘a7’-‘a10’ and those of ‘a10’-‘a13’ in the MEB regimes for (I) Tsub = 40 K. This is due to the spatial inhomogeneity of the vapor bubble behaviors on the heat transfer surface as we find in the present research; we consider that this is because the

3.2. Boiling sound and vapor bubble behavior
The spectrogram of the boiling sound at each developed state under Tsub = 40 K is shown in Fig. 5. We used a 0.02-s-wide hamming window function for an arbitrary 20 s of the data measured for 1min in total at each state. The window width is chosen by referring the previous research on the high-frequency vaporbubble oscillation of O(102Hz) in the MEB [10–12,17]. In this figure, the states under the conditions from ‘a1’ to ‘a13’ correspond to those in Fig. 4 (I). In the nucleate boiling regimes (‘a1’-‘a5’), one obtains boiling sound with a wide range of frequency around 5 × 102 Hz. As the increase of q′′, the range of the peak frequency band becomes narrower and the mean frequency slightly shifts to a lower one. The sound pressure keeps moderate in these regimes. After
Fig. 6. Boiling-sound spectrogram before and after transition to MEB (between fully developed states for ‘a6’ and ‘a7’ under Tsub = 40 K as illustrated in Fig. 4).
the transition to the MEB (‘a7’-‘a9’), the sound pressure increases significantly compared to those in the nucleate boiling regimes as indicated in previous research [6,12]. The peak frequency of the boiling sound for the MEB and their sound pressure increases beyond the state under the condition of ‘a10’. The peak frequency shifts to around 3 × 102 Hz ∼ 4 × 102 Hz, and the range around the peak frequency of the sound becomes narrower. Such high frequency of the boiling sound in the MEB regimes is observed under Tsub = 60 K in this study as well, and was also reported in the previous study under high Tsub condition [10]. A magnified view of the spectrogram is illustrated below the main frame of the figure for the developed state under the condition of ‘a13’, or the fully-developed MEB state. In this state, the peak frequency arises around 4 × 102 Hz, and the intensity of the sound pressure maintains higher than that for the nucleate boiling regime. Fig. 6 indicates the spectrogram for 20 s in which the transition from the nucleate boiling to the MEB arises in a transient regime under the condition of ‘a7’, or, between the fully developed states under the conditions of ‘a6’ and ‘a7’. The spectrogram shows that the boilingsound pressure abruptly increases accompanying with the transition to the MEB, although the frequency range does not exhibit a significant variation. Note that the state under the condition of ‘a10’ where the sound pressure changes coincides with the point where the slope of q′′ against Tsat changes in Fig. 4(a). This suggests that the MEB exhibits a transition of its regime near this state as indicated by Horiuchi et al. [12]. Fig. 7(i) shows the successive images of the vapor bubbles above the heat transfer surface for the state under the condition of ‘a6’ (transition regime from the nucleate boiling to the MEB) (left), and the corresponding the spatio-temporal distribution of the image depicted at the straight monitoring line at 1mm apart vertically from the surface (right) under Tsub = 40 K. The spatiotemporal distribution is illustrated by accumulating the horizontal pixel data at the monitoring line for 0.1 s. The time series of the brightness values on the monitoring line at (α) the center (r = 0 mm) and (β) near the edge of the heat transfer surface (r = 4 mm) are shown in Fig. 7 (ii). Greater values of brightness generally correspond to the situation that the vapor bubble does not exist on the monitoring line, and vice versa. At the center of the heat transfer surface (r = 0 mm), the brightness maintains low during the passage of the vapor bubbles due to the refraction of the light irradiated from the backside of the vapor bubble. And it becomes

abruptly high after the departure of the preceding bubble and before the arrival of the following one. It must be noted that a high intensity region also appears shortly during the passage of vapor bubbles, as can be seen, for instance, around t = 0.05 s. As shown in the left panel in Fig. 7 (i), the high brightness region emerges when the light comes through the vapor bubble, where the free surface of the vapor bubble is almost smooth and whose normal vector is parallel to the incident angle of the light and the camera. It affects on the frequency analysis because it appears at about the same period as the passages of the vapor bubbles. We would thus need to be careful for measuring the frequency twice as high as the bubble departure for the nucleate boiling regimes. Near the edge of the heat transfer surface (r = 4 mm), on the contrary, the period with high intensity becomes broader. This is because the periods to capture the region outside the vapor bubbles become dominant. By the Fourier analysis of these time series of the brightness data at different positions, one can measure the spatial as well as the temporal uniformity of vapor-bubble oscillation via the spectrum of the oscillation period of the vapor-bubble growth and condensation passing through the monitoring line. Such spatio-temporal analysis applied to the developed states under the conditions of ‘a5’-‘a13’ for Tsub = 40 K is illustrated in Fig. 8. Note again that the developed states under the conditions of ‘a5’-‘a13’ in the figure correspond to those in Fig. 4 (I). At the states under the conditions of ‘a5’ and ‘a6’ in the nucleate boiling regime, a large coalesced bubble passes regularly by exhibiting the growth and the shrinkage due to the condensation. Non-uniform growth in space at the state for ‘a5’ becomes almost symmetric against the center line normal to the heat transfer surface at the state for ‘a6’. The fundamental frequency of the vapor bubble passage is of approximately 40 Hz. When one pays their attention to the developed states for ‘a7’-‘a9’ after the MEB transition, one finds major characteristics in time and space. As for the characteristics in temporal variations, there exist two major stages: The one where long-duration passages as seen in the nucleate boiling regimes (in the state for ‘a5’, for instance), and the other where intermittent passages in short periods. As for the characteristics in spatial variations, there exist three major stages: The one where the vapor bub
ble partially covers the central region of the heat transfer surface as seen in the states for ‘a5’ and ‘a6’, the other where the vapor bubble partially covers the regions near the edge of the heat transfer surface, and another where the vapor bubbles fully cover the heat transfer surface. The combination of the first and the second ones can be seen in the states for the developing MEB under the conditions of ‘a7’-‘a9’. The combination of the second and the third one becomes dominant in the states for the developed regimes of the MEB under the condition of ‘a10’. Such inhomogeneous behaviors in space of the vapor bubbles above the heat transfer surface were also detected in the previous research [12,28]. The third one by further increasing q′′ can be seen in the fully developed MEB at the states for ‘a11’-‘a13’. The vapor bubbles oscillate violently during most of the measurement time; the vapor bubbles pass the monitoring line within short and constant period. This indicates that the vapor bubbles oscillate homogeneously in time and space at a high frequency in the fully developed MEB. Similar trend is surely reproduced in the present experiments under Tsub = 60 K. Note again that one realizes higher heat fluxes than the critical heat flux whereas the wall superheat is remained lower than that for the film boiling in the MEB. The vapor bubble oscillation of O(102 Hz) must reflect that the supply of ambient liquid to the heat transfer surface is realized in such a high frequency. We find that this transition from inhomogeneous to homogeneous oscillations in space accompanying the variation of the slope of q′′ against Tsat, or
∂ q′′/∂ Tsat, in the pseudo-‘boiling curve’ after the MEB transition (Fig. 4). In other words, the spatial and temporal inhomogeneity of vapor bubble behavior has a significant effect on these heat transfer characteristics and sound field as well. At the state for ‘a5’ of the nucleate boiling regime, several peaks are observed in the range of 2 × 102 Hz ∼ 6 × 102 Hz in (i) the boiling sounds. The vapor bubble behaviors at both locations, on the other hand, show a peak frequency at around 30 Hz. Note that high frequency in the boiling sound is found to correspond to the pinching off of the vapor bubbles as seen in the frame of ‘a5’ in Fig. 8. That is, a short period between the preceding and following bubbles makes that noise. At the state for ‘a6’ of the transition from the nucleate boiling to the MEB, (i) the boiling sound shows peak frequencies in a range of 2 × 102 Hz ∼ 6 × 102 Hz and some peaks at around 40 Hz, 80 Hz, and 120 Hz. According to the vapor bubble behaviors at both of (b) r = 0 mm and (c) r = 4 mm, the peak at 40 Hz corresponds to the growth and shrinkage of the vapor bubbles over the heat transfer surface. The peak of 80 Hz in addition to that of 40 Hz reflects the bright regions in the middle of the vapor bubbles as aforementioned. Whereas for the peak at around 5 × 102 Hz, the sound is generated by the pinching off of the vapor bubbles. This can be measured from the frame of ‘a6’ in Fig. 8; the distance in time between the preceding and following vapor bubbles is of 1/500 s. Right after the MEB transition at the developed state for ‘a7’, the boiling sound consists of several peak frequencies in the range of 2 × 102 Hz ∼ 6 × 102 Hz as seen in the nucleate boiling regime. As for the vapor bubble behaviors at (ii) r = 0 mm and at (iii) r = 4 mm, the inhomogeneity in time and space, as seen at the early stage in the frame of ‘a7’ in Fig. 8, leads almost no peaks in the spectra. At the developed state for ‘a13’ of the fully developed MEB, (i) the boiling sounds have a sharp peak frequency
of 3.9 × 102 Hz, and one finds the similar peaks of 4.1 × 102 Hz in the spectra of the vapor bubble behaviors at (ii) r = 0 mm and (iii) r = 4 mm. The homogeneous behaviors of the rapid expansion and contraction by the vapor bubbles generate the boiling sound with the same frequency in this regime. In order to quantitatively evaluate the spatial non-uniformity of vapor bubble behaviors in the MEB regimes, the time lags of the vapor bubble oscillation at different points in r are evaluated. We detect the time for the first peak of the cross-correlation between the data at those measuring points, and normalize it by the fundamental period t f obtained by the autocorrelation of the time series at r = 0 mm. The time lags are evaluated under developed states: The representative value is obtained from five different sets of the temporal variation of the vapor-bubble images in total under the same condition. The index ‘ξi’ (ξ corresponds ‘a’ and ‘b’ for Tsub = 40 K and 60 K, respectively) indicates the condition under fully developed state as shown in Fig. 4 (I) and (II).
(I) Tsub = 40 K. One finds that the representative time lag between r = 0 mm and 4 mm (or, between the center and the region near the periphery of the heat transfer surface) decreases monotonically as the MEB develops as shown in the column (i). That is, the vapor bubbles oscillate with a certain phase difference over the heat transfer surface for the developing states of the MEB with relatively lower q′′. As the MEB develops by further increasing the power input to the heaters, the vapor bubbles oscillate rather synchronously with relatively higher q′′. We then pay our attention to the representative time lags between the opposite regions near the peryphery (r = ±4 mm) as shown in the column (ii). At the states for ‘a8’-‘a10’, just after the MEB transition, the representa
tive time lag t(i)
R between the opposite regions near the periphery of the vapor bubbles remains 0.3 − 0.4, which is close to the half of a cycle of the vapor oscillation. That is, one finds that the vapor bubbles oscillate alternately with a certain phase difference at
the both sides. As for the states for ‘a11’-‘a13’, on the contrary, t(i)
R exhibits a significant drop: The vapor bubbles in the opposite regions near the periphery of the heat transfer surface oscillate al
most in phase. By considering t(i)
R between r = 0 mm and 4 mm and that between r = ±4 mm, we indicate quantitatively that the vapor bubbles exhibit a homogeneous oscillation on the heat transfer surface in high frequency of the order of 102 Hz under the fully developed S-MEB. When one increases Tsub to (II) 60 K, the time lag between r = 0 mm and 4 mm exhibits a similar trend as seen in the case of (I) Tsub = 40 K; the oscillation of the vapor bubbles becomes gradually synchronous as increasing the power input to the heaters. The time lag between the opposite regions near the periphery, on the other hand, the value maintains rather large at around 0.3 − 0.4 up to the state for ‘b13’. The time lag shows a slight drop in the state ‘b14’; the synchronous behavior of the vapor bubbles is slightly realized. It is noted, however, that such highly synchronous behaviors of the vapor bubbles as seen under Tsub = 40 K are
not realized even under high heat flux at around 13 MW/m2. The present results indicate that the value of ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat remains al
most zero in the MEB regimes under Tsub = 60 K, where t(i)
R between the opposite regions near the periphery of the heat transfer surface does not exhibit a sharp drop. Because of the limitation of the present apparatus, it is not possible to input more powers to
the heaters, thus we do not obtain the trend of t(i)
R for the MEB under higher heat fluxes. In order to discuss the homogeneity of the vapor bubble oscillation under high Tsub, further research is needed. Here, we focus on the region with the states for “a7’-‘a9” in
Fig. 4 (I)-(i), where ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat < 0 even though the power input is increasing after the transition to the MEB. As quantified by this experiment (Fig. 10 (ii)), one finds a non-uniformity of vapor bubble behaviors on the heat transfer surface in this regime, which was also observed in the previous research [10]. This brings the nonuniform temperature distribution in the radial direction near the heat transfer surface, which significantly affects on the monitoring of the temperature field in this system [12]. Further, as illustrated in Fig. 8, the nucleate boiling and the MEB occur alternately under these conditions, so that the vapor-bubble oscillation and retention fluctuate in time. It is thus essential to measure the threedimensional temperature field in the vicinity of the heat transfer surface in order to evaluate precisely the heat transfer characteristics especially in the transition process to the MEB. It is emphasized that there exist series of experiments focusing on the behaviors of a single vapor bubble in the pool for the comprehensive understandings of their contribution to the heat transfer characteristics: The vapor-bubble behaviors have been investigated via two major systems; rapid local heating in subcooled liquid [3844] and vapor injection to the subcooled liquid [45–61]. The vapor injection systems bring us a great advantage to focus mainly on the condensation process of the vapor bubble in subcooled pool by extracting interaction between vapor and liquid, whereas the boiling and the rapid heating experiments involve complicated interaction among three phases of solid, liquid and vapor. In the vapor-injection experiments, one cannot measure the effect of the interaction among the vapor bubbles neither the thermal interac
9


 H. Kobayashi, M. Hayashi, K. Kurose et al. International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 188 (2022) 122564
tion between the vapor bubble and the substrate. One would need further research to unveil the physical mechanism of the MEB by dealing with the interaction of the multiple vapor bubbles oscillating in high frequency and with the accompanying the heat transfer problem within the heating surface.
4. Concluding remarks
We investigate so-called microbubble emission boiling (MEB) on a horizontal circular heat-transfer surface in a pool. We especially focus on the correlation among the temporal and spatial behaviors of the vapor bubbles, the heat transfer characteristics, and the accompanying boiling sound. We use distilled water as the test liquid and the heat transfer surface of 10 mm in diameter under Tsub = 40 K and 60 K in the atmospheric pressure conditions. The results obtained in the present work are listed below.
1. The boiling curve and the corresponding correlation of the heat transfer coefficient against Tsat are illustrated for the regimes of the nucleate boiling, the transition after the CHF to the MEB, and the S-MEB. In the MEB regime, we indicate that there are two regions: ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat 0 for the developing regimes,
and ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat > 0 for the developed regimes in the pseudo‘boiling curve.’ 2. The spectrogram of the sound field in each state shows that there exists a strong peak at 3 × 102 ∼ 4 × 102 Hz after the MEB transition. By focusing on the MEB under Tsub = 40 K, the fundamental frequency band becomes gradually smaller in average while the sound pressure increases in the region of ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat < 0 in the boiling curve. In the region of
∂ q′′/∂ Tsat > 0, the fundamental frequency and the accompa
nying sound pressure increases with q′′. 3. The spatio-temporal behavior of vapor bubbles above the heat transfer surface is visualized by high-speed imaging. We show that the vapor bubbles oscillate in a non-uniform manner in the radial direction immediately after the MEB transition. As the MEB develops by increasing the power input to the heaters, it is found that the vapor bubbles exhibit a synchronous oscillation, as if they behave as a membrane, of the order of a few to several hundred Hz in the fully developed MEB. In other words, the vapor bubbles oscillate almost simultaneously in phase over the heat transfer surface at much higher frequency than that for the nucleate boiling. The homogeneity of the vapor bubble oscillation in the radial direction is quantified by evaluating the time lag of the oscillation at different points on the heat transfer surface via the cross-correlation. 4. The Fourier spectra of the vapor bubble oscillation on the heat transfer surface and the boiling sound are obtained. In the region of ∂ q’’/∂ Tsat < 0 after the MEB transition, the spectrum for the vapor bubble oscillations exhibits a sparse distribution and does not show any significant fundamental frequency, whereas their fundamental frequency matches that of the boiling sound in the region of ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat > 0. These fundamental frequency is of about 4 × 102 Hz for Tsub = 40 K and 60 K. The homogeneous behaviors of the vapor bubbles on the heat transfer surface are the source of the boiling sounds of high frequency. 5. The variation of q′′ with ∂ q′′/∂ Tsat < 0 for the developing stages of the MEB in the boiling curve is attributed to the spatial inhomogeneity of vapor bubble oscillation on the heat transfer surface. To understand the mechanism of the MEB development and the heat transfer characteristics with high heat flux, we indispensably need further research with the accurate measurement of the three-dimensional unsteady temperature field in the heat transfer surface.

title: Transition process leading to microbubble emission boiling on horizontal circular heated surface in subcooled pool
publicationTitle: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
abstractNote: Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) produces a higher heat ﬂux than critical heat ﬂux (CHF) and therefore has been investigated in terms of its heat transfer characteristics as well as the conditions under which MEB occurs. Its physical mechanism, however, is not yet clearly understood. We carried out a series of experiments to examine boiling on horizontal circular heated surfaces of 5 mm and of 10 mm in diameter, in a subcooled pool, paying close attention to the transition process to MEB. High-speed observation results show that, in the MEB regime, the growth, condensation, and collapse of the vapor bubbles occur within a very short time. In addition, a number of ﬁne bubbles are emitted from the collapse of the vapor bubbles. By tracking these tiny bubbles, we clearly visualize that the collapse of the vapor bubbles drives the liquid near the bubbles towards the heated surface, such that the convection ﬁeld around the vapor bubbles under MEB signiﬁcantly differs from that under nucleate boiling. Moreover, the axial temperature gradient in a heated block (quasi-heat ﬂux) indicates a clear difference between nucleate boiling and MEB. A combination of quasi-heat ﬂux and the measurement of the behavior of the vapor bubbles allows us to discuss the transition to MEB.

Jun Ando a, Kazuna Horiuchi a, Takahito Saiki a, Toshihiro Kaneko a,b, Ichiro Ueno a,b,⇑
a Dept. Mechanical Engineering, Fac. Science & Technology, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda, Chiba 278-8510, Japan b Research Institute for Science & Technology, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda, Chiba 278-8510, Japan

Article history:
Received 18 December 2015 Received in revised form 5 May 2016 Accepted 11 May 2016 Available online 28 May 2016
Keywords:
Microbubble emission boiling Subcooled pool boiling Quasi-heat flux Collapse of vapor bubble Transition process
abstract
Microbubble emission boiling (MEB) produces a higher heat flux than critical heat flux (CHF) and therefore has been investigated in terms of its heat transfer characteristics as well as the conditions under which MEB occurs. Its physical mechanism, however, is not yet clearly understood. We carried out a series of experiments to examine boiling on horizontal circular heated surfaces of 5 mm and of 10 mm in diameter, in a subcooled pool, paying close attention to the transition process to MEB. High-speed observation results show that, in the MEB regime, the growth, condensation, and collapse of the vapor bubbles occur within a very short time. In addition, a number of fine bubbles are emitted from the collapse of the vapor bubbles. By tracking these tiny bubbles, we clearly visualize that the collapse of the vapor bubbles drives the liquid near the bubbles towards the heated surface, such that the convection field around the vapor bubbles under MEB significantly differs from that under nucleate boiling. Moreover, the axial temperature gradient in a heated block (quasi-heat flux) indicates a clear difference between nucleate boiling and MEB. A combination of quasi-heat flux and the measurement of the behavior of the vapor bubbles allows us to discuss the transition to MEB. Ó 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Increases in the amount of data communication traffic have led to significant increases in the amount of exhaust heat generated by servers and network devices. In addition, electronic devices such as high-performance CPUs and GPUs have been downsized and made lighter due to technological developments, but this again causes the heat density to assume an upward trend. To remove the huge amounts of heat generated by these devices, there is an immediate need for an advanced and highly effective cooling technology. Boiling with a liquid–vapor phase change and the associated latent heat can remove huge amounts of heat from such systems despite there being only small temperature differences relative to singlephase heat exchange. It must be noted, however, that there exists an upper limit on the heat flux of the boiling heat transfer, which is known as critical heat flux (CHF). So that significant attentions have been paid to understand the mechanism of CHF in order to predict and avoid such a critical situation in the cooling systems. Over the last few decades, a unique phenomenon named ‘microbubble emission boiling (MEB)’ has been attracting attention
as a means of overcoming this challenging problem. It has been indicated that MEB realizes a higher heat flux than CHF under moderate and highly subcooled conditions. Inada et al. [1–4] firstly identified this phenomenon in 1981. They carried out a series of experiments related to boiling on a horizontal heated plate in a subcooled pool. It was reported that many tiny bubbles were emitted radially with collapse of vapor bubbles on the heated surface after the onset of MEB. Fig. 1 shows a typical example of the MEB. After that, several investigations of MEB in a pool, as well as flow boiling, have been conducted. Many researchers have investigated MEB in a pool. Inada et al. [1–5] addressed the subcooled boiling of water on horizontal circular heated surfaces of 10 mm and of 15 mm in diameter. For liquid subcooling exceeding 30 K, they identified a phenomenon whereby heat flux higher than the CHF was realized with a significant increase in the wall superheat. In that boiling regime, a number of vapor bubbles were generated accompanied by a ‘boiling sound’ that differed from that of the nucleate boiling regime. They named this phenomenon ‘microbubble emission boiling.’ Shoji [6] investigated the boiling of water on a thin wire of 100–300 lm in diameter under various subcooled conditions. Although the continuous formation of minute bubbles were observed under the conditions of degree of subcooling higher than 30 K, any pronounced increase in the wall superheat was not observed as the transition to MEB

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/ijhmt


 which is generally seen in the boiling curve for boiling on a heated plate. Therefore, they could not distinguish the regime between the nucleate boiling and the MEB on the boiling curve. Tange et al. [7] also studied MEB on a thin wire of 300 lm in diameter in subcooled boiling. They illustrated the rapid growth, condensing, and collapsing processes of the vapor bubbles and heat transfer systems on the thin wire, acquiring detailed data about the behavior of the vapor bubbles through high-speed camera observations. Subsequently they concluded that MEB does not occur on thin wires [8]. Suzuki et al. [9–11] performed a series of experiments with a range of test fluids, flow rates, and heated surface configurations. They concluded that liquid subcooling mostly affected the conditions under which MEB occurred, regardless of the test fluid, and that the threshold laid around 20 K in the degree of subcooling. Saiki et al. [12] went on to confirm that MEB occurred on a horizontal circular heated surface of 10 mm in diameter as a result of liquid subcooling in excess of 20 K in the case of water. In addition, Suzuki et al. [11] carried out the experiments in connection with the heat transfer enhancement of MEB by applying ultrasonic vibration. For a degree of subcooling in excess of 20 K, the ultrasonic vibration was found to have no observable effect on the boiling of ethanol, but led to the instability of the liquid–vapor interface, intensifying and accelerating MEB for water and aqueous solutions of ethanol. Especially, it extends the MEB region to a higher wall superheat for aqueous solutions of ethanol subject to liquid subcooling exceeding 25 K. The behavior of vapor bubbles on a heated plate in an MEB regime is both complex and very fast,
so that the direct observation of the physical processes is quite difficult. Hence, Ueno et al. [13–15] determined the vapor–liquid interaction by employing a vapor generator that injected a superheated vapor into the subcooled pool through the orifice. They revealed that the condensation rate of the vapor bubble drastically increased under liquid subcooling exceeding 30 K, and that the rapid condensation of the vapor was caused by a liquid–vapor interfacial instability, where the liquid subcooling threshold value of the instability emergence basically corresponded to the conditions under which MEB occurs. Tang et al. [16] elucidated a correlation between the injected vapor velocity and the vapor behavior by classifying the vapor bubble behavior into types by using apparatus similar to the systems proposed by the group of the Tokyo University of Science [13–15]. Recently, Ito et al. [17] researched the heat transfer characteristics of MEB on flat surfaces. They changed the surface wettability from ultraviolet and gamma-ray irradiation before performing the boiling experiments. After irradiation, the boiling curve shifts to the higher wall superheat in the nucleate boiling region. In the MEB region, on the other hand, the boiling curve shifts to the lower wall superheat. Tang et al. [18,19] reported on the vapor bubble behavior and the effect of non-condensable gas on MEB. Moreover, they tried to understand the MEB formation mechanism by numerical simulation. The results by the simulation showed that Marangoni convection was caused by the non-homogeneous temperature distribution at the liquid–vapor interface. Although Marangoni convection for ethanol is much weaker than that for water under

 the same conditions of subcooling and wall superheat, they claimed that such convection due to the surface tension difference triggered the collapse of the vapor film, which induced transition to MEB. It must be emphasized that the physical mechanism of the MEB accompanying higher heat flux than the CHF has not been understood at all. Occurring conditions and heat transfer characteristics of MEB in different systems have also been investigated. Kumagai et al. [20,21] categorized MEB into three types according to the vapor behavior, pressure fluctuation, and sound pressure level in a flow boiling system. They defined three types, namely, stormy-MEB I (S-MEB I) and stormy-MEB II (S-MEB II) which were accompanied by a loud boiling noise and large pressure fluctuations, and calmMEB (C-MEB) which was observed at a lower peak pressure. In the observation of bubble behavior in MEB via a high-speed camera, the bubble growth was found to be extremely large comparing to nucleate boiling. Therefore, they clarified that the bubble collapse phenomenon in the MEB region was dominated by inertia. Furthermore, they performed subcooled boiling in four types of boiling systems by combining the flow pattern and boiling surface orientation, referring to the presence or absence of MEB generation. Tange et al. [7] measured the pressure drop and heat transfer characteristics of MEB for subcooled flow boiling in a rectangular mini-and microchannels. They observed that the heat transfer characteristics upstream differed from those downstream. Wang and Cheng [22] illustrated the effect of the mass flux and surface conditions on MEB in a subcooled flow system in a partially heated microchannel. Although MEB was strongly influenced by the inlet water subcooling, in the fully-developed MEB region, the effect of the liquid subcooling and mass flux on the boiling curves was minimal. In such ways, research into MEB has been ongoing since it was originally identified by Inada et al. [1–4] in 1981. Although many investigations into MEB have determined the conditions under which MEB occurs, as well as the heat transfer characteristics, the physical mechanism of formation and the dominant heat transfer mechanism are not yet clearly understood. It is thought that the main reasons for these are as described below. First, it is quite difficult to understand the spatial structure of coalescing bubbles on a heated surface due to the fact that this occurs under high heat-flux conditions, relative to nucleate boiling. In addition, the growth, condensation and collapse of the vapor bubbles occur regularly in a very short time. In recent years, knowledge has been accumulated on the dominant heat transfer mechanism in the saturated and/or relatively low subcooled pool under an isolated bubbles regime by employing the high temporal and space resolution system [23–26]. In contrast, these highly technical systems have not yet been extended under a higher heat flux because the total heat output of the system is limited. In this study, we performed a series of experiments addressing boiling on horizontal circular heated surfaces immersed in subcooled pool, paying special attention to the monitoring of the temporal variation in the surface temperature, the temperature gradient, and the quasi heat flux in transition to MEB. We discuss the process of the transition to MEB for two heated surfaces of different sizes, focusing on the correlation between the quasi-heat flux and the behavior of the vapor bubbles as captured by a high-speed video camera. Moreover, the effect of the size of the heated surface on boiling, vapor behavior, and heat transfer characteristics are discussed.
2. Experimental apparatus and procedure
Fig. 2 is a schematic diagram of the apparatus used for boiling on a plate. The stainless-steel vessel has three windows to allow us to observe the bubble behavior on the test area. The width, depth and height of the tank are 200 mm, 150 mm, and 150 mm,
respectively. Our experimental system is partially opened in order to maintain the pressure in the chamber at atmospheric pressure. We prepared two circular heated surfaces made of copper with different diameter as a test area. The test area was the top surface of a cylindrical rod machined from the copper block. The one of the heated surface was of 5 mm, and the other of 10 mm in diameter. The copper block was heated from the underside using three cartridge heaters (diameter of the heaters DH = 10 mm, length of those LH = 50 mm). The maximum heating power of each heater was 200 W at 100 volts. The copper block was surrounded by a thermal insulator consisting of glass wool and Bakelite. The cylindrical copper rod, wrapped in insulation, was stuck slightly into the test vessel. Silicone rubber was used to prevent water from coming into contact with the sides of the rod and the block, as well as to provide insulation. The top of the rod, that is, the test area, was left bare. This bare part of the rod was exposed to the subcooled pool of the test fluid. In order to monitor the temperature of the test surface, three K-type sheathed thermocouples, each 0.5 mm in diameter, were inserted into the rod at points 1 mm, 3 mm, and 5 mm from the test surface. The temperature of the heated surface and the heat flux were evaluated by applying the Fourier’s law to the measured temperature gradient, assuming a one-dimensional temperature distribution in the copper block as follows;

where k is the thermal conductivity of the block, x the position in the rod from the top surface, xi the position of the thermocouples (x1 = 1 mm, x2 = 3 mm, and x3 = 5 mm), Ti the temperature measured by the thermocouple at xi, N the total number of the measuring points (N = 3 in the present system) x and T the mean values of the position and the measured temperature, respectively. Distilled water, which had been degassed before the experiment, was used as the test fluid. The temperature of the test fluid was maintained at the designated level of subcooling by using a cooling channel made of copper connected to a constant temperature bath and a U-shaped heater immersed in the pool. We conduced a series of preliminary experiments in order to obtain the averaged temperature distribution in the chamber under the degrees of subcooling concerned in the present study by using a K-type sheathed thermocouple. We thus monitored the water temperature at a point 40-mm apart from the edge of the heated surface in the horizontal direction to define the temperature as a bulk temperature Tb. The experimental procedure was as follows. First, the test surface was polished with #1000 emery paper, whose averaged particle diameter was 24.5 lm, to equalize surface roughness. After the test vessel had been washed with acetone, the test fluid was poured into the vessel. The bulk liquid temperature was then increased using a U-shaped heater and the heated cooper block. Then, the fluid was degassed for more than 60 min under saturated boiling conditions. After degassing, the cooled water was pumped through the pipes to establish the thermal conditions of the pooled fluid, or the degree of subcooling DTsub = Tsat Tb, where Tsat indicates the saturation temperature under the atmospheric pressure. The liquid subcooling covered 0, 10, 20, 30 and 40 K in the present study. Once the designated temperature was reached, we started the experiment. To vary the surface temperature, the output of the cartridge heaters was regulated by changing the applied input voltage. The surface temperature was found to increase in steps with the output of the heaters, as observed by measuring the temperature of the heated block in the steady state. In this study, we plotted the heat flux to the boiling curve when the surface temperature and temperature distribution were regarded as being constant in averaged. On the other hand, we identified a ‘quasi-heat

 flux’ by applying the same evaluation formula via the Fourier’s law even when the temperature varied temporarily along the cylindrical rod. The behavior of the vapor bubbles on the plate was captured by a high-speed video camera (Photron FASTCAM APX-RS) at a frame rate of 0.1 million frames per second (fps) with a back-lighting system.
3. Results and discussion

Row (a) illustrates the results in the case of the test surface of 10 mm in diameter, and (b) in the case of the ones of 5 mm in diameter. The solid marks indicate the results for the heat flux when the temperature distribution in average is fully developed along the rod, while the null marks indicate the heat flux evaluated within a stage when the average surface temperature and the temperature distribution along the rod vary temporarily.  of 5 mm in diameter, the transition boiling infrequently takes place after CHF without a transition to MEB. The sudden rise in the wall superheat can also be observed in the results obtained by Inada et al. [1] and Tang et al. [18,19]. The variation of heat flux against the wall superheat in the MEB regime is much higher than that in the nucleate boiling regime. Such a trend of the heat-flux rise without practically increasing the wall superheat agrees well with the trend in S-MEB I reported by Kumagai et al. [20]. Fig. 4 shows the boiling curve including the transient state of surface temperature as presented by Ito et al. [17]. Fig. 4 indicates the boiling curve for a heated surface of 10 mm in diameter for liquid subcooling of 30 K. The vertical axis expresses the quasi-heat flux because it includes the transient heat flux obtained from the temperature of the transient condition in the rod. In the nucleate boiling regime, the boiling curve is quite smooth despite the transient condition. On the other hand, we confirmed that MEB has a higher heat flux with a surface temperature fluctuation. In the case of a relatively low heat flux in the MEB region, S-MEB I appears, accompanied by a violent boiling sound. When the output of the heater is increased further, S-MEB I and S-MEB II occur alternately. This significant increase in the wall superheat is caused by the onset of S-MEB II. This tendency was also observed in the results obtained by Kumagai et al. [20]. The boiling sound is higher pitched than that for S-MEB I. The cycle of growth, condensation, and collapse of the vapor bubbles is short. The vapor bubble behavior of S-MEB II is similar to that of S-MEB I, but the bubble height becomes lower, such that the vapor appears to be poised on the heated surface. It should be noted that the appearance of S-MEB II is rarely observed; it is not stable, and the quantitative reproducibility of S-MEB II is poor. Therefore, in the present study, we paid special attention to S-MEB I. Fig. 5 illustrates a typical boiling curve for each degree of liquid subcooling. For each level of subcooling exceeding 20 K, MEB occurs after the CHF as increasing the heat flux. Although it is commonly held that the transition boiling heat transfer expresses the mixed spatiotemporal heat transfer of the nucleate and film boilings, the transfer mechanism of the MEB with higher heat flux exceeding CHF differs from that of the transition boiling. In the regime of the nucleate boiling, the boiling curves are overlaid on each other without depending on the liquid subcooling. Therefore, it is thought that the heat transfer mechanism of the nucleate boil
ing regime is not dependent on the degree of subcooling. In contrast, in light of the conditions under which MEB occurs, which is affected by the liquid subcooling [9], the process whereby the vapor bubbles condense is closely related to the mechanism of MEB.

where L is the characteristics length of surface. Lienhard et al. [29] carried out a series of boiling experiments, in which they varied the dimensionless surface length using a range of test fluids. Gogonin and Kutateladze [30] and Rainey and You [31] conducted experiments with ethyl alcohol and FC-72 at the saturated temperature on flat horizontal plates of a range of lengths, in order to change the dimensionless length. Fig. 7 incorporates these results. The critical heat flux values are asymptotic for large surfaces and then suddenly increase as the surface area falls below a threshold value. The data that we obtained exhibits the same trend as that obtained by Rainey and You [31]. These facts suggest that the present experimental apparatus is highly effective and produces valid results. As aforementioned, the CHF becomes higher as the size of the test area is reduced. Bubble departure from the heated surface occurs much more frequently in the case of a smaller test area in the range of our experimental conditions. That is, it is easier to supply the cold bulk liquid to the heated surface (i.e. liquid inflow).
3.3. Vapor bubble behavior
Fig. 8 shows typical examples of the vapor bubble behaviors on a boiling surface of 10 mm in diameter. This figure illustrates the states of the vapor bubbles for different levels of wall superheat for boiling under liquid subcooling of 20 K. In an isolated bubble regime (Fig. 8a), general nucleate boiling occurs over the entire heated surface. The bubbles grow and condense repetitively immediately above the heated surface. In the regime near the CHF (Fig. 8b), coalesced bubbles cover almost the entire heated surface (t = t0 + 6, t0 + 9). After a period of growing, a significant shrinking of the neck of the vapor bubble takes place (t = t0 + 12), after which the bubble detaches from the heated surface, drastically decreases in volume caused by condensation (t > t0 + 18). With the departure of the bubble, the ambient fluid flows into the vicinity of the boiling surface. These processes take place repetitively. In the MEB regime (Fig. 8c), we observed that the behavior of the vapor bubbles is completely different from that of nucleate boiling. A large coalesced bubble breaks up into many coalesced bubbles on the heated surface, of many complex forms. MEB generates a loud boiling sound, as noted by Inada et al. [1]. The growth, condensation and collapse of the vapor bubbles occur regularly within a very short time without detaching from the heated surface. A number of tiny bubbles are constantly emitted from the heated surface when the vapor bubbles collapse. Even if they move further away from the heated surface, the emitted bubbles do not disappear. Because our experimental system is semi-opened, not a thoroughly closed as
realized in Ref. [23], it is thought that the test fluid contains non-condensable gas during the experiment even after degas procedure before experimental run, and that such tiny bubbles emitted from the heated surface contain the non-condensable gas. Kumagai et al. [20] reported that the bubble collapse in the MEB is inertia-dominant. With an increase in the heat flux, we confirm that the bouncing cycle of vapor bubbles (that is, the vapor-bubble collapse period) is shortened. Parker and Granick [32] observed the condensation process of a single vapor bubble using a diode laser beam. When the laser has a large local power, the vapor bubble (named ‘implosion bubble’) first grew rapidly, then collapsed, ultimately breaking up into many residual bubbles. The flow resulting from the collapse was reported to drive the residual bubbles away from the nucleation site, parallel to the surface. Then, water must be drawn towards the surface, given the conservation of mass balance. Based on their results, after the vapor bubbles collapse in the MEB regime, we believe that the subcooled liquid is drawn towards the dry surface. Fig. 9 indicates the trajectories of certain bubbles under the fully developed MEB above the heated surface of 5 mm in diameter under a degree of subcooling of 20 K. The images in the figure are obtained by inverting the gradation and by gamma correction for 0.8. In the first frame, the fine bubbles to be tracked are indicated with ‘x’ marks. The path lines of the tiny bubbles concerned are plotted between t0 and t0 + Dt in the second (Dt = 11.2 ms) and third (Dt = 22.4 ms) frames by accumulating the successive images. The positions of the tiny bubbles in the last image accumulated are indicated by ‘x’ mark. It is found that there exist two types of trajectories of the bubbles above the heated surface; the ones are the trajectories heading upward or going away from the heated surface, which are typically observed in the regime of the nucleate boiling as well. Such trajectories reflect the flow of the bulk liquid due to the buoyancy. The others are the trajectories heading downward or going toward the heated surface, which are quite unique in the boiling phenomenon. It is emphasized that such trajectories are always observed just above the heated surface during MEB. These trajectories toward the heated surface reveal that there exists inflow of the bulk liquid to supply the liquid to the heated surface. The net velocity of the bubbles drawn to the heated surface is crucially lower than that of the bouncing of the vapor bubble on the surface. By considering the net bulk liquid flow toward the heated surface and the increase of the frequency of the bubble collapse comparing to that in the nucleate boiling regime, the increase in the heat flux in MEB can be attributable mainly to transient conduction.

 3.4. Temporal variation of heat flux
Fig. 10(a) illustrates the temporal variations of the temperature detected by thermocouples inserted into the cooper rod. Fig. 10(b) indicates the temporal variation in the quasi-heat flux obtained by the temperature gradient. The arrows indicate the instances at which the output of the heater is varied to change the experimental condition. Noted that the temporal resolution of the thermocouples is inadequate comparing to characteristics time especially of the bouncing vapor on the heated surface (1/800–1/2000 s), and that the heat flux is thus described as quasi-heat flux in the present study. Despite of inadequate temporal resolution, the variations of detected temperature and evaluated quasi-heat flux can be clearly classified qualitatively according to the variations of the bubble behaviors. That is, in the ‘isolated bubble region,’ the temperature maintains almost constant in the fully developed state even though the vapor bubbles depart frequently, and the quasiheat flux does in the same manner. In the region of coalescence, the temperature at Z = 1 fluctuates even under fully developed state. In the MEB, the fluctuation of the temperature near the heated surface becomes more violently. It is noted that the amplitude itself does not directly reflect the vapor bubble behaviors under various conditions, but the continuous monitoring of the temporal variations of their amplitude brings us the information of the variations of the boiling regimes under the same degree of subcooling. Noted again that magnitude of oscillation in the ‘quasi-heat flux’ is not accurate, but we could detect the ‘trend’ of oscillation. The variations of the temperature and quasi-heat flux are described in the following. For boiling on the heated surface of 10 mm in diameter under liquid subcooling of 10 K, after approaching CHF, there is a drop in the quasi-heat flux with a significant increase in the temperature of the heated block as seem in row (1) of Fig. 10. After the quasi-heat flux falls, it then temporarily remains unchanged. This state may indicate the occurrence of transition boiling. However, the quasi-heat flux does not remain constant, such that the state of the boiling transitions to film boiling and the quasi-heat flux drops rapidly again. Such a moribund quasi-heat flux is observed only under liquid subcooling of 10 K; it is not observed for saturated boiling. In the case of boiling on a heated surface of 10 mm in diameter, under liquid subcooling of 20 K (row (2) of Fig. 10), the temporal variation in the heat flux is categorized into three different regimes depending on the quasi-heat flux fluctuation qualitatively. The first regime corresponds to an isolated bubbles region in the nucleate boiling as shown in Fig. 8(a). In this regime, the variation in the measured temperature and quasi-heat flux oscillation is small despite that the growth and condensation of the vapor
bubbles take place immediately above the entire heated surface. In the second regime of the coalescence bubbles, on the other hand, the quasi-heat flux starts oscillating violently. Repeated vaporization, drying out of the heated surface, and subsequent rewetting as the bubbles depart from the surface are accompanied in this regime as shown in Fig. 8(b), resulting in such that temperature of the heated surface oscillates widely. The third regime corresponds to the MEB as shown in Fig. 8(c). The quasi-heat flux oscillates as that in coalescence regime, but more violently. It is emphasized that the vapor bubble behavior, temperature distribution across the heated surface, and the mechanism whereby liquid is supplied to the boiling surface in MEB completely differs from those in nucleate boiling. The oscillation of quasi-heat flux in the region of coalescence and MEB are all observed under liquid subcooling equal to and exceeding 20 K. The oscillation of the quasiheat flux of 10 mm in diameter, is more violent than that of the smaller surface as shown in row (2)’. Such a trend can be explained by considering the resistance to rewetting by the cold bulk liquid approaching the boiling surface. For smaller surfaces, it is relatively easier to supply liquid to the heated surface from the side; it is considered that the fraction of the dry spots on the heated surface would be small and/or the duration of the existence of a dry spot be short. Therefore, we assumed that the fluctuation in the wall superheat would be smaller, that is, the oscillation in the quasiheat flux would be smaller in the case of 5-mm diameter surface. We emphasize that a significant decrease in the quasi-heat flux occurs immediately before the transition to the MEB and the quasi-heat flux rises again after a sudden drop for both heated surfaces. This significant decrease is observed only immediately before the occurrence of MEB. Additionally, for each degree of subcooling under which MEB occurs, we observe this abrupt decrease (row (3) and (3)’). In consequence, by tracking the temporal variation in the quasi-heat flux, it would be able to detect correctly the occurrence of MEB without observing the behavior of the vapor bubbles. A significant drop in the heat flux is caused by the dissipation of the liquid on the heated surface, and the boiling regime becomes a similar state to that of the near-film boiling as well as the transition from CHF to film boiling in saturated boiling. This is particularly notable in the case of boiling on the surface of 5 mm in diameter. Fig. 11 illustrates the behavior of the vapor bubbles in the transition to MEB on the heated surface of 5 mm in diameter under the degree of subcooling of 20 K. Immediately prior to the occurrence of MEB, the heated surface is completely covered by coalesced vapor bubbles in the similar way as for film boiling under saturated condition (t = t0). Subsequently, the coalesced bubble covering the test area condenses on the heated surface without departing

 nor emitting tiny bubbles, and their volume decreases (t0 + 1 6 t 6 t0 + 8). It is thought that there is almost no liquid on the boiling area because the vapor bubbles covering the heated surface do not increase in size. For this reason, contribution of evaporation heat transfer via latent heat on the heated surface must be small, such that the temperature of the heated surface increases rapidly. Moreover, contribution of transient conduction during the rewetting process must become also small due to the state of the vapor bubbles covering the entire surface. Therefore, the quasi-heat flux would decrease drastically since the heat transfer through the latent heat and transient conduction would not contribute under such a situation. After that, the vapor film covering the heated surface is exposed to the subcooled bulk liquid to collapse (t = t0 + 11), and tiny bubbles appear above the vapor film explosively (t P t0 + 11). This collapse of vapor over the heated surface drives the ambient fluid into the surface. Accordingly, vaporization heat transfer and transient conduction would occur again, and the quasi-heat flux increases considerably. Then, the vapor covering the heated surface start bouncing violently as illustrated Fig. 8 accompanying radial emission of tiny bubbles. It is obvious that the behavior of the vapor bubbles at this time differs from those in the states of the nucleate boiling nor of the transition boiling.
To summarize the above, a regime analogous to the film boiling occurs temporarily after CHF, but such condition is unstable to be maintained. Then a transition to MEB takes place as a result of the vapor film being breached. After the onset of MEB, we observed the collapse of the vapor bubbles, ultimately breaking up into tiny bubbles while maintaining a higher heat flux than CHF. This emission of microbubbles corroborates the results obtained by Tang et al. [18,19]. Note that one cannot apparently detect any fine disturbances over the vapor bubble surface in condensing process as observed in the system of vapor injection to the subcooled pool [15]. This can be attributed to the existence of a superheated liquid layer around the vapor bubbles above the heated surface in the case of MEB; the effect of condensation is less significant comparing to the vapor injection system. In order to comprehend the physical mechanism of MEB and to predict the heat transfer quantitatively, information on the temperature distribution across the heated surface and the liquid–solid contact fraction upon the occurrence of MEB must be indispensable. In the nucleate boiling regime, Nishio et al. [33,34] observed the state of liquid–solid contacts from behind a transparent boiling surface. Such an information under MEB would bring landmark progresses, and further researches would be needed focusing on

4. Conclusions
We conduct a series of pool boiling experiments on horizontal circular heated surfaces of 5 mm and of 10 mm in diameter, under atmospheric pressure. Using apparatus that can reliably indicate the CHF value, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) is found to occur on both test surfaces. As a result of measuring the temperature in a heated block and high-speed observations, we determine the following.
(1) MEB also occurs with the heated surface of 5 mm in diameter. (2) Under liquid subcooling equal to and exceeding 20 K, MEB takes place together with a significant increase in the wall superheat for both heated surfaces. For the occurrence condition of MEB pertinent to liquid subcooling, our results are in agreement with those obtained by Suzuki et al. [9] and Saiki et al. [12]. (3) In the MEB region, the vapor bubbles grow, condense and collapse repetitively within a very short period (at a frequency of about 800–2000 Hz). We illustrate that the collapse of the vapor bubbles drives the bulk liquid toward the heated surface. (4) By tracking the temporal variation in the quasi-heat flux, we reveal that the quasi-heat flux decreases significantly immediately before the transition to MEB. We elucidate that the monitoring of the heat flux enables us to detect the onset of MEB without observing the behavior of the vapor bubbles. (5) From the results of the observations, immediately prior to the occurrence of the MEB, the vapor bubbles thoroughly cover the heated surface in a similar manner as in the case of film boiling. Therefore, heat transfer via latent heat and transient conduction before the rewetting process could not contribute as in the nucleate boiling, and a substantial drop in the quasi-heat flux occurs as indicated above by 4. Subsequently, the vapor film collapses on the heated surface, and the transition to MEB is realized. Due to the supply of the bulk liquid toward the heated surface after the transition to MEB, the heat flux abruptly rises after its sudden drop.
Acknowledgments
This work was financially supported by the 45th Research Grant in Natural Sciences from The Mitsubishi Foundation (2014–2015). We gratefully acknowledge Prof. Koichi Suzuki (Tokyo University of Science, Yamaguchi) for contributing the portion on the experimental apparatus, and Mr. Yusuke Suzuki (Photron Ltd.) for generous supports by lending the high-performance, high-speed video camera.

title: Experimental study of sound emission in subcooled pool boiling on a small heating surface
abstractNote: In this study, sound emission in subcooled pool boiling with a small plate heater was investigated experimentally. Acoustic signals were recorded using a hydrophone and analyzed using sound pressure level (SPL), fast Fourier transform, and discrete wavelet transform (DWT). The results show that the SPL combined with an analysis of the sound signal by DWT can be used to identify boiling modes and heat transfer intensiﬁcation. In contrast to those for a large plate heater or long wire, the plot of the SPL against wall superheat in nucleate boiling region exhibits an ‘N” shape. Visualization results indicate that this difference mainly originates from the effects of heater size on bubble growth and departure. Compared with the nucleate boiling, in the region of microbubble emission boiling (MEB), the SPL exhibits a step increase of 10–20 dB, and continues to increase with the further increase of the heat ﬂux. A comparison of the bubble frequency and features of the acoustic signals shows that the loud boiling sound in the MEB could be mainly attributed to the vapor ﬁlm collapse and vapor volume oscillations, which also signiﬁcantly affect the heat transfer process.

    "content": "Experimental study of sound emission in subcooled pool boiling on a small heating surface
Jiguo Tang, Guo Xie ⇑, Jingjing Bao ⇑, Zhengyu Mo, Hongtao Liu, Min Du
State Key Laboratory of Hydraulics and Mountain River Engineering, College of Water Resource and Hydropower, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
highlights
Sound emission in subcooled boiling on a small heater was investigated. Different from that on large heater, SPL versus superheat took form of ’N’ shape. Boiling sound in MEB was related to collapse and volume oscillation of vapor film. SPL combined with DWT can predict boiling situations and heat transfer rate.
article info
Article history:
Received 22 January 2018 Received in revised form 28 April 2018 Accepted 2 May 2018 Available online 23 May 2018
Keywords:
Subcooled pool boiling Boiling sound Sound pressure level Spectral analysis Discrete wavelet transform
abstract
In this study, sound emission in subcooled pool boiling with a small plate heater was investigated experimentally. Acoustic signals were recorded using a hydrophone and analyzed using sound pressure level (SPL), fast Fourier transform, and discrete wavelet transform (DWT). The results show that the SPL combined with an analysis of the sound signal by DWT can be used to identify boiling modes and heat transfer intensification. In contrast to those for a large plate heater or long wire, the plot of the SPL against wall superheat in nucleate boiling region exhibits an ‘N” shape. Visualization results indicate that this difference mainly originates from the effects of heater size on bubble growth and departure. Compared with the nucleate boiling, in the region of microbubble emission boiling (MEB), the SPL exhibits a step increase of 10–20 dB, and continues to increase with the further increase of the heat flux. A comparison of the bubble frequency and features of the acoustic signals shows that the loud boiling sound in the MEB could be mainly attributed to the vapor film collapse and vapor volume oscillations, which also significantly affect the heat transfer process. Ó 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Boiling is always accompanied by an intense noise sound, which can be heard and easily detected. Owing to the high sensitivity, small response time, and relatively low price, acoustic measurements have various applications including a detection of boiling sound to identify boiling modes (Nishihara and Bessho, 1977; Bode, 2008; Rapisarda et al., 2015), measurement of boiling liquid level (Singh and Mohanty, 2018), and monitoring of boiling incipience (Geraldo et al., 2014; Shibahara et al., 2018; Alhashan et al., 2018) in many systems inaccessible by a direct measurement, including nuclear reactors, accelerators, and space power plants. An analysis of the sound emission can also help reveal boiling mechanisms (Zhang et al., 1999; Schroder and Bode, 2000;
Dorofeev and Volkova, 2005; Lloveras et al., 2012). For example, the critical heat flux (CHF) was recently detected with a noise spectral density inversely proportional with the frequency (1/f noise) (Vinogradov et al., 2007; Lloveras et al., 2012), which indicates that the boiling crisis could be attributed to non-equilibrium phase transitions in a boiling system. Boiling sound has been investigated over half century. It can be mainly attributed to bubble dynamics including bubble growth, condensation, departure, and collapse (Lee and Higgs, 2006; Vazquez et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2015; Alhashan et al., 2018). Early theoretical investigations of sound pressure originating from bubble growth and condensation in subcooled boiling were performed by Bessho and Nishihara (1976) and Schmidt et al. (1970). They observed that the largest value of the acoustic pressure appeared at the initial stage of the bubble growth. Once the bubble grew out of the thermal boundary layer, its growth rate began to decrease. Prior to reaching the maximum size of the bubble, the acoustic pressure became even negative during the oscillation.

 Dorofeev (1985) and Bode (2008) measured the real-time acoustic pressure wave during growth and condensation of individual bubbles, confirming the results of Bessho and Nishihara (1976). Dorofeev and Volkova (2005, 2006) observed three types of sound pressure waveforms based on the growth and condensation durations of a bubble, as shown in Fig. 1. A shorter condensation period leads to a larger relative oscillation amplitude at the condensation stage. An acoustic pressure pulse with high energy and frequency was detected by Plesset (1966) and Benes and Uher (2009) when a bubble collapses in a subcooled liquid or bursts at a free water surface. Ceccio and Brennen (1991) and Husin and Mba (2010), Husin et al. (2013) demonstrated that the sound was more intense for a larger bubble. Further studies by Divoux et al. (2008) showed that a larger viscosity leads to a larger acoustic energy release during the process of bubble burst. Wu et al. (2015) compared acoustic signals from experiments of nucleate boiling on a Zirlo-alloy-cladding tube associated with bubble growth, departure, and collapse, and concluded that bubble collapse was the most powerful acoustic source in highly subcooled nucleate boiling. Boiling sound can be measured with different types of acoustic sensors installed underwater, on the tank wall, or in the air. Acoustic measurement techniques for investigations of boiling sound are summarized in Table 1. These acoustic detection techniques are also widely used in marine, metallurgy, and chemical industries (Vazquez et al., 2015; Moghadam et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018). Hydrophone is a microphone designed to be used underwater for the detection of underwater sound, with a sampling frequency up to tens of MHz. Hydrophone employs piezoelectric transducers to detect sound waves; therefore, it does not require a power source, as it converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Although hydrophone is an intrusive device, not suitable for limited and compact spaces, it is very sensitive to pressure signals originating from underwater bubble oscillation or collapse. One of its drawbacks is that it can only detect sound or pressure fluctuations, limiting the ability to distinguish multiple objects and artificial noise (Vazquez et al., 2008). An alternative technique is to detect transient elastic waves within a material caused by the rapid release of localized stress energy (Baek et al., 2017) with acoustic emission (AE) sensors attached to the outer surface of a water tank or tube. As a direct contact in the process is not required, the AE sensor can monitor boiling without intrusion. Electret condenser microphone can also be used to measure the acoustic signal of boiling. The objective of the boiling sound detection is to correlate the intensity and frequency of the acoustic signals to boiling modes and heat transfer rate. Early studies of Westwater et al. (1955) showed that the boiling sound intensity was a function of the heat transfer rate; they presented the dependence of the heat flux as a function of the sound pressure level (SPL). Methyl alcohol was employed as the working fluid in their experiments with a copper bayonet heater with a diameter of 3/8 in. The SPL in nucleate and transition boilings increased monotonously with the wall superheat; it was almost unaffected in film boiling. Schwartz and Siler (1965) obtained another type of boiling sound curve for a thinner heated stainless-steel tube with a diameter of 0.093 in. The SPL first increased in the nucleate boiling and then decreased in the transition boiling with the increase of the temperature difference between the heater and liquid. At the film boiling region, the SPL started to increase again. Based on experimental data in the nucleate boiling, they proposed an empirical correlation between the SPL and wall superheat:

A third type of boiling sound curve was obtained by Aoki and Welty (1970) in experiments of boiling of n-pentane on a copper disk with a diameter of 2 in. The SPL started to decrease rapidly in the film boiling after reaching the maximum value in the transition boiling. Effects of system variables such as liquid subcooling, system pressure, and flow velocity on boiling sound were also investigated. Ponter and Haigh (1969) investigated the formation of sound during nucleate boiling on a stainless-steel tube with a diameter of 0.23 in. at atmospheric and reduced pressures. They observed that the SPL increased with the heat flux and liquid subcooling (0–60 K) under atmospheric conditions; it started to decrease after reaching the maximum under conditions of a reduced pressure and high subcooling. Nishihara and Bessho (1977) performed experiments of nucleate boiling on thin platinum and Ni–Cr wires under atmospheric pressure and pressurized conditions. The boiling sound at a very high subcooling had frequency components up to and even beyond 50 kHz; the boiling sound curve exhibited a similar tendency with that reported by Ponter and Haigh (1969) under a reduced pressure. Nishihara and Bessho (1977) attributed this behavior to the boiling mode, which was transformed from the isolated bubble region to the coalesced bubble region. All these studies concluded that the increase of the pressure led to a reduction of the sound intensity in the nucleate boiling. Kichigin and Kesova (1967) experimentally studied the sound frequency of flow boiling in an annular channel. They observed that the liquid velocity and subcooling had almost no influence on the boiling sound with varying heat flux. Rapisarda et al. (2015) experimentally showed that the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) decreased at a higher liquid flow rate, making the detection of the onset of boiling more challenging. In recent decades, microbubble emission boiling (MEB) has been observed in the transition boiling region under highsubcooling conditions (Inada et al., 1981). As MEB emerges at a relatively small wall superheat but with a heat flux higher than the normal CHF, it has a significant potential for applications in cooling of various devices with a high heat release (Suzuki et al., 2005). The sound generated in MEB significantly differs from those in the conventional nucleate boiling, transition boiling and film boiling. Although this has already been noticed by many researchers (Zeigarnik et al., 2012; Ando et al., 2016), only a few of them focused on its features and sound sources. Kubo et al. (1993) measured the sound emitted in nucleate boiling and MEB under flow conditions with a microphone in the air, and observed that MEB exhibited a larger SPL than that of the nucleate boiling. In addition,
two peeks around 1000 Hz and 10,000 Hz were observed in the frequency spectrum of sound signals in MEB, which significantly depended on the subcooling and heat flux. In contrast to the study of Kubo et al. (1993), the experiments of Tange et al. (2004) indicated that the dominant frequency of the sound detected in the MEB was approximately 2700 Hz. These results show that there is still no consensus with respect to the effects of the heat flux and liquid subcooling on the boiling sound. As shown in Table 1, most of the previous studies focused on the sound of boiling on a large or long heaters with characteristic lengths tens of times larger than the capillary length

For a small heating surface, as in a heated circular plate with Dh/Lb 4 (Dh is the heater’s diameter), bubble behaviors, heat transfer performance, and boiling sound, could significantly differ from those for a large heating surface (Zhang et al., 2016; Carey, 1992). Similar investigations have been performed; however, there were no extensive studies on the boiling sound. This study focuses on the sound in subcooled pool boiling on a small plane heater with a diameter of 10 mm, approximately 3.73.9 times larger than the capillary length. Three boiling modes were observed in the experiments: nucleate boiling, MEB, and transition boiling. The origin and characteristics of the boiling sound were analyzed in the time, frequency, and time–frequency domains, which revealed a criterion to identify different boiling modes.
2. Experimental setup
Fig. 2 shows the employed experimental setup, which was also presented in our previous study (Tang et al., 2017). It mainly consisted of a visualized water tank and copper block. Three heatresistant glass plates were embedded in its frame made of stainless steel as the observation windows. A copper block was placed at the bottom of the water tank, whose top surface with a diameter of 10 mm was used as the heating surface. Seven internal cartridge heaters were inserted into the block to supply heating power. A ceramic thimble was mounted around the block to thermally insulate it from the water tank. Two electric heaters and cooler were employed to control and maintain the liquid temperature. Three K-type sheathed thermocouples with a diameter of 0.5 mm were embedded underneath the heating surface at 2, 5, and 8 mm along the axial direction of the block. The water temperature was measured by another K-type thermocouple. An NI acquisition system was used to record temperature data. The heat flux q and temperature of the heating surface Tw were calculated using a least-square fitting of the measured temperature profile of the block:

where xi is the distance from the position of the ith thermocouple to the heating surface. A detailed uncertainty analysis was performed in a previous study (Tang et al., 2017). The boiling phenomenon was observed using a high-speed video camera (Photron: Fastcam SA5 model 1000K-M3) and back-lighting system. The obtained snapshots were processed with a series of image processing procedures implemented in MATLAB, introduced in our previous study (Tang et al., 2015a). Sound pressure fluctuations during subcooled boiling were detected using a hydrophone, positioned approximately 20 mm above the heating surface and 50 mm away from the central axis of the copper block. The signals were recorded using an NI dynamic signal acquisition system. The sampling frequency of acoustic signals can be set to 51,200 Hz, based on the Shannon sampling theorem (Zhou et al., 2016a,b); the sampling time is 1 s.
